question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 43423/22-27, to detail (i) how the circa £899,000 was spent; and (ii) any reason the circa £3.338million will be unspent at the end of the year.
answer_text: The provisional forecasted 2025/26 spend against the SEN Transformation budget is now £868K.  This is ring-fenced funding for the range of SEN Transformation projects approved by the Public Sector Transformation Board (PSTB).The following projects have been funded in 2025/26:Transforming the Support Model for children with a statement of SEN;SEN Teacher Professional Learning; Early Years SEN Assistants training project; Programme for 2–3-year-olds with additional needs; Transforming Inclusive Play Environments.  Programme management costs were also included within the 2025/26 expenditure.A stocktake review of the PSTB-funded projects took place from December 2025 to March 2026, during which time implementation was paused.  This resulted in an underspend on the 2025/26 budget.  A re-profiling of the £27.5m total funding allocation has been submitted to the PSTB for approval following completion of the stocktake review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 43287/22-27, to detail any actions undertaken by the NI Environment Agency to protect fish stocks at the Quoile River and surrounding areas, since 2012, including, (i) any deterrent measures deployed; (ii) monitoring of predator activity; (iii) measures taken to manage fish movement through the fish pass and grill systems; (iv) any assessment carried out regarding fish migration routes, including movement via Hare’s Island; and (v) the amount spent on these interventions.
answer_text: The answer to AQW 43287/22-27 described measures taken across my Department to protect fish stocks on the Quoile River and included any actions undertaken by the NIEA in this regard.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 43423/22-27, to detail how the allocated £8.07million was intended to be spent to support SEN Transformation.
answer_text: Following a successful bid to the Interim Public Services Transformation Fund, £27.5m was allocated to my department to support SEN Transformation over five years. An opening ring-fenced budget of £8.07m was allocated to support SEN Transformation in 2025/26.  This figure was based on initial costings and indicative figures submitted as part of the bid in October 2024, which were subject to final proposals from delivery partners. The £8.07m budget allocation in 2025/26 forms part of the £27.5m funded over five years. My Department’s bid approved by the Public Sector Transformation Board included a range of priority actions within the SEN Reform Delivery Plan.    The total package of funding will be spent by March 2029, on the following projects which support transformation of SEN support:Enhanced Support Model for children with a statement of SEN;Special Schools as Resource Centres of Expertise;Parental Support Programme;2-3 year old programme for children with additional needs;Learning Support trial in pre-school and Foundation Stage;Early years assistant SEN training;Speech, Language & Communications Intervention Toolkit & Programme;Transforming Inclusive Play Environments;Teacher & Classroom Assistant Professional Learning Programme;Inclusion Capacity Building Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount spent on promoting angling in each of the last five financial years, broken down by (i) programmes; (ii) marketing campaigns; and (iii) initiatives.
answer_text: During the last five financial years my Department has not incurred any expenditure on the promotion of angling through (i) programmes or (ii) marketing campaigns. Expenditure was incurred under (iii) initiatives through the Community Outreach Angling Events Fund, which provided £52,205 in total between 2018/19 and 2022/23 to support specific, timebound angling events rather than promotional activity. Within the last five financial years this amounted to £580 in 2020/21 (activities impacted by COVID-19), £6,843 in 2021/22 and £8,125 in 2022/23. The fund concluded in 2022/23.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any specific actions his Department has taken to ensure an appropriate balance between conservation of fish stocks and the promotion of angling as a recreational activity in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Over the last five years my Department has taken specific actions, including fisheries management, regulation and enforcement, habitat protection and management of the Public Angling Estate, to ensure an appropriate balance between the conservation of fish stocks and the promotion of angling. Within this framework, angling has been promoted and supported through conservationaligned engagement, including participation in competitions such as the annual Fermanagh and Pike Classic competitions, regular public and stakeholder events, including the Northern Ireland Angling Forum, North West Angling Fair, Celebration of Sport and the Irish Game Fair at Shane’s Castle, participation in the Bushmills Salmon & Whiskey Festival, and Departmentled habitat and access works.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any specific actions his Department has taken to promote angling as a recreational and tourism activity in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Over the last five years my Department has taken specific actions to promote angling through measures such as the Community Outreach Angling Events Fund, which operated during this period in 2020/21, 2021/22 and 2022/23. This fund supported individual angling events, regular engagement with angling clubs and stakeholders at information events, and promotion of the Public Angling Estate. Other measures have included participation at public events such as the annual Bushmills Salmon & Whiskey Festival and angling competitions, including the annual Fermanagh and Pike Classic competitions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any financial or practical support his Department has provided to local angling clubs and organisations in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Over the last ten years my Department’s support to angling interests has been delivered through its statutory fisheries management functions, including conservation of fish stocks and habitats, access arrangements and management of the Public Angling Estate		 Specific financial and practical support to angling clubs and organisations has been provided, with the nature and level of support varying over the period, including £52,205 in grants through the Community Outreach Angling Events Fund between 2018/19 and 2022/23, regular practical engagement through information events and partnership activity with angling stakeholders, chairing the NI Angling Forum and associated annual conference, competitionrelated engagement such as the annual Fermanagh and Pike Classic competitions, and Departmentled NASCO habitat works on some clubmanaged waters.		The Department has also provided practical, non-financial support through engagement with angling clubs on fisheries management, conservation measures, regulatory requirements and good practice guidance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been made of trends in angling participation rates over the last ten years, including any actions that have been taken in response to findings.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken a formal assessment of trends in angling participation over the last ten years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 31112/22-27, to detail when the reporting form for the public, due for release in March 2026, will be made available.
answer_text: The Incident Management System, which will include an online reporting form for members of the public to report environmental incidents was due to go live this week, but due to an unexpected IT issue there has been a delay. My officials are urgently rectifying this to allow the system to be publicly launched as soon as possible in the coming weeks.In the meantime, waste management issues can be reported via the NIEAwastecomplaint@daera-ni.gov.uk mailbox.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of women diagnosed with breast cancer over the age of 70, in each of the last ten years, broken down by (i) age bracket; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR), based at Queen’s University Belfast and funded by the Public Health Agency (PHA), produces the annual Official Statistics on cancer incidence, prevalence, and survival in Northern Ireland.  Publications by the NICR therefore constitute the authoritative source for breast cancer incidence figures.The NICR main website is: www.qub.ac.uk/research-centres/nicr/The Cancer Official Statistics Report (2024), which specifies the breast cancer incidence in women annually from 1993 to 2022 can be accessed at:https://www.qub.ac.uk/research-centres/nicr/file-store/official-statistics-1993-2022/november-24-release/Female_breast_cancer_report.pdfFurther summary information is also available in the Factsheet and Datasheets at:https://www.qub.ac.uk/research-centres/nicr/cancer-information/cancer-data-by-tumour-type/breast-cancer/The Datasheet provides specific information on breast cancer incidence in women from 1993-2022, (i) broken down by age bracket, and (ii) Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he has taken since the publication of the ADHD Needs Assessment Report in February 2026, in relation to the (i) funding; and (ii) implementations, of the Report's recommendations.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of nurses attached or available to special schools, in each of the last ten years; and (ii) the number who were made available in the summer time, broken down by school.
answer_text: (i) The extent to which nurses have been attached or available to special schools during the school year has varied across Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCT) over the last ten years. Belfast HSCT reports that throughout this period it has provided Community Children’s Nursing support within its special schools, namely Torbank, Mitchell House, Glenveagh, Fleming Fulton and Oakwood, with Cedar Lodge previously supported until its withdrawal in 2020. They advise that nurses are available to these schools during the school day, but it does not hold yearonyear numerical data on the number of nurses attached during term time.The South Eastern HSCT has also provided nursing support within a small number of its special schools during the school year. One nurse has been present at Clifton Special School and Parkview Special School, and at the Downpatrick site of Knockevin Special School from 2016 until April 2023. From April 2025, one nurse is present at Knockevin (Downpatrick) for two mornings per week. They report that Knockevin’s Castlewellan and Dundrum sites, Brookfield Special School and Killard Special School have not required a daytoday nursing presence over the last decade.The Northern, Southern and Western HSCTs confirm that they have not attached nurses to special schools at any point in the last ten years. In these areas, children’s healthcare needs during the school year are met through delegatedcare models, with Community Children’s Nursing teams responsible for assessment, care planning, training and clinical oversight of Education Authority classroom assistants. Specialist School Nursing teams continue to deliver the Healthy Child, Healthy Future programme but are not based in or attached to individual special schools.(ii) The availability of nurses attached to or supporting special school summer schemes has varied across over the last ten years. Belfast HSCT is the only HSCT that holds yearonyear data on nursing input, and records are available from 2018 onwards. Prior to 2018, special school summer schemes were staffed independently through private arrangements, and no HSCTheld information exists for those earlier years.Belfast HSCT figures for 2018–2025 are set out in the table below.Year20182019202020212022202320242025WTE3.739.60No data held6.758.276.718.354.03In the South Eastern HSCT area, nursing support has been provided only where children with the most complex healthcare needs attended summer schemes. This support relates to five special schools: Clifton, Parkview, Knockevin (Downpatrick), Brookfield and Killard. Across the past ten years, one nurse has been present during scheme hours at Clifton and at Parkview, and one nurse was in place at Knockevin between 2016 and 2022. The South Eastern HSCT confirms that Brookfield Special School and Killard Special School have not required a daytoday nursing presence during summer schemes. The South Eastern HSCT does not hold annual numerical data.The Northern, Southern and Western HSCT

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the Programme for Government funding allocated to his Department in 2025-2026; (ii) the amount of this funding that was utilised for its intended purpose; and (iii) whether any unspent or uncommitted funding was reallocated to address wider departmental pressures.
answer_text: Funding provided to my Department is generally not earmarked or linked to any specific Programme for Government (PfG) priority, even though a large proportion of the Education Budget could potentially be linked directly, or indirectly, to one or more of the PfG priorities or missions.However in 2025-26 the Executive allocated Earmarked funding to my Department specifically to fund the Early Years Childcare (ELC) Strategy, and Special Education Needs (SEN) Transformation, both of which can be linked to PfG priorities.  As this funding is earmarked it must only be utilised for its intended purpose.In June 2025, my Department was allocated £55.0m of ELC Strategy funding for 2025-26. The entirety of this funding has been used to support the Executive priority to Deliver More Affordable, Accessible, High-Quality Early Learning and Childcare. None of this funding has been used to address wider departmental pressures.In respect of SEN Transformation, the Executive agreed total funding to my Department of £27.5m over 4 years. This funding can be linked to two PfG priorities: Reform and Transformation of Public Services, and Better Support for Children and Young People with Special Educational Needs. At the start of the 2025-26 financial year, my Department was allocated a total of £8.07m to support SEN Transformation, of which it is anticipated that my Department will spend c£899k in 2025-26.   £3.833m has been returned to the Department of Finance during 2025-26, and it is anticipated that a balance of £3.338m will be unspent at year-end.  The total returned and unspent amounts have not been used against other Departmental or Executive pressures but will be reprofiled by DoF into my Department’s 2026-27 Budget for utilisation for the intended earmarked purpose, i.e. SEN Transformation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of nurses attached or available, to special school summer schemes, in each of the last ten years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions that have been undertaken to protect fish stocks at the Quoile River and surrounding areas since 2012, including, (i) any deterrent measures deployed; (ii) monitoring of predator activity; (iii) measures taken to manage fish movement through the fish pass and grill systems; (iv) any assessment carried out regarding fish migration routes, including movement via Hare’s Island; and (v) the amount spent on these interventions.
answer_text: The Quoile Pondage is an impounded estuary created by a flood defence structure at Hare Island operated and maintained by the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) Rivers. The Quoile is managed by my Department as a wild Public Angling Estate water, while the Quoile Pondage Nature Reserve is managed by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency.	My Department has not implemented any deterrent measures to protect fish stocks from natural predation at this site.	My Department does not monitor predator activity at the Quoile Pondage.	The Quoile Barrier at Hare Island operates to prevent tidal flooding by closing at high tide and allowing fluvial discharge at low tide. There are no grill systems on the Quoile River, and responsibility for the barrier rests with DfI Rivers.	My Department has not undertaken any assessments of fish migration routes within the Quoile catchment. Juvenile electrofishing surveys carried out on the Annacloy River in July 2021 recorded trout fry, with salmon fry; sticklebacks and eels were also present.	Electrofishing surveys are not individually costed for the Quoile River and are undertaken as part of my Department’s wider Assigned Work Programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any work his Department has undertaken to secure the reopening of Isle of Man territorial seas for fishing vessels with a crew operating under a Transit Visa or a UK Skilled Worker Visa.
answer_text: I met with Chris Thomas, Isle of Man Minister for The Treasury and Clare Barber, Isle of Man Minister for Environment, Food and Agriculture on Wednesday 15th April 2026 in the Isle of Man to discuss Isle of Man immigration rules affecting NI fishing vessels access to Manx waters. The purpose of this meeting was, inter alia, to seek arrangements that would allow our vessels continued operation in Manx waters with overseas crew.I want to see a way forward where crew with UK Skilled Worker visas would be eligible to fish in Isle of Man waters.  Otherwise, we are facing a situation where boats which have licences to fish in Isle of Man waters cannot actually do so, despite having crew which can work in the UK.Discussions were constructive and helpfully provided more information on the immigration rules in relation to Isle of Man waters. Currently available routes of access by the NI fleet were identified.  We have agreed to keep lines of engagement open. I will provide an update once further information becomes available and industry has been briefed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the Programme for Government funding allocated to her Department in 2025–26; (ii) the amount of this funding utilised for its intended purpose; and (iii) whether any unspent or uncommitted funding was reallocated to address wider departmental pressures.
answer_text: I can confirm the following information for my Department:The Programme for Government funding allocated to my Department in 2025-26 was £5 million;The full amount of this funding has been utilised for its intended purpose in relation to Safer Communities with allocations of £4.75 million to PSNI and £0.25 million to NI Prison Service; andThere was no unspent or uncommitted funding reallocated to address wider departmental pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the Programme for Government funding allocated to her Department in 2025–26; (ii) the amount of this funding utilised for its intended purpose; and (iii) whether any unspent or uncommitted funding was reallocated to address wider departmental pressures.
answer_text: In 2025-26 my Department’s opening budget included £1.5m ring-fenced for its Transformation Projects, namely Natural Solutions: Urban Drainage and Transforming Planning, which contribute to the Reform and Transformation of Public Services priority.As this funding is ring-fenced, it was used solely for its intended purpose. Any unspent or uncommitted funding was returned to the Department of Finance, as this could not be retained for use within my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to develop specialist epilepsy clinics, including how these plans will align with a standardised regional epilepsy care pathway, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department’s Regional Review of Neurology report acknowledges the importance of timely access to specialist care for people with a neurological condition. In this context, the report proposes a specific recommendation for the development of Specialist Clinics for the most common conditions including epilepsy in each Trust area. Initial work has also been undertaken during the Review to develop draft conditionspecific pathways, including an epilepsy pathway. It is intended that this approach will ensure care is consistent, coordinated, equitable and personcentred, ultimately reducing variation and improving outcomes for people with epilepsy. The Review report recommends the finalisation of these pathways within the first two years of implementation.Further progress on implementing the report recommendations will be dependent on securing additional investment and expanding the neurology workforce, which will be challenging given existing budgetary and resourcing challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to expand neuropsychology services to ensure timely mental health support for people with epilepsy and other neurological conditions.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to enhance GP training in epilepsy management, as recommended in the May 2025 Regional Review of Neurology Services Final Report.
answer_text: My Department’s Regional Review of Neurology recognises that for many people with a neurological condition, effective support in primary care is a critical part of the pathway. In this context, the report proposes a range of recommendations which, if implemented, will enable GPs to better support people with neurological conditions, including epilepsy. Recommendations include the roll out of referral management processes already available in some Trusts. This will ensure timely advice to GPs to effectively manage people in the community and avoid unnecessary outpatient appointments. The report also proposes the further development of GPs with Enhanced Roles in Neurology. To support this the report notes the importance of collaborative education programmes such as Project ECHO, an online learning and support mechanism which encourages the sharing of specialist knowledge and best practice to create a community of practice. It is intended that Project ECHO will be established in primary care.My officials have completed an analysis of responses to the public consultation on the Review report. Subject to funding, it is intended that a Neurology Delivery Team will be established to develop an implementation plan and lead on implementation. I look forward to receiving the Consultation Analysis Report and an assessment of the next steps in the coming weeks.I must be clear, however, that implementation of the Review’s recommendations would require significant investment. Given that the draft multi-year budget proposed for my Department is far below what is needed, it is likely that difficult funding decisions will be required. The scale and pace of implementation will therefore have to be adjusted in line with funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the date when the new Regional Obesity Management Service will launch.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to address the shortfall in epilepsy specialist nurses, given that 14.1 whole time equivalent nurses are in post compared to the 34.2 required to meet population need.
answer_text: My Department’s investment in education and training has driven growth in the HSC workforce and reduced vacancy rates.  This strategic approach to workforce development, supported by sustained investment in pre-registration education and training, has facilitated a 16.7% (+2,436) increase in whole time equivalent registered nursing staff in post across the HSC between June 2020 and June 2025. The annual cohort size of specialist nurse training places, including epilepsy nurses, is determined by Trusts’ workforce planning and learning needs analysis, ensuring alignment with regional strategic priorities. The Department approves annual Education Commissioning Plans to ensure the Nursing and Midwifery workforce is adequately equipped with the necessary skills and knowledge to deliver safe and effective care, lead transformational change and meet the post registration workforce planning recommendations. While there is a clear need for more specialist nurses and Advanced Nurse Practitioners across the HSC system, we must acknowledge that training these professionals and sustaining a pipeline of nurses ready to progress into these roles takes time as well as investment.An updated Specialist Nurse Role Framework has been developed, outlining key knowledge, skills and educational requirements. This Framework provides clear expectations for specialist nursing practice and helps retain these nurses through consistency, structure, and direction around career progression, whilst aligning to the Northern Ireland Career and Development Model for Nursing & Midwifery, which was launched in December.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 37291/22-27, to detail (i) the rationale as to why individual costs are not available for the years preceding 2024-25; and (ii) where that data is now.
answer_text: I can confirm that the information provided in the original response for AQW 37291/22-27 fully reflects the information held by DAERA, which presented the overall spend without the breakdown by building. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the rational as to why just four football leagues were included in the eligibility criteria for the NI Grassroots Football Fund.
answer_text: The league in which a Grassroots Football Club plays is not part of the eligibility criteria for the Pilot Stage of the Grassroots Facilities Programme. When applying clubs are asked to select which of the four Irish FA Divisional Associations they are registered to. Where a club is not registered to a Divisional Association, they are asked to select the Association which is most appropriate based on the location of their project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether any assessment has been made of the need for targeted support for (a) disabled farmers; and (b) young people with disabilities who wish to participate in farming; and (ii) whether he will consider introducing a dedicated scheme to address accessibility, safety and inclusion on farms.
answer_text: All farm businesses can avail of the financial support available through the Schemes within my Department’s Sustainable Agriculture Programme providing they meet Scheme eligibility requirements. Farms delivering Social Farming in Northern Ireland are supported through the Social Farming Support Service (SFSS), which was established in 2015, and is delivered by the Rural Support Charity, on behalf of DAERA.  The Social Farming Capital Grant Scheme (SFCGS) 2025/26 offered capital grant aid to eligible Social Farms to adapt their facilities and improve accessibility with the aim of enhancing the experience for Social Farming service users and developing the social farm enterprise. Both Schemes are funded through the DAERA’s Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Framework.Health & Safety training is offered through CAFRE, as part of modules, to everyone, regardless of age or ability. The Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI) also works to improve safety standards on farms, and have a number of key campaigns and initiatives to help achieve this.As such, there are no plans for a dedicated scheme requested.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether (i) his Department; and (ii) its arm's-length bodies, have any grants or funding streams available to assist (a) farmers with disabilities; and (b) farm families with disabled members, to improve accessibility and adapt farmyards, buildings and equipment.
answer_text: Whilst my Department nor its arm’s length bodies have any dedicated grants or funding streams available specifically to assist farmers with disabilities or for farm families to improve accessibility and adapt farmyard, buildings and equipment all farm businesses can avail of the financial support available through the Schemes within my Department’s Sustainable Agriculture Programme providing they meet Scheme eligibility requirements.Farms delivering Social Farming in Northern Ireland are supported through the Social Farming Support Service (SFSS), which was established in 2015, and is delivered by the Rural Support Charity, on behalf of DAERA.  The Social Farming Capital Grant Scheme (SFCGS) 2025/26 offered capital grant aid to eligible Social Farms to adapt their facilities and improve accessibility with the aim of enhancing the experience for Social Farming service users and developing the social farm enterprise. Both Schemes are funded through the DAERA’s Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Framework.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any funding that has been allocated towards medical technology access for patients with type 1 diabetes, in each of the last five years.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps that are being taken to improve access to medical technology for patients with type 1 diabetes, in line with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidance TA943.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's plan for the implementation of National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidance TA943 for technology access for patients with type 1 diabetes.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what the quantum of Transformation funding is; (ii) what it will be spent on; and (iii) any relevant timeframes.
answer_text: My Department has to date been allocated £61million of Transformation funding for one project.The Primary Care Multidisciplinary Teams (MDT) programme will increase the range and delivery of care in primary and community settings and boost capability and capacity for early intervention and prevention.  The programme includes the deployment of mental health practitioners, social workers and first contact physiotherapists in primary care settings to work alongside existing practice teams. Phase 1 will run until 2028-29 and will complete the roll out of MDTs in the 7 GP Federation areas where implementation has already commenced and provide for MDT expansion into a further 5 Federation areas.  Subject to further funding being available, Phase 2 will run for a further 4 years from 2029-30 and will implement the MDT model in the remaining 5 GP Federation areas.The current profile for Transformation funded MDT spend is shown in the table below.Year2024/252025/262026/272027/282028/29Total£m0.96.413.117.922.761.0

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when he expects a decision or confirmation on the Public Sector Transformation funding, given it is linked to the roll-out of Together for Families, a new family support model.
answer_text: My Department submitted a proposal to the Public Sector Transformation Fund in August 2025 for Together for Families, a new relational, community-based, trauma-informed model of family support, which focusses on prevention and early intervention. Subject to funding being confirmed, it is intended to implement the model across Northern Ireland.  I understand that an announcement on transformation funding will be made in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 41257/22-27, whether any assessment has been made of the reasons why, since 31 December 2021, (i) Administration and Clerical staff have increased from 145.7 to 218.2; (ii) Paramedics have increased from 457.6 to 486.6; (iii) Emergency Medical Technicians have decreased from 325.1 to 270; (iv) Ambulance Officers have increased from 120.5 to 161.7; (v) Patient Care Services staff have reduced from 279.6 to 240.4; and (vi) Control Assistants have reduced from 166.5 to 134.6.
answer_text: My Department is working closely with the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) to develop and implement a 10year workforce plan, informed by the independent Operational Research in Health (ORH) modelling undertaken during 2025/26, to reflect ongoing service transformation, recruitment activity, workforce development and changes in operational delivery models. This will guide future capacity, skill mix, care pathways and collaborations across the system to ensure NIAS is equipped to meet population needs.The combination of ongoing recruitment, local training pathways, new clinical roles and organisational reform is central to understanding the changes observed in staffing levels across NIAS since December 2021.The increase in administration and clerical staffing is associated with the significant programme of reform and expansion underway within NIAS. Additional administrative capacity has been required to support new service models, strengthened governance, increased recruitment activity, and enhanced Emergency Operations Centre (EOC) and Emergency Preparedness, Resilience and Response (EPRR) functions.Paramedic numbers have increased as a result of sustained investment in clinical capacity and the commissioning of the paramedic degree programme at Ulster University’s Magee campus. My Department has increased annual student places from 50 to 65, supporting a pipeline of locally trained paramedics. 41 newly qualified paramedics from this programme have already taken up positions within NIAS. A UKwide recruitment process also remains in place to further supplement the workforce.The reduction in emergency medical technician (EMT) numbers is primarily linked to normal workforce turnover as well as progression into other clinical roles. NIAS has commissioned independent demand and capacity modelling, which is informing the future clinical model and workforce profile, including the balance between EMTs, paramedics and emerging enhanced roles.The increase in ambulance officers reflects the requirement for strengthened operational leadership, governance and resilience as NIAS implements a wideranging transformation programme and enhances its management capacity within operations and the EOC.Patient care services (PCS) staff numbers have reduced mainly due to staff progressing into other roles, including development into the EMT (advanced ambulance practitioner) role. NIAS continues to run ambulance care attendant (ACA) training, with one cohort currently in progress and another planned for July. Completion of these will return ACA staffing to core funded levels.NIAS advises that the reduction in control assistants is partly explained by staffing transfers associated with the establishment of the Integrated Clinical Hub (ICH). This service change created new clinical and senior roles within the ICH. A recruitment campaign for emergency medical dispatchers has recently completed its first stage, with assessments underway and a planned intake in Septem

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the source of the funding for the 60 new support officers across Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) whether any of these posts are linked to Transformation funding.
answer_text: My department has had to prioritise funding for the 60 AfC Band 5 Social Work Senior Support Officer posts from within our resource budget baseline.While the overall financial position remains extremely challenging, we continue to implement efficiencies and reduce non-essential expenditure to maximise the use of available resources. This approach supports the protection of front-line services as far as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the publication of the McBride Hill Report 2025, whether he intends to carry out any scoping exercises across all Health and Social Care Trusts to ascertain whether there are similar issues in other Trusts.
answer_text: In any large and complex organisation cultural challenges may arise, such as those identified by the McBride/Hill report. I am committed to ensuring that the overall culture of our healthcare system is welcoming to all staff and that anyone with concerns that are raised will be listened to and supported in a timely and transparent fashion. I am focused on strengthening openness, speaking-up arrangements and governance across all Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts being cognisant of each HSC Trust’s management and senior management (up to Board level) roles and responsibilities in relation to identification and rectification at the earliest stage possible. I am also focused on any cultural and behavioural challenges; as well as the provision of appropriate and timely support to all staff, who may find themselves involved in such challenges. All HSC Trusts are required to work according to reforms, such as the Being Open Framework and strengthened Patient Safety & Quality Committees. Furthermore, the duty of candour legislation will strengthen the culture of openness and accountability. In addition, I have asked officials to explore options for a regional independent Freedom to Speak Up service and a dedicated HSC whistleblowing avenue in order to provide staff working within HSC with clearer, trusted and consistent routes to raise concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount spent on nursing agency staff, in the last 12 months, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) average; and (ii) longest, waiting time for patients attending the Emergency Department at the Ulster Hospital, in each of the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Departmental Information and Analysis Directorate (IAD) publishes the median and 95th percentile time from a patient’s arrival in an Emergency Department (ED) to either discharge or admission.  These statistics are published on a quarterly basis.  The most recent publication is available at the following link: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-waiting-time-statistics-emergency-care-waiting-times-october-december-2025 IAD does not publish average or longest waiting times. Averages are sensitive to extreme values and can provide a biased outlook, therefore median is the preferred measure to report on ED waiting times. Presently, all figures sourced from encompass are considered to be ‘statistics in development’.  IAD are undertaking a validation exercise on ED data derived from the encompass system.  Until this exercise is completed, my Department cannot guarantee the accuracy of any information regarding the longest individual waits and therefore data cannot be reliably reported at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of disabled parking spaces available at the Ulster Hospital; (ii) any assessment his Department has undertaken of whether this provision meets demand; and (iii) whether there are any plans to increase the number of disabled parking spaces on site.
answer_text: The aim of the Department of Health’s policy on car parking is to ensure the effective provision and management of car parking across all of the Health Estate for patients, visitors and staff. The policy states that an adequate number of parking spaces should be provided for people with a disability and that this provision should comply with current legislation as well as taking account of other best practice guidance.	The decisions on how the Department’s policy is applied are for each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust to determine to enable them to take local issues and circumstances into account.The Ulster Hospital has 100 disabled car parking spaces located across the site, including in the dedicated Blue Badge car park at the front of the hospital, the multistorey car park, the Emergency Department car park, as well as the Ambulatory Care and Renal car parks. These spaces represent approximately 10% of all public parking provision.The South Eastern HSC Trust regularly reviews parking usage across the Ulster Hospital site to ensure that provision remains appropriate, with ongoing monitoring of occupancy levels and user feedback informing any adjustments to disabled parking provision. There is no evidence to suggest that the current supply of disabled parking spaces is inadequate, although it is recognised that demand for all parking spaces will be high at peak times.There are currently no plans to increase the number of disabled parking spaces on the Ulster Hospital site. Carparking capacity is constrained by the geography of the site, planning restrictions, and the number of entrances and exits serving the site. Blue badge setdown zones have been introduced at main entrances, with a 20minute dropoff and collection period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the current paternity leave entitlements available to teachers employed by the Education Authority, including (i) the duration of leave; (ii) the rate of pay; and (iii) whether leave must be taken in consecutive blocks.
answer_text: Following the birth of a child or the placement of a child for adoption, teachers who are eligible, have the right to take either 1 or 2 weeks paid statutory paternity leave to care for the child or support the mother or adopter.The following conditions must be satisfied to qualify for paternity leave:Have or expect to have responsibility for their child’s upbringing.Be the biological father of the child or the mother’s husband or partner (including same-sex partner or civil partner) or be the adopter (in the case of joint adoption) or adopter’s spouse or partner (including same-sex partner or civil partner); and Have completed 26 weeks’ continuous service as a teacher with one or more employing authority at the end of the fifteenth week before the baby is due or the end of week the adopter is notified of being matched with a child.Eligible teachers may take the statutory leave in blocks of either one or two consecutive weeks, but not odd days.Under the existing arrangements teachers may apply for up to three days paid paternity leave at the discretion of the Board of Governors.  Statutory and discretionary leave cannot be taken separately.During their paternity leave, eligible teachers are entitled to Statutory Paternity Pay (SPP).  The SPP is a UK wide statutory amount and is currently £187.18 per week.  Teachers will receive this for the two weeks they propose to take.If a teacher proposes to take the three days discretionary leave available, they will be paid their normal rate for those three days with the rest of the proposed two week paternity period paid at the SPP rate.All details of teachers paternity leave and pay provisions can be found in TNC 2014/4 (TNC 2014-4 Teachers' paternity leave scheme)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement he has had with Executive colleagues regarding a review of paternity leave entitlement across the public sector, including consideration of greater flexibility and enhanced provision comparable to reforms introduced elsewhere in the United Kingdom.
answer_text: My policy responsibility extends to people policy for the Civil Service. Paternity leave arrangements in the Civil Service currently exceed statutory requirements. 		Responsibility for statutory paternity leave arrangements rests with the Department for the Economy and reform of this is being considered as part of the “Good Jobs” Employment Rights Bill.I have engaged with Executive colleagues on the proposed Employment Rights Bill and am fully supportive of the progressive package of reforms to strengthen employment rights and protections across our labour market.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how paternity leave entitlements for teachers in Northern Ireland compare with those available to teachers in (i) England; (ii) Scotland; and (iii) Wales; including any differences in (a) pay; (b) flexibility; and (c) eligibility from day one of employment.
answer_text: Teachers in England, Scotland and Wales are entitled to 1 or 2 weeks of statutory paternity leave to care for a new child, provided they have worked for their employer for 26 weeks by the 15th week before the expected birth.Paternity leave must be taken within 52 weeks of the birth or adoption placement and can be taken in one or two consecutive blocks or two separate one-week blocks.Paternity Pay rate is the same throughout the UK and is currently set at £187.18 per week.Northern Ireland differs from the rest of the UK in that the two weeks paternity leave requested cannot be taken separately and the full leave requested must be taken within 56 days of the birth of the child or date of placement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will review the requirement that statutory paternity leave for teachers must be taken in consecutive weeks, and consider allowing leave to be taken in separate one-week blocks within a defined period following the birth of a child.
answer_text: Teachers’ Pay and Conditions of Service are negotiated and agreed through the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) which is made up of employing authorities, employer representatives, representatives of the five main teaching unions as well as Departmental representatives.  Decisions on amending pay and conditions guidance including paternity guidance are considered by the committee. TNC will be reviewing the Teachers' Paternity Leave Scheme TNC 2014/4 which provides advice and guidance on all aspects of paternity leave and pay.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will bring a draft Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland to the Executive for approval.
To ask the Minister of Health whether any consideration is being given to adapting the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme to cover procedures carried out in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As part of a range of measures under the Elective Care Framework (ECF), the Department works closely with providers in the Independent Sector (IS) in Northern Ireland to tackle the backlog of patients on HSC waiting lists.  Given the significant pressure on our health and care system, it has been necessary to make use of IS capacity to ensure that patients with the greatest clinical need are seen and treated as quickly as possible. This approach helps ensure that those who may otherwise have waited longer are able to access treatment in a more timely way. The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme (WLRS) sits alongside these IS arrangements, though with different parameters. The re-activation of arrangements previously referred to as the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) helps to facilitate the transfer and treatment of long waiting patients, without impacting on core or local IS capacity.  The option of including private healthcare providers in NI within the WLRS would reduce the resource available to the HSC for buying in additional capacity for the most urgent cases. This could be detrimental to patient outcomes. In addition, adapting the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme to cover procedures carried out in Northern Ireland would require a legislative change. At present, the Department’s focus remains on maximising the impact of existing IS arrangements alongside the WLRS to support the reduction of waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the timetable for consultation with the primary school sector on the draft curriculum framework.
answer_text: I expect to receive the draft curriculum framework in the coming weeks and intend to launch a full public consultation before the summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any international education systems considered when developing proposals for the new primary school curriculum.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum (Crehan, 2025) drew upon a broad and robust evidence base, including systemlevel performance data, international research, cognitive science, and extensive stakeholder engagement. The Review concluded that a new statutory curriculum should be purpose-led, knowledge-rich, continuous, coherent, specific, focused, flexible and inclusive. The Review found that the current curriculum framework is too high-level, leading to inconsistency in delivery across schools, with teachers often left to make disproportionate decisions about content. Additionally, the current framework lacks coherence, making it difficult for learning to build logically over time, as there is not clear, well-sequenced content for each subject supported by high-quality resources.The aim of introducing a ‘knowledge rich curriculum’ is to ensure the curriculum framework is clearer, more coherent and better connected across key stages. This approach is grounded in cognitive science that demonstrates that learning depends on building long-term memory and structured schemas because working memory is limited. Sequenced, coherent knowledge enables higher-order thinking and problem-solving. Internationally there is a growing trend towards this approach to curriculum design, often described as ‘knowledge rich’.Singapore consistently leads global rankings by combining rigorous content with cognitive principles such as mastery and spaced practice, resulting in the highest proportion of top performers and one of the lowest proportions of low achievers.Portugal also offers a clear example of impact, experiencing a dramatic improvement during the 2011–2015 knowledge-rich phase which was then reversed when the country shifted to a competency-based model.England’s 2014 curriculum reform coincided with a sharp rise in PISA rankings, moving from 28th in mathematics and 25th in reading in 2009 to 14th and 13th respectively by 2022.Estonia, which emphasises core knowledge alongside teacher autonomy, ranks first in Europe for mathematics, science, and reading, with strong equity outcomes. Similarly, Canadian provinces such as Quebec, which maintain structured syllabi and prioritize foundational knowledge, outperform most OECD peers, particularly in mathematics and reading.The knowledge-rich curriculum – what is the evidence? (Muijs et al., 2025) reviewed empirical evidence on the impact knowledge rich curricula has on pupil outcomes. The paper provides examples of where such curricula approaches have supported improved reading comprehension as well as broader attainment. These benefits are often strongest for pupils at risk of educational disadvantage providing the approaches are implemented early and systematically, accompanied by structured support.The Curriculum Policy Report (Centre for Education Systems 2025) explains that curriculum specificity is important for equity as this provides clear, detailed curriculum expectations that help ensure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the proposed new curriculum framework will retain the current emphasis on play-based learning within Foundation Stage in primary schools.
answer_text: The Independent Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum highlighted the importance of developmentally appropriate approaches in the early years, including Foundation Stage, and this will inform the ongoing development of the new curriculum framework.   Furthermore, work is ongoing to develop a set of key pedagogical principles that will be sensitive to subject and stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of practising primary school teachers involved in the working groups currently drafting the new curriculum framework.
answer_text: The number of practising primary school teachers who are members of the Curriculum Review subject working groups is 23.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 38049/22-27, to detail the main reasons for the Western Health and Social Care Trust deficits, between 2019 and 2022.
answer_text: The main reasons behind the deficits reported between 2019 and 2022, were: savings retractions that had not yet been delivered; growth in community care packages and looked after children; andincreased medical and nursing costs as a consequence of a higher level of workforce gaps (vacancies and gaps in rotas), and market pricing of temporary and locum staff.In each of these financial years, the Department of Heath allocated the WHSCT a Control Total which the Trust was able to operate within. In 2019 the Trust agreed to a three year Recovery Plan in relation to the underlying deficit, and commenced a three year programme of work which aimed to return the Trust to a financially sustainable position. In January 2021, the DoH agreed the plan could be extended for a further year in recognition of the impact of COVID-19.The Trust returned to a breakeven position in 2022/23, this was achieved through a combination of cash savings, regional funding and management/prevention of pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 38049/22-27, to detail the total net financial position, including (i) underspend; and (ii) overspend, in each of the last five financial years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust,
To ask the Minister of Health to detail why £80 million was set aside for Programme for Government targets last year when only £6.5 million was required.
answer_text: Within the 2025-26 final budget, the Executive earmarked £80 million to support increased elective care capacity, however this funding had already been included in my Department’s draft budget baseline as a general allocation.  The Executive also provided flexibility with this funding for it to be redirected to address other significant pressures within the Health and Social Care (HSC) system.While I remain determined to drive down waiting lists, I also have a duty to manage within the budget my department has been allocated. At the beginning of this financial year my department was faced with a funding gap of some £615 million and therefore all options to reduce this gap had to be considered.Given the flexibility provided around the £80 million waiting list funding my officials advised me that an option to reduce the funding gap would be to redirect any uncommitted element of this. At the time this was being considered some £6.5 million had already been committed, leaving a balance of £73.5 million. Taking account of the scale of the Department’s financial deficit, and the progress being achieved against our Programme for Government (PfG) targets through other waiting list funding streams, I gave approval that the remaining £73.5 million could be used to help reduce the overall funding gap.If we hadn’t been anticipating exceeding our PfG targets and in a better financial position I would have directed my officials to develop proposals to use more of this funding in building capacity within elective care. Looking forward to next year it is my firm intention to fully utilise all the funding earmarked for Waiting Lists as proposed by the finance minister in the 2026-29 Draft Budget in line with the Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the amount that has been set aside for Programme for Government targets for the upcoming financial year; and (ii) how this figure has been determined.
answer_text: Budget settlements for 2026-27 and beyond have not yet been agreed by the Executive, however, the Finance Minister’s draft budget proposals include commitments for Executive Earmarked funding aligned with the Programme for Government (PfG) priorities. For my Department, this includes £165 million for Elective Care and Waiting Lists.My Department submitted a bid for £300 million for Elective Care and Waiting Lists. The proposed allocation of £165 million reflects the reinstatement of 2025/26 funding. While this is welcome, as it is essential to meet inescapable pressures for red-flag and time-critical patients and to continue building vital capacity, it falls short of what is required to fully address the scale of waiting list demand.In addition to this proposed earmarked funding, the transformation funding agreed for Health will allow the further roll out of Multidisciplinary Teams (MDTs) to continue, supporting the commitment to reform and transform public services.There are many other areas of my Department’s expenditure that align with PfG commitments. These range from primary and community care reform to workforce development and digital transformation.However, given the total funding available to Northern Ireland and the budget proposals published by the Finance Minister, the future financial position for my department will remain extremely challenging.Despite this, I remain committed to delivering the best possible outcomes with the resources allocated.Finally, I would emphasise that meaningful progress against PfG targets and improvements in public services more broadly, will depend on collective action and cooperation across the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the decision-making process that led to £73.5 million of the £80 million for Programme for Government targets being diverted to reducing wider budget pressures.
answer_text: Within the 2025-26 final budget, the Executive earmarked £80 million to support increased elective care capacity, however this funding had already been included in my Department’s draft budget baseline as a general allocation.  The Executive also provided flexibility with this funding for it to be redirected to address other significant pressures within the Health and Social Care (HSC) system.My officials advised me that improvements in productivity together with the additional activity being driven forward with the ringfenced £85 million red flag/time critical waiting list funding and the new activity planned from the investment of the £50 million waiting list funding, meant that the Programme for Government target of 70,000 interventions was going to be exceeded. While it remains my priority to drive down waiting lists, I also have a duty to manage within the budget my department has been allocated. At the beginning of this financial year my department was faced with a funding gap of some £615 million and therefore all options to reduce this gap had to be considered.Given the flexibility provided around the £80 million waiting list funding my officials advised me that an option to reduce the funding gap would be to redirect any uncommitted element of this. At the time this was being considered some £6.5 million had already been committed, leaving a balance of £73.5 million. Taking account of the scale of the Department’s financial deficit, and the progress being achieved against our Programme for Government targets through other waiting list funding streams, I gave approval that the remaining £73.5 million could be used to help reduce the overall funding gap.Looking forward to next year it is my firm intention to fully utilise all the funding earmarked for Waiting Lists as proposed by the finance minister in the 2026-29 Draft Budget in line with the Programme for Government.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the staffing complement of the NI Ambulance Service, broken down by (a) frontline staff; (b) dispatch staff; and (c) Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) employs staff across various staff groups and roles.  As a regional ambulance service, NIAS operates from one Emergency Operations Centre (EOC) in Belfast. Ambulance staff employed by NIAS are not allocated to a regional Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust, but are dispatched according to geographical availability at the time of emergency and non-emergency calls, regardless of where ambulances and their crews are based. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount spent by his Department on private ambulance operators, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) utilises independent ambulance sector (IAS) providers to ensure that patients continue to receive timely and safe care during periods of increased demand and operational pressure.Table 1 below sets out the total costs incurred by NIAS for the 12-month period from February 2025 to January 2026 for services provided by IAS contractors.  These figures represent the net cost to NIAS after recharges to other Health and Social Care Trusts have been applied.Table 1 – Cost of IAS providers to NIASFebruary 2025 to January 2026IAS Cost Net to NIAS£9,736,998**Please note these figures have not been audited and include estimates (accruals)In addition to non-emergency transport, IAS providers also contribute to the NIAS emergency response function in three key areas:providing relief at emergency departments to improve ambulance turnaround times;conveying lower-acuity emergency patients to hospital, thereby allowing NIAS emergency crews to remain available for higher-priority calls; and,supporting the planned transfer of patients between locations when required.The continued use of IAS providers enables NIAS to manage operational pressures, maintain service resilience and ensure that patients across Northern Ireland continue to receive appropriate and timely care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of individuals admitted to hospital with a psychiatric presentation in 2024-25, who were re-admitted within six months of discharge, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

answer_text: Regionally, psychiatric units are not identified as a long stay provision, though unfortunately some patients that are medically optimised experience delayed discharge due to various factors.The average cost per week for an Acute mental health adult inpatient in Northern Ireland is £4,851.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the proposed recommendation in the 2025 Five Year Review of the Office of the Police Ombudsman NI consultation, that states legislation should provide for the disqualification from holding the office of Police Ombudsman of persons who are or were in a police service.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the proposed recommendation in the 2025 Five Year Review of the Office of the Police Ombudsman NI that states that two provisions in the Westminster Public Office (Accountability) Bill, in relation to a duty of candour and in relation to misconduct in public office, should apply to Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether they will work with the Minister of Justice to amend the Bill so that these provisions apply to Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on any (i) legislation she expects to introduce before the end of the 2022-27 Assembly mandate; and (ii) Bills which will not be introduced, due to time pressures.
answer_text: In this current mandate I have introduced a Justice Bill and a Criminal Justice (Sentencing etc.) Bill.  I also intend to introduce a Victims and Witnesses of Crime Bill, subject to Executive approval.   I have no plans to introduce any other Bills in the current Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the recommendation in the Office of the Police Ombudsman NI's consultation on its 2025 five year legislative review, that legislation should provide for the disqualification from holding the office of Police Ombudsman of persons who are or were in a police service.
answer_text: The proposed recommendations in the Police Ombudsman’s five year legislative review consultation will be considered by my Department.  Engagement will take place with the Police Ombudsman’s office on the outcome of their consultation following its conclusion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the recommendation in the Office of the Police Ombudsman NI's consultation paper on its 2025 five year legislative review, that two provisions in the Westminster Public Office (Accountability) Bill, in relation to a duty of candour and in relation to misconduct in public office, should apply to Northern Ireland and that the Minister will table a paper at the Executive for the Bill to be amended, so that these provisions apply to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The proposed recommendations in the Police Ombudsman’s five year legislative review consultation will be considered by my Department.  Engagement will take place with the Police Ombudsman’s office on the outcome of their consultation following its conclusion.Along with Executive Colleagues, I have signalled support for extending the provisions within the Westminster Public Office (Accountability) Bill to Northern Ireland, which relate to the introduction of a duty of candour and two new statutory offences for misconduct in public office.  The Department of Finance is leading on coordinating this exercise.  The Legislative Consent Memorandum is available at the following link The Public Office (Accountability) Bill

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Fisheries Management Plan for Strangford Lough.
answer_text: My department has a statutory obligation under the Fisheries Act 2020 and Annex A of the Joint Fisheries Statement to develop and publish a Northern Ireland Non-Quota Shellfish Fisheries Management Plan (FMP) by the end of December 2026. To meet that obligation a public consultation on the draft FMP will commence shortly.This consultation draft FMP will set out policies designed to restore or maintain non-quota shellfish stocks such as crabs, lobsters and scallops at sustainable levels within the entire Northern Ireland zone, including Strangford Lough.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 20505/22-27, to detail any scientific advice he has (i) sought; and (ii) received, regarding the sustainable fishing of Strangford Lough.
answer_text: My department commissions science and advice through the Assigned Work Programme (AWP) managed by the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute. The AWP provides for data collection and independent scientific advice for fisheries and underpins policy development as well as statutory and reporting duties.In the 2025/26 AWP my department has requested assessments of regional fish and shellfish stock resources and advice on management, including a survey of stocks in Strangford Lough. My department awaits the 2025/26 reports of these assessments which will inform objectives and measures for the sustainability of the stocks within scope of the Northern Ireland Non-Quota Shellfish Fisheries Management Plan.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 17137/22-27, to detail (i) the number of licences that were issued for fishing in Strangford Lough, in each of the last ten years; and (ii) the number of these licences that were utilised, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Strangford Lough Fishing Licences 2016 - 2025 Strangford Lough Licences IssuedStrangford Lough Licences Utilised201618162017211720181612201914132020141220211412202214122023121020241192025109The table below provides the number of licences issued for fishing in Strangford Lough and those utilised in the last ten years:
