question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department, as the competent authority for inland fisheries under the Fisheries Act (Northern Ireland) 1966, holds any responsibility for the (i) structural condition; (ii) operation of; or (iii) safety of fisheries weirs on, the Shimna River, Newcastle.
answer_text: Under the Fisheries Act (Northern Ireland) 1966, my Department is the competent authority for the protection and regulation of inland fisheries. DAERA does not hold responsibility for the structural condition, operation or safety of this weir on the Shimna River.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has carried out any assessment, inspection, or review of the structural condition of the Shimna River fisheries weir, in the last five years.
answer_text: The structural integrity and maintenance of the Shimna River weir is not the responsibility of my Department; therefore, it has made no assessment, inspection, or review of the structural condition of the weir within the past five years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has assessed the potential impact on (i) fish passage; (ii) spawning habitat; or (iii) river ecology, in the event of a partial or full collapse of the Shimna River fisheries weir.
answer_text: The Department has not conducted an evaluation regarding the effects of a partial or complete collapse on fish passage, spawning habitats, or river ecology, as such an assessment would need to be based on the specific circumstances present at the time. In the event of a collapse my Department will seek to ensure compliance with the Fisheries Act 1966 and will engage, as appropriate, with those concerned.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he is aware of reports that the fisheries weir on the Shimna River has been undermined and may be at risk of structural failure.
answer_text: I am aware of concerns regarding the condition of the weir on the Shimna River; however, responsibility for maintaining this structure does not rest with my Department. My officials will continue to monitor the situation to ensure fish passage is maintained at the site in accordance with the Fisheries Act (Northern Ireland) 1966.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has undertaken any hydraulic modelling or flood risk assessment, to determine whether failure of the fishing weir on the Shimna River would impact (i) drainage efficiency; (ii) upstream water levels; and (iii) downstream infrastructure.
answer_text: My Department holds no responsibility, statutory or otherwise, for the maintenance, safety, or structural integrity of any of the fishery weirs on the Shimna River. The weirs do not form part of the river drainage infrastructure that my Department’s Rivers Directorate maintains for the free flow of water under the Drainage (NI) Order 1973, nor do they constitute any element of flood defence infrastructure for which my Department has responsibility.My Department therefore does not hold information on these structures and has carried out no related hydraulic modelling, flood risk assessment, inspections or remedial works, nor does it have any plans to do so. Matters relating to the ownership, condition or operation of fishery weirs should be directed to the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) Inland Fisheries, who are the competent authority for inland fisheries under the Fisheries Act (NI) 1966.Where proposed works within a watercourse, including the Shimna, could affect the free flow of water or the drainage function of the channel, Schedule 6 consent under the Drainage (NI) Order 1973 is required from my Department. As part of that process, my Department consults DAERA Inland Fisheries where a watercourse has a fisheries interest and proposals have the potential to affect fish passage or fisheries interests, in line with established arrangements between both organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide details of the consultation undertaken by the Education Authority Youth Service with the voluntary sector in relation to the new administrative tasks to account for funding.
To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any reforms he is considering to the revaluation process of non-domestic properties following his decision to pause Reval 2026.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the panel members who will be responsible for (i) sifting applications for; and (ii) first interview of candidates for, the position of Police Ombudsman, further to the recruitment competition for the position which commenced in November 2025.
answer_text: The panel members for the Police Ombudsman recruitment competition who were responsible for sifting the applications and the formal interviews of candidates, following the initial assessment by Cassin Scott Associates, were: Ms Jayne Brady, Chair; Mr Peter May, Ms Judith Gillespie and Mr Jim Beattie. While the initial assessment by Cassin Scott Associates included interviews by an occupational psychologist, these did not lead to a pass or fail decision. They were designed to inform the formal interviews by the panel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that the UK Government has accepted Young Lives vs Cancer's proposal for a £10 million Travel Fund to support all under 25s with the cost of travelling to cancer treatment, whether any steps will be taken to ensure parity for children and young people under 25 with cancer in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have engaged with Young Lives vs Cancer on the issue of travel cost support for children and young people undergoing cancer treatment. I also met with Young Lives vs Cancer to hear their concerns directly. My Department has considered the charity’s proposals, however, given the significant pressures on the Department’s budget, it has not been possible to consider any additional financial support other than that which is currently available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any engagement his Department has had with Young Lives vs Cancer regarding travel costs support for cancer patients under 25 who face significant costs when accessing treatment; and (ii) whether any steps will be taken to establish dedicated travel cost support aligned with the UK Government's accepting their model for a £10 million Travel Fund.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have engaged with Young Lives vs Cancer on the issue of travel cost support for children and young people undergoing cancer treatment. I also met with Young Lives vs Cancer to hear their concerns directly. My Department has considered the charity’s proposals, however, given the significant pressures on the Department’s budget, it has not been possible to consider any additional financial support other than that which is currently available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the recommendation in the 2020 Police Ombudsman NI Review under Section 61(4) of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 1998, that legislation should provide for disqualifications from holding the position of Police Ombudsman for persons who are or have been serving police officers.
answer_text: The Police Ombudsman put forward recommendations on legislative change for consideration in a Five Year Review report in 2020.  The report presented proposals for changes to the statutory functions of the Police Ombudsman’s office.  Recommendation 3 of the report proposed that “The Police Ombudsman legislation provide for disqualifications from holding the position of Police Ombudsman for persons who are or have been serving police officers.”Engagement on this review was undertaken with key stakeholders, who were invited to give their initial view on the recommendations during pre-consultation stage.  Following this, proposals were put forward for public consultation, which ran from 21 December 2021 to 1 March 2022.  The results of this consultation concluded that there was no consensus for this recommendation at that time.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the recommendation in the 2020 Five Year Review of the Office of the Police Ombudsman NI, which states that legislation should provide for the disqualification from holding the office of Police Ombudsman of persons who are, or were, in a police service.
answer_text: Section 61(4) of the Police (NI) Act 1998 requires the Police Ombudsman to -(a)	keep under review the working of Part VII of the Police (NI) Act 1998; and(b)	at least once every five years, make a report on it to the Department of Justice.Given the role of the Department of Justice in considering the five-year reviews under this Act, they are best placed to assess any associated recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail, in each year from 2020-21 to 2025-26, (i) the hourly rate set by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group for privately provided domiciliary care, (ii) the percentage increase or decrease year-on-year; and (iii) the total public expenditure associated with each rate, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group of the Department of Health (previously the Health and Social Care Board to 31/3/22) did not set a regional rate for domiciliary care providers until 1 November 2021.The hourly rate set for independent sector domiciliary care provision from 1 November 2021, and the associated percentage increase or decrease in this rate, is set out in Table 1. Table 1: Domiciliary Care Hourly rate and annual percentage increaseFinancial Year(i) Domiciliary Care Hourly Rate£(ii) Percentage increase/ (decrease) year on yearNovember 2021- March 202218.00 1-2022-2318.08-2023-2418.543.00% 22024-2520.017.93%2025-2621.356.70%Notes: 1 The regional rate of £18.00 per hour was intended to reflect the rate required from April 2022, however this was introduced in advance from November 2021, to support providers.2 A further small uplift to £18.08 per hour was however also introduced from April 2022.  This related to a statutory increase in Employer National Insurance contribution (NIC) rates in that year.  This Employer NIC increase was subsequently reversed and therefore the comparative uplift percentage is 3% (£18.54 from £18.00 per hour).The total public expenditure associated with each of these rates is recorded via the HSC’s regional costing system.  The costs produced are fully absorbed, meaning they include direct, indirect, overhead and capital charges expenditure. Consequently, it is important to note that hours actually delivered cannot be derived from a calculation of this expenditure and the rates set out in Table 1.The information available on these costs has been provided to you in my response to AQW 38481/22-27, which sets out the annual domiciliary care costs by Trust as currently available from the regional costing systems for 2020/21 to 2023/24.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 29726/22-27, to detail when the planting of native trees will commence in Donard Forest.
answer_text: I am advised that Forest Service plan to re-plant the area of Donard forest felled as a result of Storm Éowyn within the current planting season, spring 2026. In line with the previous AQW 29726/22-27, this area will be re-established with Douglas fir as the primary species within which a number of small groups of native Oak trees are to be incorporated. This is consistent with re-design principles for biodiversity and increased forest resilience.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when the (i) Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme; and (ii) Rural Business Development Grant Scheme, will reopen for new applications.
answer_text: My officials are currently developing plans for the delivery of the Rural Business Development Grant Scheme and the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme during 2026/27. This will be subject to approved business cases, funding availability and my approval.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether, when setting the hourly rate paid to private domiciliary care providers, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group explicitly factors in an allowance for provider (i) profit; and ii) management fees.
answer_text: The domiciliary care provider payment rate per hour includes a provision for the gross cost of those social care workers delivering this home care service and a provision for other general costs.  There is no allowance for provider profit or management fees explicitly factored into this provider payment rate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the current domiciliary care hourly rate set by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group, to detail (i) the amount per hour attributed to provider management costs; and (ii) the amount per hour attributed to provider profit.
answer_text: In line with my response to AQW 40098/22-27, the domiciliary care provider payment rate per hour includes a provision for the gross cost of those social care workers delivering this home care service and a provision for other general costs.  There is no allowance for provider profit or management fees explicitly factored into this provider payment rate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans his Department has for the Game of Thrones stand, at the Quoile River.
answer_text: I am aware that the angling stand beside the Game of Thrones sign at the Quoile River has been damaged and closed for safety reasons. My Department is currently assessing whether it should be repaired or replaced subject to obtaining the necessary landowner approvals.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the rationale for all fishing stands being removed from the Quoile river walk.
answer_text: Any angling stands removed along the Quoile river walk were due to safety concerns as they had reached the end of their operational life and were beyond repair. These have been replaced either at or near their original locations, ensuring no overall reduction in provision.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether pike is still considered as an invasive species.
answer_text: Whether pike are native, non-native, introduced or naturalised to Ireland is a topic of highly speculative debate. There are still considerable gaps in knowledge about Ireland's postglacial landscape.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether an economic impact assessment was carried out when planning the road closure of the A25 Downpatrick Road and Castleward Road, Strangford.
answer_text: The £600,000 carriageway resurfacing scheme on A25 Downpatrick Road commenced on 19 January 2026. Subject to favourable weather conditions, the works should be complete by Friday 3 April 2026.The Department has programmed the work operations and traffic management arrangements to minimise traffic disruption and has communicated details of the closures to the public through, electronic information signage placed at all approaches to the works and use of the Department’s social media and TrafficWatchNI platforms.  I also issued a press release with information relating to the works on 12 January 2026.My Department also took forward a letter drop to all residential and commercial premises within the extent of the closure and liaised closely with the Ferry Service who also provided information via SMS to over 5,000 subscribers to their update service.  My officials also engaged directly with Translink and the school impacted by the closure ensuring that the afternoon bus services are being escorted through the site.The works are predominantly restricted to weekdays, 9.30am to 4.30pm to minimise their impact however several weekend closures are likely to be required for final surfacing and details of these will be provide closer to the time.  I am also pleased to confirm that having secured additional funding, the scheme length will increase to almost 2km and will reduce the need for future works and disruption at this location. It is anticipated that this additional work can be completed within the original timescale.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consultation carried out by her Department with (i) local businesses; or (ii) schools and playgroups, when planning the road closure of the A25 Downpatrick Road and Castleward Road, Strangford.
answer_text: The £600,000 carriageway resurfacing scheme on A25 Downpatrick Road commenced on 19 January 2026. Subject to favourable weather conditions, the works should be complete by Friday 3 April 2026.The Department has programmed the work operations and traffic management arrangements to minimise traffic disruption and has communicated details of the closures to the public through, electronic information signage placed at all approaches to the works and use of the Department’s social media and TrafficWatchNI platforms. My officials also engaged directly with Translink and the school impacted by the closure ensuring that the afternoon bus services are being escorted through the site.The works are predominantly restricted to weekdays, 9.30am to 4.30pm to minimise their impact however several weekend closures are likely to be required for final surfacing and details of these will be provide closer to the time.  I am also pleased to confirm that having secured additional funding, the scheme length will increase to almost 2km and will reduce the need for future works and disruption at this location. It is anticipated that this additional work can be completed within the original timescale.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the average daily traffic volume on the B8 road between Castlewellan and Newry; and (ii) how traffic volume is used when determining maintenance and investment priorities for B Class roads.
answer_text: When determining resurfacing priorities for all roads, my Department’s programmes are prepared and delivered based on several factors including road usage, general surface condition, structural deformation, public enquiries and public liability claims. The most recent traffic volume data for the B8 relates to the period 2011 to 2014 where volumes ranged from 3,794 to 7,813 vehicles per day.Details of the planned resurfacing schemes are included in my Department’s annual report to council.My officials continue to undertake regular inspections and repair all actionable defects in line with the Department's Limited Service Policy (DEM 160/21). During a routine inspection of the B8 Castlewellan Road on 3rd February 2026, a number of actionable defects were identified, including some in the vicinity of Stang Road junction. Our contractor was instructed to carry out a series of large-scale patching works which commenced on Monday 9th February 2026 and will take approximately two weeks to complete.   My Department does not hold information regarding maintenance expenditure on the B8 Castlewellan Road or comparable expenditure figures on B class roads in the format requested and any attempt to do so would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.I can however advise that 2km of resurfacing at Derryleckagh was undertaken in 2024/25 at a cost of £590,000 and 800m of resurfacing in Hilltown was undertaken in 2019/20 at a cost of £180,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the export list for (i) refuse-derived fuel waste; and (ii) solid recovered fuel waste, for 2025.
answer_text: This information is published at the end of each calendar year on the DAERA website. The information for 2025 will be published in February 2026. Reports for the calendar years 2014 to 2024 are available through the following link: www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/export-records-rdf-shipped-northern-ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 38702/22-27, to detail the percentage rate for recycling, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council.
answer_text: Information regarding Local Authority Collected (LAC) municipal waste and household waste recycling rates are publicly available online via the DAERA website (www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/northern-ireland-local-authority-collected-municipal-waste-management-statistics).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of temporary traffic signals, currently in operation; including (ii) the (a) location, road and town; (b) date first installed; and (c) total duration to date, for the temporary traffic signals that have been in operation for the longest continuous period.
answer_text: The number of traffic signals in operation across the network changes daily and any attempt to provide this information would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the Department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.In many cases, where my Department authorises a traffic restriction, for example, for external contractors acting on behalf of the Department or utility companies, we do not specify the control method or know the days on which a control measure is used during the period of the authorised traffic restriction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 38701/22-27, whether there are targets for (i) energy recovery; and (ii) landfill, for (a) 2035; and (b) 2030.
answer_text: Northern Ireland has a statutory target to reduce municipal waste sent to landfill to 10% by 2035; however, there is no equivalent target for 2030, and no specific targets have been set for energy recovery.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the report to address congestion on Edward Street, Downpatrick; and (ii) to detail the reason why the report has not yet been shared with MLAs.
answer_text: The study has now been finalised, with several improvement options identified. Officials met with the Downpatrick DEA in December 2025, and a copy of the options report was given to the representatives of the DEA which included Councillors from the SDLP, Sinn Fein and the Alliance Party.Subject to available funding, officials will progress further investigation into the preferred options.I have asked officials to make a copy of the report available to you.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any steps he will take to ensure that flood-affected businesses, whose material damage was already formally recognised within the NI Reval2026 process, are not financially penalised by the pause in revaluation; and (ii) whether he will (a) ring-fence funding; or (b) honour draft reductions, based on verified flood damage.
answer_text: revaluation is a statutory process carried out by Land & Property Services (LPS) to reset the valuation list to a new valuation date, ensuring ratepayers are assessed on uptodate rental evidence. The stop in Reval2026 does not change how properties are valued: draft figures simply reflected movements in the rental market. The only consequence of stopping Reval2026 is that valuations will remain based on estimated rental values at the current statutory valuation date of 1 October 2021, rather than at 1 April 2024, had the revaluation proceeded.Material or physical changes can be recognised at any time under existing legislation. Properties are valued “as they stand”, so verified physical damage, such as that arising from flooding, can be reflected immediately without the need for a revaluation to be completed.Where such damage has already been recognised, it will continue to be reflected in current valuations until such time as physical impacts are remedied. In light of these existing statutory mechanisms for recognising physical damage, there is no requirement to ring-fence separate funding or to give effect to draft revaluation figures, which were provisional market-based estimates prepared for a valuation list that will not now be introduced.As the Member will be aware, I announced on 29 January that I was stopping the Reval 2026 process. I would direct the Member to my oral statement to the Assembly:Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the reason why only half of National Insurance mitigation payments relating to the 2025-26 financial year for General Dental Practices will be paid during the 2025-26 financial year, in which these extra costs have been incurred.
answer_text: £2.5m has been allocated to General Dental Services to support with the increase to National Insurance Contributions (NIC). This funding is being allocated based on the proportion of Practice Allowance received by dental practices for 2025/26.Payment is being made in two instalments, aligned with the Practice Allowance payments for 2025/26, the first of which was made in December 2025.The second instalment is due for payment in June 2026 following the next Practice Allowance payment.This allocation method and schedule, which is being piloted for 2025/26, was agreed with the British Dental Association and they will have the opportunity to provide feedback and suggest amendments for future years. It should be noted that this funding is recurrent, and Northern Ireland is the only part of the UK providing dentists with this type of support to mitigate against NIC increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that the Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration pay arrears for General Dental Practitioners not being paid until June and July 2026 will have on health service dental practices.
answer_text: Dental practices are independent businesses with each practice having their own unique business model based on the size of the practice, location, health-service to private mix, and if they are part of a larger chain.  As such my Department cannot fully assess the impact of GDPs not being paid arrears until June 2026.  Feedback from the British Dental Association indicates that some practices may experience cashflow problems however this cannot be quantified.As such I have instructed the BSO to issue the first stage of arrears payments in March 2026 with the remainder to be paid in April and June 2026.  This was communicated to the BDA and to GDPs on 11 February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Business Services Organisation not processing the Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration pay arrears for General Dental Practitioners until June and July 2026; and (ii) to detail any steps he will take to expedite this.
answer_text: When I approved the 2025/26 pay uplift in November 2025, the working expectation was that all payments would be made in February 2026. The Business Services Organisation however subsequently advised that whilst it was possible to process payments for salaried staff (including salaried dentists) within this timeline, processing the uplift for General Dental Practitioners was more complex, since it includes a reliance on third-party software providers, with lead-in times set at 12 weeks. This meant that arrears payments for General Dental Practitioners would not be processed until June/July. I have instructed my officials to put in place the necessary arrangements to facilitate an earlier remuneration for General Dental Practitioners, outside of the usual process, for 2025/26. This is a one-off intervention and should not be considered a precedent for future years. I have also directed officials to review the current processes and procedural elements associated with the pay uplift, with a view to improving efficiency for future years’ payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when will the £1.6 million activity enhancement payments he announced in May 2025 to support health service dentists will be paid.
answer_text: The Department is currently considering the eligibility criteria and most appropriate way to allocate this funding to those practitioners who are treating Health Service patients. This involves some necessary exploratory work and analysis of practice data, as well as consideration of feedback received from the British Dental Association.Once this work is complete, the Department will seek to make the payments within the 2025/26 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any (i) assessment; (ii) inspection; or (iii) remedial works her Department has carried out, or plans to carry out, in relation to the fishing weir on the Shimna River, Newcastle, in light of concerns raised by local anglers and residents.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, where a structure within a river channel presents a risk to water levels, flooding or downstream infrastructure, whether her Department has responsibility to assess and intervene, regardless of land ownership.
answer_text: Schedule 6 of the Drainage (Northern Ireland) Order 1973 applies to both designated and undesignated watercourses and deals with actions likely to interfere with the effectiveness or efficiency of a watercourse for drainage or flooddefence purposes.Where a structure is determined to be impacting the flow of water, or otherwise affecting the efficient functioning of a watercourse, Rivers Directorate applies the statutory procedures set out in Schedule 6. Under these powers, my Department may investigate and, where necessary, request remedial works, issue statutory notices, or undertake emergency action in defined circumstances.These procedures, which begin with informal engagement and escalate to statutory enforcement where required, apply regardless of land ownership and ensure that issues arising from structures within watercourses can be properly assessed and addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the success of the NHS 111 helpline service introduced during the Covid-19 pandemic.
answer_text: Firstly, I must clarify that a regional telephone triage service, similar to the ‘NHS 111’ service which is in place in England, does not exist in Northern Ireland.  A review of Urgent and Emergency Care Services was published in October 2022 and identified the need for a new regional care model to address increasing pressures on Emergency Departments. One recommendation was the introduction of a regional, 24/7 telephone triage service under a single number. This service would aim to ensure patients access the right care, in the right place, first time, reducing unnecessary Emergency Department attendances and improving patient outcomes.During the Covid-19 Pandemic and pending publication of the report, local “Phone First” services were introduced as an interim measure. While these services have delivered benefits, I recognise they are fragmented, operating under multiple numbers with limited hours and inconsistent integration across Trusts.My Department plans to review the recommendation of introducing an HSC 111 telephone triage service, to reflect new digital opportunities and consider the relationship with existing services such as GP Out of Hours, Same Day Emergency Care, Urgent Care Centres and NIAS Hear and Treat. The intention is to establish a dedicated project team to take this work forward.  However, the timing of the work relies on available funding to establish a project team in the first instance and then associated funding to support any transition to a proposed model.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the fishing weir on the Shimna River, Newcastle, forms part of the (i) river channel; or (ii) flood defence infrastructure, for which her Department has responsibility under drainage, rivers or flood risk legislation.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department holds any responsibility, statutory or otherwise, for the (i) maintenance; (ii) safety; or (iii) structural integrity, of the fishing weir on the Shimna River, Newcastle.
answer_text: My Department holds no responsibility, statutory or otherwise, for the maintenance, safety, or structural integrity of any of the fisheries weirs on the Shimna River. The weirs do not form part of the river drainage infrastructure that my Rivers Directorate maintains for the free flow of water under the Drainage (Northern Ireland) Order 1973, nor do they constitute any element of flood defence infrastructure for which the Department has responsibility.My Department therefore does not hold information on these structures and has carried out no assessments, inspections or remedial works, nor does it have any plans to do so. Matters relating to the ownership, condition or operation of fisheries weirs should be directed to the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) Inland Fisheries, who are the competent authority for inland fisheries under the Fisheries Act (Northern Ireland) 1966.Where proposed works within a watercourse, including the Shimna, could affect the free flow of water or the drainage function of the channel, Schedule 6 consent under the Drainage (Northern Ireland) Order 1973 is required from my Department. As part of that process, my Department consults DAERA Inland Fisheries where a watercourse has a fisheries interest and proposals have the potential to affect fish passage or fisheries interests, in line with established arrangements between both organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31701/22-27, whether environmental exposure factors, including proximity to industrial sites, waste facilities, or pollution sources, are routinely considered as part of suspected cancer cluster assessments.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will (i) repair; or (ii) replace, Moygannon Bridge, Warrenpoint, following its collapse as a result of Storm Chandra.
answer_text: I am aware of the widespread disruption and damage that occurred to many parts of the North as a result of the Storm Chandra.  It is regrettable that the bridge has suffered damage as a result of the storm, however as the footbridge over the Moygannon River is situated on an unadopted stretch of Rath Road in Warrenpoint it does not fall under my Department’s ownership or maintenance responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any Departmental mechanism in place to (a) review; and (b) uprate, the Personal Expenses Allowance for people in residential care; and (ii) when the allowance of £34.10 per week was last formally reviewed or revised.
answer_text: The Personal Expenses Allowance (PEA) rate is specified in the annual Charging for Residential Accommodation Guide (CRAG) circular. This amount is reviewed each year and is generally uplifted in line with the increase in Northern Ireland Average Earnings. My Department will notify Trusts of any change in the rate of personal expenses by way of an issued circular. The PEA amount is the same for each resident, whether in a Trust managed home or an independent sector home.The PEA increased from £32.69 to the current rate of £34.10 on 7 April 2025.The annual review of the PEA will conclude in March 2026, and any amendments will take effect from 6 April 2026.The CRAG circular for 2026/27 advising of the revised PEA will be circulated and made available on the Department's external website on completion of the review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31701/22-27, to detail any guidance that exists for informing affected communities when a suspected cancer cluster is identified and assessed, including whether communities are informed where concerns are raised but the standardised incidence ratio is not deemed statistically significant.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) is regularly involved in responding to reports of clusters of disease, both infectious and non-infectious. Each report is assessed on a case-by-case basis. Information is gathered from those raising the concern, and further investigation is undertaken to determine whether there is an actual increase in cases. Any subsequent actions, including communication, are informed by the findings of this process.In relation to a suspected cancer cluster, there must be a clear ethical justification for any form of public communication. This may include proactive communication where a significant concern has been identified that warrants informing the community, or reactive communication where concerns have emerged, but investigations find no evidence of a cluster, and explanation or reassurance is required.By way of update, from 2015 to the present, the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR) has undertaken 18 assessments of suspected geographical cancer clusters, as well as three assessments of suspected workplace-associated cancer clusters, with some of these involving repeat assessments. Over this period, NICR identified four potential cancer clusters (including one repeat assessment) where cancer rates were significantly higher than the Northern Ireland average. These results were shared with the PHA.On further assessment of these potential cancer clusters, in one situation, based on available information and taking into consideration the small number of cases and variety of cancer types involved, there was insufficient evidence to support the hypothesis that higher rates were a result of an external factor. For another potential cancer cluster, investigation found that when analysis was undertaken over a longer time period rates similar to NI were found, with differences likely due to year-on-year variation.  Another potential cluster was associated with cancers with well-known demographic, socio-economic or screening-related factors and which did not require additional assessment, with repeated assessment some years later also finding similar associations (i.e. accounting for 2 separate cluster assessments).A wide range of geographical areas were involved in these assessments, covering 10 of Northern Ireland’s 11 Local Government Districts. Some assessments examined all cancers combined, while others focused on specific cancer types, including breast, bowel, lung, prostate, upper gastrointestinal, brain, haematological, adrenal, thyroid and childhood cancers. Several assessments looked at multiple cancer types.Further detail on the specific locations or cancer types involved cannot be provided due to the risk of identifying individuals or small communities. Similarly, additional information on workplace-related assessments cannot be shared for confidentiality reasons.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 37756/22-27, to detail the number of (i) roadside compliance checks involving waste management vehicles carried out by the Driver and Vehicle Agency, in each of the last five years; and (ii) cases where enforcement action was taken, broken down by year.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency enforces a broad range of regulatory requirements that govern the use of HGVs on our roads, including drivers’ hours, tachograph use, vehicle roadworthiness, weights, and licensing.While DVA holds detailed statistical information within its enforcement datasets, it does not categorise or record data specific to vehicles transporting waste. I am therefore unable to provide the breakdown of information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a waste management vehicle, which has been issued with a Fixed Penalty Notice for exceeding maximum authorised weight, is considered by her Department to represent a road safety risk.
answer_text: Any vehicle operating in excess of its maximum authorised weight, resulting in the issue of a Fixed Penalty Notice (FPN), would have been detected breaching a regulatory weight requirement. This type of sanction is imposed to protect fair competition and to reduce or prevent damage to the road and bridge infrastructure.A vehicle would be considered to represent a danger to road users if its manufacturer’s design carrying capability had been exceeded to such an extent that its wheels, tyres, steering, suspension, braking, or chassis systems were likely to have been compromised. Overloading of this severity would not ordinarily be dealt with by way of a FPN but would instead be referred for prosecution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 37756/22-27, to detail the number of (i) Fixed Penalty Notices; (ii) prohibitions; and (iii) prosecutions, that have been issued by the Driver and Vehicle Agency in each of the last five years, in relation to vehicles transporting waste, for offences relating to (a) exceeding maximum authorised or combination weight; and (b) insecure or unsafe loads, broken down by offence and year.
answer_text: While DVA holds detailed statistical information within its enforcement datasets, it does not categorise or record data specific to vehicles transporting waste. I am therefore unable to provide the breakdown of information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of inspections of reinstatement works, carried out by statutory undertakers, that were completed by her Department, in each of the last five financial years, in accordance with Article 21(3) of the Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers and requires the undertaker to comply with the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads.Article 24 is the legislation governing the Department being able to prescribe the criteria for designation, or removal of the designation for Traffic Sensitive Streets   There is no connection, between defective reinstatements or the remedy of defective reinstatements, and Article 24 quoted in AQW 38921/22-27 or AQW 38923/22-27.Article 21(3) is the legislation governing the street authority being able to require a fee for legal or other expenses incurred in connection with the giving of its consent or an annual fee of a reasonable amount for administering the consent for placing apparatus in a Protected street.There is no connection between the number of reinstatement inspections and Article 21(3) quoted in AQW 38922/22-27.My Department applies the procedures for inspection of reinstatements and for dealing with reinstatement failures as set out in the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Code of Practice for Inspections.  Where a statutory undertaker fails to carry out reinstatement to an acceptable standard, the undertaker is duly notified to carry out remedial works to bring the reinstatement to the required standard.The number of reinstatement inspections and reinstatement defects identified requiring follow up until such time as the defect is repaired by the statutory undertaker in each of the last five years are detailed in the table below: 20242023202220212020      Reinstatement Inspections 955896341006297058948 Reinstatement Defects62790912591011759

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of (i) waste permit variations issued by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency; and (ii) cases in which a permit variation was subsequently identified as giving rise to planning compliance concerns, enforcement action, or referral to a planning authority, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: NIEA has issued the following waste permit variations in the last five years.2021 = 42022 = 22023 = 62024 = 32025 = 6My officials consult the relevant planning authority on any substantial variation applications, and any consultation response is taken into consideration. My officials are not aware of any case where a waste permit variation has been referred to planning authorities for enforcement action. Planning enforcement is a matter for local authorities and my officials will continue to work with all relevant stakeholders and consultees.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the total tonnage of (i) refuse-derived fuel; and (ii) solid recovered fuel, exported from Warrenpoint Harbour, in each of the last 12 months.
answer_text: This information is published at the end of each calendar year on the DAERA website. The information for 2025 will be published in February 2026. Reports for the calendar years 2014 to 2024 are available through the following link: www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/export-records-rdf-shipped-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any additional support that is available to schools that have undertaken transformation through amalgamation to assist with the transition to becoming one school.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any formal arrangements in place for information-sharing and coordination between the Northern Ireland Environment Agency and planning authorities when enforcement cases are opened against waste management operators, including when and how planning authorities are notified.
answer_text: My officials liaise with other regulatory bodies as appropriate and dependent on the specific issues identified within any enforcement work. This may include working with planning authorities. My officials also formally consult with planning authorities on waste applications received by the NIEA.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the enforcement mechanisms used by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency to ensure compliance with Regulatory Position Statement storage requirements for (i) refuse-derived fuel; and (ii) solid recovered fuel, including (a) inspection frequency; (b) monitoring arrangements; and (c) the range of enforcement actions available where non-compliance is identified.
answer_text: The storage of waste is regulated via the waste authorisation for the specific site, and the frequency of site inspections is generated using a risk-based model which calculates the number of inspections for that site. My officials may also inspect a site in response to incidents or complaints. Any non-compliance is addressed in line with the Enforcement Policy which can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/dard/23.24.043%20DAERA%20Enforcement%20Policy%20-%202023.pdf .
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when the report into the commissioning of ADHD services will be published; and (ii) the reason why it has not yet been published.
answer_text: The needs assessment was received by the Department on 30 November 2025. I and my officials are now considering next steps and potential options for such a service, subject to funding becoming available. It is anticipated that the report will be published in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the rate of recycling, in each of the last five years, broken down local council district.
answer_text: Information regarding Local Authority Collected (LAC) municipal waste and household waste recycling rates are publicly available online via the DAERA website (www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/northern-ireland-local-authority-collected-municipal-waste-management-statistics).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the target for recycling, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Northern Ireland has legislative recycling targets of 65 % by 2035 (Waste and Contaminated Land Order 1997) and 70% by 2030 (Climate Change Act 2022).  Annual recycling targets have not been set. I have developed proposals to help ensure that Northern Ireland meets its legislative targets for recycling.I am seeking Executive approval for my Waste and Recycling Reforms. In parallel, I launched the draft Waste Management Strategy launched for consultation on 15 January 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there is a legal requirement for the rate of recycling.
answer_text: There are two key pieces of legislation which impose targets for recycling rates in Northern Ireland.The Waste (Circular Economy) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2020, transposed the EU Circular Economy Package into domestic legislation and amended the Waste and Contaminated Land Order 1997. This legislation set recycling a target for municipal waste of 65% by 2035 with interim targets of 55% by 2025 and 60% by 2030.The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 set a recycling target of 70% by 2030.The recently released Local Authority Collected Municipal Waste Statistics reveal a stagnating household recycling rate. We need to do more if we are to meet our legislative targets. My proposals under the two workstreams of the ‘Rethinking Our Resources programme (Waste and Recycling Reforms and the draft Waste Management Strategy) aim to elevate recycling rates across households, businesses and public and third sectors.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether Martha's Rule applies in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Martha’s Rule does not currently apply in Northern Ireland. My Department continues to monitor the approach to the roll-out of the scheme and its impacts in England and to further understand plans in relation to Martha’s Rule, or equivalent policy, in the other Devolved Administrations with colleagues in Scotland and Wales. This will help inform scoping of potential options for Northern Ireland. While the Martha’s Rule model  being rolled out in England is not in place in Northern Ireland, if a patient or family member in Northern Ireland is concerned about the deterioration of a patient in hospital, they can raise their concerns with healthcare staff who will decide on further assessment and intervention including through discussion and escalation of the concerns to an appropriate colleague.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people who are waiting for domiciliary care packages, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department receive data from each of the HSC Trusts in relation to the number of individuals awaiting full and partial packages of Care. I have listed the position as of 31 December 2026. Full Package waitsHSCT NameDec-25Belfast                            189 Northern                            532 South Eastern                              72 Southern                            764 Western                            481 NI                        2,038 Partial Package waitsHSCT NameDec-25Belfast                            380 Northern                            303 South Eastern                              11 Southern                              53 Western                            333 NI                        1,080 You will note that the number of individuals awaiting a package of care is significantly lower in the South Eastern Trust (SET) than that for the other Trusts.  The reason for this is that over the past two and a half years SET have embarked on a review of home care services in their Trust.  The result has been that they have developed a number of new initiatives to include; an unmet need management system, a short-term and long-term model for home care to facilitate hospital discharge while offering rehabilitation, a community review panel, greater emphasis on the use of data to drive the service monitoring and future service planning and development of a user guide for staff and users to enable them to navigate community services.  The result has been that these initiatives along with other initiatives developed through my Department has enabled the SET to drive down unmet need. My Department have held a number of round table meetings over the past 8 months with all key stakeholders and that has been used as an opportunity for HSC Trusts to learn from each other.  I know that the other four Trusts are keen to replicate some of the SET initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount spent on domiciliary care, in each of the last five years, broken down Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: I note your previous question relating to this subject (AQW 12201/22-27).  The information previously provided in this regard was sourced from HSC Trusts as the implementation of a new regional costing system was in progress. The regional costing system is now fully implemented and the information in the table below is therefore based on the available information from this system, which is based on the principle of regional consistency of approach in reporting cost.Table 1:  Annual Domiciliary Care CostTrust2019/20£m2020/21£m2021/22 £m2022/23 £m2023/24 £mBHSCT47.048.249.664.868.1NHSCT64.365.967.975.483.0SEHSCT56.457.859.682.282.1SHSCT61.763.265.275.984.3WHSCT44.545.647.046.056.1It should be noted that: The annual costing process was paused for two years (2020/21 and 2021/22) primarily due to general Covid pressures across the HSC system and to allow for the procurement and implementation of a new Patient Level Costing System (PLICS).  PLICS was implemented from the financial year 2022/23 and the most recent finalised data set available is in respect of the 2023/24 financial year.The figures are sourced from HSC Trust Cost Submissions for the financial years 2019/20, 2022/23 and 2023/24. These figures represent actual expenditure, including any non-recurrent expenditure by Trusts;Costs for 2020/21 and 2021/22 are based on uplifts applied to the 2019/20 cost returns;Figures quoted are fully absorbed costs; this means they include direct, indirect, overheads and capital charges costs. Consequently, it is important to urge caution in any consideration of comparability of expenditure bases.As costs are not always organised discretely by service, there is always an element of apportionment and mapping of costs to allow reporting at the service level.It should be noted this data has been taken from the first and second year of the new PLICS system and may not yet be fully mature

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 37098/22-27, to detail (i) the location; (ii) the nature of the works; and (iii) whether these works were high risk.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail all (i) minor; (ii) major; and (iii) repair, roadworks in South Down, that have been scheduled for 2026.
answer_text: Details of the schemes planned for South Down in the 2025/26 financial year are included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council. Schemes to be taken forward during the 26/27 financial year will be confirmed in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 37096/22-27, (i) to detail why (a) the Southern Health and Social Care Trust; and (b) the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust do not record this information; and (ii) whether he will request that such information is collected.
answer_text: The South-Eastern and Southern Health and Social Care Trusts have advised that they do not maintain a single central database that can routinely separate short term, local estate related restrictions or closures by ward, theatre, diagnostic room, or clinical areas. Both Trusts report that isolated estate issues do occur across a complex 24/7 healthcare environment. These incidents may be recorded locally for operational management and prompt resolution rather than for Trust wide reporting in the categories requested. The Trusts have advised they will review how these events are recorded in future to improve consistency and support clearer reporting where appropriate.My department’s position is that recording of this data is an operational matter for the Trust to decide upon in support of their management of their estate.  The collection of this data may support a Trust’s business case for capital funding for estate maintenance related matters however I do not intend requesting that this information is collected routinely.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 36117/22-27, whether Forest Service (i) maintain; and (ii) have responsibility for, the culvert traversing NIE Networks' land.
answer_text: Forest Service has responsibility for maintenance of the drainage infrastructure inside the forest boundary.   The culvert in question services water leaving the forest from the forest boundary and traverses land occupied by NIE networks under a lease from a third party.  Forest Service does not maintain or have responsibility for the culvert on NIE land.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether she will review the current regulatory and enforcement approach to road safety for waste in transit, including cross-jurisdictional haulage into Northern Ireland; and (ii) to detail any actions she plans take to prevent a serious injury or fatality occurring, as a result of insecure waste loads.
answer_text: The licensing, regulatory, and enforcement framework for transport operators using our roads in the north, including those engaged in cross-jurisdictional haulage, is robust and meets compliance with EU Regulation 1071/2009 as adopted under the EU/UK Trade and Cooperation Agreement (TCA). The enforcement powers available to DVA and the PSNI are also effective in safeguarding load security where standards are not met. For that reason, I do not intend to review the current regulatory and enforcement approach to road safety for waste in transit.To protect road safety and prevent serious incidents, DVA will continue to target non-compliance on our roads, including vehicles with insecure loads, and work collaboratively with PSNI, HMRC, and NIEA, during future concerted compliance checks. Serious regulatory breaches will be addressed through prohibition, fixed penalties, and prosecution, and where appropriate reported to the Department’s Transport Regulation Unit for further regulatory consideration.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the actions they are taking to ensure the effective delivery of the Strategic Framework to End Violence against Women and Girls across all Departments.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any (i) inspections; (ii) enforcement actions; or (iii) compliance checks, that are carried out in relation to vehicles transporting waste on Northern Ireland roads, including (a) waste being imported from Great Britain and Ireland; (b) her assessment of whether these measures are sufficient to protect public safety; and (c) details of who these actions are carried out by.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) has primary responsibility for ensuring that goods vehicles used on our roads comply with licensing, drivers’ hours, and construction & use requirements. Infringements are addressed through prohibitions, fixed penalties, and prosecutions. Further information is available at Roadside compliance | nidirectI consider this a proportionate approach to safeguarding public safety and would highlight that these requirements are enforceable by both the DVA and the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) under current road traffic legislation. Although DVA collects granular data within broader enforcement datasets, it does not maintain information specifically on vehicles transporting waste or on waste imports from Britain or Ireland. The NIEA, an executive agency of DAERA, is the primary regulator for waste management in the north.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made on the need for robotic-assisted surgery within thoracic services.
answer_text: Robotic Assisted Surgery has been identified through the General Surgery Network as part of the Review of General Surgery within Northern Ireland.  Worked has commenced to look at what the requirements would be, and to discuss how this could support the workforce and population. My officials are leading these discussions with Trust colleagues at present. A sub group has been established and preparation work commenced to consider the development of a regional, phased, pan specialism plan for Robotic Assisted Surgery within Northern Ireland.There is agreement across the Trusts of the need for a regional pan specialism approach, which is co-produced and agreed with clinicians and other stakeholders.  The specialism of Thoracic surgery, is one service in a state of readiness to proceed.  There are challenges and barriers to implement Robotic Assisted Surgery into NI, given the current financial position and the significant investment required.  Nevertheless, I have asked my officials to continue to proceed with this work to ensure Northern Ireland maintains at pace with other nations and an agreed regional direction is set.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether funding taken from Health and Social Care Trust budgets for tertiary services and allocated to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, is accounted for separately from secondary care expenditure.
answer_text: Funding of secondary and tertiary services across the Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland is informed by a Capitation Formula. This is in line with most modern health economies.  		The NI Capitation Formula is a complex statistical formula which has been developed specifically around the current core service user groupings (Programmes of Care). It is used to measure the relative (not absolute) need for resources across local populations by identifying fair shares for local populations, rather than for Trusts or specific services.		Whilst the local areas are coterminous with the 5 Trust boundaries it must be borne in mind that the fair shares are for a locality and residents often access to services outside their locality (cross boundary flow), such as tertiary services provided by Belfast Health and Social Care Trust. Therefore, the locality fair share of resource does not equate to Trust fair share. Tertiary services are not accounted for separately, rather the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s overall resource allocation reflects the tertiary services they provide.The demand for HSC is greater than the resources available, therefore the formula cannot guarantee that all care needs are met but does help to ensure that populations have fair access to the resources that exist, based on relative, not absolute, need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 36307/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made of current surgical capacity, in line with the Bengoa recommendation to count before reforming, specifically in relation to (i) the number of available theatre lists; and (ii) the number of secondary-care general surgeons.
answer_text: It is common practice for my Department to carry out demand capacity work to inform any service review.  All relevant papers on the review of general surgery, including an analysis of demand and capacity, are available from  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/topics/review-general-surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the number of secondary care surgical consultants is aligned with current and projected surgical demand, broken down by (i) Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) available surgical list capacity.
answer_text: As with other areas of care, the general surgery service is not meeting current demand as a result of increasing surgical specialisation and a growing elderly population. The changing nature of surgical speciality means that meeting current pressures, while delivering unscheduled general surgery across multiple smaller sites, is becoming increasingly difficult in terms of supporting rotas and succession planning.The Review of General Surgery, undertaken in June 2022, sets out the direction of travel for surgical services. One of the key priorities was the development of a new model of care for the delivery of complex and non-complex elective services. The development of high volume, low complexity regional day procedure centres, complemented by the establishment of regional Elective Overnight Stay Centres, is a key component in delivering this required service change. The report also emphasised the need to invest in the surgical medical workforce. While it is not possible to calculate the alignment of consultant workforce relative to projected demand, due to the movement of both patients and consultants across Trusts, the report did highlight that the number of people needing inpatient or day case general surgery across all Trusts exceeded available capacity, with over 2,300 patients needing treatment relative to available capacity. To help address this gap the updated Elective Care Framework has earmarked funding to expand both the number of consultant surgeons and anaesthetists across a range of surgical services. In addition, my Department is working to expand the number of surgical training places. A recent workforce review indicated that an annual uplift of three general surgery trainees per year will be required for the foreseeable future. The Elective Care Framework has made a commitment to fund an additional 50 training posts, across a range of specialties, by the end of March 2028.I can assure you that progress is being made and my Department’s Workforce Policy team will continue to engage with the Public Health Agency and local Trusts to ensure that appropriate actions are taken to address our workforce challenges for both medical and surgical specialties.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why the traffic lights at the A25 Barnmeen, Newry Road, Rathfriland, have remained inoperable, including any (i) assessments carried out; and (ii) actions taken to date.
answer_text: I recognise the inconvenience caused by the temporary closure of one lane on the Newry Road, Rathfriland.  The Department is aware of two occasions whereby the traffic lights at A25 Barnmeen, Newry Road, Rathfriland became inoperable, one of which was caused by a battery fault which was replaced within an hour of being reported.  The second was due to an act of vandalism on the traffic lights which were replaced within 90 minutes of the reported fault being received.  I can confirm that a work instruction has been issued to the Department’s contractor, and it is hoped that work to repair the landslip will commence in mid-February.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail on which date the traffic lights at the A25 Barnmeen, Newry Road, Rathfriland, (i) first became inoperable; and (ii) were last fully operational.
answer_text: I recognise the inconvenience caused by the temporary closure of one lane on the Newry Road, Rathfriland. The second was due to an act of vandalism on the traffic lights which were replaced within 90 minutes of the reported fault being received.  I can confirm that a work instruction has been issued to the Department’s contractor, and it is hoped that work to repair the landslip will commence in mid-February.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 36624/22-27 and AQW 36625/22-27, to detail the specific (i) legal; and (ii) policy grounds, under which her Department has deemed the financial cost of roadworks on the B27 outside Shannon Park, Hilltown, to be commercially sensitive, including (a) which exemptions to disclosure have been applied; and (b) why publication would prejudice the Department’s or contractors’ commercial interests.
answer_text: The Department has determined that this information is commercially sensitive and, therefore, exempt from release.  This determination is based on Section 43(2) of the Freedom of Information Act 2000, which permits public authorities to withhold information if sharing it would, or could likely, harm commercial interests. Disclosing contract pricing and related details such as quantities / timescales could enable competitors to deduce contractor rates, potentially undermining both the Department’s and its contractors’ ability to compete fairly in future tenders. Additionally, such disclosure may erode market confidence, reduce competitive bidding, and compromise value for money in public procurement. Departmental policy prioritises the protection of commercially sensitive information to maintain fair and competitive tendering processes and to safeguard the integrity of public sector procurement. For these reasons, the financial cost related to the B27 landslip to date cannot be released.However, once the scheme is complete, my Department will be able to provide the overall cost of the work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to increase the £4.7 million budget in-year for GPs to carry out local enhanced proactive care in care homes.
answer_text: The Enhanced Service for Proactive Care currently has an annual fixed budget of £4.7m.  My Department are aware of the pressures on this budget.Following an exercise to realign budgets an additional £200k has been secured to increase the budget for the current financial year to £4.9m.My Department will continue to work within the current financial budget, there may be the opportunity to identify a further £200k, bringing this years budget to £5.1m. I am optimistic my Department will be able to confirm this additional £200k in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department has taken, in each of the last five years, to improve sightlines at the blind corner on the B1 Ardglass Road, particularly given the heavy traffic volumes and previous widening works that excluded this section.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any statutory responsibility her Department has for ensuring adequate sightlines and road safety, on B Class roads.
answer_text: My Department, as the Road Authority, has a responsibility to ensure the safety of the road user and travelling public, which would include consideration of appropriate sight lines. The Department uses the Design Manual for Roads and Bridges (DMRB) to establish minimum standards for new sight lines on all roads, including B class roads, through the implementation of CD123 - Geometric Design of at grade priority and signal controlled junctions. DCAN 15 and the Department’s Creating Places document provide guidance on sight lines in residential developments outside the immediate approaches to the road network. My officials regularly undertake reviews of collision data from the PSNI relating to all roads on the network, to determine where engineering measures can deliver the most significant road safety benefits.  The B1 Ardglass Road has not highlighted any particular issues of concern, however I have asked officials to review the existing signage and road markings to ensure they are appropriate for the area.
