question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount that has been spent on locum consultants, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether his Department is aware of reports that some Foundation year two doctors expect to be without employment when their placements end.
answer_text: I am aware of the concerns you have raised. I wish to reiterate my clear desire to ensure that positions are available across the HSC for our local graduates who have completed their training and wish to pursue their career here. Officials in my Workforce Policy Directorate are currently working with colleagues in England, Scotland and Wales on the development of changes to the current application process for both the Medical Foundation and Medical Specialty Training programmes. These changes are intended to address the recognised issue of bottlenecks into training programmes, in particular between the Foundation and Medical Specialty programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of bids submitted to (i) his Department; (ii) the Strategic Planning and Performance Group; and (iii) the Public Health Agency, by GP Federations and GP Practices for (a) recruitment; and (b) training of (i) Advanced Nurse Practitioners; and (ii) General Practice Nurses, broken down by the number of such requests that were approved or not approved, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not recruit or employ any Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs) or General Practice Nurses (GPNs) so is unable to comment on recruitment requests.  This is also the case for Public Health Agency (PHA) who do not recruit ANPs or GPNs.ANPs and GPNs within General Practice are either employed directly by GP Practices, in which case the recruitment takes place through the individual GP Practice or by GP Federations.  Where employment is through the GP Practice, this will be funded by the individual practice.  In the case of GP Federations, funding is available for ANPs and GPNs as set out below. My Department do not invite funding requests from GP Federations as all funding available is utilised:Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs)My Department allocate funding to six GP Federations (Armagh & Dungannon, Derry, Down, East Belfast, Mid-Ulster and South West) to recruit and employ ANPs to work in GP Practices within their Federation area.  For the 2025/26 year, a total of £3.481m has been allocated across six GP Federations to facilitate the employment of 38 WTE ANPs.  However, recruitment and employment of these ANPs takes place though the GP Federations.General Practice Nurses (GPNs)My Department allocate funding to five GP Federations (Antrim & Ballymena, Ards, Armagh & Dungannon, South West and West Belfast) to recruit and employ GPNs to work in GP Practices within their Federation area.  For the 2025/26 year, a total of £1.833m has been allocated across five GP Federations to facilitate the employment of 28 WTE GPNs.  However, recruitment and employment of these GPNs takes place though the GP Federations.With regards to training for ANPs and GPNs, this too is either arranged by GP Practices for the staff employed by themselves or by GP Federations. On an Annual basis, PHA undertakes discussions with Primary Care Nursing to ascertain training requirements and facilitates training opportunities with the financial allocations from SPPG and the Nursing Post Graduate Education Group led by the Department of Health.  In the 5 years from 2021/22 to 2025/26, 16 training places for GPNs have been made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any analysis conducted on the causes of oversubscription to specialty training programmes, including (ii) the impact of increased medical graduate numbers; and (iii) any constraints on expanding training posts.
answer_text: The available information would indicate that the increasing numbers of applications to medical specialty training programmes across the UK is primarily driven by higher numbers of International Medical Graduates applying to these programmes.Officials in my Workforce Policy Directorate are currently working with colleagues in England, Scotland and Wales on the development of changes to the current application process for Medical Specialty Training programmes, which are intended to address the recognised issue of bottlenecks into training programmes, in particular between the Foundation and Medical Specialty programmes. Despite the significant financial difficulties facing my Department in recent years, I have been able to secure funding to ensure that foundation training posts align with the output from both local medical schools. I have also been able to secure some expansions in medical specialty training posts. However, any future expansions will be dependent on available funding and must align with workforce planning requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment made of whether there are currently sufficient specialty training posts for junior doctors progressing from Foundation Year two.
answer_text: I am aware of ongoing concerns about whether there are sufficient specialty training posts for resident doctors about to complete their Foundation training.  I wish to reiterate my clear desire to ensure that positions are available across the HSC for our local graduates who have completed their training and wish to pursue their career here.  Officials in my Workforce Policy Directorate are currently working with colleagues in England, Scotland and Wales on the development of changes to the current application process for Medical Specialty Training programmes, which are intended to address the recognised issue of bottlenecks into training programmes, in particular between the Foundation and Medical Specialty programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of business cases submitted to (i) his Department; (ii) the Strategic Planning and Performance Group; and (iii) the Public Health Agency, by GP Federations and GP Practices for (a) recruitment; and (b) training of (i) Advanced Nurse Practitioners; and (ii) General Practice Nurses, broken down by the number of such requests that were approved or not approved, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not recruit or employ any Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs) or General Practice Nurses (GPNs) so is unable to comment on recruitment requests.  This is also the case for Public Health Agency (PHA) who do not recruit ANPs or GPNs.ANPs and GPNs within General Practice are either employed directly by GP Practices, in which case the recruitment takes place through the individual GP Practice or by GP Federations.  Where employment is through the GP Practice, this will be funded by the individual practice.  In the case of GP Federations, funding is available for ANPs and GPNs as set out below.  SPPG do not invite funding requests from GP Federations as all funding available is utilised:Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs)My Department allocate funding to six GP Federations (Armagh & Dungannon, Derry, Down, East Belfast, Mid-Ulster and South West) to recruit and employ ANPs to work in GP Practices within their Federation area.  For the 2025/26 year, a total of £3.481m has been allocated across six GP Federations to facilitate the employment of 38 WTE ANPs.  However, recruitment and employment of these ANPs takes place though the GP Federations.General Practice Nurses (GPNs)My Department allocate funding to five GP Federations (Antrim & Ballymena, Ards, Armagh & Dungannon, South West and West Belfast) to recruit and employ GPNs to work in GP Practices within their Federation area.  For the 2025/26 year, a total of £1.833m has been allocated across five GP Federations to facilitate the employment of 28 WTE GPNs.  However, recruitment and employment of these GPNs takes place though the GP Federations.With regards to training for ANPs and GPNs, this too is either arranged by GP Practices for the staff employed by themselves or by GP Federations.    On an Annual basis, PHA undertakes discussions with Primary Care Nursing to ascertain training requirements and facilitates training opportunities with the financial allocations from SPPG and the Nursing Post Graduate Education Group led by the Department of Health.  In the 5 years from 2021/22 to 2025/26, 16 training places for GPNs have been made available.

answer_text: It should be noted that the Trusts have confirmed that where facilities remain occupied whilst requiring high-risk backlog maintenance they continue to manage the risks associated with same, introducing mitigation measures where appropriate allowing the facilities to be assessed as remaining in a safe state to support service delivery. A breakdown of the £251 million of high-risk backlog maintenance, including examples of specific risks identified and the potential impact on patient and staff safety is detailed in the table below:Category / CostElementExamplesPotential Risk/ImpactBuilding/£115,000,000RoofsFlat roof coverings that need to be replaced to avoid leaksRoof leaks resulting internal damage and disruption to servicesRainwater GoodsGuttering and downpipes in need of replacementPotential for sections of existing gutters and pipes to fall and cause injury to staff, service users or visitorsStructuralSpalling concrete and brickworkPotential for loose material to fall from height and cause injury to staff, service users or visitorsElectrical / £42,000,000 SwitchgearOperating beyond design life rangePotential for unplanned electrical interruptions causing disruption to servicesDistribution BoardsOperating beyond design life range and not compliant with current standardsPotential for unplanned electrical interruptions causing disruption to services. Risk of electric shock to staff and contractors working on the boards (risk managed by Estates)Sub-Main CablingOperating beyond design lifePotential for unplanned electrical interruptions causing disruption to servicesFire Alarm WiringOperating beyond design lifeIntermittent faults with fire alarm system requiring immediate repair to maintain adequate fire detection.Building Management SystemOperating beyond design lifeFailures resulting in issues with heating, ventilation and hot waterMechanical / £34,000,000  Heating / Hot WaterHeat exchangers / calorifiers very aged and in poor conditionWater leaks and interruptions to heating and hot water suppliesPipeworkInsulation missingRisk of scalding to staff working in plant roomsFire Safety / £12,000,000 Fire DoorsRemedial actions required to ensure all doors are up to current standardInadequate prevention of fire spreadFire CompartmentationRemedial actions required to ensure integrity of all fire compartmentationsInadequate prevention of fire spreadStatutory Safety / £48,000,000 LegionellaSome remedial actions from legionella risk assessments outstandingSpread of Legionella bacteria in water systems - Managed as per Trusts Water Safety PlanCOSHHSome COSHH registers need to be updatedRisk of injury to staff handling hazardous substances (COSHH audits biannually)Health & SafetySome task specific risk assessments for confined space, lone working, noise, working from height etc)Risk of injury to staff if correct procedures not adhered to

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) wards; (ii) theatres; (iii) diagnostic rooms; and (iv) clinical areas, closed or restricted in each of the last five years due to estate failure, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care Trusts responses are provided below.Western Health and Social Care TrustThe Western Health & Social Care Trust does not maintain a list of areas closed or restricted due to estate failure in the past five years. The Estates Senior Management Team confirmed that closures of wards, theatres, diagnostic or clinical areas are highly unusual, and no logged shutdowns exist. Robust business continuity arrangements ensure minimal impact during critical failures, with services decanted to alternative areas when necessary.  Recorded incidents resulting from estate failure include:             Southwest Acute Hospital (SWAH) Intensive Care Unit (ICU) was decanted due to flood from 03/05/23 to 18/05/23; provision of services continued under continuity plan.2025 - Altnagelvin Neo Natal Unit Roof Defect. Unit decanted to Maternity Unit for 5 weeks.  The neo natal unit was closed to regional admissions during the decant period.SWAH Theatre 1 was closed 26/07/23 to 03/08/23 because of Reverse Osmosis Plant leak.  Theatre lists were maintained using alternative theatres.2025 - Altnagelvin Cardiac Cath Lab 1 was closed for 24 days due to an electrical failure.  Cath Lab 1 activity was relocated to Cath Lab 2 during the period. Northern Health and Social Care Trust Minor estates issues such as roof leaks, heating faults and localised electrical trips etc happen regularly.  These are normally dealt with very quickly and do not require service cancellations or closures. The following incidents have resulted in services being cancelled, restricted or relocated in the last five years: (i)Wards2022 - Causeway Hospital Paediatric ward provision restricted for 1 day due to soil pipe failure. (ii) Theatres 2021 – Mid Ulster Hospital Theatres - 2 days restricted provision due to summer high working temperature.2022 – Mid Ulster Hospital Theatres - 2 days restricted provision due to summer high working temperature.2023 – Mid Ulster Hospital Theatres - 2 days restricted provision due to summer high working temperature.2024 – Mid Ulster Hospital Theatres - 2 days restricted provision due to summer high working temperature.2025 – Mid Ulster Hospital Theatres - 4 days restricted provision due to summer high working temperature and extract fan failure. (iii) Diagnostic rooms 2025 - Causeway Hospital - X-ray Dept provision affected for part day due to power failure issue.2025 - Causeway Hospital - Ultrasound Room relocated for 6 weeks due to underfloor leaks.  (iv) Clinical areas2025 - Causeway Hospital Emergency Department restricted for 1 day due to soil pipe failure.Southeastern Health and Social Care TrustThe Trust does not hold this information.Southern Health and Social Care TrustThe Trust does not hold this information.Belfast Health and Social Care TrustThe Estates department are not aware of any recorded closures or restrictions to wards, theatres, diagnostic rooms or clinical areas as a result of estate failure within BHSCT. The Estates dep

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any statutory regulation currently not being met across the health and social care estate, given that almost 60 per cent of the estate is not fully compliant.
answer_text: The State of the Estate Report notes that 60% of the estate is compliant with statutory legislation with 40% not fully conforming. When making this assessment, it is important to acknowledge that approximately 46% of the Estate is over 50 years old. When constructed, these facilities complied with the relevant standards at the time of installation, however as building codes and safety regulations evolve over time, these buildings no longer comply when assessed against current legislation. It is important to note that whilst backlog maintenance liability is significant and is considered a corporate risk all buildings in use have been assessed as remaining in a safe state to support Trust service delivery. Legislative authorities generally do not require existing buildings to be updated to meet new legislative standards, unless significant renovations are planned or the building's use changes.Statutory regulations currently not being fully met across the health and social care estate are listed below alongside several mitigations that are in place to enable the non-compliant premises to remain in use:RegulationMitigations  Electricity at work (Northern Ireland) 1991Control of Asbestos Regulations 2012Control of Substances Hazardous to Health (COSHH) e.g. Water Borne pathogens, LegionellaThe Fire Safety Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2010Disability Discrimination Act (DDA)Robust governance arrangements are in placeIndependent Assessment of Governance Arrangements by Authorised Engineers Regular Inspections of Facilities  Inspections by external bodies (both planned and adhoc)Asbestos surveys and registerTrusts Water Safety PlanCOSHH audits biannuallyFire risk assessments completed for all facilitiesAll Trust fire safety advisors hold appropriate external accreditation

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the proportion of the £1.6 billion backlog maintenance requirement that the current £5.5 million December Monitoring bid would address; and (ii) any criteria will be used to prioritise works.
answer_text: The £5.5 million will address approximately 0.34% of the £1.6 billion backlog maintenance requirement.As we approach the final quarter of the financial year, the capacity of the HSC Trusts and NIFRS to take forward and deliver additional funded backlog maintenance projects by the 31 March 2026 greatly diminishes due to the time available. Therefore the ability to deliver in financial year was the only criteria applied in this case.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any data (i) his Department; (ii) the Strategic Planning and Performance Group; and (iii) the Public Health Agency, currently collect on (a) workforce demand for Advanced Nurse Practitioners; and (b) General Practice Nurses within general practice.
answer_text: ANPs and GPNs within General Practice are either employed directly by GP Practices, in which case the recruitment takes place through the individual GP Practice or by GP Federations.  Where employment is through the GP Practice, this will be funded by the individual practice.  In the case of GP Federations:Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs)SPPG allocates funding to six GP Federations (Armagh & Dungannon, Derry, Down, East Belfast, Mid-Ulster and South West) to recruit and employ ANPs to work in GP Practices within their Federation area.  For the 2025/26 year, a total of £3.481m has been allocated across six GP Federations to facilitate the employment of 38 WTE ANPs.  Recruitment and employment of these ANPs takes place though the GP Federations.General Practice Nurses (GPNs)SPPG allocates funding to five GP Federations (Antrim & Ballymena, Ards, Armagh & Dungannon, South West and West Belfast) to recruit and employ GPNs to work in GP Practices within their Federation area.  For the 2026/26 year, a total of £1.833m has been allocated across five GP Federations to facilitate the employment of 28 WTE GPNs.  Recruitment and employment of these GPNs takes place though the GP Federations.With regards to training for ANPs and GPNs, this too is either arranged by GP Practices for the staff employed by the Practice or by GP Federations.  On an annual basis, PHA undertakes discussions with Practice Managers, frontline General Practice Nurses and Federation Nurse leads to ascertain training requirements and facilitates training opportunities with the financial allocations from SPPG and the Nursing Post Graduate Education Group led by the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the level of independent sector capacity that is being used for health service breast cancer clinic waiting lists; (ii) whether there are plans to bring teams from outside Northern Ireland to run waiting list Initiative clinics; and (iii) how his Department is being assured that capacity is used optimally across all provisions, including (a) out of hours clinics in health service hospitals; (b) independent sector clinics; and (c) bringing in NHS teams from outside Northern Ireland.
answer_text: (i) the level of independent sector capacity that is being used for health service breast cancer clinic waiting lists; Independent sector provision, commissioned under the Reset Plan and waiting list initiatives, has supplemented in-house services by delivering additional outpatient and diagnostic activity for time-critical patients. For Breast Cancer Clinics current arrangements include both in-house and Independent Sector capacity, while strategic work continues to strengthen core HSC services through sustainable and efficient care pathway models.  From April to September 2025, £344,000 of non-recurrent funding supported Breast Services waiting list initiatives, enabling assessment and treatment through a mix of in-house and Independent Sector capacity.  Additional non-recurrent investment is anticipated to address backlogs and improve timely access to care.(ii) whether there are plans to bring teams from outside Northern Ireland to run waiting list Initiative clinics;My Department is in advanced discussions with an NHS Scotland team to provide additional capacity for red flag breast assessment clinics in Northern Ireland.   The team operates a consultant-led, multidisciplinary model that will help reduce our backlogs, and support workforce development to allow more innovative approaches to local service delivery and demonstrate the Scottish model in practice.  iii) how his Department is being assured that capacity is used optimally across all provisions, including (a) out of hours clinics in health service hospitals; (b) independent sector clinics; and (c) bringing in NHS teams from outside Northern Ireland.My Department is working closely with Trusts to ensure optimal use of all available capacity for breast assessment services, including core, extended and out-of-hours clinics within HSC Trust hospitals, specialist UK NHS insourcing and IS provision.  Red Flag referrals are prioritised within core capacity to safeguard timely access for clinically urgent cases. The Department is assured that capacity is being used optimally across all provisions through a combination of contractual oversight, performance monitoring and regulatory review.  Out of hours HSC clinics - Health and Social Care Trusts report regularly on the utilisation of out of hours clinics, including activity levels and waiting times.  These are reviewed through established governance structures and performance monitoring arrangements to ensure clinics are scheduled and staff in line with demand and available resources.Independent sector clinics – The Trusts commissions activity from independent sector providers to bolster HSC capacity under contracts that include clear key performance indicators (KPIs) for throughput, quality and integration with HSC system. Utilisation is monitored against elective care priorities, ensuring that commissioned capacity addresses the greatest areas of need.Bringing in NHS teams from outside Northern Ireland – Where additional specialist support

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timetable for (i) analysing the findings of the Learning Disability Service Model consultation; (ii) publishing the findings; (iii) processing the final framework; (iv) securing the financial investment needed; and (v) implementing the plan.
answer_text: The 14-week public consultation on the Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM) and the supporting three-year Service Delivery Plan (SDP) closed on 25 November 2025. I am pleased to advise that 264 formal responses were received to the consultation, while approximately 350 people attended in-person and on-line consultation events. I am grateful to everyone who took the time to provide a response. Feedback from each consultation event has been published on my Department’s website.My officials have begun work to collate and analyse the responses and prepare an initial consultation summary report, which is planned to be available in January 2026 with the full consultation analysis report expected to be completed by March 2026. Both reports will be published on my Department’s website.The findings from the consultation will be used to inform any further refinements to the LDSM and the SDP, which sets out key outcomes, actions and indicators over a three year period. Once these are finalised, they will inform decisions on the future reconfiguration of services for people with a learning disability and any additional investment required. Delivery of the ambitions outlined within the Model will be subject to funding made available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following his attendance at the Disabled People’s Parliament on 21 November 2025, to detail any plans he has to improve the lives of disabled people, including those with a learning disability.
answer_text: Disabled People should have equal access to the equipment, and services that will enable them to live independently and well, and to provide choice and decision making in their own lives. You may be aware of the Health and Social Care (HSC) Reset Plan, which was published on 9 July 2025, and represents the next steps in the outworking of my three-year strategic plan for HSC. It builds on the three pillars of stabilisation, reform and delivery. The Reset Plan is designed to reshape our HSC system into one that is more accessible, equitable, and sustainable. For disabled people, this means better local access, faster treatment, and stronger protections against inequality in service provision. We know that disabled people, who very often require timely interventions, benefit directly from shorter waits and faster access to treatment.At the heart of the reform agenda must be concrete progress on neighbourhood care, bringing more services as close as possible to people’s front doors. This ‘shift left’ has been a long-term objective but meaningful delivery is required, including a new model of primary care and early intervention.This neighbourhood approach will help tackle health inequalities, and support individuals to look after their own health and well-being, while recognising that health interventions are only one element to improving well-being. Of equal importance are employment, housing, education and other important services delivered across Government.In recognition of the need to ensure equal access to appropriate equipment my Department recently completed a Regional Review of Assistive Technology and Equipment Provision. This has resulted in the setting up of a Regional Assistive Technology Equipment Oversight Board, which is working towards implementation of the recommendations from the Review. This work is also an important component within the Reset Plan and it focuses on a regionally consistent approach to assessment and provision. Disabled people face a number of challenges, particularly in regard to communications. There is ongoing work within the regional communication support service, contracted by the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group, to Sign Language Interactions (SLI).  This work will enhance Deaf and Deafblind individuals’ experience of  communication when they access health and social care.   In respect of the provision for people with learning disabilities, my Department recently concluded a public consultation on a draft Learning Disability Service Model, which closed on 25 November 2025. The Model has been developed in collaboration with HSC Trusts, independent sector providers and people who use HSC services and outlines a roadmap for transforming learning disability services in Northern Ireland over the next 3 years. The draft model sets out a regionally consistent framework to improve the key outcomes we want to achieve, and the principles that will guide delivery and actions needed to improve service

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any progress made in the planning and formulation of the Grassroots Facilities Programme element of the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: Answer:Significant progress has been made in the planning and formulation of the Grassroots Facilities Programme of The Northern Ireland Football Fund.I have approved high level policy proposals for the Grassroots Strand and my officials are working to complete a business case. The application process is being finalised, and I intend to make a formal announcement in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the level of consultation carried out as part of the Northern Ireland Football Fund, to date, to identify the needs of grassroots football clubs.
answer_text: Answer:My officials continue to work at pace and have engaged with the Irish FA, Irish FA Foundation, councils and representatives of grassroots leagues to identify the needs of grassroots football clubs.I have visited a number of grassroots clubs and witnessed first-hand the significant need for upgrades to their facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how the Northern Ireland Neighbourhood Model of Care will support the delivery of the Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy 2022-2032.
answer_text: The Neighbourhood Model of Care seeks to establish a new approach to primary, community, and social care, requiring providers across all areas of Health and Social Care to work collaboratively in new ways to deliver services closer to home.Although this work is currently in the early stages of development, it is clear that many of the actions within the Cancer Strategy closely align with the ethos of the Neighbourhood Model, including for example the provision of specialist care in primary-care settings, and the provision of cancer services closer to home, where this is clinically appropriate. The Cancer Strategy also focuses on reducing health inequalities for those affected by cancer by ensuring that support services are accessible across our local communities.The current budgetary constraints across the Health and Social Care system significantly limits the pace of Cancer Strategy delivery and the level of service transformation that can be achieved.  Sustained, multi-year investment is required to ensure that we have the best possible cancer services available, that can support the early diagnosis and treatment of cancer at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) proposed; and (ii) actual, level of funding allocated to the Grassroots Facilities Programme element of the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: Answer:No expenditure decisions have yet been made in relation to any of the three strands of The Northern Ireland Football Fund (TNIFF).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any services which are operating on a non-recurrent funding basis, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There are a number of reasons why allocations to Trusts may be made on a non-recurrent basis. Services may be operating on a pilot basis or the service is currently in development. Therefore, non-recurrent funding is often used to test new services before committing to long-term investment. This allows us to evaluate effectiveness and impact without locking in permanent costs. To date some £17.7m has been issued non-recurrently to Trusts for SureStart. Other areas where funding is only available non-recurrently are; Magee Medical School (£6m), Transformation funding relating to Multi-Disciplinary Teams (MDTs) (£3.3m), NI Protocol (£2.2m) and Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme (£1.1m).Table 2 Non-Recurrent Funding BHSCTNHSCTSEHSCTSHSCTWHSCTNIAS£’000£’000£’000£’000£’000£’000Total6,8035,8395,6938,7193,83624There are other circumstances where the costs may vary from year to year, as such, funding is allocated on a non-recurrent basis. The most significant is in areas such as training for medical and dental education (SUMDE) (currently £31m), training and education (currently £9.5m), PPE (currently £10m), digital services (currently £3.8m) and drugs pressures (currently £4m). question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will answer AQW 36117/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 36117/22-27 was answered on 9 December 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of dental-related presentations at Emergency Departments, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Trust information systems do not have coding to identify data in respect of dental-related presentations at Emergency Departments. Securing this information would likely require a specific search of all patient records in each of the five years requested. Whilst I am unable to provide an estimate of the timeframe for this, I understand the scale of search required would involve redirection of staff time with a likely impact on direct patient care or the identification of funding to support a specific initiative over and above core hours.Whilst appreciating the concerns in respect of this issue, I would caution against asking the service to direct resource to undertake this work given the current pressures on the service, the scale of the ask and the potential for erroneous results as outlined.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the financial cost, to date, for the roadworks on the B27, outside Shannon Park, Hilltown.
answer_text: I recognise the inconvenience caused by the temporary closures of one lane on the B27 Kilkeel Road Hilltown following the land/verge slip caused by the unprecedented weather in November 2023.The requested financial information is considered commercially sensitive.  Disclosure of such data could enable others, particularly economic competitors, to identify commercial rates and costs associated with these works, which may potentially undermine the legitimate economic interests of the Department and its contractors.I can confirm that site investigation works were completed in recent days and a contractor has been appointed to complete the works. It is hoped that work will commence early in the new year and will take eight to ten weeks to complete once started. My Department will continue to prioritise the reopening of the B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown to two-way traffic as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the roadworks on the B27, outside Shannon Park, Hilltown, will be concluded.
answer_text: I recognise the inconvenience caused by the temporary closures of one lane on the B27 Kilkeel Road Hilltown following the land/verge slip caused by the unprecedented weather in November 2023.The requested financial information is considered commercially sensitive.  Disclosure of such data could enable others, particularly economic competitors, to identify commercial rates and costs associated with these works, which may potentially undermine the legitimate economic interests of the Department and its contractors.I can confirm that site investigation works were completed in recent days and a contractor has been appointed to complete the works. It is hoped that work will commence early in the new year and will take eight to ten weeks to complete once started. My Department will continue to prioritise the reopening of the B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown to two-way traffic as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the outline business case for Downpatrick flooding solutions will be published.
answer_text: My Department has commenced work to develop the business case for the potential flood alleviation scheme in Downpatrick. The business case will be published following completion of this work, which is currently expected to be Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the dates on which the Statement of Financial Entitlement has been published, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The Statement of Financial Entitlements (SFE) outlines the key financial arrangements for GMS Contractors. The Statements of Financial Entitlements, which include the dates on which the Directions were sealed, are available on my Department’s website at:GP contract statements of financial entitlements | Department of HealthSFE effective from Date sealed/published1 April 201529 September 20151 April 201621 October 20161 April 20172 July 20181 April 20186 June 20191 April 202213 March 2024During the period of Covid, with arrangements in place at that time, the extant SFE then in place was rolled over. With the closure of the Health and Social Care Board and the transfer of its functions into the Department, the previous arrangements whereby the Department issued Directions to the HSCB to carry out those functions on its behalf, is no longer appropriate.  The Statement of Financial Entitlements for 2022/23 was therefore not issued as a Departmental Direction.  Whilst the latest version of the SFE (2022/23), published on 13 March 2024, is not up to date, all payments made to GPs for elements included in the SFE have been paid correctly and in line with up-to-date values. My officials have committed to providing the 2025/26 SFE to the NI General Practitioners Committee by the end of January 2026, with the remaining SFEs for 2023/24 and 2024/25 provided by the end of March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 34447/22-27, to detail (i) when her Department last reviewed the suitability of Care and Supervision Units for individuals with learning disabilities; and (ii) whether further reviews are planned.
answer_text: Many individuals with neurodiverse conditions, including learning disability, arrive in prison with complex and unmet needs. The Northern Ireland Prison Service delivers a safe, secure and decent environment for all those who are in the prison community.In December 2022, the Northern Ireland Prison Service implemented a person-centred Care and Supervision Unit Operational Framework to manage and support those who present a risk to the safety of themselves and others. A key principle of the framework is that individuals who have additional needs are supported, and their regime is assessed based on their needs.The Care and Supervision Unit Operational Framework is due for review in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 34447/22-27, to detail the (i) average; and (ii) longest, duration of placements in Care and Supervision Units for individuals with an identified learning disability, since July 2024.
answer_text: From July 2024 the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust agreed to provide confirmation to the Northern Ireland Prison Service of people in custody who have an intellectual disability/learning disability (ID/LD) upon committal. Data is therefore only available from July 2024. The average duration of a placement of a person with an ID/LD within a Care and Supervision Unit since July 2024 is 16 days.The longest duration of a placement of a person with ID/LD within a Care and Supervision Unit is 116 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 34447/22-27, to detail any alternative, less restrictive options available within the prison estate, specifically for individuals with an identified learning disability who may be struggling in the general prison environment.
answer_text: Many people who arrive in prison have complex, challenging and often unmet needs. Each prison has specific accommodation for individuals who have additional needs, including those who have an identified learning disability. This is supplemented by well-being hubs in each prison and a range of interventions available through NIPS and its partner agencies.Together with environmental changes, access to garden spaces and therapeutic activity at each prison site, Prison Service Prisoner Safety and Support Teams provide support to people who have additional needs, including those with learning disability. The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust provide primary, mental health and addictions services in prisons and have learning disability nurses and speech and language therapists and a wide range of other health professionals at each site. Prison Service staff are provided with a range of training interventions to equip them to do their job.  This includes training in neurodiverse conditions and mental health.  In addition, the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust support Prison Service staff by providing advice on how best to communicate with individuals who have specific needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail whether Health and Safety Executive approval is required for the spraying of (i) Vulcan P RFU; (ii) Permost Uni Fly Killer Spray; (iii) Archem NI Deodoriser; (iv) Cypermax Plus; (v) Hospec Pine Disinfectant; (vi) Perbio Choc RTU; and (vii) Cypermethrin Insecticide.
answer_text: Health and Safety Executive approval is not required for the spraying of products listed in the question.The role of HSENI is to check that duty holders use products such as those listed, in compliance with the Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003 (COSHH) and the approval and authorisation granted under their respective biocide legislations, detailed below and take appropriate enforcement action where necessary.Biocide Regulations:REGULATION (EU) No 528/2012 OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL of 22 May 2012 concerning the making available on the market and use of biocidal products.THE CONTROL OF PESTICIDES REGULATIONS 1986Under COSHH, dutyholders must adequately control exposure to hazardous substances in the workplace.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact that the spraying of (i) Vulcan P RFU; (ii) Permost Uni Fly Spray; (iii) Archem NI Deodouriser; (iv) Cypermax Plus; (v) Hospec Pine Disinfectant; (vi) Digrain Perbio Choc RTU; and (vii) Cypermethrin insecticide, could have for environmental contamination of Carlingford Lough.
answer_text: The products Vulcan P RFU, Permost Uni Fly Spray, Cypermax Plus, and Digrain Perbio Choc RTU are insecticides that contain synthetic pyrethroids such as tetramethrin, permethrin, and cypermethrin as the active ingredient.The products Archem NI Deodoriser and Hospec Pine Disinfectant are biocidal disinfectants.  Cypermethrin and permethrin are considered highly toxic to aquatic organisms and must be used in line with the manufacturer instructions. Where these products are used in line with manufacturers’ instructions there should be minimal risk of environmental contamination of Carlingford Lough.The Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003, known as COSHH, are specific regulations in place regarding the safe use of chemicals. DAERA is not the enforcing authority for the COSHH regulations, and any concerns should be addressed to the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that the spraying of (i) Vulcan P RFU; (ii) Permost Uni Fly Spray; (iii) Archem NI Deodouriser; (iv) Cypermax Plus; (v) Hospec Pine Disinfectant; (vi) Digrain Perbio Choc RTU; and (vii) Cypermethrin insecticide, could have for human health.
answer_text: The Department does not have policy or operational enforcement responsibility for chemical safety regulation. The Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland is the enforcing authority for biocidal products and other chemicals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the spraying of (i) Vulcan P RFU; (ii) Permost Uni Fly Spray; (iii) Archem NI Deodouriser; (iv) Cypermax Plus; (v) Hospec Pine Disinfectant; (vi) Digrain Perbio Choc RTU; and (vii) Cypermethrin insecticide, in close proximity to a residential area of Warrenpoint.
answer_text: The Department does not regulate the use of these products and, therefore, is not required to assess their use.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail whether there are any Health and Safety Executive (i) restrictions; (ii) conditions on mixing; or (iii) maximum quantities for the spraying of (a) Vulcan P RFU; (b) Permost Uni Fly Killer Spray; (c) Archem NI Deodoriser; (d) Cypermax Plus; (e) Hospec Pine Disinfectant; (f) Perbio Choc RTU; and (g) Cypermethrin Insecticide, in close proximity to a residential area of Warrenpoint.
answer_text: The Health and Safety Executive do not place (i) restrictions; (ii) conditions on mixing; or (iii) maximum quantities for the spraying of the products listed.The role of HSENI is to check that duty holders use products such as those listed, in compliance with the Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003 (COSHH) and the approval and authorisation granted under their respective biocide legislations, detailed below and take appropriate enforcement action where necessary.Biocide Regulations:REGULATION (EU) No 528/2012 OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL of 22 May 2012 concerning the making available on the market and use of biocidal products.THE CONTROL OF PESTICIDES REGULATIONS 1986Under COSHH, dutyholders must adequately control exposure to hazardous substances in the workplace.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when she will answer AQW 33996/22-27.
answer_text: The minister responded to this question on Monday December 1st 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of reports made to her Department, regarding overflowing manhole covers, on (a) Stream Street; (b) St Patricks Avenue; (c) Killough Road; and (d) Pound Lane, Downpatrick, in each of the last ten years; and (ii) the remedial action that was taken.
answer_text: The Department does not hold details of drainage related issues in a format which allows for the number of reports of overflowing manholes to be identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any timescales in place for the next steps, to address flooding in Downpatrick.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 34339/22-27, whether he will place a map in the Assembly library of the extent of (i) his Department's; and (ii) arm's-length bodies', land lease in (a) Donard Wood, Newcastle; and (b) the surrounding area.
answer_text: In my response to AQW 34339/22-27, I indicated that the land referred to at Donard is owned by a 3rd party and I have no plans to place the ownership map and surrounding area in the assembly Library as these are matters for Land Registry and Register of Deeds processes.  However, as the Department is the lessee and manages the forested area of Donard, Forest Service provides mapped information associated with the area held under lease in its Forestry Planning Interactive Maps – Down Forest Plan section of the DAERA website.  The boundary of the leased area is available for viewing by the public on this platform by selecting the Regeneration Plan tab.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) number of available beds in the Downe Hospital; and (ii) location of each bed, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information on the number of available beds in Downe Hospital for each of the last five years is published annually in the ‘Hospital Statistics: Inpatient and Day Case Activity’ report, the most recent of which covers the period April 2024 to March 2025 and is available on my Department’s website:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/hospital-statistics-inpatient-and-day-case-activityFor data prior to the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust’s launch of encompass, this information can be found in the downloadable dataset titled “Hospital Statistics: Inpatient and Day Case Activity Pre-encompass”. For data following encompass implementation, the relevant figures are available in the dataset titled “Hospital Statistics: Inpatient and Day Case Activity by Programme of Care (POC)”.Please note that these figures include beds in wards which are open overnight and represent both occupied and unoccupied commissioned beds at the time of the midnight count. Escalation spaces used throughout the year are not included in these available bed figures.Recognising the challenges associated with hospital capacity and patient flow, my Department has worked closely with Health and Social Care Trusts to implement a range of actions aimed at improving bed availability and reducing delays in admission and discharge.To help alleviate pressures, targeted initiatives have included:Expansion of Same Day Emergency Care (SDEC) pathways, enabling patients to receive assessment and treatment without requiring overnight admission; andEnhanced discharge planning and community support, ensuring patients who no longer need acute care can transition safely to home or community settings.These measures form part of a broader system-wide approach to improve patient flow, reduce congestion, and ensure timely, safe, and effective care across the health and social care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) why the hydraulic modelling for Marguerite Avenue and the wider Newcastle area, which NI Water previously informed elected representatives, would be completed by summer 2025 and then early autumn 2025, has now been delayed until Spring 2026; and (ii) the immediate steps she will take to ensure this work is expedited, in light of ongoing flooding concerns.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information and have been advised as follows:NI Water has undertaken additional targeted survey work and detailed analysis in the Marguerite Park area of the Newcastle catchment. This has included reviews of sewer network telemetry and rainfall data. These investigations, in tandem with NI Water’s supply chain consultants, have been important to ensure the hydraulic model accurately reflects current network performance such that a robust, long-term solution, which reduces the risk of out of sewer flooding, can be developed. NI Water anticipates that it will be able to share the findings of this work, together with any proposed solution(s) in Spring 2026. (ii) NI Water has implemented a temporary protocol in advance of the outcome of the modelling and the identification/delivery of any long-term solution. Any works identified as part of a solution will be subject to consideration in the next price control.  In addition, the gullies in this area are cleaned once a year and are on a priority list to be checked when the Department is notified of a weather warning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any cost incurred as a result of (i) investigatory work; and (ii) remedial works, taken by Forestry Service in Donard Wood to prevent flooding, since 19 October 2025.
answer_text: The scope of works undertaken to address the flooding in Donard Forest is detailed in AQW 36117/22-27.  Works are being undertaken by Forest Service directly and by contractors.  Staff costs incurred are associated with investigation of the impact of the flooding, and supervisory management of onsite works. To date, these are estimated to be £5,700 (direct staff costs), £5,200 for materials and contract machine and operator cost of £12,200.  As the work progresses these costs will increase accordingly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any (i) investigatory work; and (ii) remedial works, Forestry Service have conducted in the Donard Wood area of Newcastle since 19 October to address flooding.
answer_text: In the immediate aftermath of the flooding, Forest Service inspected the drainage infrastructure and culverts in Donard Forest to determine the extent of damage caused by the exceptional heavy rainfall and associated flooding.  Forest Service officials advise that a culvert traversing NIE land, outside the boundary of the forest, during the peak rainfall had not the capacity to deal with the exceptional waterflow leaving the forest, which led to the flooding adjacent to Sunningdale Drive.  Work is ongoing by Forest Service to repair damage caused to culverts and forest roads and will include increasing flow capacity of culverts within the forest.Forest Service is also seeking to engage with NIE Networks and Department for Infrastructure to discuss the immediate drainage outlet from the Forest. Forest Service also has agreed to meet with other relevant agencies to examine what more can be done collectively to reduce flooding impacts caused by future extreme rainfall events in the area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any assessment she has made of the information provided by NI Water to (a) her Department; (b) elected representatives; and (c) the Newcastle Regional Community Resilience Group, regarding the status of hydraulic modelling and the identification of a design solution; and (ii) whether NI Water will publish the modelling work undertaken to date, along with a clear timeline for completing a permanent flooding solution for the area.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information and have been advised as follows:The information provided by NI Water to my Department, elected representatives and the Newcastle Regional Community Resilience group in respect of hydraulic modelling advises that NI Water has undertaken additional targeted survey work and detailed analysis in the Marguerite Park area of the Newcastle catchment. This has included reviews of sewer network telemetry and rainfall data. These investigations, in tandem with NI Water’s supply chain consultants, have been important to ensure the hydraulic model accurately reflects current network performance such that a robust, long-term solution, which reduces the risk of out of sewer flooding, can be developed. NI Water anticipates that it will be able to share the findings of their work, together with any proposed solution(s) in Spring 2026.  Any permanent solution identified will be subject to funding availability in future years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any (i) assessment undertaken before responsibility for invasive non-native species management was transferred to the NI Environment Agency; and (ii) measures being taken to address any capacity or coordination issues affecting timely decision making.
answer_text: Invasive Non-Native Species (INNS) management has always been an important work area for DAERA (including the NIEA) with the responsibility held across multiple teams. My Department has a legal obligation to consider and take proactive steps to reduce the risk of all potential pathways, such as aquaculture movements, in relation to the introduction and/or spread of INNS. 		In respect of aquaculture, Council Regulation (EC) No 708/2007 concerning use of alien and locally absent species in aquaculture is listed in Annex 2 of the Windsor Framework and the implementation of Regulation 708/2007 and the Alien and Locally Absent Species in Aquaculture (Northern Ireland) Regulations 2012 is a Departmental priority. As a result, DAERA has secured the necessary resource to fully implement the obligation of the legislation.	DAERA officials consult with NIEA colleagues where aquaculture movements relate to freshwater species and attend regular meetings with the Aquaculture Representatives Group to secure open communication on policy and operational matters, to keep the industry informed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of applications for aquaculture or shellfish movements requiring biosecurity review that have exceeded his Department’s stated three week assessment period, in each of the last two years; and (ii) the reasons for these delays.
answer_text: All shellfish movements must be accompanied by an Invasive Non-Native Species (INNS) risk assessment and biosecurity plan. DAERA officials advised operators of their obligation via written correspondence dated 22 April 2024. In the past two years, at the time of this response, seven movement requests have exceeded three weeks to make a determination. This has been due to the following reasons: •	Three movements required further information from the operators on source sites, risk assessments and/or biosecurity plans. The new information received then required internal consultation prior to a decision being made. •	Two high risk movements required further internal consultation, and the movements were subsequently prohibited. •	One movement is currently under consideration and officials are conducting internal consultation prior to determination.  •	One high risk movement is currently under consideration and officials are consulting on the effectiveness of potential mitigation measures prior to making a determination.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any mechanisms in place to ensure that scientific assessments are not overridden, stalled or left unresolved by internal administrative processes within his Department or the NI Environment Agency.
answer_text: My Department has a legal obligation to consider and take proactive steps to reduce the risk of all potential pathways, such as aquaculture movements, in relation to the introduction and/or spread of Invasive Non-Native Species (INNS). DAERA officials have advised operators that shellfish movements must be accompanied by an INNS risk assessment and biosecurity plan. Officials will request further information from the operator if it is required to make an informed decision. A lead case officer is assigned to each movement request and follows the same process to ensure a robust, science-based decision can be made while preventing adverse impacts to the environment and aquaculture industry. On completion of the decision process the case officer ensures a determination is communicated to the operator.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that delays in stock movement decisions do not threaten jobs or the viability of aquaculture businesses in coastal communities.
answer_text: My Department has a legal obligation to consider and take proactive steps to reduce the risk of all potential pathways, such as aquaculture movements, in relation to the introduction and/or spread of Invasive Non-Native Species (INNS). Effective regulation of aquaculture movements to prevent the potential introduction and/or spread of INNS protects our marine environment and contributes to future viability of aquaculture business. INNS pose a significant economic risk to the aquaculture industry should they be present in movements.  DAERA officials have advised operators that shellfish movements must be accompanied by an INNS risk assessment and biosecurity plan. Furthermore, DAERA officials are currently developing a new streamlined process of notifications and permissions for aquaculture movements and associated guidance. The Aquaculture Representatives Group is being engaged throughout this process, which will secure robust, scientific evidence-based decisions to be made in an efficient manner.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) whether departmental scientists have validated any proposed movement sites as free from invasive non-native species while movements from those sites remain blocked due to administrative delay; and (ii) how scientific findings are acted upon in such cases.
answer_text: My Department has a legal obligation to consider and take proactive steps to reduce the risk of all potential pathways, such as aquaculture movements, in relation to the introduction and/or spread of Invasive Non-Native Species (INNS). DAERA officials have advised operators that shellfish movements must be accompanied by an INNS risk assessment and biosecurity plan. Officials will request further information from the operator if it is required to make an informed decision. Each movement request follows the same process to ensure a robust, science-based decision can be made while preventing adverse impacts to the environment and aquaculture industry. Movements are authorised where scientific evidence finds the risk of transferring INNS is low or can be effectively mitigated against. Movements are prohibited where scientific evidence finds the risk high of transferring INNS and there is not evidence of effective mitigation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timeframe for the delivery of (i) full; and (ii) recurrent, funding for the Learning Disability service at Thompson House, Lisburn.
answer_text: My Department has recently completed a public consultation on the DRAFT Learning Disability Service Model for Northern Ireland. Within the model a stepped care model of care is outlined within the key ambitions. It is envisaged that service provision will be prioritised for development within community intervention and prevention services, such as Thompson House, Lisburn.The Learning Disability Service Model will be finalised and costed next year. The delivery of priorities outlined within the model will be subject to funding made available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail where, in practices without a first contact physiotherapist as part of a multidisciplinary team, should GPs refer patients presenting with musculoskeletal conditions, following the cessation of the General Practice Elective Care Service musculoskeletal service.
answer_text: For patients presenting to their GP practice with MSK conditions, GPs can refer these patients to the secondary care orthopaedics/ pain management/ MSK/ rheumatology service.Some GP practices may offer joint injections for musculoskeletal conditions where clinically appropriate through a Directed Enhanced Service (DES).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update regarding discussions he has had with (i) HSC Pension Service; and (ii) Government Actuary's Department, regarding outstanding guidance and information about the application of legislation relating to implementation of the McCloud Remedy.
answer_text: The Department continues to work closely with both the HSC Pension Service and GAD on the implementation of the McCloud remedy. The legislative basis for implementing the McCloud remedy is provided for under the Public Sector Pensions and Judicial Officers Act 2022 and is supported by the HSC Pension Scheme Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015.   These regulations require the scheme to apply guidance issued by GAD when recalculating benefits for affected members.  Regular engagement takes place between with HSC Pension Service on the operational delivery of the remedy, including progress on issuing information to members.  The Department also meets with GAD monthly to monitor progress on the actuarial guidance required to support the recalculation of benefits. As of 17 November 2025, GAD has issued the majority of actuarial guidance required for the HSC Pension Service with some minor areas outstanding.  The HSC Pension Service continues to work with the guidance to ensure that the remedy is implemented accurately and in line with legislative requirements.The Department will continue to work closely with both HSC Pension Service and GAD to ensure timely delivery of the McCloud Remedy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on delivering an MRI scanner at the Downe Hospital.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any discussions he has had with the (i) HSC Pension Service; and (ii) Government Actuary's Department, regarding retired healthcare workers negatively impacted due to the delays in implementing the McCloud remedy.
answer_text: My Department continues to work with the HSC Pension Scheme and GAD on the implementation of the McCloud remedy.  Officials remain in regular contact to monitor and support progress.The HSC Pension Service have advised that the scale and complexity of developing and testing the bulk calculations required for affected retired members has been significant.  To ensure that the affected retired members receive accurate and robust information to make an informed choice, additional time has been required to complete this work.  Consequently, the delivery timeframes for these cases have been revised accordingly.My Department also meets with GAD regularly.  As of 17 November 2025, GAD has issued the majority of actuarial guidance required for the HSC Pension Service with some minor areas outstanding.  The HSC Pension Service continues to work with the GAD guidance to ensure that the remedy is implemented accurately and in line with legislative requirements and have advised that the first cohort of retired McCloud affected members will receive their first-choice packs by the end of March 2026.  The revised timeline has been communicated to affected members through pension newsletters via email.  They have also published the information to their website and social media platforms. My Department will continue to work closely with both HSC Pension Service and GAD to ensure timely delivery of remedy benefits for all affected retired members.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to provide an update on plans for the Birch Hill Centre for Mental Health; and (ii) to detail when funding will be made available for the project.
answer_text: The Outline Business Case for the Birch Hill Mental Health Centre facility was approved in 2020 to facilitate completion of RIBA Stage 3 Design.The RIBA Stage 3 design is complete, and Planning Approval has been granted.Funding to complete the remaining design and deliver the project is subject to finalisation of the Department’s Strategic Capital Development plan, the outcome of which is anticipated early 2026.My ability to commit funding for this project will depend on the Department’s capital allocation from the budget process and the identification of sufficient recurrent funding to meet any additional resource costs associated with the facility.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total cost to date of the Muckamore Abbey Hospital Inquiry, including (i) legal costs; (ii) staffing costs; and (iii) associated departmental expenditure.
answer_text: The Muckamore Abbey Hospital Inquiry (MAHI) has published its costs on their website, which are £14.8m as at 22 September 2025 including both legal and staffing costs. The associated departmental expenditure available to date is £413k.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of agency staff employed at Muckamore Abbey Hospital (i) in the three years prior to the establishment of the Inquiry; and (ii) in each subsequent year, broken down by band.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust is responsible for staffing at Muckamore Abbey Hospital (MAH), and the Trust has provided the requested information which is set out in the tables below:(i) number of agency staff employed at MAH in the three years prior to the establishment of the Inquiry;Year Agency Workers engaged as Nursing AssistantsAgency Workers engaged as Registered Nurses2017/2018 932018/201927532019/202051102(ii) number of agency staff employed at MAH in each subsequent year.Year Agency Workers engaged as Nursing AssistantsAgency Workers engaged as Registered Nurses2020/2021601132021/2022631172022/2023701322023/2024361072024/202541992025/2026* 2166*As of 30 November 2025Agency workers are not directly employed by the Trust, and are engaged through external agencies to provide temporary staffing support where required.The Trust has advised that these numbers represent the total headcount of individuals who have provided cover at any point during the financial years listed. This includes workers who engaged on an ad hoc basis for a single shift as well as those who engaged regularly throughout the year. The figures therefore do not reflect continuous employment or full-time equivalents, but rather the cumulative number of individuals engaged through agencies during the period in question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount spent on (i) staff; and (ii) maintenance at Kilkeel Medical Practice, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In the 2023/24 financial year, the partners of Kilkeel Medical Practice handed their GMS Contract back to the SPPG.  The Southern Health and Social Care Trust managed the Contract from 1st November 2023.The SHSCT commenced managing the Kilkeel Medical practice on the 1st November 2023, on a rolling contract basis with SPPG.During the period from 1 April 2020 to 1 November 2023 the GMS contract(s) for patients registered with Kilkeel Medical Practice was managed by GPs. Funding was allocated to the GPs, via their Global Sum, to cover all of the running costs of the Practice, including staff costs. As independent contractors, the GP Practices do not provide my Department with details of the staff costs incurred in running the Practice.Expenses directly relating to the management of Kilkeel Medical Practice incurred by SHSCT are:Financial YearPayroll*Goods and Services£k£k24/255272023/24**15312*The SHSCT payroll costs do not include any medical expenditure as this is managed directly by SPPG.**The Financial year 23/24 is for 5 months from 1st November 2023 until the 31st March 2024.Kilkeel Medical Practice is within a Trust owned building and the direct maintenance costs of operating this building are as follows:YearOperating Costs20212022202320242025Maintenance£15,945£35,760£31,332£24,488£17,714These Service charges are for operating the building for the 12 months of the financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) her Department’s (a) schedule; and (b) target frequency, for gully clearance in each section office area; and (ii) whether this target has been met over the last year.
answer_text: My Department places high importance on the need to adequately maintain the road network through day-to-day essential maintenance activities such as gully emptying, to maintain the structural integrity of the road network. Allocations of funding to maintain our roads have been less than required for many years and as a result, my Department has only been able to deliver a limited service across a range of functions including gully emptying. However, in recognition of the importance of this area of work, I have allocated further funding to deliver additional works in the form of cleaning and maintenance of drainage lines and manholes.My Department’s current Limited-Service Policy aims to inspect and clean, where necessary, all gullies once annually and work is scheduled on this basis.  During the 2024/25 financial year, 86% of this target was achieved.  Please note that some gullies were not accessible at time of cleaning due to parked vehicles, etc. Further ad-hoc cleaning may also be undertaken to address any specific localised issues as required, within available budgets, and in advance of and in response to severe weather events. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made so my Department can deliver important aspects of road maintenance, such as gully cleaning, ensuring public safety and an efficient transport network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when Kilkeel Medical Practice out of hours will reopen.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) is maintaining a safe and effective Urgent Care Out Of Hours service, to all patients residing in the Southern Trust remains our priority. The current Urgent Care Out of Hours service provides•	Telephone triage•	Base appointments•	Home visits for those patients with an urgent need.The temporary closure of the Kilkeel Out of Hours (OOHs) base was undertaken to ensure the continued delivery of a safe and sustainable service for all SHSCT patients.By consolidating services across the two main bases at Craigavon Area Hospital (CAH) and Daisy Hill Hospital (DHH), the Trust have been able to maintain Out of Hours provision within the available funding envelope.However, I recognise the Trust continue to experience challenges in staffing, particularly during Saturday and Sunday afternoons.  That said, their primary objective remains to deliver a responsive service that meets the needs of the greatest number of patients, while maintaining the highest standards of safety for both patients and staff.I would emphasise that no final decision has been made regarding the permanent status of the Kilkeel OOHs base. The current arrangements remain under review and the Trust will continue to monitor service demand, staffing levels, and overall system resilience.
