question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to reduce the potential impact of chronic respiratory conditions on the health service during winter 2025-2026.
answer_text: The Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025/26 identifies measures that my Department, Trusts and other key partners are taking to alleviate winter pressures in the Health and Social Care (HSC) system, taking a whole systems approach and ensuring the best use of resources.The plan focuses on five key themes: prevention, the role of pharmacy and General Practice, hospital care, local planning and preparations and whole system flow improvement emerging from our “Big Discussion” workshops.This year’s plan also details the extensive vaccination programme, which aims to minimise the impact from winter infections such as influenza, Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) and COVID-19.The Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan includes a series of measures across primary, secondary and social care. These include:Tackling ambulance handover delays through a new approach to collaborative working between Trusts and the Ambulance Service, to reduce maximum handover delays from four hours to two hours from September to December, with further reductions to achieve a 15 minute standard.Vaccination programmes against diseases such as influenza, COVID-19, RSV, and shingles.  These are crucial to help protect our population and reduce pressure across the system. The Department has taken steps to allow for the COVID-19 booster and influenza vaccination to be largely administered together in one visit, making it as simple as possible.Community Pharmacy initiatives, such as the ‘Living Well Campaign’ and ‘Stay Well This Winter’, which encourage and support people to take actions to stay well over winter. The ‘Pharmacy First’ service will be provided across Community Pharmacies, including the ‘Sore Throat Service’, which offers advice, clinical assessment and treatment for sore throats, without having to wait for a GP appointment.Additional assistance for General Practitioner (GP) practices, providing 10,000 medical care plans for patients in nursing and residential care homes and over 98,000 additional sessions to help manage winter pressures. This will be delivered by providing support to increase capacity to meet demand across General Medical Services (GMS) and Out of Hours; and additional support to deliver proactive support to those in nursing and residential homes.Specific paediatric measures, which aim to clarify demand and capacity in a whole system approach via communication on a daily basis. The measures will also see patients repatriated from Belfast to other areas, and secure additional twilight hours into NISTAR (Northern Ireland Specialist Transport and Retrieval) to ensure fair and equitable access for neonatal paediatric patients.Improving mental health and learning disability bed pressures, reducing demand on Emergency Departments and ensuring the right care in the right place.Supporting social care delivery in the community and improve system flow from hospital.Improving the whole system approach to managing and meeting the care needs of the elderly and t

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to mark World COPD Awareness Day on 19 November 2025.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on tranche one of the respiratory working groups; to detail (ii) when work will start on tranche two; (iii) what tranche two will priorities include; (iv) when the regional respiratory forum last met; and (v) when the regional respiratory forum will next meet.
answer_text: Interstitial Lung Disease (ILD)Following a series of meetings and workshops with members, a comprehensive pathway has now been drafted with referral to treatment time (RTT) agreed along with 5 prioritised areas for opportunity for change within the NI-ILD network:a)	Primary Care Educationb)	Standards of Carec)	Proformasd)	Data Driven Changee)	Community and Co-ordinationPrimary Care DiagnosticsThe sub group undertook scoping and explored options for a new service model for carrying out diagnostics in Primary Care including FeNO and Spirometry testing.  A proposal for the preferred model has been submitted to SPPG.Adult Asthma Sub group members agreed to use the basis of the AAC Consensus Pathway, Management of Uncontrolled Asthma in Adults produced by the AHSN network, and adjust it for a gold standard service for NI.  This pathway examines various stages:a)	Indicators of Uncontrolled Asthmab)	Primary Carec)	Secondary Cared)	Severe Asthma CentreThe work is slightly behind schedule due to staff capacity and it is expected to be finalised in early 2026.PaediatricsWork was paused in this area to explore if an existing forum, Northern Ireland Paediatric Respiratory and Allergy Network (NIPRAN) would be better placed to lead on this work to avoid duplication.Work on tranche two will begin in early 2026.Tranche two priorities include:COPDObstructive Sleep ApnoeaNon-Invasive / Long-Term ventilation            Home Oxygen ServiceBronchiectasisPulmonary RehabilitationTransition (Paediatric to Adult Service)The first meeting of the Respiratory Forum took place on 19th November 2025The next meeting of the Respiratory Forum will be February 2025 (date to be confirmed)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of Emergency Departments (ED), broken down by type; (ii) the population of each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT); (iii) the attendance at each ED; and (iv) the average waiting time at each ED, in each of the last ten years, broken down by HSCT.
answer_text: Information on the number of Emergency Departments (EDs), broken down by type and the number of attendances at EDs is published on an annual basis in the ‘Hospital statistics: emergency care activity’ report, the most recent of which covers the period April 2024 to March 2025, and is available on my Department’s website: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/emergency-care-and-ambulance-statisticsInformation on the average waiting time for each ED is not available. However, information on the median time spent in each ED from arrival to discharge or admission is available for each ED in the report above.The Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) provides population estimates, including a break down by HSC Trust, and can be found at the following link: https://www.nisra.gov.uk/publications/2023-mid-year-population-estimates-small-geographical-areas-within-northern-irelandRecognising the challenges faced by EDs, my Department has worked closely with Trusts and community services to implement a range of actions aimed at improving patient flow and reducing delays. To help alleviate pressures in EDs, my Department has implemented a range of targeted initiatives. These include the expansion of urgent care centres, Phone First services and minor injury units, offering patients timely alternatives to ED attendance. Patients who are seen in urgent care centres and minor injuries units, which fall under the category of Type 3 EDs will be seen, treated and discharged in a much timelier manner.Ahead of the coming winter, my Department has worked with the Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) and the Trusts to ensure that an additional 20 emergency medicine consultants have been appointed to work in EDs across Northern Ireland in the last year. There are currently plans to employ an additional nine consultants with recruitment underway in the Northern, Western and Southern Trusts. This will provide further resilience and senior decision-making capacity to our EDs this winter.Ultimately, all of these measures are aimed at improving flow throughout the hospital, reducing congestion in our EDs and improving waiting times.These efforts are part of a broader system-wide approach to ensure patients receive timely, safe, and effective care, both within EDs and across the wider health and social care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what services each Health and Social Care Trust offer (i) individuals; and; (ii) the families of those, with acquired brain injuries
answer_text: Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts across Northern Ireland provide a range of services for acquired brain injury (ABI).  Support is offered to individuals, families, and carers through interdisciplinary assessment and rehabilitation delivered in clinics, homes, or community facilities in each Trust and there are also regional units for adults and paediatrics.The following regional services are provided;The Regional Acquired Brain Injury Unit (RABIU) located in the Belfast HSC Trust provides comprehensive inpatient and outpatient neurorehabilitation services for the entire region. The Belfast Trust also runs a specialist day rehabilitation facility called The Mourne Project.The Neuro-behavioural Rehabilitation Unit is located in Knockbracken Healthcare for individuals with challenging behaviours resulting from brain injury that cannot be managed in other community settings.Thompson House Hospital (THH) is a regional non-acute hospital managed by South Eastern Health HSC Trust. The hospital has 24 beds providing post-acute assessment/rehabilitation to adult patients, with ABI or Complex Neurological Disability who require a multidisciplinary programme of assessment, management and/or rehabilitation whilst also supporting their families. The Paediatric Regional Acquired Brain Injury Service (PRABIS) is a multi-professional service that supports the needs of children and young people with ABI.Each HSC Trust has community-based teams offering tailored, interdisciplinary assessment and therapy to help individuals reintegrate into the community. Teams typically comprise clinical psychologists/neuropsychologists, physiotherapists, occupational therapists, speech and language therapists, and social workers.Trusts offers support services for the families and carers of individuals with ABI, recognizing that the injury impacts the entire family unit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of new cases of acquired brain injuries, in each of the last six years, broken down Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: It has not been possible to provide the specific data on the number of new cases of acquired brain injuries (ABI) in each of the last six years broken down by Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust. My Department and HSC Trusts do not hold this level of information. The lack of detailed, localized data on new ABI cases by individual HSC Trust is due to a combination of factors related to data collection methodologies, standardisation issues, and the complex nature of the condition itself.It is estimated that approximately 2,000 people in Northern Ireland sustain a brain injury each year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people classified as having an acquired brain injury, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: It has not been possible to provide the specific data on the number of people diagnosed with an Acute Brian Injury (ABI) broken down by Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust.  My Department and HSC Trusts do not hold this level of information. The lack of detailed, localized data on people classified as having an ABI by individual HSC Trust is due to a combination of factors related to data collection methodologies, standardisation issues, and the complex nature of the condition itself.It is estimated that there are approximately 2,000 people in Northern Ireland who sustain a brain injury each year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of people, currently in prison, who have a learning disability, broken down by (i) number; and (ii) the percentage of the overall prison population.
answer_text: From July 2024 the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (the Trust) agreed to provide confirmation to the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) of people in custody who have an intellectual disability/learning disability (ID/LD) upon committal. On the 1 November 2025 the prison population was recorded as 2,160 people:46 people were recorded as having an ID/LD,This equates to 2% of the prison population on that date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement her Department has had with the Department of Health, over the last 10 years, to ensure that people with a learning disability receive care and rehabilitation, rather than custodial punishment.
answer_text: A significant proportion of those in contact with the justice system have a range of health and social care needs and my Department has worked with other Departments, including the Department of Health, in order to address these.  The introduction of a number of initiatives, including enhanced combination orders and multi-agency support hubs, has been done with the aim of keeping vulnerable individuals out of custodial environments, and indeed out of the justice system altogether, wherever possible.  A range of sentencing options are also available to the judiciary and may include the use of an out of court disposal or probation order.  Ultimately, decisions are made on an individual basis, taking account of any relevant supporting information provided. Where an adult with a learning disability receives a custodial sentence, health and social care provision during the custodial period sits with the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.  Support provided by the Trust includes access to learning disability nurses and speech and language therapists.   Together with environmental changes, access to garden spaces and therapeutic activity at each prison site, Prison Service Prisoner Safety and Support Teams provide support to people who have additional needs, including those with a learning disability. The Youth Justice Agency (YJA) uses a Child First approach with a focus on care and rehabilitation.  A needs assessment is undertaken for every child who receives a statutory referral to YJA.  This aims to assess the child’s wider needs, including any health conditions that may be contributing to their offending behaviour.  YJA then works with the family and other agencies to access appropriate services.  There are already good health services within Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre which are well-placed to identify and meet need.  Since 2024, there is also a YJA Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS) in place within YJA’s community-based teams.  This is co-funded by YJA and Health and Social Care and helps identify and meet need among children who have offended.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the specific supports in place to ensure that prisoners, with a learning disability, are treated appropriately within the prison estate; and (ii) whether specialist training is provided to staff.
answer_text: Responsibility for the health and social care of prisoners in Northern Ireland sits with the Department of Health who have commissioned South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (the Trust) to provide primary, mental health and addictions services within prisons. Many people arrive in prison with complex needs.  Support is available for people who have additional needs, including those who have been confirmed by the Trust as having an Intellectual Disability/Learning Disability (ID/LD). This includes access to learning disability nurses,  speech and language therapists and a wide range of other professionals via the multi-disciplinary Healthcare in Prisons team. Together with environmental changes, access to garden spaces and therapeutic activity at each prison site, Prison Service Prisoner Safety and Support Teams provide support to people who have additional needs, including those with ID/LD. Prison Service staff are provided with a range of training interventions to equip them to do their job.  This includes training in neurodiverse conditions and mental health.  In addition, the Trust support Prison Service staff by providing advice on how best to communicate with individuals who have specific needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will place a map in the Assembly library of the extent of (i) his Department's; and (ii) arm's-length bodies', land ownership in (a) Donard Wood, Newcastle; and (b) the surrounding area.
answer_text: My Department leases Donard Forest from the estate of G F Annesley and has no land ownership in immediate area surrounding Donard Forest. The Department of Finance’s Land Registry holds the public record of registered property ownership in Northern Ireland and for unregistered lands the Registry of Deeds (RoD) is the public record.On the basis that my Department does not own land at Donard Forest and surrounding area, I have no plans to place a land ownership map for Donard Forest in the Assembly library.

answer_text: NIEA recognises the importance of meeting FOI/EIR targets as a key measure of transparency and accountability. NIEA remains committed to fulfilling its statutory responsibilities within required timeframes. Several complex cases have required legal advice, and these will be addressed and responded to as promptly and efficiently as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 33589/22-27, to detail the engagement she has had with (i) the Executive; and ii) the Minister of Finance Minister, to secure additional funding for maintenance of drainage systems.
answer_text: I continue to engage with my Executive colleagues, including the Minister of Finance in relation to the funding requirements for our infrastructure, following years of underfunding and austerity. In June Monitoring, I secured an additional £1.8million of funding towards the annual cost of road drainage charges needed to remove road surface water into the drainage network.   In addition, I submitted a bid for additional funding for Structural Maintenance (including structural drainage). While this bid was not successful, I continue to make the case for additional funding. As part of December Monitoring, I will be considering further funding requirements.  I have also set out my Department’s funding needs as part of future budget exercises and will continue to engage with my Executive colleagues on the investment required in our infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any action their Department has taken to further develop planning for community resilience in preparation for future severe weather events.
answer_text: As set out within our Civil Contingencies Framework, the Department for Infrastructure is the Lead Government Department (LGD) for many types of severe weather events. Both of the departments for Infrastructure and Communities, which have the significant roles is supporting communities impacted by severe weather, are embedded at every level of the resilience structures established by The Executive Office’s Civil Contingencies Framework. These structures cover all three phases of: prepare, respond and recover. These multi-agency structures have a requirement to consider and address community resilience and vulnerable groups on a continuous basis during planning, response and recovery activities. The Executive Office supports the departments individually and collectively through these established civil contingencies structures and supports the lessons identification process following severe weather events. Through this lesson identification process TEO ensures that multi-agency learning occurs and supports the impacted departments refine and enhance their planning for, and response to, events which impact on community resilience.  The Department of Infrastructure provides expert guidance and information to other organisations, councils and departments who are involved in the emergency response to severe weather. The Department has a key leadership role in the multi-agency Regional Community Resilience Group (RCRG).  In operation since 2013, the RCRG is working with 50 communities to help improve their preparedness and community resilience. Key to the success of the RCRG is the strong working relationship with local community groups, many of whom often carry out their role on a voluntary basis. The Department for Communities hold regular engagements with its stakeholder bodies during the year. These engagements address ongoing governance and emergency needs, including preparedness for severe weather, particularly in winter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount the NI Ambulance Service has spent on private ambulance operators, in each of the last five years, broken down Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the accountability mechanisms in place for private ambulance staff who are employed by the NI Ambulance Service.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) utilises Independent Ambulance Services (IAS) primarily to supplement its capacity to deliver routine Patient Care Services (PCS).NIAS uses the support from IAS providers in the same way it utilises its own PCS crews i.e., for low-acuity, routine, non-emergency ambulance transport. NIAS does not permit the use of blue lights and does not utilise IAS crews for emergency work, unless it is to support a NIAS crew at a cardiac arrest, or to respond to an emergency call which has been assessed by NIAS’s own clinicians as suitable for a non-emergency crew.Currently, governance and assurance are provided via NIAS, who undertake twice-yearly unannounced inspections of provider ambulances. Quarterly governance meetings take place with all providers, which includes a plenary learning and update session, followed by a one-to-one meeting with each provider.Additionally, NIAS have employed a Quality Assurance Manager whose primary role is to provide robust governance and assurance of IAS providers.

answer_text: Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of support offered to businesses over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal. The portal contains the following information which will facilitate access to the data requested:		Portal | Invest NI Total Tables 1 April 2020 and 31 March 2025	Provides a complete picture of Invest NI support through summary tables that aggregate both published and non-published data (indirect support, like advice and guidance, and offers that cannot be released due to data protection issues). This information is broken down into several different variables, including council and constituency areas.				Portal | Invest NI Financial Offers of Assistance (Detail)	Provides detail on Individual offers of direct financial assistance issued by Invest NI to businesses between 1 April 2020 and 31 March 2025. Does not include indirect support, like advice and guidance, or offers that cannot be released due to data protection issues.		Portal | Invest NI Open Data Guidance	Provides guidance on the data contained in 1 and 2 above.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement she has had with (i) the Utility Regulator; and (ii) NIE Networks, regarding the resilience of the electricity network in South Down, over the last two years.
answer_text: Energy security is a key priority for my Department. We work closely with the Utility Regulator and the wider energy industry, including NIE Networks to review and monitor security of supply, and ensure the resilience of the network across the North. On an annual basis, my Department engages with NIE Networks on its winter preparedness plans, to help ensure the resilience of the network during the winter months. Mitigations in place by NIE Networks to increase resilience include increased contractor availability, an emergency assistance agreement with ESB Networks in the South, membership of the NEWSAC consortium enabling access to resources from network operators in Britain, and mutual aid agreement with other local utility providers. Following the unprecedented impacts of Storm Ewoyn in January 2025, my Department had extensive liaison with NIE Networks on lessons learned, and wider engagement with the Utility Regulator on a review of compensation standards and severe weather exemptions, leading to a consultation and working group involving both organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on whether the drug cabotegravir will be made available via the NHS.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.On 17 October 2025, NICE published its final draft guidance recommending the use of cabotegravir (Apretude®) as an option for pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) alongside safer sex practices to reduce the risk of sexually acquired HIV-1 infection in adults and young people at high risk of getting HIV if they cannot have oral PrEP.  Final guidance is expected on 5 November 2025.When the final guidance for this treatment is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdfMoreover, cabotegravir (Vocabria®) with rilpivirine for the treatment of HIV-1 infection in adults, was endorsed by my Department in February 2022 and is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland in line with the current NICE guidelines (TA757).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the impact the 162 staff deficit of DFI Rivers, on her Department's ability to (i) prepare for; and (ii) respond to, the weekend's flooding in Newcastle.
answer_text: The emergency flooding response in Newcastle was led by DfI Roads due to the surface water nature of the flooding. DfI Rivers provided essential support and helped protect many properties in areas also impacted by heavy rainfall. The staffing levels within DfI Rivers did not affect the Department’s ability to prepare for or respond to this specific event.Prior to the adverse weather arriving, Rivers operational staff across the North undertook proactive culvert screen maintenance and continued this work throughout the weekend, while actively monitoring river levels in real time through our network of hydrometric stations. These combined efforts helped keep designated watercourses and drainage infrastructure flowing freely and prevented flooding in many areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether each Health and Social Care Trust is required to publish or submit its own winter preparedness plan for 2025–26.
answer_text: All Trusts have developed an Unscheduled Care Improvement Plan across all aspects of Trusts services, with a community and an acute focus. While these plans have been developed to coincide with the publication of the Department’s Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan, they go wider than that and outline efforts to manage year-round pressures. Each Trust published a summary of their Improvement Plans on their own websites on Thursday 16 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any training Personal Independence Payment assessors receive in relation to customers with autism and ADHD.
answer_text: Capita assessors undergo a robust training programme to undertake Personal Independence Payment (PIP) functional assessments. This includes practice cases, e-learning modules, guidance materials, and Condition Insight Reports, which are specific to autism and ADHD.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether any additional inpatient or step-down capacity has been created for paediatric or mental health services under the Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025/26; and (ii) to detail how this compares with the 12 bed capacity increases outlined in the Winter Preparedness Plan 2024-25.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any actions are being taken through the Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025-26 (a) to improve ambulance handover times; and (b) to increase Northern Ireland Ambulance Service capacity; and (ii) how these compare with measures included in the Winter Preparedness Plan 2024-25.
answer_text: My Department recognises the significant pressures faced by emergency departments (EDs) and the impact these have on ambulance handover times and overall system flow. Through the Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025–26, a comprehensive set of actions has been developed to address these challenges.With regard to improving ambulance handover times, in August 2025, a regional workshop involving Trusts and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) was convened, drawing on best practice from the London Ambulance Service and King George Hospital. Based on this, new ambulance handover guidance is being finalised. Phase 1 targets, agreed with Trusts, aim to eliminate ambulance handover delays exceeding two hours by 1 December 2025. Trusts are also working to prevent deterioration in shorter delay thresholds (15, 30, and 60 minutes), with a longer-term objective of achieving the 15-minute standard.With regard to NIAS capacity and system flow, to support improved ambulance offload and response times, my Department has repurposed £10 million to fund targeted initiatives across Trusts, to be implemented by the end of October 2025. These measures include:Expansion of Hospital at Home services in Belfast, South Eastern, and Northern Trusts, increasing community-based capacity;Additional short stay inpatient beds (for example, eight in Altnagelvin) and intermediate care beds (for example, 12 in the Western Trust) to facilitate timely discharge;Frailty assessment and multidisciplinary team reviews at the front door of acute hospitals;Expansion of Same Day Emergency Care (SDEC) and ambulatory pathways across acute medicine, respiratory, cardiology, and surgical specialties; and Enhanced capacity for Early Review Teams and Discharge Coordination.These measures are embedded within each Trust’s Unscheduled Care Improvement Plan, developed in collaboration with the Regional Co-ordination Centre (RCC) and informed by the ‘Big Discussion’ workshops. Trusts are on course to have these initiatives in place ahead of winter.With regard to how these compare with measures included in the Winter Preparedness Plan 2024-25, the 2025–26 plan builds on the foundations of the previous year, which focused on expanding urgent care alternatives such as Phone First, minor injury units, and urgent care centres. The current plan introduces:Defined performance targets for ambulance handovers;Expanded consultant capacity (18 appointed, 10 more by December);System-wide collaboration through winter planning workshops; andSignificant investment in fracture care services (£12 million), with additional operating lists and beds already in place.Trust-level delivery highlights include the following:Belfast Trust: The full implementation of the Regional Emergency and Acute Team (REACTT) model by 30 October; expansion of Hospital at Home; and new roles in physiotherapy, social work, and discharge coordination.Northern Trust: The establishment of the Hospital at Home service; enhanced seni

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why the Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025-26 provides for capacity to open 258 additional hospital beds, compared with 323 in the Winter Preparedness Plan 2024-25; and (ii) any assessment that has been made of the impact of this reduction on hospital resilience during periods of peak demand.
answer_text: Escalation beds are temporary beds used in response to significant pressure in a hospital as part of Trusts’ internal escalation plans. As they are often opened in undesignated spaces on existing wards, they present challenges for staff and are not an ideal environment for patients.The number of escalation beds which can be opened has been reduced across Trusts for a number of reasons. A previous review of bed locations has also required Trusts to reduce the number of beds they can open where it was deemed these could potentially interfere with fire safety.In parallel, my Department has taken a strategic approach to enhancing system-wide capacity beyond traditional inpatient beds. To assist Trusts in their plans to manage unscheduled care pressures, £10 million has been re-purposed to provide additional capacity going into winter and beyond. Trusts are on course to have these measures in place ahead of winter.The measures across Trusts include:increased capacity of Hospital at Home in the South Eastern and Belfast Trusts;a new Hospital at Home service in the Northern Trust, providing additional beds in the community;additional short stay inpatient beds;additional step down/intermediate care beds; expansion of ambulatory and Same Day Emergency Care (SDEC) for acute medicine, frailty, respiratory and cardiology services across Trusts; multidisciplinary team assessment at the front door; and,enhanced capacity for early review teams to recycle existing capacity in the community.This additional capacity in the community and in our hospital services supplements existing bed capacity and aims to avoid admission and provide alternative pathways for patients, which are often more appropriate than additional inpatient escalation beds.Collectively, these measures are designed to ensure timely access to inpatient beds for patients requiring admission from emergency departments, while improving overall hospital resilience during periods of peak demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any additional funding allocated in 2025–26 to support winter pressures across (a) general practice; (b) out-of-hours services; and (c) social care; and (ii) why the Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025-26 does not include the same specific financial allocations that were set out in the Winter Preparedness Plan 2024-25.
answer_text: Due to the constrained financial position no additional funding was available to specifically support winter pressures in primary care, out of hours services or social care in 2025/26. Instead the focus was on actions that could be taken within the existing financial envelope (including redeployment of existing resources where possible) to improve services to enable the system to better cope with winter pressures. As a result it is not possible to disaggregate all spending on winter pressures from other expenditure and this is why the plan has not been specific in this regard.I can however advise that this winter, the Department will: Provide additional support from within existing budgets to both General Medical Services (GMS) via a winter pressures enhanced service available across all GP practices (2.5m) and GP out-of-hours services (900k) to support increased capacity to meet the anticipated growth in demand over the winter; and Continue the support from within existing budgets to assist GP practices across Northern Ireland to deliver proactive support and care to those in nursing and residential care homes, through completing medical care plans, including assessing whether referral to an ED is needed, and a GP led fortnightly proactive review (4.7m is invested in this enhanced service across the 25/26 year)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of; and (ii) percentage of, Personal Independence Payment claims from people with (a) autism; (b) ADHD; and (c) autism and ADHD, that were (iii) awarded at initial assessment; (iv) disallowed; and (v) subsequently overturned at mandatory reconsideration or appeal, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: For the purposes of Personal Independence Payment (PIP), autism can be recorded as a specific disorder or recorded as the broader category ‘Autistic Spectrum Disorders’ (ASD), which also includes Asperger’s syndrome and Rett’s disorder. The tables below provide figures for autism as a specific disorder, and the broader category ASD. As requested, also shown are figures for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD).  As the PIP system records one primary disabling condition only, my Department is unable to provide figures for PIP recipients with both autism and ADHD as requested at (c).(i) The number of, and (ii) percentage of PIP claims from people with autism or ASD; and ADHD that were (iii) awarded or (iv) disallowed at initial assessment are included in the table below:Outcome20202021202220232024Autism (a)Claims Awarded 2707608001100113065.0%79.2%80.9%80.3%70.6%Claims Disallowed14019019027047033.1%19.9%19.1%19.7%29.4%Autistic Spectrum Disorder Claims Awarded 3509209501210122063.2%73.7%79.8%77.5%68.8%Claims Disallowed21034024036055036.8%26.3%20.2%22.5%31.2%ADHD (b)Claims Awarded13035029042042043.6%64.6%65.6%57.8%51.0% Claims Disallowed16019015030040056.3%34.1%34.3%41.1%49.0%Notes1.Figures under 10 have been suppressed for data protection purposes.  Volumes are rounded to the nearest 10 and percentages are rounded to the nearest 0.1%. Therefore, percentage totals may not sum to 100% either due to rounding or the suppression of figures.(v) The number and percentage of PIP claims from people with autism or ASD; or ADHD where the initial decision was changed at Mandatory Reconsideration (MR) are included in the table below:Outcome20202021202220232024Autism MRs ClearedAward Changed204020307026.9%43.8%31.2%40.2%38.1%ASD MRs ClearedAward Changed306040509028.3%39.2%31.9%44.0%34.4%ADHD MRs Cleared Award Changed103020203021.1%34.6%33.3%25.6%25.2%Notes1.PIP Mandatory Reconsideration clearance data does not include specific award outcomes.  Therefore, claims with a change in award could represent an increase or a decrease from the original claim decision.2. Figures under 10 have been suppressed for data protection purposes.  Volumes are rounded to the nearest 10 and percentages are rounded to the nearest 0.1%. Therefore, percentage totals may not sum to 100% either due to rounding or the suppression of figures.	Finally, my Department does not hold appeals information in the format requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how she plans to address staff shortages in her Department.
answer_text: My Department continues to face significant resource challenges – in terms of available budget and the recruitment and retention of staff.  The financial pressures faced by all departments is well rehearsed.  With regards recruitment and retention, we face particular challenges in relation to specialist roles, which have impacted our ability to deliver essential services and infrastructure projects. I want to assure Members that my officials are taking proactive and decisive action to address these issues in liaison with colleagues in the Department of Finance, who provide HR services for all departments.To strengthen our workforce planning arrangements internally, we have established a People Sub-Committee which reports to the Departmental Board enhancing governance and oversight. This ensures that workforce capacity risks are identified and managed. To build long-term resilience, we are investing in innovative recruitment models, and my Department is working with our HR delivery partners in the Department of Finance to implement a strategic, prioritised Workforce Plan. At present, 22 recruitment campaigns are underway and in the last 12 months our efforts have led to approximately 200 new appointments, with a further 300 anticipated soon.However, I accept that progress has not happened at the pace we would like, and my officials continue to engage with colleagues in the Department of Finance to identify potential improvements in the speed and efficiency of recruitment.Our planning also considers broader workforce trends, including an ageing population, changes to the way people work and the implications of a multi-generational workforce. These factors are central to our long-term workforce modelling and policy development, especially given that a significant proportion of our workforce are approaching retirement age, presenting a real risk to our institutional knowledge and delivery capacity.All these efforts are underpinned by the DfI People Strategy (2024–2026), which aligns with the broader NICS People Strategy. Our focus is on building capacity, supporting capability, and fostering a culture of inclusive leadership and staff wellbeing. This strategic framework ensures that we are not only addressing immediate staffing needs but also laying the foundation for a more resilient and agile organisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the engagement she has had with Northern Ireland Electricity Networks to (a) coordinate response plans; and (b) share information, during major weather events; and (ii) whether her Department has raised concerns regarding the communication with the public during prolonged outages.
answer_text: Thank you for your question regarding engagement with NIE Networks during major weather events, and concerns about public communication during prolonged outages.(i) Engagement with NIE Networks(a) Coordination of Response Plans: During major weather events such as Storm Éowyn, the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) activates its Major Emergency Response Plan (MERP) to manage impacts to the Department, as well as Translink and NI Water. However, it is important to note that the Department for the Economy (DfE) holds the lead government department (LGD) responsibility for electricity supply disruptions under the Civil Contingencies Risk Register. My Department participates in multi-agency coordination structures, in line with the Civil Contingencies Framework, during emergencies, at which NIE Networks is represented along with other key stakeholders. These forums allow for information sharing and situational awareness. NIE’s operational decisions and response planning are not under the remit of my Department.(b) Information Sharing: DfI shares relevant impact data, such as road obstructions, transport disruptions and water supply issues, with other agencies as part of the co-ordinated multi-agency response.(ii) Concerns Regarding Public Communication During Prolonged Outages: Communications and public messaging during emergencies are agreed and co-ordinated as part of the multi-agency coordination structures, as referenced above.I note that a multi-agency review led by the Executive Office took place into the response to Storm Éowyn and my Department will work with others to implement any recommendations, including improvements to communication with the public, that may arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any measures in place to ensure that the Personal Independent Payment assessment process is accessible to people with communication difficulties or sensory sensitivities associated with autism.
answer_text: The Personal Independence Payment (PIP) assessment is designed to treat all applicants respectfully and fairly. Each assessment is carried out by registered health professionals who have specialist training in assessing the impact of disabilities and health conditions on an individual’s functional ability. Measures in place within the assessment journey to support applicants including those with communication and sensory challenges include: An initial review by trained Capita clinicians designed to select the most appropriate assessment channel and to identify individuals who may require additional support;The opportunity to flag any additional requirements in advance of an assessment, with Capita endeavouring to meet any requests for reasonable adjustments; and	People attending assessments, particularly those with communication difficulties who may struggle to provide an accurate account of their health circumstances, are encouraged to be accompanied.  	Also, while not specific to the assessment process, any applicant requiring additional time to return information or help to complete forms can access this assistance from the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the process by which his Department monitors the quality and accuracy of Personal Independence Payment assessments involving claimants with autism.
answer_text: All Personal Independence Payment assessments are completed in line with the standards outlined in the Department’s Personal Independence Assessment Guide.  In addition, there are robust processes in place to monitor the quality and accuracy of assessments including: in house audit of assessment reports by Capita, further scrutiny by the Department’s independent health professionals; and regular clinical quality events to ensure that standards are aligned and areas for improvement are identified and actioned. This ensures that assessment reports, including those involving claimants with autism, are fit for purpose, clinically justified and sound, and provide sufficient information for the department to make an informed decision on entitlement to benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has made any assessment of how the Personal Independence Payment assessment process meets the needs of people with autism and other neurodevelopmental conditions.
answer_text: The Personal Independence Payment (PIP) assessment is designed to treat all applicants fairly. Capita’s assessors are registered health professionals who have specialist training, which equips them to establish the impact of a disability or health condition on an individual’s functional ability. There are processes within the PIP customer journey to ensure that applicants who require additional support, including those with autism and other neuro-diverse conditions can access assistance. These include: the review of each application by a Capita clinician to identify the most appropriate assessment; the opportunity to request reasonable adjustments and to be accompanied during an assessment; and, to have extra time to return information or assistance with form completion. 	In addition, my officials engage regularly with stakeholders via the Department’s Disability Consultative Forum to better understand the needs of the range of PIP applicants and to identify areas for improvement. There has also been periodic engagement with Autism NI, most recently in March this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the delayed healthcare workers' pay uplift.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of staff currently assigned to emergency response and recovery operations, in each divisional area; and (ii) how this compares to her Department’s full staffing complement.
answer_text: My Department’s reaction to emergency response and recovery operations is based on a multi-agency approach with preparations being commenced as far ahead of the event as possible. This is operated with regard to the severe weather warnings which are issued by the Met Office.Staff are not drawn from dedicated emergency response and recovery roles, rather my Department responds alongside our multi-agency partners with a range of staff, both internal and external, to provide emergency response and recovery operations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of how the ongoing staff shortages have affected her Department’s capacity to undertake routine maintenance of (i) roadside trees; and (ii) drainage systems, which are key to storm preparedness.
answer_text: There are only a limited number of trees in my Department’s ownership.  The ownership and primary maintenance responsibility for almost all roadside trees lies with the adjacent landowner.My Department continues to clean road gullies on an annual basis, while also seeking to check any known potential flooding locations where adverse weather is expected. Much of the work relating to gully cleaning and the maintenance of drainage systems, such as desilting, is reliant on external contractors and therefore I continue to seek additional funding to address the impact of the limited service, in place due to years of underfunding and austerity by the British Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the significant number of vacancies currently being recruited for within her Department, to outline what assessment she has made of her Department’s ability to (i) prepare for; and (ii) respond effectively to, storm-related incidents.
answer_text: My Department’s reaction to emergency response and recovery operations is based on a multi-agency approach with preparations being commenced as far ahead of the event as possible. This is operated with regard to the severe weather warnings which are issued by the Met Office.Staff are not drawn from dedicated emergency response and recovery roles, rather my Department responds alongside our multi-agency partners with a range of staff, both internal and external, to provide emergency response and recovery operations. This approach manages the impact of staff vacancies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department intends to set a target for the restoration of ancient and long-established woodland.
answer_text: The Environmental Improvement Plan for Northern Ireland includes actions for afforestation that incorporates conservation and protection measures for long established woodland.Forest Service manages approximately 700 ha of woodland classified as ancient woodland and a further 2,622 ha of long-established woodland. Forest management plans are in place that recognise the high conservation value of these sites which are managed with a focus on protecting and enhancing biodiversity.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why continuous glucose monitoring sensors, such as Guardian 4, are not funded; and (ii) whether he intends to take steps to ensure parity of access to closed-loop insulin pump systems for type 1 diabetes patients across all UK regions.
answer_text: Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications.   Guardian 4 sensor is a Continuous Glucose Monitor which enables closed loop technology. Work has been completed in terms of overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support. Funding is required for devices in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  I have recently supported the establishment of a clinical working group to work with relevant stakeholders across Health and Social Care to develop an implementation plan which outlines an appropriate timescale for rollout. The implementation plan is currently being drafted and will support equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how his Department will scale up delivery of the Forests for Our Future programme.
answer_text: It is widely acknowledged that the current rate of tree planting and woodland creation must increase significantly to meet the ambitious goals of the Forests for Our Future programme and contribute to governments net zero targets by 2050.In my recent address to stakeholders at the Woodland Trust launch of its report 'State of The UK's Woods and Trees: Northern Ireland', I announced the development of a Northern Ireland Tree Planting Action Plan. The development of the plan will be facilitated by the Strategic Investment Board (SIB), and co-designed in partnership with stakeholders.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how tree preservation orders are enforced; and (ii) how his Department oversees that trees are protected.
answer_text: The responsibility for making and enforcing Tree Preservation Orders (TPOs)	is a matter for local councils. 		My Department’s responsibilities in relation to protecting trees are governed under the Forestry Act (Northern Ireland) 2010. Under the legislation, felling of trees within woodlands covering an area of 0.2 hectares or more require authorisation from my Department. Landowners must make an application to my Department for a felling licence and provide details of the associated woodland management plan, which demonstrates compliance with sustainable forest management as set out in the UK Forestry Standard.	Additionally, where woodlands are proposed for felling to accommodate a change of land use, approval is required under planning legislation for development purposes. The Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) (Forestry) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2006 applies in circumstances where proposals to fell trees are not part of planned development.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions his Department is taking to (i) protect; (ii) map; and (iii) manage, kelp forests on the coastline.
answer_text: Within Northern Ireland kelp forests are currently afforded direct protection as a sub-feature on Annex I Reef habitats within a number of Marine Protected Areas (Skerries and Causeway SAC, Rathlin Island SAC, Strangford Lough SAC and The Maidens SAC). Kelp forests are blue carbon habitats. Under the Northern Ireland Blue Carbon Action Plan 2025–2030, launched in April, a key objective is to gather evidence on their location, extent, and condition. To support this, my Department is surveying the NI coastline using acoustic and imaging techniques to map kelp beds.The use of demersal mobile gear is prohibited in Marine Protected Areas under the Marine Protected Areas (Prohibited Methods of Fishing) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2022, directly protecting kelp forests. Small-scale hand harvesting is permitted under permit from the Department, allowing the potential impact of removal and harvesting techniques to be assessed. Commercial kelp harvesting by vehicle or vessel requires a marine licence, but none have been issued to date.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on progress towards developing a new Forestry Strategy to replace the 2006 strategy.
answer_text: The current forestry strategy, Northern Ireland Forestry – A Strategy for Sustainability & Growth sets out the long-term objective to increase woodland cover to 12% of Northern Ireland’s land area by 2050. This aim aligns with recommendations of the Climate Change Committee regarding the level of afforestation needed to contribute to net zero 2050 targets.At the recent Woodland Trust launch event of its report, State of the UK’s Woods and Trees: Northern Ireland, I announced the commissioning of a Northern Ireland Tree Planting Action Plan will be facilitated by the Strategic Investment Board (SIB). The Tree Planting Action Plan will be co-designed in collaboration with stakeholders over the coming months. The Plan will highlight key actions needed to deliver the 12% forest cover by 2050 and inform forestry’s contribution to net zero targets within the Climate Action Plan.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 32815/22-27, to detail what (i) safeguards; and (ii) regulatory checks, are in place to ensure what is constructed without planning permission matches the infrastructure which the Pollution Prevention and Control permit was approved for.
answer_text: Where planning permission is required, the relevant local planning office is consulted as part of the permitting process. Should key environmental infrastructure be identified within the planning permission NIEA inspectors may restrict waste activities on site until the infrastructure is in place.  Any breach of a planning permission would be enforced by the local planning office.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether funding has been identified to cover the estimated £200 million cost of implementing the 2025–2026 Agenda for Change framework pay uplifts, as referenced in the Minister of Health's Ministerial Statement 28 May 2025.
answer_text: ANSWERWe cannot deliver public services without public servants, and it is essential that all public sector workers, including health and social care staff, are appropriately rewarded and receive any pay award in a timely manner. I plan to hold a December monitoring round at which time we will understand the funding available for allocation following the Chancellor’s Autumn Budget. The Executive have agreed that the Department of Health will have first call on the available funding in that monitoring round for health service pay costs, alongside funding to meet the pay awards of other public sector workers.It remains the responsibility of each Minister to manage all pressures including pay costs within their department’s allocated budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether he intends to allocate additional in-year funding to the Department of Health to meet the costs of the 2025–2026 pay awards for nurses, doctors, and other health service staff.
answer_text: ANSWERWe cannot deliver public services without public servants, and it is essential that all public sector workers, including health and social care staff, are appropriately rewarded and receive any pay award in a timely manner. I plan to hold a December monitoring round at which time we will understand the funding available for allocation following the Chancellor’s Autumn Budget. The Executive have agreed that the Department of Health will have first call on the available funding in that monitoring round for health service pay costs, alongside funding to meet the pay awards of other public sector workers including teachers, police, Translink and NI Water staff.It remains the responsibility of each Minister to manage all pressures including pay costs within their department’s allocated budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of firework licences granted for (i) private; (ii) organised; and (iii) commercial, firework displays for 2025, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: As of 13 October 2025, there has been a total of 263 firework licences granted by the Department for the year 2025.  Private displays will include home addresses and also private functions such as wedding venues.  Larger displays for public viewing will range from smaller community events to larger council organised and funded events such as Halloween and Christmas celebrations.  The types of fireworks used at these events, e.g. garden or display fireworks, will be dependent on the experience of the fireworks display operator.  Halloween is the busiest time for firework display applications and a number continue to be processed.The year-to-date breakdown, by type of event, is as follows:Private Displays – 191Public Displays – 49Council Organised Displays - 23Total - 263The year-to-date breakdown, to include council area, is as follows:PrivatePublicCouncilTotalAntrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council101213Ards and North Down Borough Council131014Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council121114Belfast City Council86115Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council811928Derry City and Strabane District Council1225Fermanagh and Omagh District Council393042Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council5218Mid and East Antrim Borough Council242228Mid Ulster District Council 91515Newry, Mourne and Down District Council6219081Total1914923263

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of (a) open; (b) temporary; and (c) permanent, vacancies currently in his Department; and (ii) the number of these that his Department is actively recruiting for.
answer_text: (i) 	As at the end of September 2025, the Department of Finance had 500 vacancies which have been declared to NICSHR to be filled (these are considered ‘open’ vacancies). Of the 500 vacancies, 494 are for permanent posts and 6 are for temporary posts. When any business area within the Department identifies a vacant post which needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. Vacancy figures are taken at Month End September 2025 and are derived from the NICSHR Vacancy Management trackers which is an administrative data source. NICSHR, which is part of the Department, are responsible for all recruitment in the Civil Service. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise resourcing activity within allocated financial and staff resources. As the vacancies mentioned in part i) are currently open, NICSHR is actively evaluating suitable strategies to fill them through both internal and external resourcing processes.  NICSHR have a range of over 280 planned and ongoing recruitment and selection methods to fill vacancies across all departments including volume recruitment competitions for General Service grades and specialist competitions for Industrial and Professional and Technical grades.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of (a) open; (b) temporary; and (c) permanent, vacancies currently in her Department; and (ii) the number of these that her Department is actively recruiting for.
answer_text: NICS HR, part of the Department of Finance, are responsible for all recruitment in the Northern Ireland Civil Service.  As at the end of September 2025, my Department had 449 vacancies which have been declared to NICS HR to be filled (these are considered ‘open’ vacancies). All 449 vacancies are for permanent posts.When any department identifies a vacant post which needs filled, the request is referred to NICS HR to initiate the process to fill the post.Vacancy figures are taken at Month End and are derived from NICS HR Vacancy Management trackers which is an administrative data source.NICS HR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise resourcing activity within allocated financial and staff resources.NICS HR have a range of over 280 planned and ongoing recruitment and selection methods to fill vacancies across all Departments including volume recruitment competitions for General Service grades and specialist competitions for Industrial and Professional and Technical grades.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of (a) open; (b) temporary; and (c) permanent, vacancies currently in his Department; and (ii) the number of these that his Department is actively recruiting for.
answer_text: As at the end of September 2025, my department had 927 vacancies which have been declared to NICSHR to be filled (these are considered ‘open’ vacancies). All 927 vacancies are for permanent posts, spread across responsibilities that rest with me and also those that rest with the Defra Secretary of State since the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 came into force on 12 April 2024.As the member will be aware, DAERA’s vacancies are, like those in other departments, filled in accordance with NICS policies and procedures. Recruitment is led by the Department of Finance’s NICSHR teams which work with all departments on an ongoing basis to prioritise recruitment across the NICS within their allocated financial and staff resources.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the number of (a) open; (b) temporary; and (c) permanent, vacancies currently in their Department; and (ii) the number of these that their Department is actively recruiting for.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of (a) open; (b) temporary; and (c) permanent, vacancies currently in his Department; and (ii) the number of these that his Department is actively recruiting for.
answer_text: The Department of Education has 137 permanent vacancies which are with the Northern Ireland Civil Service Human Resources, in the Department of Finance, to be resourced.There are no temporary vacancies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 31705/22-27, whether the red line drawing can be overlaid on a true to life satellite map of the area, such as a Google Earth image.
answer_text: The Department is not required to utilise satellite mapping systems to overlay redline drawings related to waste authorisations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the process by which the NI Environment Agency inspectors check to determine if stored waste is (i) refuse-derived fuel; and (ii) solid-recovered fuel.
answer_text: Waste materials are categorised via European Waste Catalogue codes (EWC). The waste code for Combustible Waste is EWC 19.12.10 which does not differentiate between Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF) or Solid Recovered Fuel (SRF). Therefore, for sites authorised to accepted EWC Code 19.12.10, there is no requirement for the NIEA to check that stored waste is either RDF or SRF.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of bales of stored waste opened as part of NI Environment Agency inspections at waste management sites, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information in a readily accessible format. However, I can advise that the NIEA do not routinely seek to open baled waste as part of scheduled inspections.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any assessment her Department has made of the impact on the proposed Ballynahinch Bypass of the factual position and legal position regarding (a) emission targets; (b) carbon budgets; and (c) the Climate Action Plan; (ii) when assessments were carried out, and (iii) the number of times the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs was consulted.
answer_text: On 12 March 2025 I announced the progression of the A24 Ballynahinch Bypass scheme with my officials moving forward on the development of the business case and procurement of the scheme. The Environmental Statement for the scheme was originally published in March 2015, prior to the introduction of the Climate Change Act 2022. Due to the passage of time, this scheme will require relevant documents to be updated, which will consider the impact of the Act.  At this stage of the work there has not been a need to consult with our colleagues in DAERA.

answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency The Northern Ireland Environment AgencyThe Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 31499/22-27, to detail (i) what steps his Department is taking to address the regulatory gap whereby operators may obtain (a) a Pollution Prevention and Control permit; and (b) a waste authorisation in the absence of, or beyond the scope of, planning permission; and (ii) whether his Department will strengthen coordination between the Northern Ireland Environment Agency and local planning authorities to prevent such loopholes.
answer_text: Where the proposal is for a waste management activity requiring a permit under the Pollution Prevention and Control (Industrial Emissions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2013 or a licence under the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, planning permission must be in place before the permit or licence is issued.For non-waste activities, there is no requirement under the regulations to ensure planning permission is in force.NIEA does not consider this to be a regulatory gap. NIEA routinely provides advice on the requirement for a PPC permit during planning consultations for proposals which contain processes likely to require a permit; and NIEA does not consider this a loophole and as such, there are no plans to change any processes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 31246/22-27, to outline why his Department cannot detail the amount of waste imported into Northern Ireland when it can detail the amount collected in waste shipment notifications.
answer_text: Only international notifiable waste imports are received by the Department. Other data, including tonnages, from non-hazardous ‘Green List’ waste imports are not held and thus the Department does not hold data for all waste imports.The Department is currently involved in the development of a United Kingdom (UK) wide digital waste tracking system, led by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs. Once fully operational, this system is expected to provide a much-improved picture on waste movements within the UK, including capturing ‘Green List’ waste imports and exports.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 31246/22-27, to detail the amount of the (i) environmental permits and licences; and (ii) producer responsibility compliance schemes, income is specific to waste management operators.
answer_text: 2024/2025 waste permit subsistence invoices total = £1,292,3322024/2025 waste licence subsistence invoices total = not currently available2024/2025 Producer responsibility invoices specific to waste management operators is £102,799

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 31244/22-27, whether there is a legislative requirement on his Department to record all waste movements (i) in to; and (ii) out of, Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There is no legislative requirement on my department to record all waste movements in and out of Northern Ireland.However, under the Transfrontier Shipments of Waste Regulations 2007 and Regulation (EU) 1013/2006 on shipments of waste, operators are required to notify the Department of shipments of green list waste and notifiable waste.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the measures taken to mitigate the negative impact on pensions of consistent late payment of pay awards.
answer_text: Once a pay award is implemented, for members of the HSC Pension Scheme, HSC Pension Service will retrospectively align active members pay with the correct contribution tier from the date the pay award is backdated to. This ensures that pension contributions reflect actual pay, maintaining fairness and accuracy in pension accrual.If a member retires in a year where a retrospective pay award is made, the members pension benefits will be recalculated to reflect the backdated award and any arrears owing will be paid.For Annual Allowance purposes, where a previous year’s pay award is implemented in the next financial year the pension input amount may spike in that year. Members can use unused Annual Allowance from the previous three tax years to offset this spike. In recent years the Annual Allowance rose from £40,000 to £60,00, giving members more room for pension growth before facing tax penalties.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 30787/22-27, to detail the nature of the 110 storm overflows assessed as unsatisfactory.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:The Northern Ireland Environment Agency classified these storm overflows as unsatisfactory and would therefore be best placed to provide the information sought. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 30786/22-27, with no Event Duration Monitors in place, whether NI Water therefore has no recorded data on the number or duration of storm overflows from the Quoile Rive into Strangford Lough, and relies solely on modelled predictions, rather than measured events.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water currently has no recorded data on the number or duration of storm overflows from the Quoile River into Strangford Lough, and relies solely on modelled predictions, rather than measured events.These predictions are informed by the analysis and findings of industry standard hydraulic models to simulate observed flows and predict spills across its wastewater networks. As agreed with the Utility Regulator and the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA), NI Water has been investing in developing integrated suites of models to understand more accurately the spill performance of storm overflows and their impact on the receiving aquatic environment. In addition, NI Water’s Drainage Area Study models are built and calibrated to industry best practice (Chartered Institute of Water and Environmental Management) and approved by  NIEA. These models are typically simulated using 20 years of historical rainfall to generate an annual average for frequency and volume of spills.	For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

answer_text: The NI Environment Agency will consider publishing total fee income received on an annual basis in relation to each of its regulatory charging schemes.  Information will also be provided as to how this fee income is allocated.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: A map showing the extent of the authorised storage area, attached at Annex A,  has been placed in the Assembly library.
