question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of supports in place for those living with motor neurone disease; and (ii) what provisions are in place for early diagnosis.
answer_text: I recognise that there are significant pressures on neurology services in Northern Ireland, including services for those diagnosed with motor neurone disease (MND).My Department is currently reviewing consultation responses on the final report of the neurology review which includes recommendations for the optimum configuration of neurology services for the next 10-15 years which will have an impact on the development of MND services, including timely access to neurological assessment, diagnosis, and ongoing support. In terms of early diagnosis, people with symptoms should see their GP who will then be referred for further tests with a specialist neurologist, leading to a potential diagnosis. The patient will then have access to a specialist multidisciplinary team, consisting of neurologists and specialist nurses, who will provide ongoing care and support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 31248/22-27, to detail the amount of the fee income collected by the NI Environment Agency from (i) waste imports; and (ii) waste exports, in each of the last five years, that has been reinvested in (a) strengthening local waste infrastructure; (b) addressing waste crime; and (iii) the amount that has been transferred to his Department’s general budget.
answer_text: (i), (ii)The total fee income as detailed in AQW 31248/22-27 is retained by the NIEA and used to resource the regulatory work of the agency. There is no reinvestment in local waste infrastructureThe NIEA does not hold that information. (iii)       The total fee income as detailed in AQW 31248/22-27 is retained by the NIEA and used to resource the regulatory work of the agency.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action taken in response to each suspected cancer cluster, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Cancer clusters are analysed in line with UKIACR (United Kingdom and Ireland Association of Cancer Registries) guidance. The Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR) assess whether the cancer incidence rate in the investigated area is greater or lower than expected using a statistical measure called the standardised incidence ratio (SIR).  Where the cancer standardised incidence ratio (SIR) is not found to be significantly higher than expected, information is provided to the requestor with an explanation of the results. Where the cancer standardised incidence ratio (SIR) is found to be significantly higher than expected, these results are passed to the Public Health Agency (PHA).  If the cancer in question may be related to certain factors, such as lifestyle or genetic factors (e.g. lung, breast), this will be highlighted in the response, which may help in decisions regarding whether any further action is taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the nature of each suspected cancer cluster, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: There have been 17 suspected geographical cancer clusters during the period 2015 to present. The nature of the suspected clusters covers a range of cancer types including adrenal, brain, breast, colorectal, haematological, lung, prostate, thyroid, upper gastrointestinal and childhood cancers.There have also been 3 investigations related to suspected cancer clusters associated with workplaces over the period 2015 to present.Cancer clusters are analysed in line with UKIACR (United Kingdom and Ireland Association of Cancer Registries) guidance. The Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR) assess whether the cancer incidence rate in the investigated area is greater or lower than expected using a statistical measure called the standardised incidence ratio (SIR).  Where the cancer standardised incidence ratio (SIR) is not found to be significantly higher than expected, information is provided to the requestor with an explanation of the results. Where the cancer standardised incidence ratio (SIR) is found to be significantly higher than expected, these results are passed to the Public Health Agency (PHA).  If the cancer in question may be related to certain factors, such as lifestyle or genetic factors (e.g. lung, breast), this will be highlighted in the response, which may help in decisions regarding whether any further action is taken.

answer_text: There are currently over 440 waste management licenses issued in Northern Ireland. The number of these that operate solely between 07.30hrs to 18.00hrs would not be retained in a format which is readily available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the Departments process in the event of a Pollution Prevention and Control Permit having been granted and subsequently discovering that the licenced activity did not have planning permission.
answer_text: The Department has no process in place for this scenario, as planning permission is not required for PPC permits except where it relates to a specified waste management activity, and for those applications a permit cannot be issued in the absence of planning permission. Any noncompliance with a permit or licence would be assessed in line with the DAERA Enforcement Policy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how officials verify that planning permission is in force for the activity to be regulated by a Pollution Prevention and Control Permit, prior to the granting of the Permit.
answer_text: The Department consults with statutory consultees in accordance with to The Pollution Prevention and Control (Industrial Emissions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2013. The Department must consult the relevant planning authority as part of a new permit application in cases where the proposal is for a specified waste management activity to verify planning permission is in place. In all other cases there is no requirement under the regulations to ensure planning permission is in force.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether encompass within hospitals reboots once a month.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what regulations are in place for waste operators to minimise the fire risk of stored solid recovered fuel waste, given its dry nature.
answer_text: The Fire & Rescue Service Order (NI) 2006 sets out the fire safety duties of employers and employees of relevant premises.The NIEA has also issued a regulatory position statement  - ‘Reducing fire risk at sites storing waste and other combustible materials’ https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/doe/RPS%20-%20Fire%20Risk%20Storing%20Combustible%20Materials-2016.pdf to assist waste operators in understanding fire risk and ways to reduce fire risk on authorised waste facilities treating and storing combustible materials.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail why dry solid recovered fuel waste would need to be sprayed.
answer_text: The rationale for any specific treatment applied to waste would be a matter for you to enquire about with the company concerned.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has explored providing additional resources to the Department of Health to resolve the current contract dispute with healthcare staff.

To ask the Minister of Education to outline the proportion of statements of Special Educational Needs that are currently being issued outside the 26-week timeline for the statutory assessment process.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of revenue the NI Environment Agency collected from (i) imported; and (ii) exported, waste shipment notification fees, in each of the last five years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the amount of fee income the Northern Ireland Environment Agency received from (a) waste shipment notifications; (b) environmental permits and licences, in each of the last ten years; and (c) producer responsibility compliance schemes; and (ii) how has this revenue been allocated or reinvested to support local waste infrastructure and enforcement.
answer_text: The amount of fee income the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) received from a) waste shipment notifications; b) environmental permits and licenses; c) producer responsibility; in each of the last 10 financial years is outlined overleaf.This revenue is retained by the NIEA and is used to resource the regulatory work of the NIEA.Waste shipment notifications2025/26 – £945,063 *to date2024/25 – £583,8382023/24 – £499,9632022/23 – £581,1942021/22 – £601,2482020/21 – £478,3102019/20 – £552,2032018/19 – £192,6122017/18 – £332,7192016/17 – £332,114Environmental permits and licenses2025/26 – £2,508,613 * to date2024/25 – £9,356,2122023/24 – £8,565,7102022/23 – £8,302,1352021/22 – £7,645,2942020/21 – £7,248,6702019/20 – £7,414,7052018/19 – £7,663,3292017/18 – £7,322,9222016/17 – £6,802,010Producer responsibility2025/26 – £77,710 * to date2024/25 – £604,7492023/24 – £446,1532022/23 – £444,1172021/22 – £418,9932020/21 – £418,6162019/20 – £425,0172018/19 – £431,9092017/18 – £397,2302016/17 – £426,488
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail how many tonnes of waste were imported annually into Northern Ireland from (a) England; (b) Scotland; (c) Wales; and (d) the Republic of Ireland; and (ii) what was the cost to the public purse of processing this material, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: (i) The Department does not hold information on the tonnages of all waste imported into Northern Ireland from Great Britain. As regards the Republic of Ireland, not all the waste movements are reportable to the Department therefore the total tonnage of waste imported cannot be provided.(ii) This information is not held by my department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment made of the impact of imported waste on the capacity of our local infrastructure to deal with local waste.
answer_text: My Department is currently undertaking an assessment of Northern Ireland’s future waste infrastructure needs and expects to complete this work in Spring 2026. This assessment includes waste imports and exports in both the baseline and the projections of future waste arisings together with the infrastructure needed to manage that waste.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail why full data on imports is not routinely published; and (ii) whether he will commit to publishing an annual breakdown by source country and waste type.
answer_text: There is currently no legislative requirement for my Department to routinely publish full data on international waste imports.No, there is no legislative requirement directly on my Department to publish this information. However, under the provisions of the new EU 2024/1157 regulations on shipments of waste the European Commission will publish data relating to EU international waste movements via their website. This will include waste data for Northern Ireland falling under the scope of these regulations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 30317/22-27, to detail her Department's role in the operation of ports.
answer_text: My Department contributes to the development of policy in the ports and harbours sector and ensures that the regulatory framework in which the local ports and harbours operate in is kept under review.  The Department also has a role in the appointment of board members to Belfast Harbour Commissioners, Foyle Port and Warrenpoint Harbour Authority.  Responsibility for shipping services, navigation and marine safety matters remain reserved functions for the Department for Transport and the Maritime and Coastguard Agency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the statement from Warrenpoint Port on 15 September 2025.
answer_text: My Department does not have any role in the day-to-day operations of our ports, however I am aware of reported odour issues at Warrenpoint Harbour.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the NI Environment Agency will respond to all concerns raised by members of the public regarding waste management, in the absence of an online reporting form.
answer_text: The NIEA regularly receives reports from members of the public with concerns regarding waste management activities. The NIEA will record and assess reports received, and take the necessary action dependent on the nature of the complaint and available resources.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the proposed reporting form for members of the public to raise concerns over waste management; and (ii) why this has not been made available yet.
answer_text: My officials are continuing with the process of developing a new Incident Management System which will include an online reporting facility for members of the public across a range of regulatory regimes. It had been hoped to deliver this new System by the end of 2025 but due to delays in the project, the completion date is expected to be the end of March 2026. In the meantime, waste management issues can be reported via the NIEAwastecomplaint@daera-ni.gov.uk mailbox.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of supports in place for those living with motor neurone disease; and (ii) what provisions are in place for early diagnosis.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the work of the Local Economic Partnerships.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of (a) open; (b) temporary; and (c) permanent vacancies currently in her Department; and (ii) how many of these her Department is actively recruiting for, broken down by section area.
answer_text: NICS HR, part of the Department of Finance (DoF), are responsible for all NICS recruitment.  As at the end of August 2025, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 664 vacancies for the Department for Infrastructure; 17 of the vacancies are for temporary posts and 647 are for permanent posts. When a department identifies that a vacant post needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise resourcing activity within allocated financial and staff resources. Vacancy figures are derived from the NICSHR Vacancy Management tracker which is an administrative data source. My department is taking action to tackle long-standing staff resourcing challenges that have affected our capacity to deliver essential works and services. In collaboration with NICS HR, we are implementing a strategic approach to attempt to address these issues. Recent prioritised recruitment efforts have seen the appointment of approximately 300 new staff members. Recent figures from NICS Resourcing show that approximately 400 new staff are expected to start in the department in the coming months, following the completion of a range of P&T and Specialist Competitions.Currently there is supply at most general service grades and we are availing of these where supply is available, however we continue to experience significant gaps in supply at Professional & Technical (P&T) and Specialist grades.  NICSHR have a range of planned and ongoing recruitment and selection methods to fill vacancies submitted by all Departments including volume recruitment competitions for General Service grades and specialist competitions for Industrial and Professional and Technical grades.DfI have currently circa 21 P&T registered competitions with NICS HR which are at various stages of development and progression (Annex A). The DfI People Development & Workforce Planning Directorate (PD&WPD) has been working closely with NICS HR Resourcing Team to prioritise recruitment competitions for those grades which are having the most impact on the organisation. This work includes progressing innovative recruitment models like the recent DfI Skills Academy for Trainee Civil Engineering Assistants and Apprenticeship and Graduate Programmes. Despite these proactive measures, significant challenges remain. Budgetary constraints and historic underinvestment continue to constrain recruitment and retention, particularly in specialist roles. Pay disparities with the private sector further exacerbate retention difficulties. NICS HR are responsible for the pay strategy and are currently leading and developing a pay strategy for NICS. Until the outworkings of this is known, we will continue to struggle with this issue.That said I can assure you that my department is working very hard to ensure that critical vacancies are being filled and that the available staff are allocated to posts that delive

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail each storm overflow event from the Quoile River into Strangford Lough, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised that NI Water is installing Event Duration Monitors (EDMs) in order to monitor the performance of storm overflows.  It is taking a phased approach to EDM installation and its initial focus has been to install EDMs on storm overflows which spill into designated bathing waters, shellfish water protected areas and at key operational sites.As of September 2025, NI Water currently has 222 EDMs, including EDMs across bathing waters and shellfish areas which are operational, have been fully verified and assured, and are returning information on frequency (spill count) and duration of spill, with annual publication on NI Water’s Storm overflow webpage, “Storm Overflow Performance”.Whilst there are currently no EDM monitors installed on assets that discharge directly from the Quoile River into Strangford Lough, NI Water is in the process of extending its coverage of EDMs to storm overflows that spill into waterbodies which support shellfish growth and production, and other inland waterbodies and rivers.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of waste processing compliance inspections the NI Environment Agency have conducted between 8pm and 8am, in each of the last five years
answer_text: Whilst the core work hours for NIEA field staff are 07.30hrs to 18.00hrs, Monday to Friday, inspections are conducted at weekends and out of hours as and when necessary. Unfortunately, the information requested is not retained in a format which is readily available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of unregulated sewage discharges into Strangford Lough, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water uses industry standard hydraulic models to simulate observed flows and predict spills across their wastewater networks.  The results of the models consider average performance in a typical year, and as such it is not possible to provide information on performance for specific years.  Each storm overflow is assessed with the Northern Ireland Environmental Regulator (NIEA) based upon several criteria and informed by the results of hydraulic models. Those storm overflows that fail to meet the modelled standard are designated as ‘unsatisfactory’. In the Strangford Local Management Area, 110 storm overflows have been modelled and assessed as being ‘unsatisfactory’.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.  Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the process by which an enforcement notice for waste processing, served by the NI Environment Agency, is then enforced?
answer_text: Enforcement notices are monitored by the NIEA for compliance. Non-compliance with an enforcement notice may result in the escalation of the NIEA’s enforcement approach. Any escalation is taken on a case-by-case basis and will be dependent on particular circumstances of the non-compliance, with further action taken in consideration of the DAERA Enforcement Policy. For convenience the Enforcement Policy can be found at the following link:  https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/dard/23.24.043%20DAERA%20Enforcement%20Policy%20-%202023.pdf
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when his Department will be seeking applications for the Core Grant Funding Scheme 2026-2027.
answer_text: It is my intention to launch a new application process for Core Grant funding in 2026/27 within the coming weeks. Detailed guidance for applicants will be published alongside the application form on the Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the steps his Department has taken to ensure that Operation Begrain is proceeding as a robust inquiry; (ii) why no senior officials from the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust or his Department have to date been questioned in connection with the withholding of records, patient harm, or governance failings; and (iii) what actions his Department is taking to promote transparency and justice for affected families.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland’s (PSNI) Operation Begrain is a live police investigation into the clinical practices of the former consultant neurologist Michael Watt.  The PSNI has operational independence in its leadership of this inquiry. The Department of Health does not comment on live police investigations or operational policing matters.In common with all parts of the Health and Social Care system, my Department will cooperate fully with the PSNI as it progresses its investigations. On 11 April 2025 an Information Sharing Agreement was signed between the Department of Health and PSNI to this end. The PSNI’s website does provide updates and points of contact in regard to Operation Begrain – the following link may be helpful to you  -  www.psni.police.uk/latest-news/detectives-police-services-major-investigation-team-issue-update-operation-begrain

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what proportion of the commissioning budget for the Children’s Haematology and Oncology Unit at the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children is unspent when beds are closed.
answer_text: My officials have spoken to Belfast Trust and they have confirmed that the budget is currently fully utilised within the haematology/oncology service as the service is maintained at 10 beds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether the Children’s Haematology and Oncology Unit at the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children remains commissioned for 10 beds; and (ii) how the remaining rooms are currently being used.
answer_text: My officials have spoken to Belfast Trust and they have confirmed:(i)	The service remains commissioned for 10 beds(ii)	The rooms within the unit will be used when possible as day care beds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of bed days lost in the Children’s Haematology and Oncology Unit at the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children when fewer than 10 beds were open in the last 24 months.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust has responded that they believe that calculating “bed days lost” is not an appropriate metric in this context. During the relevant period, there were no actual bed days lost. When additional admissions were required (such as a 9th or 10th patient), beds were utilised in other wards that were suitable for haemato-oncology patients. This approach ensured that all patients received the necessary care without interruption.It is important to note that, particularly over the summer, the hospital was not operating at full capacity across all children’s services. This is part of our normal operational practice, where we balance resources throughout the year to meet varying levels of demand.Admissions to the unit can occur both on a planned and unplanned basis, depending on the needs of the children. As such, the unit has continued to provide inpatient care as required, matching resources to patient needs. Therefore, it is not possible or accurate to describe any “bed days lost” during this period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of beds open in the Children’s Haematology and Oncology Unit at the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children in each of the last 24 months.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust have confirmed that all 10 commissioned inpatient haematology/oncology beds were open until 25 June 2025.  These beds were subsequently re-profiled with 8 of the beds remaining as inpatient haematology/oncology.  The balance of 2 beds within the inpatient haematology/oncology ward were changed to day care to support throughput and maintenance of capacity.  However, the service continued to utilise 10 beds to maintain clinical care using appropriate beds in other ward areas with clinical discussion and agreement on patient placement.  This ensured the appropriate level of skilled staff were retained within the specialised unit to undertake the critical level of care required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in cases where fewer than ten beds were open in the Children’s Haematology and Oncology Unit at the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children during the last 24 months, to detail the reasons for this reduction.
answer_text: My officials have spoken to the Belfast Trust who have confirmed the service is maintained at 10 beds overall.  Within the wider haematology and oncology departments, all nursing roles have been reviewed, which enables the Trust to utilise staff from the wider team in other ward areas and bank staff.  This ensures the service is safely covered and that the most time critical treatments are protected.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what funding arrangement has been set for the delivery of the Learning Disability Service Model.
answer_text: I approved a 14-week public consultation on the Learning Disability Service Model, which commenced on 19 August 2025. The Model proposes a new approach to supporting adults with a learning disability through Health and Social Care services in Northern Ireland. The draft implementation plan has been developed in collaboration with key partners across the sector. In parallel to this work, my officials have completed a financial review of learning disability services, however a full costings exercise will only be completed following the outcome of the consultation. In addition, a review of high-cost placements has been completed and work is underway to deliver better value for money and outcomes. While there is the potential to recalibrate some of the existing £500m annual spending on adult Learning Disability services, it is likely that additional investment will be required, which may include transformation funding. Implementation will be phased and prioritised in partnership with service users and families. It will depend on sustained investment, workforce development, and, where appropriate, legislative reform to unlock new models of support and rebalance existing provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) an update on the report on the Muckamore Abbey Hospital Inquiry; and (ii) when will it be published.
answer_text: The Muckamore Abbey Hospital Inquiry finished its formal public hearings on 10 March 2025. The Inquiry held a number of informal sessions between 3 June and 6 June 2025 on recent experiences of resettlement.  A summary of the key points from these sessions was published by the Inquiry on their website on 10 July and can be accessed at the following link: 25.07.10 - Resettlement Information Sessions Summary .pdf.  The Inquiry Chair has indicated that he hopes to publish the report this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of derelict properties currently in Downpatrick.
answer_text: Information on the number of derelict properties in Downpatrick is not held by my Department. Information on property vacancy rates is published quarterly by Land & Property Services and is available via the Department of Finance website. These statistics are reported at District Council level.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on (i) the proposed park and ride facility in Downpatrick; and (ii) the work she has undertaken since taking office to advance this project.
answer_text: A suitable site for a Park & Ride at Downpatrick, has been identified within the Down Business Park along the A7 Belfast Road. The project has the potential to provide parking for approximately 400 vehicles.Environmental assessments and detailed designs have been completed. Once a business case has been approved, the Department will begin the formal submission of a planning application. Whilst work on the Park and Ride programme is ongoing, including the site at Downpatrick, budgets are significantly constrained as the capital budget for my Department is less than required.  Progression of this park and ride will be dependent on the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the economic harm to Warrenpoint, following the diversion of the Azamara cruise ship from Warrenpoint to Bangor on 27 August 2025.
answer_text: The call by Azamara Journey to divert to Bangor on 27 August 2025 was pre-planned with Bangor Marina having been formally advised on 2 April 2025, following a request from the Cruise Line’s agent. This was due to tidal constraints at Warrenpoint Harbour which made a call there unsuitable. Despite the necessary schedule amendment, approximately 200 (30%) passengers participated in pre-arranged scheduled excursions, which were to the South Down area.These excursions included:Irish Bread Making (Mrs C's Home Kitchen, Newcastle)The Mountains of Mourne (Slieve Donard Hotel)Alpaca Experience in the Kingdom of Mourne (Cranfield Alpacas)St Patrick’s Country (Saint Patrick Centre, Saul Church, Down Cathedral)Electric Bike Ride & Picnic (Bike Mourne)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline her Department's plans to improve traffic flow in Downpatrick.
answer_text: The preparation of a new Transport Strategy is now at an advanced stage which will inform the planning and delivery of transport infrastructure and services up to 2035.I am aware that there have been a number of traffic studies and detailed traffic modelling completed for Downpatrick town centre which have considered options to improve traffic circulation.  These studies have  resulted in the significant upgrade for the Collins Corner, Killough Road / St. Patrick’s Avenue / Stream Street Junction, Downpatrick which was completed in 2020.My officials are continuing to investigate options for improvements to road safety and traffic progression along Edward Street in the town to enhance access to the hospital and provide safe access to Our Lady and St Patrick Primary School.My Department is also planning to upgrade the existing traffic lights through the town centre later this year to a MOVA system. This will improve the traffic flows and journey times through Market Street / Irish Street and Church Street which are the main routes through the centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why the cruise ship Azamara was diverted from Warrenpoint to Bangor for docking on 27 August 2025.
answer_text: My Department does not have any role in the day-to-day operations of our ports. I am therefore not in a position to advise why the cruise ship Azamara was diverted from Warrenpoint to Bangor for docking on 27 August 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, as part of the regeneration of Donard Wood, to detail (i) what percentage of trees planted will be (a) native; and (b) non-native; and (ii) whether this will increase the overall number of native trees in the wood which currently sits at approximately 30 per cent.
answer_text: Forest management plans, including for regeneration of Donard Forest are compliant with UK Forestry Standard requirements, which are subject to independent audit annually.  The area recently clear-felled to the eastern boundary of the Glen River comprised mainly of fir species and is due to be replanted with Douglas fir, consistent with the regeneration plan.  This harvesting and regeneration programme will not change the ratio of 30% native woodland species in the overall forest.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the plans for the regeneration of Donard Wood in the 2026 planting season.
answer_text: Plans for the regeneration of Donard forest include the restocking of 5.9 hectares in 2026 which was recently cleared on the eastern side of the Glen River.The management plan approved for Donard forest, which ensures that the forest continues to meet the UK Forestry Standard requirements, including for diversity and sustainability, includes the replanting of this area with Douglas fir.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the regeneration of Donard Wood, to detail (i) how much it will cost; and (ii) whether the money has been ring-fenced.
answer_text: In my response to AQW 25715/22-27, I advised that regeneration of native species was included in plans for the transition of Donard forest to continuous cover woodland.  The forest management plan is long-term and its implementation is ongoing and will be continuous over a number of years, including periodic reviews.  Funding is not ringfenced and will be allocated through Forest Service budgetingprocesses relevant to the period within which regeneration costs are to be incurred. Currently an area of 5.9 hectares has been recently harvested and is due to be replanted in 2026.  The initial replanting cost estimate for this harvested area is approximately £2,000 per hectare.  This reflects typical replanting cost however, additional costs may subsequently be required in the event that some trees may need to be replaced due to losses or damage caused by external factors, for example pests.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what (i) oversight; and (ii) input, his Department has over any agreement between arc21 and waste management licence holders for the storage of waste materials.
answer_text: My department has neither oversight nor input for any agreements between Arc21 and waste management licence holders regarding waste storage; these are contractual matters between those parties.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how many complaints has the NI Environment Agency received via the complaint email address, specific to waste management licence LN/13/35 in each of the last 12 months, broken down by month.
answer_text: MonthNumber of direct reportsJune 20245July 20242August 202411September 20240October 20240November 20241December 20240January 20250February 202576*March 2025119*April 202567*May 20250* The majority of reports received between February and April (259) were not received directly from the complainants and were made in one monthly submission by a third party.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of parking fines issued across South Down, in each of the last five years, broken down by each town that is patrolled.
answer_text: Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) are recorded by council areas, towns and streets. It is not possible to provide details by constituency. The number of PCNs issued for On-street, Off-street and Bus Lane contraventions by year and by town/city for the last 5-years can be accessed at the following link https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/penalty-charge-notice-dataFrom the 1st April 2024, local councils have taken responsibility for the enforcement of their own off-street car parks. The Department remains responsible for the three Park & Ride locations in Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what pathways or support her Department provides for students to avail of top-up degrees in the field of (i) food safety; and (ii) environmental health.
answer_text: Both Queen’s University and Ulster University provide a range of courses related to food safety and environmental health. Details for specific courses, including entry requirements, are a matter for the universities and can be found on their websites. My Department provides a range of financial support for students. Students may be eligible to apply for support to “top up” an existing qualification to a higher-level qualification. However, this will depend on the detail of their previous study and other eligibility criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the budget for smoking cessation services, for each year since 2015.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) receives its budget for Tobacco Control through its total opening budget allocation from the Department, but this is not ring- fenced. Since 2015, the PHA has typically allocated approximately £4m per year towards implementing the Tobacco Control Strategy and from that they have typically spent between 71 and 78 per cent on specialist smoking cessation services and the associated use of Nicotine Replacement Therapy (NRT). Smoking Cessation services and the associated NRT expenditure is demand-led therefore variable from year to year based on the number of people accessing the services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current budget for Tobacco Control Officers.
answer_text: In 2025/26, the Public Health Agency has a budget for Tobacco Control Officers of £880,310.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 27787/22-27, why the annual expenditure on road safety campaigns was not provided for 2022-23.
answer_text: In reviewing spend on Road Safety, it has been identified that the figures previously reported were provided on a cash basis, rather than on an accruals basis which is the normal basis for reporting. The actual expenditure on Road Safety Campaigns since 2020-21, reported on an accruals basis, consistent with annual accounts and outturn is as follows: •	2020/21 £1,279k•	2021/22 £1,375k•	2022/23 £209k•	2023/24 £478k•	2024/25* £842k*remains subject to audit finalisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 27787/22-27, whether she plans to increase the funding allocation for road safety campaigns.
answer_text: While I have not taken final decisions on my Department’s 2025-26 budget outcome, as this is subject to equality considerations, I can assure members that road safety will continue to be a priority, just as it should be for all of us as a society.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 27787/22-27, why there has been a reduction in funding allocation since 2022.
answer_text: Due to ongoing financial constraints felt by all Departments, funding has become more difficult to secure.However, I am pleased to note that my Department was able to spend  £478k in 2023/24, with a further significant increase to £842k in 2024/25. This upward trend reflects a renewed commitment to promoting road safety and supporting initiatives that help save lives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what steps his Department is taking to support people living with motor neurone disease including (i) raising awareness and facilitating access to early diagnosis; and (ii) financial support for their families and carers.
answer_text: (i) My Department recognises the need to have prompt access to appropriate services and support for patients diagnosed with Motor Neurone Disease (MND).  Work is currently being progressed with the HSC Trusts to ensure that appropriate arrangements are in place for patients to receive consultant led care on a local basis, if possible, and supported by specialist nursing teams.Local access to innovative clinical trials testing potential treatments for Motor Neurone Disease is important to patients and their carers. A clinical trial, using the MND-SMART platform and involving participants taking different treatments and then compared to a group was  given placebo drugs, was opened for recruitment in Craigavon Area Hospital in February 2023. The trial has been funded to a level that will allow registration of approximately 12-15 people per year and is expected to run until 2030.My Department has also undertaken a Regional Review of Neurology Services, tasked with identifying the optimum configuration of neurology services for the next 10-15 years. The review has now concluded and whilst not condition specific, it is anticipated that the recommendations regarding person-centred care, significant growth in the workforce and the expansion of referral management processes will significantly improve access to timely diagnosis and supporting services for people with a neurological condition, including those with MND. A public consultation on the final report commenced on 7 May 2025 and will close on 6 August 2025. Responses to the consultation will inform a final implementation plan. It is, however, important to note that implementation of the review’s recommendations will require significant additional funding. Given the current challenging financial environment, it is likely that the pace of implementation will be constrained by the budgetary pressures faced by my Department.(ii) Health and Social Care Trusts continue to proactively reach out to identify informal unpaid carers to ensure they are able to access support and advice in their caring role. I would urge unpaid carers to contact their Trust Carer Support Services Hub to find out about local supports available within their area. Under the Carers and Direct Payments Act (Northern Ireland) 2002, carers have the right to a Carer’s Assessment and to be considered for services to meet their assessed needs.  It places a statutory duty on Health and Social Care Trusts to inform carers of their right to a Carer’s Assessment and the right to provide personal social services to support carers directly, an option of which can be a one off financial payment towards a break away from their caring role.Accessing Self Directed Support gives service users more choice and control over their social care services, providing greater flexibility, promoting independence, and the ability to tailor individual support packages to fit their specific needs and preferences.  This can also help to facilitate family carers i

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how many enforcement staff the NI Environment Agency employ at nature reserves, broken down by location.
answer_text: I can advise that the Northern Ireland Environment Agency does not employ any specific enforcement staff in respect of nature reserves.  Enforcement responsibility for antisocial and/or criminal activities rests with the relevant district council or with the Police Service of Northern Ireland, as appropriate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether additional signage advising dog owners to keep their pets on a lead can be installed at the Quoile Nature Reserve, Downpatrick.
answer_text: My officials advise that issues related to dog control remain a matter for local council to enforce under provision of The Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983, which requires that dog(s) remain under control.  In line with this legislative requirement, it is expected that those visiting nature reserves do so in a responsible manner and signage is provided as part of overall visitor information when visiting such sites.Where specific additional signage is required in respect of dog control issues, I would advise that this is brought to the attention of Newry, Mourne and Down District Council for consideration and action as appropriate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount of rates that have been exempted for companies availing of industrial derating, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: ANSWERIndustrial derating policy supports over 4,400 manufacturers within a strong sector of our economy, supporting a significant number of local jobs aligned to our economic vision of good jobs, improving productivity, decarbonisation and regional balance.The total amount of rates that have been exempted for companies availing of Industrial Derating, in each of the last five years, is detailed in the table below.Rating YearIndustrial Derating2020/21£59,174,0172021/22£64,062,6022022/23£63,100,1862023/24£73,022,1592024/25£76,314,810

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact that the current number of PSNI officers and civilian staff will have on delivering commitments to hold accountable those people responsible for the recent disorder in Ballymena.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 8 - AQO 2220 22-27			   ANSWER I wish to reiterate that I am utterly appalled by the recent disorder in Ballymena and, indeed, beyond.  There is no place in our society for such destruction and disorder and it cannot be justified in any way.  While I acknowledge that the PSNI is currently facing record low staffing levels in terms of both police officers and staff, this does not deter from delivering on the commitment to hold accountable those people responsible for the recent disorder.     I am assured that the PSNI Public Order Enquiry Team is working day and night to identify those involved.  Encouragingly, a number of people have already been arrested and charged with criminal offences.  Additionally, to quell any further disorder or violence, I called upon community and political leaders and all those with influence to condemn this violence and work with the police to restore calm and promote recovery. There is a particular onus on elected representatives to make informed contributions to the discussion and use their platform to diffuse tensions, not inflame them.  I wish to reiterate my support for policing and the vital role the service plays in ensuring public safety.  I have heard the comments from the First Minister and fully agree that this funding must now be prioritised.  I urge the Finance Minister to bring forward a paper to the Executive so that a decision can be taken without delay.   Securing funding for the PSNI Recovery business case would be game-changing.  It would allow us not only to grow police numbers but to sustain that increase in the years ahead.  Every party in this Assembly has acknowledged that the PSNI is not adequately staffed - the time for action is now.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans his Department has to end the industrial derating policy.
answer_text: ANSWERThere are no plans to end the industrial derating policy which supports over 4,400 manufacturers. We have a strong local manufacturing sector that supports a significant number of jobs within our economy, and long term policy decisions need to align with our economic vision of good jobs, improving productivity, decarbonisation and regional balance.There is already a process in place for all rate support measures to be reviewed as part of the ongoing Strategic Review of Rating, this includes industrial derating. For 2025/26 this work will be concentrating on Small Business Rate Relief and Non-Domestic Vacant Rating in line with the published Terms of Reference.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail which companies are in receipt of a rates exemption through industrial derating, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: ANSWERA list of the companies in receipt of a rates exemption through Industrial Derating, for each of the past five rating years, is included in the spreadsheet in Annex A. A copy has been placed in the Assembly library.  Under the General Data Protection Regulations (GDPR), Annex A does not provide the details of businesses that operate as sole traders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her plans for capital expenditure for 2025-2026.
answer_text: I am keen to get infrastructure right and in taking final decisions on capital allocations, I will seek to ensure that investment in infrastructure supports our society, economy and well-being.This year, my Department received £917m capital with £1.4m committed for June Monitoring. Of this, £367.2m is ring fenced for Flagship, City and Growth Deal projects, EU schemes and RRI borrowing. Flagships schemes include the A5, the A6 and the Belfast Transport Hub.City and Growth Deal schemes include Belfast Rapid Transit, the Enniskillen Bypass, Lagan Bridge, Derry Waterfront, Strabane footbridge and the Newry Southern Relief Road.The general allocation of £549.8m will deliver a range of schemes, however, it will not deliver all that I want to deliver.Although I have not taken final decisions, I intend to allocate funding to roads areas such as: the A1;street lighting; roads structural maintenance; Local Transport Safety Measures, including 20 mile per hour speed limits for schools; and a range of Active Travel schemes. For Public Transport, planned expenditure includes:the Coleraine to Derry track renewal scheme;completion of works related to Zero Emission buses;Network Wide Track Condition Improvements; andcontinued roll out of the Translink Future Ticketing System.NI Water will be funded to make improvements to our wastewater infrastructure to release capacity constraints; and undertake base maintenance on existing assets. NI Water will continue to invest in key projects including the Castor Bay Water Treatment Works to Ballydougan Service Reservoir.There are also plans for various River flood alleviation studies, including Newry, Derry and Antrim and progression of the economic appraisal for Downpatrick and the commencement of the procurement phase for Portadown.I will be setting out my Department’s investment needs as part of future budget exercises to ensure funding is directed to key infrastructure projects to maximise benefit to our communities and generate economic growth.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how long the temporary traffic lights on the B27 at Shannon Park, Hilltown, have been in place; (ii) the amount her Department has spent on the temporary traffic lights; and (iii) how much longer the traffic lights will be in place.
answer_text: The temporary traffic lights on the B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown at Shannon Park, have been in place for approximately 13 months.  The cost information requested is commercially sensitive and disclosure may allow the public, in particular economic competitors, to identify commercial rates/costs for carrying out these activities which may damage the Department’s and contractor’s legitimate economic interests.  At this stage, it is not possible to indicate how long the temporary traffic signals will be required. Specialist structural and geotechnical engineers are working on a proposal for a permanent and lasting solution.  Once a design solution has been developed it will then go through an approval process before works can begin.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department holds a comprehensive list of postcode areas located within designated flood plains.
answer_text: My Department has produced flood hazard and risk mapping for fluvial, tidal and surface water flood sources, and has made it available to the public via the Flood Maps NI web viewer (https://dfini.maps.arcgis.com/apps/webappviewer/index.html?id=fd6c0a01b07840269a50a2f596b3daf6). The extents of the floodplains for all of the flood sources have been estimated using predictive computer modelling techniques that are commonly used by other flood management authorities throughout the UK, Ireland and beyond.Unfortunately, the flood map datasets held by my Department are not broken down by postcode and subsequently my Department does not hold a list of postcodes located within floodplain areas. However, the Flood Maps NI web viewer does have an address search function to allow users to search addresses and postcodes and view the corresponding flood hazard information up to a scale of 1:5,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to enable patients to receive better and more rapid diagnosis of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease through the introduction of the N-Tidal Diagnose device.
answer_text: As part of the work on the Respiratory Improvement Plan 2024 – 2027, the Primary Care Diagnostics Sub Group have developed a position paper on current respiratory diagnostics provision in Primary Care and have made recommendations for a new service model for carrying out diagnostics in Primary Care which include FeNO and Spirometry testing.  There are currently no plans to launch the N-Tidal Diagnose device in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the recommendations of the STRONG-HF trial will be applied in Northern Ireland following in the footsteps of St. George's Hospital, London.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care sector follow recommendations provided by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE), rather than individual trials. NICE recommendations are evidence-based and the recommendations first published in 2018 in relation to chronic heart failure drug treatment are currently being updated with the draft recommendations relating to the timing of treatment. Heart failure services are one of the key priorities of the Department. The Department’s role will be to adhere to the revised recommendations when published. The Department is already taking steps with regards the most effective ways of managing heart failure medication as part of its work with the five Health and Social Care Trusts towards standardising the heart failure pathway across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the budget for smoking cessation services.
answer_text: The total Public Health Agency baseline investment for Tobacco Control is £3,930,604 per annum.  Since 2011/12, the PHA has typically spent between 71 – 78% of its tobacco control budget on specialist smoking cessation services (which are demand-led) and the associated use of Nicotine Replacement Therapy (NRT), which aims to encourage smokers to quit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how much has been spent on the operation of health service-run GP surgeries in each of the last five years, broken down by individual practice.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 15081/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline what reporting mechanisms are in place for staff to report bullying within each Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) to detail how many times instances of bullying have been reported in each of the last ten years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i) The HSC Regional Conflict, Bullying and Harassment Policy was developed and agreed in 2019.   Following review, a revised policy was agreed by HSC Employers and Trade Unions and published in November 2023.  All HSC organisations are required to adopt the policyI have emphasised publicly the need to ensure that the overall culture of the entire HSC system is welcoming for all those who work there. Every employee with concerns about bullying or other unacceptable behaviours must be properly listened to and supported.Each HSC organisation’s conflict, bullying and harassment policy outlines mechanisms for staff to report instances of conflict, bullying and/or harassment. The general principles of the regional policy are:Staff members can report instances of conflict, bullying and/or harassment to their line management in the first instance. If a staff members concern/complaint relates to their line manager, they should report their concern/complaint to the next management level.Management (seeking HR advice when necessary) will explore if the concern/complaint is appropriate for informal resolution. Where appropriate, this will be the first channel explored. Where informal resolution is not appropriate or is unsuccessful, further consideration will be given to more robust action such as mediation or a formal investigation (in consultation with HR).(ii) The number of cases of bullying in the last ten years is shown in the table below;YearBHSCT^NHSCT+NIAS+SEHSCT+SHSCT+WHSCTTotal2015/16*227**12232016/17*651**26382017/18*837**11292018/19*348**9242019/20*328**2***152020/215423150652021/226827951922022/23774711301022023/246014821762024/2558322422***91Total3173439621984555^ Cases include those being addressed both informally and formally by the Belfast Trust.* The current Employee Relations Tracker system used by the Belfast Trust was introduced in May 2020, as such it is not possible for the Trust to report accurately on information that pre-dates this.**The Southern Trust have used an Employee Relations tracker system since 2020.  Reliable data prior to 2020 as requested is unavailable.*** Following the launch of the Western Trust’s Conflict, Bullying and Harassment in the Workplace Policy in April 2019 (since superseded by the current Conflict, Bullying and Harassment Policy October 2023), only cases requiring formal investigation of Bullying/Harassment were recorded by the Trust. Effective 1 April 2024 the Trust began recording all reports of Conflict, Bullying and Harassment.+ The figures for NHSCT, NIAS, SEHSCT and SHSCT include formal cases only.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the UK Government's Spending Review 2025.
answer_text: The agreement I reached with the Treasury has resulted in an additional £600 million over the Spending Review period. This brings the total of additional provided funding provided through successful negotiations by my predecessor and I with the Treasury to £1.3bn for public services here. This means the cliff edge I have previously warned about has been avoided.However, the Spending Review itself was disappointing in that it did not provide the economic stimulus it could have. While in accountancy terms we have received additional Barnett funding of £3.1 billion in day-to-day funding over three years it must be viewed in the context of the many growing demands and the increased costs of delivering public services.The reality is the Executive will still remain in a financially constrained position particularly in 2026-27 with the majority of the funding not coming until future years.It is clear that significant challenges remain for our public services.While we will see more capital funding there was the potential to be more ambitious and spur economic growth.That said, we must now focus on the future. I will work together with my Ministerial colleagues to develop a multi-year budget – this will enable the Executive to plan on a longer-term basis.I have been clear that I want to create the conditions and circumstances for transformation and develop a multi-year budget that supports families, workers and communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many times in (i) 2024; and (ii) 2025 did he release a statement to the press before Members.
answer_text: While communicating with the public is vitally important, I seek to avoid making statements on major policy decisions to the media before informing the Northern Ireland Assembly. My approach is to issue Oral or Written Ministerial Statements, as per Assembly process (Standing Orders 18a and 18b). Written Ministerial Statements are circulated to MLAs a minimum of 2.5 hours before publication. In the case of an Oral Statement, I always endeavour to ensure that a written copy of the statement is made available to Assembly Members as early as possible, and in any event at least 30 minutes before delivering the statement in the Assembly.On occasion, journalists may ask questions during media interviews and I will indicate my intention to publicly announce a decision or planned course of action in due course, after I have followed Assembly process by informing the Northern Ireland Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Public Health Agency will commission an independent review in smoking cessation services (i) to establish why service uptake has reduced so steeply; and (iI) to consider how delivery models could be redesigned to increase demand.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) is undertaking an internal review of Stop Smoking Services delivered in HSC Trust and pharmacy settings. In partnership with the HSC Trusts, the PHA is reviewing the various service delivery models for Stop Smoking Services across all five Trusts. Managers and staff who deliver these services have been involved in workshop sessions to identify what works well and consider areas for improvement, with a view to updating and improving all services in line with recently revised NICE guidance and current good practice.In the pharmacy setting, an internal review is taking place, under the guidance of the PHA and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), with involvement from Community Pharmacy NI and the Department of Health. This review is looking at the reach of existing services into areas of high deprivation, value for money, service quality and service quit rates. It is also considering scope for more workforce training and development to increase the capacity of local pharmacies to support clients to stop smoking. An independent review has not been commissioned at this stage.A key factor in the reduced footfall to Stop Smoking Services across Northern Ireland can be attributed to the success of the Tobacco Control Strategy in Northern Ireland and the work of partners to implement this. Smoking prevalence rates in NI have fallen from 24% to 13% and fewer smokers’ results in reduced numbers seeking to access cessation services. During the Covid-19 pandemic, footfall to Stop Smoking Services was heavily impacted by the inability of providers to deliver face to face services, particularly in the pharmacy setting, where the services were stood down to focus on testing and vaccinations.  Efforts have been ongoing over the past few years to re-instate these services.  The decline in numbers accessing cessation services is not unique to NI and this trend long precedes the Covid-19 pandemic. Service reach in NI continues to compare favourably with other UK countries.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has immediate plans to close Tollymore National Outdoor Centre.
answer_text: Responsibility for the management of Tollymore National Outdoor Centre lies with Sport NI.As this issue is an operational matter for Sport NI I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“During Storm Eowyn in January, the site at Tollymore was badly impacted with damage to trees, trails and other on-site outdoor facilities. Whilst Sport NI has been restoring the site for safe use by the public, it has also been looking at its operations at the Centre. During that period following the storm, it has emerged there are challenges relating to how the Centre has been operating, as well as a very significant annual financial subvention from Sport NI in relation to the costs of the Centre. Later this month, the Sport NI Board will consider whether the current temporary cessation should be continued. This would allow Sport NI to undertake a Strategic Review on how it can best meet the needs of, and support the outdoors sector, and reimagine how the Centre will play its part in that effort.There are no plans to close TNOC on a permanent basis”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to emergency mental health departments, to detail (i) how many there are; (ii) the location; (iii) the staffing compliment required; and (iv) the staffing compliment in place in each of the past ten years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There are no dedicated emergency mental health departments within Trust Areas. Emergency Departments receive, assess and treat all emergency clinical presentations, inclusive of those related to mental illness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the recent £15 million investment for projects at Warrenpoint Wastewater Treatment Works will support the redevelopment at Warrenpoint Baths.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised my department that the £15m investment in Warrenpoint Wastewater Treatment Works does not currently support the redevelopment at Warrenpoint Baths as no such application has, to date, been received.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of whole time equivalent speech and language therapists funded to work in (i) acute care; and (ii) community teams, broken down by the stroke service within each Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many Assembly Written Questions are unanswered by his Department.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of whole time equivalent speech and language therapists in the (i) North; and (ii) South, teams within the Western Health and Social Care Trust, broken down by how many are (a) vacant; and (b) filled.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Irish Street development scheme in Downpatrick.
answer_text: The Irish Street Development Scheme is a high priority strategic project for my Department, and we are seeking to develop the site as a commercially led, sustainable urban regeneration project.  A previous attempt to take forward the scheme failed due to a lack of private sector interest.  In order to boost the appeal of the site and improve the prospects of the scheme being successful this time, my officials developed a Strategic Masterplan for the site.  They have also overseen the vesting and careful dismantlement of a derelict adjacent property, which was proving an obstacle to the scheme.  This site has now been included within the development boundary. My officials are currently preparing a business case to assess the support needed to secure a preferred developer for the Irish St Development Site.  Subject to a positive business case, and the necessary approvals being in place, a development brief for the site will launch later in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what (i) financial; or (ii) practical support is available to cycling clubs or individual cyclists for the purchase of safety equipment, such as on-bike cameras or visibility aids.
answer_text: My department does not provide direct financial support to cycling clubs or individual cyclists for the purchase of safety equipment such as on-bike cameras or visibility aids.My department provides funding support to councils for the delivery of greenways and other active travel enabling initiatives and works in partnership with schools on the delivery of practical road safety and active travel programmes such as child pedestrian road safety training and the Cycling Proficiency Scheme. My department and the Public Health Agency also jointly fund the Active School Travel programme which is delivered by Sustrans. More widely, some employers operate the Cycle to Work scheme, an employee benefit scheme which covers the purchase of bicycles and qualifying cycling safety equipment. Safety equipment can be purchased on its own without the need to purchase a bicycle.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether her Department has conducted any recent assessments of driver behaviour towards vulnerable road users, including cyclists; and (ii) what actions are being taken to address reported increases in aggression.
answer_text: i) My Department's Analysis, Statistics and Research Branch (ASRB) has completed problem profiles on cyclists (2014-2018) and pedestrians (2019-2023).  The reports focus on casualties and those responsible and have been used to inform development of the Road Safety Strategy to 2030 and its accompanying action plan. ii) any actions to address any increased incidents of aggression would be a matter for the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will work in coordination with the Department of Justice and the PSNI to ensure that incidents of dangerous driving or intimidation towards vulnerable road users are appropriately investigated, prosecuted, and monitored.
answer_text: My Department will continue to work in coordination with the Department of Justice and the PSNI to ensure that any evidence it holds relating to alleged incidents of dangerous driving or intimidation towards vulnerable road users is made available to PSNI in accordance with legal requirements. In addition, Driver and Vehicle Agency enforcement officers will continue to report incidents of dangerous driving observed during roadside enforcement operations to PSNI and examine poorly maintained vehicles to determine if they have been used in a dangerous condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the annual expenditure on road safety education and awareness campaigns, in each of the last five years; and (ii) to outline how such campaigns are assessed for effectiveness in reaching all categories of road users.
answer_text: (i) Annual Expenditure on Road Safety Campaigns:2020/21	£1,387,647.892021/22	£1,472,397.092022/23	-2023/24	£476,000.002025/25	£535,000.00(ii) Evaluation of Campaign Effectiveness:My Department’s road safety initiatives are designed to reduce fatalities and serious injuries on our roads. All campaigns are grounded in evidence-based research, focusing on road safety challenges, public attitudes, and strategies to influence unsafe behavioursMy officials identify the most cost-effective ways of promoting road safety and improving road user attitudes and behaviours. My Department’s road safety campaigns are data-led, research-led and psychology-led to target the most at-risk cohorts and address the most dangerous behaviours.My Department continually adapts its communication strategies, using a mix of traditional and digital platforms, including, streaming services like Netflix, to reach a broader and younger audience.PSNI road traffic collision data is used to identify the key road safety issues and identify the main road user groups by age and gender who are responsible. This information is used to identify target audiences for campaigns. My Department has engaged a professional advertising agency, who use specialised tools to identify what groups by age and gender are watching or listening to which programmes on which platform. This enables us to ensure campaigns to reach the people they are designed to influence. Campaign effectiveness is measured through awareness and influence metrics. Campaigns have achieved awareness levels above 90%, compared to an industry average of 50% for TV advertising. Influence levels have ranged from 72% to 93%, higher than the industry norm of 30% for being ‘fairly influenced’. This gives us some confidence that the campaigns are effective.While it is difficult to measure the sole or unique contribution that any specific area of road safety initiative makes towards reducing casualties, the combined effect of education, enforcement, penalties, and engineering is recognised worldwide as collectively contributing to improved road safety outcomes.
