question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what engagement has taken place between her Department and local cycling clubs to improve road safety and promote mutual awareness among all road users, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My department does not undertake direct engagement with individual local cycling clubs. However, officials regularly engage with key active travel advocacy organisations such as Sustrans and Cycling UK on a range of active travel related matters.The department also holds public consultations on a wide range of areas which cycling stakeholders are encouraged to participate in.  For example, the Roads Safety Strategy consultation outcome report is available at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/consultation-outcome-report-final.pdf. More widely, my department uses social media platforms to promote road safety messaging, primarily through ShareTheRoadToZero.com, a road safety community programme. Subscribers to ShareTheRoadToZero.com receive a weekly newsletter with road safety advice. Road safety advice is also issued via associated social media platforms @ShareTheRoadToZero on Facebook and @roadtozero for X, (previously Twitter).Share the Road to Zero aims to reduce road deaths by encouraging all of us to think about our shared responsibilities, to be aware of our surroundings, and take more care when out and about.  Social media messages are targeted towards at risk groups and focus on the main causations of collisions resulting in fatalities and serious injury for drivers, such as careless driving and speeding. We also have campaigns which are targeted towards novice drivers and young driver distraction, in addition to seatbelt wearing, and anti-drink and drug driving. Campaigns which focus on vulnerable road users such as pedestrians, cyclists and motorcyclists are also regularly issued on our social media channels. My department has two specific campaigns targeting cyclist safety which are regularly shared through Share the Road to Zero social media channels and these can be viewed https://www.sharetheroadtozero.com/TV. These campaigns focus on blind spots, signalling, space and junctions. Share the Road to Zero also issues regular supplementary social media messages regarding cyclist safety including messages direct from the highway code and advice on cycle lanes. These messages attract a significant amount of engagement and interaction from the public.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when the online reporting form for waste management incidents will go live.
answer_text: My officials are continuing with the process of developing a new Incident Management System. A date for the launch of this new system is not yet available. It is, however, still anticipated that this will be ready to go live during 2025. In the meantime, waste management issues can be reported via the NIEAwastecomplaint@daera-ni.gov.uk mailbox.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 13444/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 25792/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the comments by the PSNI Chief Constable at the meeting of the Northern Ireland Policing Board on 8 May that legacy remains one of the most significant barriers to achieving a representative police service and to rebuilding trust and confidence in policing.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of each occasion when the Warrenpoint Wastewater Treatment Works released effluent into Carlingford Lough, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:	Warrenpoint wastewater treatment works (WwTW) receives wastewater from connected properties in the wider Warrenpoint catchment and treats the incoming wastewater prior to discharging the treated effluent to a consented discharge point in the vicinity of Narrow Water, into the Newry Estuary waterbody, which flows into Carlingford Lough.  The treated effluent is discharged on a continual basis from Warrenpoint WwTW.  The quality of effluent is monitored as per regulatory requirements and has been assessed as compliant in each of the past 5 years. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their engagement with the UK Government regarding a response to the ‘Restoring Control over the Immigration System’ white paper.
answer_text: The deputy First Minister and Junior Minister Reilly attended an Inter-Ministerial Group meeting where the immigration white paper was discussed. They raised key local issues and highlighted local needs. The Executive Office have also issued written correspondence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the reform of the allocation of fishing licences for Strangford Lough.
answer_text: Fisheries Management Plans (FMP) are evidence-based plans designed to ensure the future sustainability of fish stocks in our waters. In accordance with the Fisheries Act 2020 and the Joint Fisheries Statement my department is responsible for the preparation and publication of 4 FMPs including the Northern Ireland Non-Quota Shellfish FMP. The scope of this FMP includes species such as scallops, lobsters, brown crabs and velvet crabs in Northern Ireland waters, including in Strangford Lough.My department is continuing to draft this FMP and is reviewing existing management measures, available scientific evidence and stock assessments to develop goals and objectives for sustainable fishing for these species. Determining sustainable levels of fishing effort in Northern Ireland’s waters is essential in delivering an effective FMP for these non-quota species. My department intends to consult on this draft FMP later this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline (i) why Warrenpoint was not a designated bathing water site until 2025; and (ii) the process by which it was identified as a candidate.
answer_text: My Department carried out a full review of Bathing Waters in Northern Ireland in 2022/23, that included a public consultation.  Up until this point, Northern Ireland has had 26 identified bathing waters.  The formal process has involved a public consultation, an assessment of site suitability against agreed specific selection criteria that covered public usage, health and safety and a willing bathing water operator prepared to cover site management.  This is then followed by a formal legislative process.		During the 2022/23 review, Warrenpoint was nominated as a potential new bathing site and was subsequently assessed. Newry, Mourne and Down District Council agreed to take on the bathing water operator role and the site met the other criteria.  		The formal identification of Warrenpoint in the legislation will be completed on 31 May 2025, through The Quality of Bathing Waters (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025. 		You may wish to note that while this process was being undertaken, Warrenpoint, along with six other candidate sites, was monitored in the same way as the existing 26 sites, and water quality information was made public through the DAERA website, NI Direct and also by posters display on site.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 24445/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of Ulster University’s Economic Policy Centre's May 2025 report entitled Cost of Doing Business Impact Report, specifically in relation to the findings on the future provision of NHS dentistry.
answer_text: I am aware of the challenges facing General Dental Services, including those outlined in the report, and action is being taken to address or mitigate against the issues where possible.My Department has invested significant funding into support for General Dental Services in the past several years, with schemes aimed at supporting practices in provision of Health Service dentistry with increased funding for high priority treatments, and increased access, in particular for vulnerable patient groups including unregistered adults and children. To assist with the increase to National Insurance contributions, £2.5million has been made available for General Dental Services.I am currently considering investment options for 2025/26 to continue supporting the industry until reform can be progressed. However, decisions are having to be made in face of a projected funding gap of £500m for my Department.Longer term, my Department’s Health and Social Care NI three-year plan includes a commitment to develop an action plan and programme of work for the reform of General Dental Services by April 2027.The first step on this path is to determine the real cost of providing dental services so that realistic options for reform of the service can be developed.To achieve that I have approved the commissioning of a cost-of-service review to be completed in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 26271/22-27, to provide a breakdown of the capital pressures which the £5.3 million was used for.
answer_text: From its initial 2024/25 budget allocation my department prioritised both capital and resource DEL for flood support. Due to the demand led nature of the scheme, £0.6m of RDEL and £2.6 million of CDEL was reallocated within the department as part of the normal budget process in 2024/25.  The £2.6 million Capital DEL was reallocated to Higher Education Research.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the process by which his Department (i) conducts investigations into blue green algae; and (ii) determines whether the algae is toxic.
answer_text: My Department has led in the development of an Inter-Agency Blue Green Algae Monitoring Protocol which was published in May 2024. Although this was developed primarily as part of the Lough Neagh Action Plan, it can be used anywhere in Northern Ireland where blue green algae develop.  The other agencies involved are the Agri-Food & Biosciences Institute (AFBI), NI Water, Food Standard Agency and the Public Health Agency. The Protocol presents five categorisations of surface waters, with concerns at bathing waters representing the highest risk in terms of public health.  It then details the monitoring approach for each water type, the use of appropriate thresholds for providing public advice, and a tiered approach to monitoring which is increased in the event of a bloom event. Reports of suspected blue green algae blooms are submitted to NIEA via the Bloomin’ Algae App and/or via the NIEA Operations Room for a visual assessment by photo or if it is not possible to confirm by photo, a qualitative assessment from samples based on taxonomy is carried out. Toxicity testing is carried out on samples lifted at identified bathing waters by an accredited laboratory. DAERA Marine and Fisheries Division is also trialling a rapid screen using a technique called ELISA (enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay) analysis.  More guidance can be found in the Inter Agency Protocol located on the DAERA website www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/inter-agency-blue-green-algae-monitoring-protocol
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of times his Department has instructed that notices be installed to advise of the presence of blue green algae in Castlewellan Forest Park in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Responsibility for the installation of signage advising about the presence of blue green algae rests with the landowner and/or operator of the site. Prior to March 2022, DAERA Forest Service were responsible for public access management of Castlewellan Lake and from that date, the recreation management transferred to Newry Mourne and Down Council, including the lake.  During the last 5 years, NIEA confirmed the presence of blue green algae to the appropriate operator on 15 occasions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to a waste management inspection report, under which category would the condition of bales be included.
answer_text: In a waste management inspection report, the condition of bales may be classified under the categories of Specified Operations, Storage of Specified Wastes, Potentially Polluting Leaks and Spillages, or Control of Litter. Authorised officers will exercise their judgement in determining and recording their assessment of licence conditions within the appropriate category.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what constitutes damaged bales in terms of waste management licences.
answer_text: In terms of waste management licences various types of waste can be stored in bale format i.e. paper, cardboard, plastic, packaging, metal, tyres, clothing/textiles, general waste. Damaged waste bales may include bales torn, punctured, crushed, compromised, or deteriorated due to exposure to the elements.

These consultants deliver care within Belfast Trust but also provide support to patients in both Northern and Southern Trust.Southern Trust has two consultant neurologists with an interest in Multiple SclerosisSouth Eastern Trust has one consultant neurologist with an interest in Multiple SclerosisPatients with MS in the Northern and Western Trust are supported by the Belfast clinical team.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the unspent and returned £5.3 million in flood support funding has been re-allocated, including a breakdown of any funding allocation.
answer_text: Whilst Conor Murphy agreed to allocate up to £10 million from his 2024/25 budget to deliver three schemes, the cost of delivering the schemes was lower than anticipated.  There has, however, been no flood support funding returned to the Department by local councils.  Adequate funding remains with the councils to allow them to pay grants to eligible businesses up to the value of their letters of offer providing the businesses submit the required documentation within the specified timeframes.When it became clear that the cost of delivering the schemes would be lower than anticipated, Minister Murphy asked officials to explore options for utilising any unused funding, including options to transfer funding to other Departments for flood related spend, but given the time frame available, no realistic options were identified.  Any unspent money was reallocated across the Department of Economy to meet other capital pressures.DfE officials are currently exploring options for implementing a more permanent flood support scheme for businesses.  A cross departmental group is also being established to explore options for future flood schemes to support recovery and resilience for all those impacted by flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) structures; and (ii) processes (a) he has established; and (b) he will establish, to drive the transformation agenda for maternity and neonatal care and services.
answer_text: I recently announced that I will be establishing a Maternal and Newborn Partnership to take forward a regional action plan for improving maternity and neonatal services. Plans for the establishment of the Partnership are currently under development. A comprehensive Regional Action Plan for Maternity and Neonatal Services is being developed, which consolidates all the actions / recommendations from the suite of work on maternity and neonatal safety that has been taken forward by the Maternity and Neonatal Services Safety Oversight Group, which includes – Professor Renfrew’s report, Enabling Safe, Quality Midwifery Services and Care in Northern Ireland; anda gap analysis of –the implementation of all relevant NICE guidance;reports developed or endorsed by the Department, including the RQIA’s 2023 report, Review of Governance arrangements in place to support safety within Maternity Services in Northern Ireland;learning from relevant SAIs; andother relevant reports, which whilst not endorsed by the Department, would have important points of learning.This is a significant programme of work that will require regional co-ordination and oversight.  It is proposed that there will a governance structure developed which will put in place arrangements for monitoring and improving the care provided to individuals as well developing strategic and regional direction to inform the commissioning of services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment that has been made of the funding required to deliver his Department's plans for the transformation of maternity and neonatal care and services, including the findings of any assessment; and (ii) the extent to which additional money has been earmarked to meet the funding need.
answer_text: It is clear that there will be a funding requirement associated with the full implementation of the regional action plan for improving maternity and neonatal services, however while it is not possible to allocate additional funding due to the significant funding deficit my department faces, my officials are working closely with the Public Health Agency and Trusts to improve services within existing resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department will amend the required criteria for flood support to enable organisations such as Downpatrick and County Down Railway to avail of financial support, following the floods in September 2023.
answer_text: The flood support schemes have closed. Newry Mourne Down District Council have informed departmental officials that Downpatrick and County Down Railway applied to the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme (EFSS) and subsequently received a Letter of Offer (LoO) for £100k.  To date, Downpatrick and County Down Railway have claimed £95,638.93 of funding and have confirmed that they do not intend to submit a claim for the remaining balance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the JobStart 50+ scheme that began in September 2024.
answer_text: The JobStart 50+ Pilot Scheme commenced in July 2024 and closed to new participants end of September 2024.  On 31 March 2025 the Scheme fully closed when the last participants completed their JobStart 50+ 6-month opportunity. I am pleased to advise that 83 (52%) participants moved into sustained employment either with their JobStart employer or with an alternative employer following their participation on the scheme.  An independent final evaluation of the JobStart 50+ pilot scheme is expected at the end of July 2025. The JobStart 50+ Scheme was funded by HM Treasury’s Labour Market Evaluations and Pilots fund (LMEP) as a test and learn pilot scheme. Unfortunately, budget for this Treasury funded pilot programme has not been recurrent in 2025-26. I am in the process of agreeing my Department’s 2025-26 Budget allocations. In allocating my Budget, one of my priorities is employment support, to help people of all ages to overcome barriers faced in moving into, retaining and progressing in employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the total amount of flood support funding returned to local councils; and (ii) whether she will redirect the underspend to those businesses still affected and yet to receive financial support.

Adequate funding remains with the councils to allow them to pay grants to eligible businesses up to the value of their letters of offer providing the businesses submit the required documentation within the specified timeframes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 24015/22-27, to detail (i) what planting of non-native trees has been undertaken in Donard Wood; and (ii) what species have been planted.
answer_text: Forest Service do not hold planting records of species planted in the establishment of Donard forest when the lease commenced in 1924.  However, the forest management plans do reflect the current species composition, including trees that remain from the initial planting and areas re-established following harvesting. The forest management plans for Donard forest are to transition the forest to a continuous cover system, which includes regeneration of native species.Currently the composition of native species in the forest is approximately 30%, or 90 hectares, of the forest area comprising of Scots pine, including in broadleaf mixtures, planted in its initial establishment. An equal ratio of non-native and native tree species has been planted over past 20 years following harvesting and re-establishing of approximately 50 hectares.The non-native species that currently constitute 150 hectares of the forest area, include Sweet Chestnut, Beech, Western Hemlock, other pine, spruce, larch, fir species.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the health risk posed to individuals from bathing in water bodies with high levels of (i) Escherichia Coli; and (ii) Intestinal Enterococci.
answer_text: Escherichia Coli (E. coli) are a group of organisms found in the intestines of animals and humans and survive for a while in the environment when excreted. Many types of E. coli are not harmful but some, such as E. coli 0157, can directly cause illness. Intestinal Enterococci are found in the intestines of animals and humans although some types are environmental in origin. The presence of these bacteria indicates that human or animal faeces may be present in bathing water and could contain other potentially harmful organisms that could cause illness.Higher levels of these bacteria can increase the risk of illness to bathers, such as gastrointestinal illnesses, skin rashes and ear/eye infections.Concentrations of these bacteria are used to classify bathing waters into one of four classifications – excellent, good, sufficient or poor (where advice will be issued against bathing). The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) is responsible for monitoring bathing waters during the bathing water season which runs from 1 June to 15 September. Within the sampling programme, when a higher bacterial count is detected, DAERA officials work with the Bathing Water Operator to post ‘advice against bathing’ at the site. This is to allow for resampling and investigation into the source of the pollution. The advice is updated within 24 hours.DAERA also gives general advice about not bathing for 48 hours after heavy rainfall. All data is posted on both the DAERA and NI Direct websites to enable bathers to make informed choices about the sites that are more vulnerable to pollution in wet weather.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 24013/22-27, to detail the most recent findings for bathing water quality in Carlingford Lough.
answer_text: Carlingford Lough has two bathing water locations, one identified site at Cranfield and another candidate site at Warrenpoint.  The most recent classifications after the 2024 bathing season are: Cranfield – Excellent; and Warrenpoint - Sufficient.  The classification at Warrenpoint is preliminary in that it is based on 2 years of data, rather than the recommended 4 years.Current and historic findings for all bathing water sites are provided on the DAERA and NI Direct websites.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 24013/22-27, to detail the last time the nutrient levels for Carlingford Lough met Good Status or higher.
answer_text: The nutrient (dissolved inorganic nitrogen (DIN)) status of Carlingford Lough has been at less than good (i.e. moderate) status throughout all the monitoring cycles as set out in the Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017.  Data indicates that at the end of the first (2009-2015) cycle, 2015 DIN values just failed to meet good status. Since then, however, DIN values have shown a steady and significant increase i.e. a deterioration in quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 24013/22-27, to detail (i) what contaminants are monitored for in Carlingford Lough; and (ii) which are meeting Good Status.
answer_text: Over 20 Specific Pollutants have been identified that serve as a supporting quality element in the assessment of Ecological Status. Some 18 of these substances have been monitored over the past reporting cycles. These substances include: 2,4-Dichlorophenol, 2,4-Dichlorophenoxyacetic acid, 3,4-Dichloroaniline, Ammonia (un-ionised), Arsenic, Chromium (VI), Copper, Diazinon, Dimethoate, Glyphosate, Iron, Linuron, Mecoprop, Permethrin, Phenol, Toluene, Triclosan, and Zinc. All substances monitored have met the standard, as set out in The Water Framework Directive (Classification, Priority Substances and Shellfish Waters) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015. Some 55 Priority Substances contribute to the assessment of water body Chemical Status.  Monitoring includes water and biota (shellfish).  To date, 45 substances have been monitored in Carlingford Lough. These substances include: 1,2-Dichloroethane, Aclonifen, Alachlor, Anthracene, Atrazine, Benzene, Bifenox, Brominated diphenylethers, Cadmium and its compounds, Carbon tetrachloride, Chlorfenvinphos, Chlorpyrifos, Cyclodiene Pesticides, Cypermethrin, DDT (total), DDT (pp-DDT), Di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate, Dichlorvos, Dicofol, Diuron, Endosulphan, Fluoranthene, Heptachlor and Heptachlor Epoxide, Hexachlorobenzene, Hexachlorobutadiene, Hexachlorocyclohexane, Isoproturon, Lead and its compounds, Mercury and its compounds, Naphthalene, Nickel and its compounds, Nonylphenol, Octylphenol, PAH's (Benzo[a]pyrene), PAH's (Benzo[b]fluoranthene), PAH's (Benzo[g,h,i]perylene), PAH's (Benzo[k]fluoranthene), Pentachlorobenzene, Pentachlorophenol, Quinoxyfen, Simazine, Trichlorobenzenes, Trichloroethylene, Trichloromethane, and Trifluralin.  In terms of water, monitoring results indicate failures for the pesticide cypermethrin.  Failures in shellfish were reported for brominated diphenylethers and mercury; both compounds are considered ubiquitous, persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic (uPBT) legacy chemicals, which are causing widespread failures in Northern Ireland water bodies and beyond.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 24013/22-27, to detail the additional four sites that are sampled between November to February.
answer_text: The four sites that are monitored from November to February in Carlingford Lough extend from the mouth of the Lough near Cranfield Bay to Warrenpoint. The sites are approximately equidistant apart and are fixed. The co-ordinates for these sites are: CL1: 54.091 N; 6.244 WCL2: 54.072 N; 6.192 WCL3: 54.046 N; 6.141 WCL4: 54.031 N; 6.093 W
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 24013/22-27, to detail (i) the location of the representative site within Carlingford Lough; and (ii) whether this site changes.
answer_text: The representative site that is monitored on a monthly basis is located at Killowen Point; this site is fixed and does not change.  The co-ordinates for this site are: 54.075 N; 6.183 W.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 24013/22-27, (i) whether the findings of the sampling of Carlingford Lough are shared with the Irish Government; and (ii) whether such information sharing is reciprocated.
answer_text: The Department is responsible for monitoring the international water bodies of Carlingford Lough and the Newry Estuary under the The Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017 and the Water Framework Directive (Classification, Priority Substances and Shellfish Waters) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015.  The results are made publicly available once published as official statistics by the Department.  The Department also undertakes collaborative monitoring with organisations within the Republic of Ireland such as the Environmental Protection Agency and Inland Fisheries Ireland.  The marine environment is an area of joint collaboration through the North South Ministerial Council of Ministers as part of the wider Environment Sector.  Both information and approaches are shared through this forum.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any limitations on the work that can be undertaken within a 24-hour period by surgeons working in (i) Health and Social Care Trusts; (ii) private healthcare; and (iii) hybrid working between Health and Social Care Trusts and private healthcare.

To ask the Minister of Health whether the approach taken by his Department to make healthcare intervention technology available is equivalent to that taken in NHS England, as prescribed by the Health and Social Care Act 2012.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations in accordance with NICE criteria and the process outlined in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdfThis link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the (i) commissioning; (ii) implementation; and (iii) any obstacles in delivering the MRI scanner at the Downe Hospital.
answer_text: (i) 	The Department continues to work closely with the Trust to commission an MRI scanner at the Downe Hospital as soon as possible, The proposed service requires capital and recurrent funding not yet identified.(ii)	The implementation of an MRI service at the Downe Hospital is dependent on the availability of capital funding as well as the submission and approval of a business case with associated funding approvals.(iii)	The South Eastern HSC Trust remains committed to the installation of a new MRI Scanner in the Downe Hospital and this is a capital priority for the organisation. The primary obstacle to this service, however, is the absence of confirmed capital and revenue funding streams.  The forthcoming Department of Health’s Regional Capital Plan, will prioritise capital developments across the HSC in the next 3 years and identify when funding is available to commence this project.  Once the prioritisation of this project is known, a revised Business Case will be prepared by the Trust for consideration by the Department.The draft Budget for 2025-26 can be accessed at Draft Budget 2025-26 Consultation | Department of Finance. The Department’s proposed capital allocation for 2025/26 is £391 million.  This is less than what is required to fund committed projects, critical ICT and to maintain existing services. It does not provide the funding required to commence work on new projects that are deemed essential to protect health services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the efforts made by his Department to (a) collate; and (b) review data of diabetes prevalence; and (ii) whether this in line with the rest of the UK.
answer_text: As part of the Diabetes Programme of Care Workforce Review, led by the Public Health Agency, commissioned by the Department of Health, data on diabetes prevalence was collated and reviewed.While the workforce requirements have been modelled for delivery of diabetes treatment and care services today, the review is making recommendations and future planning requirements, that will inform workforce requirements 5-10 years hence.  The workforce review is near completion and one of the recommendations is the development of an action plan for implementation.Similar to the rest of the UK, prevalence of diabetes within Northern Ireland is growing year on year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail each time the Diabetes Network met in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Diabetes Network brings together people living with diabetes, carers, and health and social care professionals working in partnership with Diabetes UK on the design and delivery of better diabetes services.The dates of the Diabetes Network Board meetings took place as follows:17th April 2019;6th June 2019;9th September 2019;5th March 2020;2nd October 2020;2nd December 2020;21st April 2021;30th June 2021;1st October 2021;8th December 2021;18th March 2022;15th June 2022;14th September 2022;14th December 2022;15th March 2023;13th September 2023;13th December 2023;13th March 2024; and18th February 2025.In addition to the Diabetes Network Board meetings, the Diabetes Network also held various sub groups meetings, task and finish group meetings and workshops over the past five years to support the implementation of the Diabetes Strategic Framework and the development of the Regional Integrated Pathway for Diabetes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on any review of the efficacy of the Diabetes Strategic Framework ahead of its end date in 2026.
answer_text: The NI Diabetes Network is one of a series of initiatives to support the ‘Health and Well-being 2026, Delivering Together’ plan and was established to support the implementation of the DoH’s Diabetes Strategic Framework, launched in November 2016.The Diabetes Strategic Framework, alongside NI Audit Office Type 2 Diabetes Prevention and Care (2018) recommendations, is the cornerstone of the Diabetes Network’s activity which is set out in the Strategic Programme Plan (2021-2026) and Annual Operational Plan, year on year.Working in partnership with stakeholders, including Public Health Agency, Diabetes UK and People Living with Diabetes, the Network has enabled development and progress in the treatment and care of People Living with Diabetes through regional service provision across a number of areas. This has improved access, equity of provision and better outcomes for People Living with Diabetes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the 2016 to 2026 Diabetes Strategic Framework, including the actions that have (i) been implemented; and (ii) yet to be implemented.
answer_text: The Diabetes Network was established in 2016 to support the implementation of the Department of Health’s (DoH) Diabetes Strategic Framework.  The work of the NI Diabetes Network is taken forward through workstreams, many of which have supporting subgroups and task and finish groups, which have clinical, service user and Diabetes UK involvement. Annual actions and planned progress are detailed within the Operational Plan. Many of the actions within the Diabetes Strategic Framework have been completed through the implementation of the Regional Diabetes Footcare pathway, the Regional Insulin Pump Service, the Regional Integrated pathway for diabetes and Diabetes Programme of Care workforce review, which is nearing completion.  As with any ambitious programme of work, there are some actions to be fully implemented. Many of these are resource dependant and impacted by other factors such as workforce, technology and finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the latest information she has received in relation to whether Treasury reserve funding will be provided to the PSNI to mitigate its costs for claims arising from the data breach in August 2023.
To ask the Minister of Health why fetal valproate syndrome does not have a regional clinical coding.
answer_text: Within the World Health Organization’s (WHO) ICD-10 5th edition International Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems there is no specific code to identify Fetal Valproate Syndrome and there is no UK Clinical Coding Standard or Guidance published to direct clinical coders to an appropriate code. In practice, this can be clinically coded to Q86.8 - Other Congenital Malformation Syndromes Due to Known Exogenous Causes, although doing so is not specific to Fetal Valproate Syndrome alone as other similar syndromes could be classified to this code. The ICD-10 classification groups link entities together and its purpose is for statistical analysis and therefore does not provide specific codes for every condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of conditions carry a regional clinical coding.
answer_text: The ICD-10 5th edition International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems is a variable-axis classification. It contains 22 chapters which are divided into the following three types: Special group chapters; Body System Chapters and Other Chapters.  Within these 22 chapters are thousands of codes which represent various diseases, syndromes, symptoms and injuries.  These codes can be sequenced following specific rules and conventions of the classification or adherence to UK & NI Clinical Coding Standards which expands the capacity of the classification to capture more diagnoses.  It is impossible to provide a definitive number of conditions that could have an ICD-10 code(s) assigned.Not every diagnosis will have a specific code and where there is a diagnosis that does not have a specific code the use of .8 within an appropriate category will allow it to be coded as the classification groups entities that are alike together but it will not allow the identification of a specific diagnosis.For the year 23-24 there was a total of 4,237,220 ICD-10 codes assigned to 765,255 episodes of acute inpatient and day case activity across all health and social care trusts, for activity which was recorded on the Health & Social Care Patient Administration System.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 22993/22-27, to detail whether palliative care services for people with a learning disability will form part of the Learning Disability Service Model.
answer_text: The Learning Disability Service Model is being finalised within my Department at present. The Model sets out the Department of Health’s vision regarding improved outcomes for adults with a learning disability regarding health & wellbeing, transitions, meaningful lives & citizenship, families & carers, home and mental health & behavioural support; through a lens of regional consistency of service provision. In terms of the health and well being section, the focus is on ensuring adults with a learning disability have equity of access to universal health services as appropriate, including general cancer and palliative care service provision, in accordance with assessed need. Public Consultation is anticipated in coming months, pending finalisation of the model.

answer_text: Following significant engagement, my Department has developed a proposal for a new Lifespan Gender Service to create a more efficient patient pathway, a faster patient flow rate through endocrine, to ensure waiting lists decrease and patients receive the care they need in a timely way.My officials are currently considering the business case submitted on 6 March for the new Lifespan Gender Service including funding requirements. They will shortly provide me with draft advice for my consideration.

If agreement is reached, the centre would re-open following that agreement.  While at present there is no timeframe for this, the existing public toilets onsite remain open and serviced for visitor use.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail all site inspections carried out by Northern Ireland Fire Rescue Services at waste management centres in each of the last five years, broken down by waste management licence holder.
answer_text: NIFRS does not maintain a list of waste management sites.  NIFRS holds information on categories, such as Factory or Warehouse, Office, or Other Workplace. NIFRS would require a site address to be able to detail a fire safety inspection at a waste management centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any work undertaken by Forest Service to plant native flora and fauna in Donard Wood, Newcastle.
answer_text: Donard Forest is predominately planted with mixed species conifer trees, with the first planting recorded in 1924. Forest Service manages the forest under sustainable forest management principles. Donard Forest’s design plan includes a progressive increase in area of native tree species within the forest, managed under a continuous cover forestry system.   Since the 1980s, native trees such as birch, rowan, oak, ash and Scots pine have been planted in forest areas which were previously coniferous.  This management regime is conducive to the development of naturally occurring ground flora and in addition Forest Service carries out removal of laurel and rhododendron overstory to encourage ground vegetation.This diversity of tree species and ground vegetation provides important native fauna habitats for species such as the Red squirrel and pine marten. Forest Service also supports the actions of local conservation groups, including the Tollymore Red Squirrel Group in the Newcastle area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how often Carlingford Lough's water quality is tested; (ii) the extent of such testing; (iii) the last time the water quality was tested; and (iv) the most recent findings of this testing.
answer_text: My Department routinely monitors water quality parameters at a representative site within Carlingford Lough once per calendar month.  Water samples taken at this site are analysed for nutrients, chlorophyll, chemical pollutants and phytoplankton.  Further measurements are taken for temperature, salinity, dissolved oxygen, pH, and turbidity. During the winter months, from November to February, an additional four sites within Carlingford Lough are sampled monthly and analysed for nutrients, chlorophyll, and basic physico-chemical parameters.In addition, farmed mussels are sampled monthly from the designated shellfish waters in Carlingford Lough and analysed for microbial and chemical pollutants. Wild mussel populations are also sampled annually for chemical pollutant analysis. Cranfield Bay bathing water is monitored weekly for microbial contaminants during the bathing water season from June to September.The most recent classification results, from 2024, for Carlingford Lough water body under the Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017 indicates that nutrient levels are below ‘Good Status’, although for chemical pollutants the status is ‘Good’ or ‘High’. For contaminants, like mercury, in wild shellfish the lough is failing to achieve ‘Good Status’. Carlingford Lough Shellfish Water Protected Area met the guideline microbiological standard for shellfish flesh as set out in the Water Framework Directive (Priority Substances and Classification) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 in 2024.Under the Quality of Bathing Water Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2008 the water quality of Cranfield Bay bathing water was classified as ‘excellent’ in the 2024 bathing season.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to address (i) funding; and (ii) staff shortages in paediatric gastroenterology in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The paediatric gastroenterology service is resourced as follows:3 WTE paediatric gastroenterology (GI) consultants2.5 WTE Band 7 Clinical Nurse Specialist 0.8 WTE Band 8a Dietician 2.2 WTE Band 7 Dietician  0.5 WTE Band 8a Pharmacist 0.5 WTE Band 7 At 2024/25 pay scales this equates to almost a £1m recurrent investment in the service.(ii) My Department has worked with the Belfast Trust to mitigate ongoing issue with the paediatric gastroenterology services due to a combination of planned and unplanned loss of capacity.Successfully recruited to the vacant consultant post with a start date of September 2025Increased Dublin’s out of hours provision to a 7-days/week out of hours remote on-call consultant supportSourced and employed a locum consultant paediatrician with gastro experienceWorked in closely with specialist colleagues in the wider NHS (Alder Hey Children’s hospital) to secure in reach, out of hours care and specialist advice to stabilise the Paediatric Gastroenterology service from June 2024- December 2024.Developed approaches to enhance local care with support for additional hours where these could be secured Secured Independent Sector support for patients on the waiting list for colonoscopies and/or urgent outpatient colonoscopies outside the current capacity within RBHSC.   Arrangements commenced on 29th April 2024 and are ongoing with over 120 additional patients treated in the first 8 months of the initiative.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the Health and Social Care Trusts that intend to recognise stroke-specific counselling within the referral pathway for stroke as part of the Stroke Action Plan's commitment to timely psychological and emotional support; and (ii) the plans the Health and Social Care Trusts have to fund this (a) in the 2025-26 financial year; and (b) in the subsequent two years.
answer_text: (i) All Health & Social Care Trusts recognise the importance of psychological and emotional support as part of the stroke pathway in line with the Community Stroke Service model and the Stepped Model of Psychological Care for Stroke Services, contained within  Life After Stroke. Psychological and emotional support is delivered by various professions including Nursing, Occupational Therapy and Clinical Psychology. Some stroke survivors  may be onward referred to services within the Voluntary and Community sector. (ii) (a&b) In the absence of a final budget for 2025/26 and subsequent years, it is not possible to provide a commitment to fund emotional support services in those years. I do, however, recognise the need for additional investment to develop suitable psychological support for stroke patients and, in this context, my Department has ensured that this funding pressure is suitably reflected in engagement with the Department of Finance on budget building exercises. This includes a recent information gathering exercise as part of the 2025/26 budget process which will inform the Executive’s consideration of the budget for the three years 2025-28.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount her Department has paid out in compensation for (i) vehicle damage; and (ii) personal injury, in each of the last five years, broken down local council area.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20192020 Please note that my Department maintains its records by Roads Section Office, which broadly align with Council areas.  In addition, my Department maintains its records by financial year rather than by calendar year.  Table 7 in the above link provides details of (i) the vehicle damage compensation; and (ii) the personal injury compensation paid out during the last five full financial years broken down by Roads Section Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of resurfacing works completed in the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area in each of the last five years, including the cost of resurfacing.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes undertaken by my department are included in the annual reports to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council which can be found in the links below:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-roads-southern-report-newry-mourne-down-district-council-24-25.pdfhttps://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-council-report-newry-mourne-down-district-council-2023.pdfhttps://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-to-newry-mourne-and-down-district-council-2022-23.PDFhttps://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-to-newry-mourne-down-council-2021.pdfhttps://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-newry-mourne-down-council-dec20.pdfhttps://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-council-report-to-newry-mourne-down-district-council.pdfRegrettably, my Department does not hold the information in the format requested and any attempt to do so would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.
