question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the invitation list for the recent meeting between his Department, the NI Fishermen's Federation and MLAs; (i) who determined this invitation list; and (iii) why Opposition MLAs were not invited.
answer_text: During the 26 November 2024 adjournment debate on the Ardglass fishing industry, I accepted an invitation from Michelle McIlveen MLA to accompany her to meet with the NI Fishermen’s Federation to discuss fishing industry concerns.While the invitation for the meeting originated from Michelle McIlveen MLA, Cathy Mason MLA, Andrew McMurray MLA and Diane Forsythe MLA subsequently requested to be involved in this meeting and to which I had no objection. Attendees at the meeting also included the Chief Executives of the two Northern Ireland Fish Producers Organisations. As the meeting had been requested by Michelle McIlveen MLA and industry representatives, it was not appropriate for me to extend invitations to others who did not formally express an interest in attending nor had been invited by the organiser or the fishing industry representatives to participate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 22907/22-27, to detail (i) why preference is being given to delivering a one-way system at Edward Street; and (ii) whether a request was made at the meeting between her officials and the District Electoral Area forum to not release the findings of the Department for Infrastructure report.
answer_text: As you are aware, the initial consultation with representatives from Downpatrick DEA took place on Tuesday 11 February 2025.  My Department is now taking forward a more detailed review of the preferred options and on completion, my officials will analyse the report and arrange a further meeting with the DEA. I can confirm that a number of options have been identified for further detailed consideration. A one-way system has been identified as one possible solution but will be subject to further detailed analysis before a preferred option is finalised.I can also confirm that the initial report has not yet been finalised for release but when completed, my officials will provide an update to the constituency MLAs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 22907/22-27, to detail (i) whether the proposal for a school street scheme was included within the discussion; and (ii) whether a school street scheme is being progressed.
answer_text: As you are aware, the initial consultation with representatives from Downpatrick DEA took place on Tuesday 11 February 2025.  My Department is now taking forward a more detailed review of the preferred options and on completion my officials will analyse the report and arrange another meeting with the DEA. I can confirm that a School Streets option has been identified in the initial draft report and is now among a number of options to be taken forward for further detailed consideration. When completed, my officials will provide an update to the constituency MLAs on the outcome of the completed study.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 22907/22-27, whether her response is a change in policy to the answers provided by her predecessor which stated constituency MLAs and the MP would be invited to this meeting.
answer_text: My Department’s position has not changed in that my predecessor advised that officials would consult with Downpatrick DEA following the outcome of a draft study.  It has always been the intention for my Department to consult with constituency MLAs on the outcome of the completed options study. However, this is not yet completed.  As you are aware, the initial consultation with representatives from Downpatrick DEA took place on Tuesday 11 February 2025.  My Department is now taking forward a more detailed review of the preferred options and on completion my officials will analyse the report and arrange another meeting with the DEA. However, I have asked my officials to contact each of the constituency MLA offices inviting them if they would wish to be provided with an update on the current position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why funding from the Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025-26 has been cut to 25 key community and voluntary sector groups; (ii) the anticipated impact this will have on health service delivery; and (iii) any work he will undertake to assist unsuccessful organisations to source alternative funding.
answer_text: The Core Grant Funding Scheme for 2025-26 received 259 applications, with bids totalling over £13m. As agreed during the co-design with the sector the amount of funding requested by each organisation would be capped at £100k. The Scheme has a budget of £1.8m and so demand was therefore unfortunately always going to outstrip supply. Applications were assessed, scored and ranked in merit order; funding was then awarded until all available funding was exhausted. This resulted in 25 successful organisations. The general standard of applications was incredibly high, and the progression of 25 high scoring applications to stage 2 of the assessment process was based solely on agreed scoring criteria, rather than a departmental determination of the value of the applicants. The scoring criteria is publicly available on the DoH website.The purpose of the Core Grant scheme is to help Community and Voluntary sector bodies cover their fixed costs and does not directly fund service deliveryApplicants will be invited to seek feedback on their application in the coming weeks. I hope this feedback will be of assistance in applying for future funding rounds. Indeed, those who have missed out this time will have another opportunity, later this year, to bid for funding in 2026/27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21961/22-27, for a breakdown of the £500 million investment in learning disability services in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21961/22-27, to provide a breakdown of the nine referenced commissioning requests.
answer_text: As noted in AQW 21961/22-27, in the past five years there have been nine commissioning requests relating to Learning Disability Services for adults with a Learning Disability. It is not considered appropriate for my Department to provide a further breakdown of these commissioning requests due to confidentiality considerations, however, it should be noted that my Department is committed to supporting consistency of service provision for adults with a Learning Disability across Health and Social Care Trusts.To this effect, work is ongoing within my Department to finalise the Learning Disability Service Model which sets out the Department of Health’s vision regarding transitions, health & wellbeing, meaningful lives & citizenship, families & carers, home and mental health & behavioural support; through a lens of regional consistency of service provision. Public Consultation is anticipated in coming months, pending finalisation of the model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 22326/22-27, to detail who is responsible for (i) writing the terms of reference; and (ii) the invitation list for the previously referred multi-agency meetings.
answer_text: The purpose of the meeting, as advised to those attending, was to to discuss collectively the concerns raised by the local elected members, including potential statutory nuisance, potential human health impacts and potential impacts from the use of sprays.  		Officials from Newry, Mourne and Down District Council, Northern Ireland Environment Agency, Public Health Agency, Health and Safety Executive and Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21961/22-27, whether the funding plan for the Learning Disability Service Model will be (i) dependent on the upcoming budget; (ii) included within the £500 million annual investment; and (iii) new money that has been secured.
answer_text: As noted in AQW 21961/22-27 the Department of Health is allocated funding each year to commission services to meet population need, with my Department currently investing £500 million annually for people with Learning Disabilities. Work is ongoing within my Department to finalise the Learning Disability Service Model. Public Consultation is anticipated in coming months, pending finalisation of the model.  As part of work to progress the Learning Disability Service Model and associated funding considerations, my Department is currently finalising a review of the annual £500m expenditure in the Learning Disability Programme of Care in respect of service provision. My Department is also progressing a Costed Implementation Plan for the service model which will also be informed by the forthcoming Public Consultation. No new money has been allocated or ringfenced at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) why there are still no bus services from Belfast Grand Central Station to (a) Downpatrick; and (b) Newcastle; and (ii) when these services will commence.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The works at Belfast Grand Central are due to continue throughout 2025. Services will be added to the new station on a phased basis including those for Ballynahinch, Newcastle and Downpatrick. The detailed planning and timescales for this is not yet available.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will bring the draft Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland to the Executive for approval.
answer_text: Work on the draft strategy is almost complete, and we expect to receive a final version in the coming weeks. It will then be brought to the Executive for approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQO 859/22-27, to detail when the meeting between his officials, the district electoral area representatives and MLAs will take place.
answer_text: My Department commissioned a review to establish if any viable road safety and traffic progression improvements could be made along Edward Street in Downpatrick. The review has been completed and my officials met with representatives from Downpatrick District Electoral Area on Tuesday 11 February 2025 to discuss its findings. My Department will now undertake a feasibility study to further look at options to improve pedestrian safety and traffic progression along Edward Street, Downpatrick.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why the Shore Road, Killowen, was closed for such a protracted period, if it was possible to establish a two way road system.
answer_text: I recognise the inconvenience that was caused by the temporary closure of one lane on the A2 Shore Road, Rostrevor, as a result of the land and verge slip caused by the unprecedented weather in November 2023.The land is under the control of Forest Service and within an area of outstanding natural beauty with several environmental designations that restrict what work can be undertaken.My Department worked closely with DAERA, Forestry Service and NIEA to complete the complex design and environmental approval process required to progress a temporary solution to facilitate the reopening of the A2 Shore Road to two way traffic. The work was completed early February 2025.Regrettably, due to the scale and complexity of the work required, and the need to comply with the necessary environmental and geotechnical approvals, it took some time to bring about the reopening of the road to two-way traffic.  During this time, it was imperative to implement the lane closure on the A2 to minimise risk and maintain road safety until a suitable solution was found.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the reasons why ATMs were banned from bingo halls as part of his Department's Gambling Codes of Practice in 2024; and (ii) whether he has any intention to revoke this ban.
answer_text: My Department has not published a Gambling Code of Practice. A consultation on a proposed gambling code of practice closed on 24 December 2024 and responses are currently being analysed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consultation that took place with the NI Bingo Association in advance of taking the decision to ban ATMs in bingo halls.
answer_text: A decision has not yet been taken. A consultation on a proposed gambling code of practice closed on 24 December 2024. The Department consulted in line with the requirements under Article 180A(10) of The Betting, Gaming, Lotteries and Amusements (Northern Ireland) Order 1985. Responses to the consultation are currently being analysed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of funding allocated to the Hospital at Home Service in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The following table details the funding allocated to the Hospital at Home Service in the last five financial years, by HSC Trust.2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25BHSCT153,000 278,630 99,649 --NHSCT67,486 919,950 --445,155 SEHSCT300,000 662,0001 362,0002 --SHSCT532,000 2,324,7513 829,794 --WHSCT207,475 1,329,846 748,977 430,000 -Total1,259,961 5,515,177 2,040,420 430,000 445,155 Notes:Of which £300,000 was allocated recurrently.Of which the entire additional £362,000 was allocated recurrently.Of which £596,440 was allocated recurrently.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he is taking in light of the Royal College of Occupational Therapists January 2025 report, Occupational Therapy Workforce Strategy Action Plan Northern Ireland 2025–2027, which states that some children are waiting up to three years to see an occupational therapist.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Royal College of Occupational Therapy’s report, “Occupational Therapy Workforce Strategy Action Plan Northern Ireland 2025–2027” in January. I will be meeting with the Royal College of Occupational Therapy in the coming weeks to explore the detail of their report and discuss the recommendations outlined in their action plan and how they can collaborate with us to secure the future of their profession in Northern Ireland. This will provide an opportunity to explore some of the solutions Allied Health Professionals can offer to improve outcomes in Health and Social Care in Northern Ireland and reduce waiting times for Occupational Therapy interventions, particularly at this time of severe financial pressure across the HSC. The Public Health Agency continues to work with my Department and Trusts to ensure Allied Health Profession services, including occupational therapy, deliver a range of support that reflect the needs of the population of Northern Ireland and are aligned to evidence based practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any workforce planning that has been carried out for Community Dental Services (CDS); (ii) how much whole-time equivalent (WTE) growth is required for the service to meet current and future demands of the population; and (iii) any timescales in place to grow CDS workforce beyond the current 65.83 WTE.
answer_text: My Department commissioned Skills for Health’s Workforce Review for Dental Services in Northern Ireland (August 2018) which was published in July 2023.  The remit of this review included Community Dental Services (CDS).The Department planned to develop an Implementation Plan addressing relevant recommendations from this Review, informed by feedback from a stakeholder engagement event held in September 2023, following its publication.  However, the financial pressures on my Department have rendered impossible any growth or development of the dental workforce more broadly.It is not possible to commit to definitive timescales to grow the Community Dental Service workforce .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why the number of whole-time equivalent (WTE) dentists employed in the Community Dental Service has grown by 3.9 per cent between June 2018 and June 2024 compared with overall growth of Health and Social Care WTE by 16.4 per cent over the same period; and (ii) what plans are in place to grow this service.
answer_text: (i) The whole time equivalent (WTE) numbers of the various professional groups within the Health and Social Care (HSC) workforce over any specific period do not develop in a uniform way.(ii) My Department is reviewing and finalising oral health priorities in respect of children and older people following the consultation on Oral Health Improvement Plans for Children and Older People in Northern Ireland.  These include recommendations on new models of preventive and operative dental care for patients with complex needs, including the growing ageing population and those with multiple co-morbidities, as well as increasing capacity within the Community Dental Service.  However, the implementation of such recommendations will be subject to assessments of priority and affordability within the context of ongoing financial pressures on my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail who has overall or lead responsibility for ensuring Community Dental Services are sufficiently staffed.
answer_text: Individual Health and Social Care Trusts are responsible for ensuring that their respective Community Dental Services are sufficiently staffed to provide those services commissioned from them by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Stroke Action Plan, for an update on the £5 million recurrent funding to roll-out thrombectomy to a 24/7 service.
answer_text: While I remain committed to the establishment of 24/7 thrombectomy service, the financial challenges imposed on my Department’s 2024/25 resource budget mean that I am currently unable to allocate the required additional funding to support a 24/7 service.  My Department has ensured that the funding pressure is suitably reflected in engagement with the Department of Finance on budget building exercises. This includes a recent information gathering exercise as part of the 2025/26 budget process which will inform the Executive’s consideration of the budget for the three years 2025-28. In the interim I am committed to ensuring that as many people as possible benefit from the service within the current hours of operation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding that will be made available for the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 in 2025-26, when the funding plan sets out £61.72 million being needed for this incoming year.
answer_text: As you are aware, the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 was published alongside a 10-year Funding Plan. The Funding Plan outlined that £1.2bn is needed to fully implement the Strategy over the 10-year period. The investment required to deliver the Strategy is in addition to existing expenditure in mental health services.Funding for 2025/2026 for the Mental Health Strategy, against the £61.72 million set out in the funding plan, is still under consideration and has not yet been allocated. However, funding decisions will necessarily reflect the continuing constraints facing the Department in the absence of additional funding being provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to prioritising dementia in future budgets to ensure adequate funding for early diagnosis, treatment, and support services.
answer_text: My Department is striving to improve all aspects of Dementia diagnosis, treatment, care and support services across Northern Ireland.  This includes reviewing use of current allocated funding and opportunities for new service development and investment where possible.  My Department has established the Regional Dementia Project Board which will lead improvement of dementia services across the region.  A Social Care Lead, with responsibility for dementia, liaises with all HSC trusts and other key stakeholders to ensure that there is a focus on people with dementia and on continuous service improvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will commit to working with the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs to reduce restrictions on the fishing sector accessing the seasonal worker visa.
answer_text: I recognise that Northern Ireland’s fishing sector has faced several challenges in recent years, one of which has been to secure labour.  I wrote to the Defra Secretary of State in October, setting out the issues that are being faced by the fishing industry and the wider agri-food sector, and suggested modest changes to UK immigration policy to provide a resolution to these issues. The Defra Secretary of State rejected my recommendations and, with regards to the fishing sector, Minister Reed indicated that Defra would work with the seafood industry to understand concerns about labour shortages and ways to increase the attractiveness of seafood jobs to UK workers.At a meeting of the Inter-Ministerial Group for Environment Food and Rural Affairs on 18th November 2024, I reiterated to Minister Daniel Zeichner MP the labour shortage pressures that are faced by the fisheries and mushroom sectors.Challenges around access to labour are being faced by sectors across the economy and as such it has been agreed that my officials will work in collaboration with Department for Economy officials to develop an immigration policy position that will be agreed by the Executive and then sent to the Home Secretary. I met with representatives from the fishing industry on 13th February who have provided a paper outlining the fishing industry’s key asks. This paper will inform the development of the Executive papers and industry representatives have agreed that it can be used for this purpose.  As I have previously indicated, I am content to attend any possible meeting with the Home Office alongside the First Minister and deputy First Minister plus the Economy Minister. I will also continue to press access to labour issues concerning the agri-food sector with the UK Government.In addition to this, I am committed to working with the fishing industry to develop alternative workforce solutions such as apprenticeship schemes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, further to the death of Jeni Larmour, whether she has had discussions with the Home Office in relation to the reclassification of ketamine as a Class A drug.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 8 - AQO 1607/22-27			ANSWERAt the outset I want to acknowledge the overwhelming impact that drugs can have right across society, all too often lives are blighted with devastating consequences.  Tackling drugs and the harms they cause is clearly a shared priority for us all and I want to pay tribute to the ongoing efforts of the Larmour family to raise awareness of the danger posed by drugs following the tragic death of their daughter Jeni.  However, I also need to be clear that my role as Justice Minister is about the criminal justice response and enforcement of the law.  Representations on the classification or re-classification of drugs is a matter for the Department of Health given the wider health implications. Members will also be aware that ultimately the classification of drugs is a reserved matter, and any decision will rest with the UK Government. Recently both Minister Nesbitt and I attended a meeting with Ministers from all other regions of the UK on drug related issues.  During that meeting there was discussion on the reclassification of ketamine and it is something that we are likely to discuss again at future meetings.   Addressing the harms, particularly those which are irreversible, associated with ketamine are priorities across the UK.I am aware that on 8 January 2025, the Home Office announced that they are seeking expert advice on reclassifying ketamine as a Class A substance. The Minister of Health and I are intending to jointly write to the Home Office Minister to convey the concerns raised across the Assembly.   The Home Office will consult and engage with the devolved administrations on all aspects of drug policy and any future changes.I am mindful that there is no easy solution and the issues around drugs cut across the whole of the Executive – justice, health and communities.  Collectively we need to work together to resolve these issues and we can only succeed in improving outcomes if we see substance use in the broader context.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any discussions he has had with the UK Government's Children and Young People Cancer Taskforce on engaging with families impacted by cancer in children and young people.
answer_text: I have not yet had discussions with this Taskforce, whose work was paused in June 2024 and which only reopened in recent days. Whilst its work relates to cancer patients in England, my Department will however review any findings of the taskforce.The Department of Health in Northern Ireland has recently developed Minimum Standards for Adolescent and Young Adult (AYA) Cancer Services along with a new, regional networked service model. Work to implement these standards and the new service model is underway.These standards, and the new service model have been co-produced with those with lived experience of AYA cancer, and charities who play a key role in supporting, and advocating for them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether an indicative amount of funding has been allocated to each strand of the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: The current available budget for The Northern Ireland Football Fund has been shown to be inadequate to meet all identified needs. Therefore, it is not appropriate to apportion this initial budget amongst all eligible football clubs or across the Programme Strands. Expenditure will be managed in line with individual project approvals. I have committed to continue to seek additional funding as and when it becomes necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 21635/22-27, (i) why Warrenpoint Harbour Authority are not included in the invitation list to multi-agency meetings; and (ii) whether this will be reviewed in future.
answer_text: (i)	The multi-agency meetings bring together the relevant regulatory authorities concerning the site activities. Warrenpoint Harbour Authority are not considered a relevant regulatory authority, therefore are not included in these meetings.(ii)	No.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consultation that has been carried out on Strand 3 of the Northern Ireland Football Fund - the Grassroots Development Programme.
answer_text: Final proposals for Strand 2 of The Northern Ireland Football Fund, the Grassroots Facilities Programme, are in development. A full public consultation was carried out in 2015 and responses have been taken into account in developing proposals across The Northern Ireland Football Fund, including for investment in grassroots facilities. The published report on the consultation can be accessed here. https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/consultations/dcal-sub-regional-stadia-programme-soccer Stakeholder engagement is ongoing to inform detailed final proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the current stages of development for Strand 2 and Strand 3 of the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: Work to finalise detailed policy proposals is underway and I expect to be presented with recommendations on need, scope and delivery options for a programme of grassroots investment by Spring 2025. A programme level business case will then be finalised to secure approvals in advance of any awards made by my Department to support grassroots projects. I am committed to moving forward at pace to address the evidenced need for investment in grassroots facilities. The Irish FA has submitted a business case for a National Football Centre which my Department is now considering.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has (i) amended; (ii) changed; and (iii) added to the January 2025 Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority report Review of the Pathways Associated with the Temporary Suspension of Emergency General Surgery at South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: Given the independence of this review, my Department has requested no amendments, changes or additions to the report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 18648/22-27; and (ii) AQW 20970/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20705/22-27, to detail (i) the reason for the delay in the publication of the Learning Disability Service Model; and (ii) how this has been communicated to Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Delivery of this work has been heavily impacted by the volume of work on learning disability and autism services for children and adults and the redeployment of staff to respond to pressures for children with disabilities. A significant body of work was progressed in a short time to develop the £13 million support package for children with disabilities. Work is progressing to finalise an implementation plan in collaboration with key partners across the sector. In parallel to this work, officials are also finalising a financial review of learning disability services, which will form the basis for costings to implement the Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM).Officials have outlined that all the necessary work has been undertaken to outline our evidence base, direction of travel and approach for implementation for the LDSM.My officials from SPPG and Policy met as a combined project team on 22 January 2025 to agree a plan to finalise this work within the financial year ahead of wider consultation which will happen in the coming months, subject to my decision. The Department provides regular updates through established governance and reform structures for Learning Disability at a Trust Director level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20705/22-27, to detail (i) the number of requests the Strategic Planning and Performance Group has received for the commissioning of services for people with a learning disability in each of the last five years; and (ii) the status of each of these requests.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) is responsible for the planning, improving and overseeing of the delivery of safe and effective health and social care services for Northern Ireland, within available resource.SPPG is allocated funding each year to commission services to meet population need. My Department/SPPG invests £500 million annually for people with Learning Disabilities. SPPG plan service provision based on population need.  SPPG then commissions Health and Social Care Trusts to deliver services. This is the agreed process for commissioning and funding of services, rather than Trusts submitting requests regarding the commissioning of services to SPPG. It is the responsibility of Health and Social Care Trusts to build flexibility within their Service Delivery Plans to account for unforeseen circumstances or service pressures. However more recently, extraordinary commissioning requests are received due to unforeseen risk or mounting service pressures. Whilst this is not in keeping with process, these requests are reviewed by SPPG. As these requests are outside of process, timebound and are unique to a specific organisation they are managed on an individual basis. The requests are considered on receipt and either approved, kept under review as they depend on the development of strategy or process, or Trusts are advised to meet the need from within resource already allocated. In the past five years SPPG have received nine commissioning requests relating Learning Disability Services for adults with a Learning Disability. One Commissioning request is being reviewed and it has been determined that two of the requests are the responsibility of the associated Health and Social Care Trusts to resource. The remaining six will be further reviewed pending agreement on a regional model of practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the advice provided by his Departmental engineers, stating that dredging of the Quoile River may have an adverse effect.
answer_text: The independent engineering report into the Review of South East Flooding of Autumn 2023 explained that water levels in Strangford Lough were significantly higher than the bed of the Quoile River where it flows into the Lough at the Quoile Barrier.It confirms that excavating material from the bed of the river to lower the river bed would not improve drainage, adding that the water level in Strangford Lough is the limiting factor in determining the flow of water from the River into the Lough through the barrier.In light of the above, I can advise that my Department has no plans to dredge the Quoile River.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the financial cost for his Department to dredge the Quoile River.
answer_text: The independent engineering report into the Review of South East Flooding of Autumn 2023 explained that water levels in Strangford Lough were significantly higher than the bed of the Quoile River where it flows into the Lough at the Quoile Barrier.It confirms that excavating material from the bed of the river to lower the river bed would not improve drainage, adding that the water level in Strangford Lough is the limiting factor in determining the flow of water from the River into the Lough through the barrier.In light of the above, I can advise that my Department has not compiled any costs associated with dredging the Quoile because there are no plans to dredge the Quoile River.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why funding has not been rolled out to all GP Federations to deliver multi-disciplinary teams.
answer_text: The extremely challenging workforce and financial constraints that the Department of health have been operating under have been a limiting factor in our ability to progress implementation of MDT as widely, and as quickly, as desired. Despite these pressures, as of September 2024, there are over 368 whole time equivalent front-line staff working across 117 GP practices in 7 GP Federation areas in the Primary Care MDT programmeMy Department remains committed to the further implementation of the MDT model across Northern Ireland In Health and Social Care NI – A Three Year Plan to Stabilise, Reform and Deliver, I have made a commitment to the publication of a plan, by April 2025, for the completion of the Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary Teams (MDT) model across all areas of Northern Ireland.Mindful of the serious staffing and funding pressures facing health, this will be a 2-phase plan, the first phase of which will require the allocation of Transformation funding. If this is received, it would enable completion of the model in existing MDT areas, and expansion to 5 new GP Federation areas with a population of roughly 670,000 people.Following the announcement of the 2025-26 Draft Budget allocation, my Department has assessed that health will be facing a significant funding gap in 2025-26 as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18645/22-27, to provide a breakdown of the postcode areas for upgraded calls in each of the referenced years.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the recruitment process carried out for surgeons at the South West Acute Hospital in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The responsibility for recruitment rests with each individual HSC Trust as the employers of our HSC workforce lies with the employing organisation.All HSC Trusts use the same recruitment process. Once a vacancy is identified an electronic requisition form is completed to confirm funding and approval to proceed to advertisement.   The job description is then forwarded to the Royal College of Surgeons for specialty approval.  Once all approvals have been received the Trust proceeds to advertising.   The appointment of Consultants is governed by ‘The Appointment of Consultants Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1996’ and amendments thereto. The regulations stipulate how the post should be advertised. A Trust shall arrange for an advertisement in one or more publications circulating throughout the United Kingdom which are commonly used for similar advertisements relating to the profession concerned.This means a minimum of two advertisements must normally appear. These may be in nationally distributed journals or on nationally available Internet sites commonly used for similar advertisements relating to the profession concerned. At least one of the advertisements must appear in a printed journal. answer_text: No.10 December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the proposed online reporting form for waste management issues, including when it will go live.
answer_text: My officials are in the process of developing a new Incident Management System. It is intended that as part of this project we will develop an online reporting form for anyone to report environmental concerns to us. This will enable us to streamline the process and make it easier for members of the community to contact us. It is anticipated that this will be ready to go live during 2025. In the meantime, waste management issues can be reported via the NIEAwastecomplaint@daera-ni.gov.uk mailbox.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the progression of the new build for Knockevin Special School, Downpatrick.
answer_text: My Department, is working with the Education Authority to identify a suitable site for the major capital project for Knockevin School.  On the back of my announcement in April 2024 that planning for the new school for Knockevin would commence, the Department of Finance’s Land and Property Services were commissioned to carry out an initial site search in Downpatrick and the surrounding area.  This did not identify any suitable sites, and a second search of a wider area also did not yield any positive results. Through engagement with the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council a site, which is not currently on the market, was identified and this is currently being explored by Land and Property Service.  My officials are also engaging with the Department of Health regarding the potential availability land.  Down High School will soon move to their new school build and their existing site is also being considered as a possible site option. The first stage of the procurement process to appoint an Integrated Consultancy Team to take forward the design of the new school has now completed, however, before the tender can issue for this team, the proposals for the school need confirmed, this includes the identification of a suitable site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the budget; (ii) the actual spend, for out of hours primary care in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: SPPG commissions GP Out of Hours (OOHs) services across Northern Ireland from five GP OOHs Providers.  In Belfast, South Eastern and Southern areas the GP Out of Hours (OOHs) service is provided by the Health and Social Care Trust. Dalriada Urgent Care (DUC) and Western Urgent Care (WUC) provide GP OOHs services for Northern and Western areas respectively.  Dalriada Urgent Care and Western Urgent Care are independent mutual organisations and not part of the HSC Trusts.Table 1 below outlines the funding provided by SPPG/DoH to each of the five OOHs Providers.  In addition to the funding outlined in Table 1 below the three Trust OOHs Providers can secure additional funding for uplifts for pay awards directly into their budget, via normal funding increases for pay awards for staff on their payroll, independently of SPPG/DoH.   Financial break-even position was achieved by the Trusts through efficiency savings along with in-year budgetary management, and by the Mutual providers working with SPPG. A letter has been issued to the three Trusts in July 2024 inviting the Trusts to develop and submit proportionate business case(s) for consideration in respect of the above.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of requests the Strategic Planning and Performance Group received for (i) recruitment; and (ii) training of (a) Advanced Nurse Practitioners; and (b) General Practice Nurses, in each of the last five years, broken down by those requests that were either granted or not granted.
answer_text: My Department does not recruit or employ any Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs) or General Practice Nurses (GPNs) so is unable to comment on recruitment requests.  ANPs and GPNs within General Practice are either employed directly by GP Practices, in which case the recruitment takes place through the individual GP Practice or by GP Federations.  Where employment is through the GP Practice, this will be funded by the individual practice.  In the case of GP Federations:Advanced Nurse PractitionersSPPG allocate funding to six GP Federations (Armagh & Dungannon, Derry, Down, East Belfast, Mid-Ulster and South West) to recruit and employ ANPs to work in GP Practices within their Federation area.  The recruitment and employment of these ANPs takes place though the GP Federations.General Practice NursesSPPG allocate funding to five GP Federations (Antrim & Ballymena, Ards, Armagh & Dungannon, South West and West Belfast) to recruit and employ GPNs to work in GP Practices within their Federation area.  The recruitment and employment of these GPNs takes place though the GP Federations.With regards to training for ANPs and GPNs, this too is either arranged by GP Practices for the staff employed by themselves or by GP Federations.  SPPG do allocate funding to Public Health Agency (PHA) on an annual basis with regards to nurse training.  PHA arrange and fund training based on current demand from the nursing community.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work carried out to raise levels of learning disability awareness among Health and Social Care staff.
answer_text: Each of the Health and Social Care Trusts within Northern Ireland raise awareness amongst health and social care staff with regards to Learning Disability. Awareness raising is primarily through training courses, including specific Learning Disability awareness training and professional post qualifying training. The role of professional bodies responsible for health and social care staff  in Northern Ireland is to advance the practice of individual professions and to promote training and education. Each of the professional bodies ensure that the workforce is able to meet the needs of the population of Northern Ireland, including awareness raising and training regarding the needs of individuals with Learning Disabilities.My Department and Health and Social Care Trusts optimise pertinent dates within the year such a Learning Disability Awareness Week, to amplify understanding of Learning Disability and to broadcast key messages. Social media and other reporting mechanisms are also utilised by my Department to enhance knowledge regarding Learning Disability for health and social care staff and the wider population. I personally attend Learning Disability services within Health and Social Care Trusts, conferences and regional events throughout the year, in order to raise the profile of Learning Disability within the health and social care sector.It is anticipated that significant work will be completed in 2025 by my Department, in partnership with Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to awareness raising with regards to the Learning Disability Service Model for Northern Ireland. The profile of Learning Disability will be significantly heightened through public consultation and the commencement of the implementation of the model in practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the November 2023 Healthy Futures Obesity Strategy Consultation.
answer_text: Since the close of the consultation in 2024, work has been undertaken to work through the detail of the responses and look at the wider research and evidence base. In addition, my Department has been engaging with colleagues across the UK and Ireland to consider emerging development for those living with overweigh and obesity.Work is well under way to finalise the consultation report. A meeting of the Obesity Project Board has been organised for February 2025 to review the report and make recommendations. Once agreed, the report will be published, and work will start on developing the final Healthy Futures strategic framework, with an aim to publishing this in 2025, subject to the availability of resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when data in relation to the (i) frequency; and (ii) level of corridor care will be published by his Department.
answer_text: My Department publishes figures in the Northern Ireland Inpatient Activity Statistics annual report, on the number of occupied beds in wards that are open overnight.Previously this report only included those beds which were funded. With the launch of encompass, the Department has extended this to also include figures on the number of occupied escalation spaces. This will give an indication of where hospitals are exceeding their funded bed capacity and the need to use escalation spaces.Figures on the use of escalation spaces were included in the 2024/25 annual publication (available at https://datavis.nisra.gov.uk/health/ni-inpatient-stats-24-25.html) for those Trusts live on encompass during that year, apart from Northern Trust, whose data are still being developed following their transition to encompass. It is hoped that figures on the use of escalation spaces will be included in subsequent publications now that all Trusts are live on encompass.  However, this will not extend to corridor care which may involve the use of trolleys and chairs which are not classed as ‘beds’.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the outcomes within the Racial Equality Strategy 2015-2025 that were (i) met; (ii) commenced but not completed; and (iii) not commenced.
answer_text: The four key outcomes the Racial Equality Strategy seeks to achieve are: equality of service provision; elimination of prejudice, racism and hate crime; increased participation, representation and belonging; and cultural diversity is celebrated. There has been significant progress against all outcomes, and in September 2024, the Department published the Racial Equality Indicators Report: (2014 – 2022).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide details on a new draft Racial Equality Strategy, including (i) when work commenced; (ii) when it will be published; and (iii) when it will go out to consultation.
answer_text: Officials have already begun engagement with the Minority Ethnic sector to support the development of the new strategic approach to racial equality post 2025 in a visioning workshop with the Racial Equality Subgroup, and Travellers and Roma Thematic Groups. A public call for views was launched on the 6th February to support the development of the new approach to tackling racism and achieving racial equality. The outworkings of the visioning workshop along with further stakeholder engagement and the responses to the call for views will help develop our future strategic approach. We aim to launch a public consultation on a draft strategy later this year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any work carried out to review the efficacy of the Racial Equality Strategy 2015-2025.
answer_text: In December 2024, the Department published the Independent Review of Progress on the Implementation of the Racial Equality Strategy 2015 – 2025, along with a response from the Department providing a comprehensive picture of the current status of actions in the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) whether racial equality is a priority for their Department; and (ii) when they will progress the introduction of a strategic approach to racial equality.
answer_text: Addressing the needs of all our minority ethnic communities is an important matter and one which has been and will continue to be a priority. Officials have already begun engagement with the Minority Ethnic sector to support the development of the new strategic approach and a public call for views will be launched soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, based on NI Water sampling, whether any contaminated effluent has entered the Quoile River via the adjoining Wastewater Treatment Plant in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what consideration she has given to ring-fence money in the Draft Budget 2025-26 to address waiting times and the provision of care packages.

To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) when the new Learning Disability Framework will be published; and (ii) the budget in place for Health and Social Care Trusts to implement the framework.
answer_text: The Learning Disability Service Framework was a set of standards and indicators that was launched in 2015 to improve the quality of health and social care services. The Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM) is an ongoing project to develop regionally standardised learning disability services for adults. It also aims to recalibrate the programme of care to better support need at an earlier stage in the community. There has been some slippage to the original planned target date for consultation, due to the increased volume of work on learning disability and autism services for children and adults. My officials have outlined that all the necessary work has been undertaken to outline our evidence base, direction of travel and approach for implementation. The final phase is consolidating progress into a single codified document for public consultation. It is my expectation that will be happen within the coming months.It will be necessary to recalibrate how funding is currently spent for adults with learning disabilities. A financial review of the programme of care has entered its final phase, which will be used to inform costings to implement the LDSM. It is likely that transformational funding will be required to enable a shift of services at a Trust level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 20048/22-27, why he did not reference that no bids for in-year monitoring were submitted for the Fisheries and Seafood Development Programme during Adjournment Debate on 26 November 2024, despite stating in COR-1419-2024 that officials are continuing to explore opportunities to obtain the necessary budget through in-year monitoring processes and that he would provide further updates in due course.
answer_text: During the adjournment debate on 26 November 2024, I advised that my Department’s budget for 2024/25 was significantly less than the bids received and priority had to be given to meeting statutory obligations. Consequently, there was, unfortunately, no additional budget to take forward new work such as the harbour development programme, where the projected cost of delivery had increased from £98 million to £143 million. I advised that I had asked my officials to consider alternative options that provide investment on a scale that was affordable and enabled the harbours to be modernised. The cost of undertaking the environmental and technical studies to inform the Outline Business Case is now estimated to be £1.8m.  My officials explored undertaking this work across 2024/25 and 2025/26 financial years.  No bid was made during the monitoring rounds because the £400,000 needed for 2024/25 could have been obtained from internal reallocations but this would have resulted in an unaffordable financial commitment for 2025/26.My officials are continuing to explore options that will provide affordable improvements to our fishery harbours and will be engaging with stakeholders throughout the process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 20048/22-27, whether the Fisheries and Seafood Development Programme has now been cancelled.
answer_text: The Fishing and Seafood Development Programme has not been cancelled.  However, as a result of the increased construction costs outlined in response to AQW 20048/22-27, the estimated cost of appointing Integrated Consultant Teams and undertaking the environmental and technical studies, has increased to £1.8m.  This exceeds the £1.47m expenditure that has been approved by the Department of Finance, hence the necessity to reconsider the options proposed in the Strategic Outline Case.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the reform of fishing licences at Strangford Lough; (ii) to detail the timescales for this work being completed; and (iii) when new applicants will be able to seek licences for fishing at Strangford Lough.
answer_text: The Department has a statutory requirement under the Fisheries Act 2020 and Annex A of the Joint Fisheries Statement to prepare and publish a Northern Ireland Non-Quota Shellfish Fisheries Management Plan (FMP). A public consultation on the draft FMP is planned for later this year. This FMP will take into consideration the existing stock assessments and scientific advice for the inshore stocks and consider issues such as effort control to ensure the future sustainability of the inshore fisheries. It will set out goals and objectives for the future sustainability of the key inshore shellfish stocks such as lobster and crab in the Northern Ireland inshore zone, including Strangford Lough. Notwithstanding the work in developing this FMP, should scientific advice indicate that new permits to fish Strangford Lough would enable fishing therein to continue on a sustainable basis, a fair and equitable application process will be developed for those fishers with an interest in fishing commercially in the Lough.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the process by which watercourses receive a classification as designated via the Drainage Council.
answer_text: The Drainage Council determines which watercourses and sea defences should be maintained by my Department at public expense.A request to designate a watercourse may be put forward by a member of the public or Government Department.  On receipt of an application to designate a watercourse, my Department’s Rivers Directorate will prepare a submission and make a recommendation to the Drainage Council, based on the criteria for designation, which is available on my Department’s website at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/what-drainage-council#toc-0.  The Drainage Council will consider the request for designation and the Department’s recommendation and make a decision at a future meeting.In deciding to designate a watercourse the Drainage Council must be satisfied that any drainage works needed are outside the capability of landowners and that works at public expense on the watercourse would provide value for money.  The decision of the Drainage Council is open to review by the Lands Tribunal on appeal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the protections that are afforded to the site of Knock Iveagh; (ii) whether the site could be vested and taken into state care; and (iii) why the monument is not deemed an area of significant archaeological interest.
answer_text: Knock Iveagh prehistoric burial cairn is a Scheduled Monument under the provisions of the Historic Monuments and Archaeological Objects (NI) Order 1995 (‘the 1995 Order’); it is a criminal offence to carry out unauthorised works within the scheduled area. My officials monitor the condition of the monument, any proposed works affecting the scheduled monument require the consent of my Department.  Under the 1995 Order the Department has the powers to acquire monuments by agreement or compulsorily, by way of gift, or to be appointed as guardian of a monument. In this case the continued scheduled monument status of Knock Iveagh provides appropriate protection. Scheduling provides similar legal protection as would be provided if the monument were in State Care. Areas of Significant Archaeological Interest, as per the Strategic Planning Policy Statement, are designated by local councils through the Local Development Plan Process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the encompass programme has capacity to detail (i) whether someone is on a particular medication; and (ii) whether someone has a specific diagnosed condition.
answer_text: The regional Electronic Patient Record (EPR) for Acute, Community and Mental Health sectors, being implemented by the encompass programme, has the capacity to detail both the conditions a person is diagnosed with and their medications if the information has been recorded on the system. If a person has not had a recent interaction with services that use the EPR then there may not yet be a diagnosis recorded within the system. This situation will change over time with role out of the programme and more patient care being recorded on the EPR.Medication prescribed by staff using the EPR is visible and staff working in the HSC Trusts can also see what has been prescribed in primary care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work that has been carried out to regulate the (i) prescribing; and (ii) dispensing of sodium valproate.
answer_text: There have been a number of measures over recent years to regulate the prescribing and dispensing of sodium valproate with the aim of reducing the known risks to an unborn baby associated with exposure to valproate during pregnancy.  These include:In 2018, the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) advised that medicines containing valproate must not be used in any woman or girl able to have children unless the conditions of the Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PPP) are fulfilled. From January 2024, following advice from the Commission on Human Medicines and an expert group, the MHRA introduced new regulatory measures that valproate must not be started in new patients (male or female) younger than 55 years, unless two specialists independently consider and document that there is no other effective or tolerated treatment, or there are compelling reasons that the reproductive risks do not apply. At their next annual specialist review, women of childbearing potential and girls should be reviewed using a revised valproate Risk Acknowledgement Form.In January 2024, the MHRA introduced new safety and educational materials for men, women and healthcare professionals to reduce the harms from valproate, including the significant risk of serious harm to a baby if taken during pregnancy and the risk of impaired fertility in males.From June 2024, legislative changes apply in Northern Ireland that require manufacturer’s original full pack dispensing of valproate-contained medicines to help ensure that patients taking valproate-containing medicines have access to information setting out the risks and need for patients of childbearing potential to fulfil the conditions of the PPP.In September 2024, the MHRA issued precautionary advice recommending that male patients and their partner use effective contraception during valproate treatment and for at least three months after stopping valproate, due to a possible association between valproate use by men and a small increased risk of a range of neurodevelopmental disorders in their children.The Department continues to work with Health and Social Care partners to improve the safety of medicines in pregnancy more widely and ensure that sodium valproate is only prescribed where clinically appropriate, including communications with professionals to support new regulatory measures to reduce the known harms from valproate.  In Northern Ireland a Valproate Implementation Group (VIG), jointly led by the PHA and the SPPG, lead on the delivery of the Valproate safety programme, co-ordinating and implementing MHRA regulatory changes, working with BSO, HSC Trusts, Primary Care and other relevant organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the discharge consent agreement between NIEA and NI Water for effluent that is discharged into the Quoile River, Downpatrick.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates discharges to the water environment under the Water Order (as amended) 1999. These Consents require dischargers to treat sewage to a level that can be sustained by the receiving environment and to maintain their treatments to avoid pollution.The Wastewater Treatment Works (WwTW) at Downpatrick is owned, operated and maintained by NI Water. Downpatrick WwTW final treated effluent and storm overflow both discharge to the Quoile River under a Water Order Consent. The detail of this Water Order discharge consent, Consent No 6456/2007, is on the Public Register and it is available for viewing at NIEA’s offices. An appointment to view can be arranged by contacting the office by email: WaterRegulationCorrespondence@daera-ni.gov.uk
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he submitted (i) a £1.6 million bid; and (ii) any other bid for the Fisheries and Seafood Development Programme via the two in-year monitoring rounds in 2024.
answer_text: The estimated cost of the of the capital investment for Kilkeel and Ardglass has increased from £98m when originally estimated in 2021/22 to £143m. In light of the increased costs and the need to ensure the infrastructure projects will support the fisheries sector in the transition towards decarbonisation, my officials are engaging with the Northern Ireland Fishery Harbour Authority to consider alternative options to those proposed in the Fishing and Seafood Development Programme Strategic Outline Case.  As a result no bids were submitted as part of the in-year monitoring rounds in 2024.  As part of Budget 2024/25, a bid of £1.2m was identified within my Department to progress the appointment of integrated consultant teams for the Kilkeel and Ardglass harbour projects and to undertake the necessary environmental and technical studies. However, the budget allocated to DAERA for this year was significantly less than the bids received and priority had to be given to statutory obligations and existing commitments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 19468/22-27, to detail the findings of each sampling of effluent in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) sampling; and (ii) year.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:There are two types of samples collected for regulatory wastewater compliance assessment, namely spot samples for assessment of the Water Order Consent compliance and composite samples for assessment of compliance against the Urban Wastewater Treatment Regulations (UWWTR).The findings from the wastewater effluent monitoring at Downpatrick wastewater treatment works (WwTW) for the past 5 years, 2020 to 2024 inclusive, are summarised below in Tables 1 and 2.Table 1: Water Order Consent Spot SamplesNo. of Parameter Exceedances Recorded / YearParameter20202021202220232024Ammonia11000Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)01100Suspended Solids (SS)00100Dichlorvos00000Iron (Soluble)00000Zinc00000Total Phosphorus (annual average)00000Total Nitrogen (annual average)00000Hydrogen Ion (pH)00000Table 2: UWWTR Composite SamplesNo. of Parameter Exceedances Recorded / YearParameter20202021202220232024Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)00000Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)00000Total Phosphorus (annual average)00000Total Nitrogen (annual average)00000The overall compliance assessment for wastewater is based on the Urban Wastewater Treatment Directive’s 95th percentile look-up tables, which means that a wastewater treatment works is permitted to record a given number of exceedances in any calendar year.  Therefore a works is only classified as failing when the number of permitted parameter exceedances is breached.   For Downpatrick WwTW, there were no occasions in the last 5 years when the number of individual parameter exceedances breached the number of permitted fails, meaning the works has been assessed as compliant with the regulatory quality discharge standards for this period.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the impact that the Terrorism (Protection of Premises) Bill, currently under consideration in Westminster, will have in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 10 – AQO 1326/22-27		ANSWERThe Terrorism (Protection of Premises) Bill is a Westminster Bill and the outworkings of the Bill are not for the Department of Justice, they are for other Ministerial Departments to engage directly with Home Office. The Department of Justice are not the conduit for this Bill.As Justice Minister, these statutory obligations sit out with my remit, and therefore on the 9th October 2024, I wrote to Ministerial Colleagues to recommend that their officials proactively engage directly with the Home Office on the elements of the Bill within their Departmental areas of responsibility.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18406/22-27, to detail the last time the Quoile River bed was (i) desilted; and (ii) dredged.
answer_text: My Department carries out a rolling programme of routine inspection and maintenance works in relation to  this designated watercourse and associated infrastructure within the Quoile River catchment, using environmentally sensitive river engineering techniques. Whilst inspections showed the Quoile River to be free flowing during an inspection on the 16th September 2024, pre-emptive maintenance work was deemed necessary.  As a result, extensive watercourse maintenance has been undertaken in the Downpatrick area including on the Plank Drain, Saul Street Stream and the Quoile River. Maintenance works at these locations has included the removal of aquatic weed, obstructions from the channel that may restrict the free flow of water with time and, vegetation management. The Quoile Barrier, at Hare Island, has also had routine maintenance, with all tidal gates being cleaned and serviced. This maintenance work to maintain the free flow of water should not be confused with dredging and desilting, which as previously explained is not considered by my Department’s Engineers’ to be an effective measure to improve the flow of drainage water from Downpatrick; a view supported by an independent consultant and referenced within the Review of South East Flooding Autumn 2023 report.In line with effective measures, my Department has not undertaken any significant dredging / desilting of the Quoile River since approximately 1991. If there is some particular maintenance concern regarding the Quoile River you wish to raise, please do so by contacting the Lisburn Area office directly on 028 9260 6100 or via email: rivers.lisburn@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the criteria in place for a property to be accepted as a place of emergency accommodation.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The NIHE does not provide emergency accommodation but rather provides temporary accommodation as is our statutory duty as outlined in the Housing (NI) Order 1988. The Housing Executive avails of a number of different temporary accommodation options when facilitating placements for households who are homeless or threatened with homelessness. In the acquisition of Single Lets the Housing Executive will inspect the property and ensure that the following criteria is met: Proof of ownership such as solicitor’s letter or copy of deedsElectric meter – meter card typeSmoke AlarmsGas Safe or Oil Boiler test certificateCarbon monoxide alarmsProof of Public Liability InsuranceConfirmation that provider is registered on the Landlord Registration schemeConfirmation that property is registered with Land & Property Services (LPS)Provision of fire blanket or extinguisher in the kitchenIf any of the above criteria is not met, and/or the inspection at the point of acquisition is unsatisfactory, the Housing Executive will not use the property.The Housing Executive also avails of a range of temporary accommodation options provided by the voluntary sector. These services are funded and managed by Supporting People to ensure quality support is delivered and that the temporary accommodation is appropriate for households who are homeless or threatened with homelessness. For placements in non-standard accommodation (B&B/Hotels) the Housing Executive is acquiring the use of accommodation in the same manner as other customer groups whereby the respective Hotel / B&B is subject to the relevant checks and accreditation in order to operate as a short-term accommodation. The Housing Executive is not responsible for such inspections.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of whether a property that is adjacent to a school should be utilised as a place of emergency accommodation.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The NIHE does not provide emergency accommodation but provides temporary accommodation as is its statutory duty as outlined in the Housing (NI) Order 1988. The Housing Executive is mindful of creating or reinforcing stereotypes against the homeless population and therefore there is no general rule applied in respect of the location of temporary accommodation premises. The use of such premises is decided on a case-by-case basis. While there is no general rule applied in respect of the location of temporary accommodation premises, the Criminal Justice Order (NI) 2008 created the Public Protection Arrangements for Northern Ireland (PPANI) that ensure appropriate risk management on the basis of individual applicants. The arrangements bring together a number of agencies and departments including the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), Probation Board for Northern Ireland (PBNI), Prison Service, Health & Social Care Boards and Health & Social Care Trusts, Northern Ireland Housing Executive, NSPCC and a number of other agencies to work together to provide effective assessment and management of the risks posed by certain sexual and violent offenders, including individuals who have committed violent offences within a domestic setting.It should be noted that in respect of housing individuals with relevant sex offences within PPANI, the decision as to whether an offer of accommodation is suitable is a matter for the relevant PPANI agency, primarily the PSNI or PBNI.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount spent on emergency accommodation in each of the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18405/22-27, whether regular testing is carried out to determine if any discharges are contaminated.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water has advised that the continuous final treated effluent discharge from Downpatrick wastewater treatment works (WwTW), into the Quoile River, is subject to an operator self-monitoring announced sampling programme, agreed with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA). The continuous final treated effluent discharge from Downpatrick WwTW has a sample frequency of 24 times per year and the sampling parameters are based on the standards in the discharge consent, issued by NIEA. NI Water’s sampling, including sample collection and analysis, is UKAS (United Kingdom Accreditation Service) accredited. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of emergency accommodation properties in Northern Ireland, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to my response to AQW 16697/22-27 which was answered on 31 October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18405/22-27, whether contaminated effluent enters the Quoile River from this discharge pipe.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:NI Water has advised that Downpatrick wastewater treatment works currently treats wastewater in line with the parameters and limits set within its discharge consent, issued by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency.		For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the proposed school street scheme for Edward Street, Downpatrick; and (ii) when his Department will meet with locally elected representatives on this issue.
answer_text: My Department commissioned a study to establish if any viable road safety and traffic progression improvements could be made along Edward Street in Downpatrick. Part of this study involved the development of a range of options to alleviate the current traffic situation on the local road network, including consideration of a School Street scheme. This study has now been completed, and officials in my Department are currently analysing its findings.  Arrangements will be made for officials to meet with representatives from the local District Electoral Area to discuss the report’s recommendations on conclusion of this analysis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the Health and Safety Executive were consulted by the NIEA as part of the initial waste management licence application LN/13/35.
answer_text: Under the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, NIEA are required to refer the proposal to HSENI before granting the licence. HSENI may or may not comment on such consultations. In this case HSENI did not receive a consultation.
