question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the 25 per cent increase in the past year in the number of patients waiting for echocardiography to over 16,000 as detailed in the waiting list figures for September 2024, to detail the measures he is taking to address this waiting list.
answer_text: Measures being taken to address echocardiography waiting lists are active monitoring of the service delivery and engagement with Health and Social Care Trusts requiring additional support. As part echocardiography waiting list management, HSC Trusts have been provided with additional non-recurrent funding to facilitate waiting list initiatives and two HSC Trusts have received recurrent funding for the recruitment of additional clinical physiologists. A regional clinical physiologist workforce review relating specifically to transthoracic echocardiography, has also commenced.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will commit to a comprehensive review, led by qualified experts, of alternative funding and governance arrangements for NI Water, as encouraged by the Northern Ireland Audit Office in its report Funding water infrastructure in Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline any public consultations undertaken regarding changes to the operating model of the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service cover from February 2025 in the South-Eastern Division.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) regularly examines all available data to determine how best to manage resources so that it can provide the best possible service for patients.As part of their ongoing review of performance NIAS has identified periods where demand exceeds capacity to respond to calls, and at other times their capacity exceeds the demand on the service.A review of data has indicated that demand on resources is greatest between 2pm and 5pm and least between 2am and 6am. In order therefore to better match capacity with demand NIAS are proposing to re-align resources.This re-alignment of the hours of cover does not result in a reduction in the total hours of cover.The initiative will run on a pilot basis in the South Eastern area for a period of 48 weeks from February 2025. In order to facilitate this re-alignment, there is a requirement to restructure staff rotas. NIAS has undertaken significant engagement with affected staff and their Trade Union representatives.As there is no removal or reduction in the hours of service, there is no requirement for public consultation on the proposed change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the funding position for the Royal Victoria Hospital endocrinology unit specialising in gender reassignment in each of the last five years; and (ii) whether there is a projected budget for the service for the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust have advised that the funded position in respect of the endocrinology provision for Gender Identity services is as follows:The funded endocrinology component of the Gender Identity service* in the Belfast Trust in the last five years has been for the provision of 0.2 WTE Consultant, which would equate to approximately £31,392 at current 2024/25 standard staff general costing rates.  *Please note Belfast Trust provides Gender Identity services only and does not provide gender reassignment surgery. NI commissions gender reassignment surgery as part of a UK Four Nations contract, delivered by NHS England.The 2025-26 budget has not yet been agreed by the NI Executive however discussions are ongoing between the Trust and SPPG to decide how this service will be delivered in the future. During this financial year (2024/25), Belfast Trust has been working with colleagues within SPPG to develop a business case for the further development of Gender Identity services for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether NI Water has conducted an investigation into leachate into Carlingford Lough from Waste Licence LN/13/35 in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 18048/22-27, to detail the date on which (i) the waste licence operator requested the increase in maximum storage capacity; and (ii) the NIEA agreed the increase in maximum storage capacity.
answer_text: NIEA received the request from the waste licence holder to increase the maximum storage capacity on 16 February 2022.		NIEA agreed the increase in maximum storage capacity on 3 August 2023 and issued a modification to the waste licence holder on 7 September 2023.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the process by which the NIEA would amend a waste licence; and (ii) how much non-compliance would be required for this to happen.
answer_text: A waste management licence may be amended in accordance with Article 10 of the Waste and Contaminated Land (NI) Order 1997.In accordance with the DAERA Enforcement Policy, the department takes a graduated and proportionate approach to dealing with non-compliance with conditions of a waste management licence. The DAERA Enforcement Policy can be viewed at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/daera-enforcement-policy. In most cases the department will seek to work with the operator of a waste management licence to address non-compliance effectively and efficiently as possible, before considering more formal enforcement action.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients that died while awaiting an ambulance in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Further to the response provided on 9 December 2024, it has been brought to my attention that the figures provided in the original response were inaccurate.  Accurate figures have now been provided in the table below.The table below shows the number of incidents reported where a patient has passed away while awaiting an ambulance over the past five financial years.YearNumber of Incidents2020-2142021-2282022-23172023-24312024-25 (To 21/02/25)10Sadly, given the nature of the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service’s work, patients do, at times, pass away while awaiting an ambulance to arrive. However, the determination as to whether a delayed ambulance response has directly contributed to a patient’s death is complex and requires the consideration of a number of factors. As part of NIAS’s adverse incident management procedures, all reported incidents of patient death are evaluated to assess whether the timeliness of ambulance response may have impacted the outcome, and, where required, learning and corrective actions are implemented to service delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to review the ratios and numbers of children that a childminder can look after.
answer_text: As part of the forthcoming review of the Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care I will be seeking views on adult:child ratios through a public consultation exercise early in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when his Department intends to publish the report into childminding, including the recommendations for the sector.
answer_text: My officials will be taking forward a review of the Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care in the coming months. As part of the review, we will seek views on matters relating to vetting and adult:child ratios through a public consultation exercise early in the new year. Separately, officials will seek views from a range of stakeholders on the wider Minimum Standards and produce a revised set of Standards for consultation later in 2025. Any proposed changes will be made in the context of my overall priority of ensuring that childcare remains a safe, high quality experience for children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to install a bereavement room within the Regional Fertility Centre at the Royal Victoria Hospital.
answer_text: In the Regional Fertility Clinic based in the Royal Maternity Hospital, Belfast HSC Trust, patients who are being advised that their fertility treatment has not been successful are taken into a private clinical room with their Consultant and they are supported by the Nursing Team.A quiet room / non-clinical room is not available for use when sharing upsetting news on treatment failures due to current space constraints in the Royal Maternity Hospital.  A quiet, non-clinical room will be included in an expansion plan for the Regional Fertility Centre in the overall expanded footprint for Royal Maternity Hospital services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the policy overseeing temporary promotions within the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work that has been undertaken to further enhance service users' experience of the Regional Fertility Centre at the Royal Victoria Hospital.
answer_text: The Regional Fertility Centre (RFC) is compliant with the Human Fertilisation & Embryology Authority (HFEA) Regulations which stipulates the requirement to select opportunities for improvement and implement any necessary actions to enhance customer satisfaction in line with the ISO 9001 and ISO 15189 British Standards as of October 2024.The Regional Fertility Centre (RFC) actively seek to further enhance service users’ experience by regularly engaging with service users to seek their valuable feedback.  In addition, the Centre carries out bi-annual stakeholder engagement surveys to seek feedback from Health Care Professionals in other HSC Trusts and General Practitioners to enhance service users’ experience.Activities have included completing a range of patient surveys in line with the National Standard to determine patients views on outpatient care, in clinic treatment and care, and Andrology services.  Most recently identified areas for improvement arising from patient survey feedback include waiting times, information on expected timeframes for first referral to treatment and options for patients to contact the RFC admin team more efficiently.   These issues are being addressed by the RFC team.    In the last year the RFC have undertaken a range of actions to address feedback, this has included:creating a video for a RFC and Andrology Clinic walkthrough which provides patients with an insight on what to expect when they begin their treatment journey in the RFC;a Patient Support Policy developed and available to patients on the website;a website with signposting and patient information – regular review of information to ensure this is up to date including a monthly update of waiting times. This includes a twice yearly audit of the website;quarterly Governance to monitor patient feedback, comments and complaints. All complaints involve a root cause analysis and corrective and preventive actions being put in place to address any failures in systems and make the patient experience better;Quality Objectives where necessary to monitor quality improvements;regular Clinical meetings to share learning of incidents and complaints, governance reports, patient feedback and ways to continually improve;‘Every Customer Counts’ – improved information to patents on how they can ask for reasonable adjustments; as a result of undertaking a Quality Improvement project:delivered a training session for RFC staff on Disability Awareness; added a question to the Patient Details Form, so that patients have the opportunity to inform the RFC if they need support at their first appointment; updated the Patient Support Policy to include reasonable adjustments that the RFC can make;created the RFC Patient Information Video which is now on the website and includes information on available support and adjustments that can be made for patients with subtitles and language translation options if required;made patients aware of the sighted guide service by putting this poste

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the expected cost impact for General Practices of the changes to employer National Insurance contribution rates from April 2025; and (ii) the plans his Department has to mitigate any negative impact on General Practices.
answer_text: I recognise that the changes arising from the Autumn Budget will have a significant impact on future budget and costs.  My Department is currently undertaking work to assess the potential impact of the changes to employer National Insurance on Primary Care providers however this is not yet complete.It is not possible to provide a definitive assessment on our future funding position until a budget settlement for future years has been reached. Despite the positive news about the 2024/25 budget, it is still likely we will be facing a significant funding gap in 2025/26 as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand.I acknowledge there are a number of healthcare providers that will be affected by the rise in employer National Insurance contributions announced in the Budget and I understand that as well as GPs, General Dental Practitioners, care homes and hospices have voiced concerns about the impact this will have on them.  Recognising this and the valuable role played by those providers, these funding pressures will nevertheless need to be considered and prioritised alongside other priorities before I am in a position to make any commitments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether consideration has been given to finding an alternative operator for the Quoile Countryside Centre, such as Newry, Mourne and Down District Council.
answer_text: My officials have had initial discussions around the potential for the Quoile Countryside Centre to be re-opened by a third-party operator.  Discussions are ongoing.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the consultation that was carried out with (a) the public; and (b) Tourism NI in relation to the closure of the Quoile Countryside Centre; and (ii) any assessment made on the impact the closure might have on tourism at the wider site.
answer_text: My officials have confirmed there was no legal requirement to consult the public or Tourism NI regarding the closure of the Quoile Countryside Centre and no such consultation was undertaken when the Centre was closed in 2020 (initially because of the COVID-19 pandemic).  In respect of tourism in the area, toilet and car parking facilities onsite remain open for public use.  My officials have confirmed that experience shows that most visitors to the site come to the area for outdoor recreation purposes and the closure of the centre has no bearing on this.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the annual visitor numbers to the Quoile Countryside Centre in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Since 2020 the centre has been closed to members of the public. Information relating to visitor numbers prior to this is not held.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of public money spent on (i) creating the Quoile Countryside Centre; (ii) operating the site; and (iii) maintenance costs for the site in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: (i)Due to the age of the Quoile Countryside Centre, my Department does not hold financial details regarding the cost of its creation. (ii) When open to the public, operational costs for a fully staffed Quoile Countryside Centre would be circa £165,000 per annum.  Current costs for operation of the Quoile Countryside Centre, which has been closed to public access since 2020, are circa £50,000 per annum. (iii) From the information available to my officials, maintenance costs have been as follows: Financial Year:Maintenance cost (£):2015/1614,149.002016/178,880.452017/183,381.72018/1915,142.762019/2016,430.102020/2116,241.872021/225,360.532022/2310,560.622023/248,854.422024/252,007.56 (to date)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail each time the Quoile River bed has (i) been desilted; and (ii) had sediment removed in each of the last five years.
answer_text: A key limiting factor to the drainage of the Quoile River is the average water level in Strangford Lough, dredging would have no positive impact on flood levels as the riverbed would be below the level in Strangford Lough, would fill with water, and not improve the drainage of flood water from Downpatrick. As such my Department has not undertaken any significant dredging / desilting works over the last 5 years. That said we did remove flood debris from the Quoile River that accumulated during the flooding event in Autumn 2023.My Department’s Rivers Directorate will continue its inspection and maintenance regime in relation to designated watercourses and their associated infrastructure within the Quoile River catchment. If an inspection determines that there is a blockage significantly impeding the free flow of water within the watercourse or that drainage infrastructure is in an unsatisfactory condition, then works will be programmed.If there is some particular maintenance concern regarding the Quoile River you wish to raise, please do so by contacting the Lisburn Area office directly on 028 9260 6100 or via email: rivers.lisburn@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 18048/22-27, whether he is content that the working plan for this site poses no risk to human health and the environment.
answer_text: The operators of authorised waste facilities are required to have a working plan that details the potential environmental impacts of operations on the immediate and surrounding environment and locale and should address the substantive measures in place to prevent, minimise and control these environmental impacts.The department considered the initial working plan, submitted for LN/13/15 in 2013, and consulted with relevant stakeholders. The department was content that the initial working plan and subsequent amendments to it, met the requirements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether the Health and Safety Executive has licenced and regulated the use of (a) Hydrodor; (b) ReFresh New Mown Grass; (c) ONA Liquid; (d) Hydrocor 204; and (e) Cypermethrin at the operating location of waste licence WM/13/35; and (ii) how long these have been in use for.
answer_text: Hydrodor / ReFresh New Mown Grass and / Ona LiquidThe Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI) is not  required to issue licences for these products or their use in the North of Ireland. None of these products are classified as being hazardous under the Classification, Labelling and Packaging Regulations (CLP). As HSENI does not licence or regulate these products, it does not hold information on how long these products have been in use for.Hydrocor 204 This is a corrosion inhibitor product for use in enclosed water systems. HSENI is not required to issue a licence for this product.As HSENI does not licence or regulate these products, it does not hold information on how long these products have been in use for.CypermethrinThis is an active substance approved by the European Commission for use in biocidal products coming under the scope of the Biocidal Products Regulation EC528/2012 (EU)BPR. HSENI does not approve or licence the use of active substances.    Cypermethrin was approved as an active substance under (EU)BPR on 1st June 2020 and is approved until 31st May 2030.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the current Health and Safety Executive guidelines for the spraying of Hydrocor 204 in areas where it reaches the public; (ii) whether a licence is required to undertake such spraying; and (iii) whether any licences have been issued in the last three years.
answer_text: The Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI) does not issue any guidelines on spraying of Hydrocor 204. This is not an activity licenced by my department. This is not information held by my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Downpatrick Wastewater Treatment Works has a discharge pipe into the Quoile River.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water has confirmed that Downpatrick wastewater treatment works has a discharge pipe into the Quoile River.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 18048/22-27, to detail (i) the written assessment provided by the licence holder in relation to the increase of maximum storage capacity at any one time from 10,000 tonnes to 20,000 tonnes that implementing the proposed change to the working plan would have on the risk posed by the site to human health and the environment; and (ii) the assessment that was provided by the NIEA in response to this.
answer_text: The holders of waste management licences are required to have a site working plan that details how potential environmental impacts and/or pollution risks are managed and should also address the substantive measures in place to prevent, minimise and control these. The department considered the initial working plan, submitted in 2013 for LN/13/35, and referred this to relevant bodies as part of consultation. The department was content that the initial working plan and subsequent amendments to it, met their requirements. The department requirements for working plans for licensed waste facilities can be found herewww.daera-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/daera/working-plan-guidance-june-2017_0.pdf Please refer to AQW 18048/22/27.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 18048/22-27, to detail his Department’s written consent that was provided to the licence holder for an increase in storage capacity at any one time.
answer_text: Written consent for the increase to storage capacity was formally approved in August 2023 and issued to the operator on the 7 September 2023, when it was included as part of the NIEA revision to waste management licence LN/13/35 concerning annual throughputs at the site.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he submitted a bid to the October Monitoring Round for the restoration of the cross-border healthcare directive.
answer_text: The Department of Health reported, during October Monitoring, a total short fall of £450m.  This included £130m to fund the continuation of existing services and £320m for non-contractual pay to match the English pay offers.Recognising that the funding available was unlikely to cover existing pressures, including pay, the Department of Finance did not invite bids at October monitoring outside of these pressures.  However, given its prominence in the Programme for Government the Department did submit a bid for waiting lists. This bid was unsuccessful. Given the severity of the financial position for both the Department and the wider Northern Ireland Block no other bids were submitted. If funding for waiting lists had been made available, investment would be targeted on those areas deemed to be of highest priority in order to maximise clinical outcomes. Given the significance of urgent and red flag waiting referrals, it is not likely that a cross border scheme could be prioritised at this time

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the cross-border healthcare directive will be restored.
answer_text: The Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme was introduced on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce NI waiting lists. The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive which ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU.A bid for funding for a replacement scheme was submitted to the Department of Finance during this year’s budget setting process. The Reimbursement Scheme bid of £31million was part of a wider bid for £135million of additional funding to tackle waiting lists. It was intended that a new reimbursement scheme could have treated over 4,000 patients this financial year. This was in addition to a separate bid of £53million, within the wider £135million, specifically to address some 4,400 patients waiting over 2 years for orthopaedic procedures. The financial pressures across the Health and Social Care (HSC) system are certainly severe and the 2024/25 budget outcome falls far short of what is needed to tackle the unacceptable waiting lists. The funding allocated to my Department in June monitoring was insufficient to enable me to cover the cost of delivering existing services. My Department has continued to seek additional funding to be provided to assist with addressing waiting lists. A £10m bid was submitted as part of October Monitoring which unfortunately was unsuccessful. As such, there is insufficient funding at present to reinstate a cross-border reimbursement scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 17331/22-27 and AQW 17332/22-27, to detail (i) the stages undertaken in the Directed Statutory Functions process; and (ii) how this process will help to improve short-break and respite availability in Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: (i) The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the Department of Health has specific responsibilities with regard to the professional oversight, governance, performance management and accountability and strategic overview of HSC Trusts in relation to the exercise of statutory functions across both children’s and adults programmes of care. The Trust’s Annual Directed Statutory Functions (DSF) reports provide an annual review of the discharge of the statutory functions and highlights areas where compliance has not been achieved. The SPPG has put in place a system to measure performance of Trusts throughout the year. This system is based on the identification of challenges and pressures across social care.  Key areas of non-compliance in terms of performance and service delivery are set out in each Trust’s DSF Action Plan, and are the subject of monitoring throughout the year. The SPPG teams meet with HSC Trust Managers during the year to review the specific DSF Trust Action Plans, and to agree on measures to be put in place to work towards compliance.  Therefore, the Action Plan forms the basis of regular meetings during the year to review progress and identify and agree remedial action.If there are specific issues which are proving difficult to resolve, the SPPG will consider these for escalation to the Performance Management Directorate in SPPG for review at the regular meetings between SPPG and Trusts in relation to their Service Delivery Plan (SDP).(ii) With regard to how the monitoring of the Directed Statutory Functions process can improve Short Break and Respite Availability. The analysis of the data that Trusts provide to SPPG in terms of service delivery ensures that areas of specific concern are formally identified and plans put in place to work towards improvement. The annual DSF overview report (1st April 2023 – 31st March 2024) had identified that services for children with a disability face particular challenge. Through the reporting and monitoring of the Statutory Functions process – it was identified that Residential Short Break availability for children with a disability has been impacted by increased demand for short breaks and insufficient capacity of Trusts to meet the rising need. In addition, it was noted that four out of the five Trusts (with the exception of the Southern HSC Trust) have experienced significant disruption to their Short break’s facilities, and capacity to provide these. Children with disability services have also been impacted by workforce vacancies.Short Break capacity, unallocated cases and vacancies remain a focus of DSF in 24/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 17423/22-27, to detail (i) when waste licence LN/13/35 was amended to increase the maximum storage capacity from 10,000 tonnes to 20,000 tonnes; and (ii) why this variation was not noted in the Minister’s original response.
answer_text: The increase to on-site storage capacity from 10,000 tonnes to 20,000 tonnes was requested by the operator through an amendment to the site working plan, submitted to the NIEA in February 2022. However, due to an administrative oversight, the submission was not logged by the NIEA until November 2022. The amendment to the working plan was considered together with a modification application made by the operator in May 2023. This was subsequently formally approved in August 2023 and issued to the operator on the 7 September 2023, when it was included as part of the NIEA revision to waste management licence LN/13/35 also concerning annual throughputs at the site.		As per part (i).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the timescale by which the Children With Disability Workstream will (a) complete its work; and (b) report on its findings; and (ii) whether this will be made available to Members.
answer_text: (a) The Children with Disability Workstream is currently undertaking exploratory work and will develop to having a continued implementation reform role. The workstream has no set end date for completion. (b) Several reports have been completed detailing capacity, demand, threshold and access criteria across Trusts to inform strategic planning.The Department has and will continue to share findings with the Health Committee. Officials are submitting a written briefing on Children with Disabilities to the Committee in the coming weeks and are scheduled to provide a verbal briefing in March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of incidents of non-compliance the Department has identified through the Directed Statutory Functions process in each of the last five years, broken down by (a) Health and Social Care Trust; (b) the nature of non-compliance; (c) year; and (ii) the subsequent actions taken to address these incidents.
answer_text: This information is not readily available in the format requested. The HSCTs provide an annual statutory functions report to the Department’s SPPG which includes areas of non-compliance across all programmes of care working in adult and children’s services, however this does not provide a breakdown of all individual incidents. Over the last few years SPPG have been working with HSCTs to develop an improved report format to provide better quality data on areas of compliance.  Due to this development, it is currently difficult to compare data across a five year period.  However, some key areas of information are included in the table below:Children ServicesThemeStatusNumber of unallocated cases 588 in April 2021 to 1571 in March 2024Number of unregulated kinship care placements342 in March 2024 due to backlog of uncompleted assessments Number of incidents of the use of unregulated care placements (non-kinship care)172 incidents in 2022/2023 to 219 incidents during 2023/2024Number of monthly visits by allocated social worker in foster care501 in 2023/2024 decreased from 659 in 2022/2023Number of short breaks for children with Disability 1296 children using 17,510 overnight stays in 2019/20 to 592 children using 6364 overnight stays in 2023/24Allocation of Personal Advisor 310 young people waiting allocation in 2023/2024 Adult ServicesRegional psychiatric inpatient occupancy rate107.6% in 2023/2024 causing protracted delays in accessing inpatient careAllocation of home care services 3775 service users waiting for 27,539 homecare hours at March 2024 decreased from 4,482 service users waiting 35,704 hours at March 2023.The statutory function reporting process requires SPPG to meet with HSCT’s to review the report during which action plans are agreed for areas of non-compliance which are kept under regular review throughout the year.  In addition, the DOH leads the Adult Social Care Collaborative Forum and the Children’s Services Strategic Reform Programme Board which provides a formal mechanism for the DOH and representatives across statutory, voluntary/community and private sectors to deliver improvements in social care and address a range of known service challenges including areas of non-compliance.  Furthermore, the Office of Social Services within the DOH leads on social work workforce transformation, some of which support areas of non-compliance relating to social work workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the evaluation that is carried out to analyse the impact of flooding on farms, where compensation is sought.
answer_text: When responding to crises, including flooding crises, government tends only to intervene when the crisis event is infrequent, but catastrophic. In an agricultural context, a flooding event would have to cause significant damage to productive assets or ‘route to market’ infrastructure affecting many or all farmers over a wide area. The negative effects would have to be beyond the capacity of farmers or markets to cope. When making a case for government intervention, the need for, affordability and additional impact of an intervention is evaluated. This includes evidencing the details of the weather event (including why it is defined as ‘exceptional’), evidencing the impact of the event (the sectors and businesses most affected by the event) and also the strategic fit of an intervention, including consideration of the need for government intervention and the additional NI economy-level impact from government intervention. Ultimately, the case seeks to evidence that the provision of assistance would deliver a better outcome for the NI economy than that which would occur in the absence of assistance and, therefore, the likelihood that such an intervention would provide value for money.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any financial support available to potato farmers, impacted by flooding, where compensation is sought.
answer_text: Whilst there is presently no financial support available to potato farmers who have lost crops due to flooding, this is a matter I will keep under review. 		In the event of flooding that causes significant damage to productive assets or ‘route to market’ infrastructure that affects many or all farmers over a wide area, my Department may assess the need for, affordability and additional impact of a financial intervention. Each event is considered on a case-by-case basis.		A key objective of DAERA’s approach through the new Farm Support and Development Programme is to encourage farmers to better manage risk within their own businesses, to become more resilient and better prepared to cope in situations such as bad weather. A Crisis Framework is being developed under the Programme that will enable my Department to assess potential industry impacts such as weather or market failure. It will determine the most appropriate timing and form of intervention necessary for a specific crisis. This will involve setting out trigger points (such as scale of impact or market price reductions) to inform the operation of this Framework.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why Re-Gen Waste was permitted an export of waste increase from 116,000 tonnes to 500,000 tonnes per annum at Warrenpoint Port since February 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail each time waste licence LN/13/35 has been amended, including the nature of the amendments, broken down by licence version.
answer_text: On 5 June 2019, following an application from the licence holder, a variation LN/13/35/V2 was granted. This varied condition 1.3.1 (permitted waste quantities) by reducing the maximum storage capacity from 20,000 to 10,000 tonnes. This was then specified through condition 1.2 (site working plan).  On 7 September 2023, following an application from the licence holder, a variation LN/13/35/V3 was granted. This varied condition 1.3.1 (permitted waste quantities) by amending the maximum annual throughput from 116,244 to 500,000 tonnes per annum.The current version LN/13/35/V3 can be viewed on the waste management licensing public register at https://public-registers.daera-ni.gov.uk/waste-licenses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support being provided to the families that were featured in the BBC Spotlight programme entitled I Am Not Okay, broadcast on 24 September 2024.
answer_text: The South Eastern Trust is aware of the range of challenges faced by the families featured in the BBC Spotlight programme and many more families who are in a similar situation in caring for children and young people with severe learning disabilities. The BBC programme has played a key role in bringing a focus to this critical issue.The South Eastern HSC Trust has confirmed that since 2020 there has been a marked increase in the number of children who require residential care. The upward trajectory in the residential care population has had a significant impact upon the South Eastern Trust’s capacity to deliver residential short breaks, as the residential short breaks homes across the trust have been repurposed into homes that accommodate children who require longer term residential care.  Residential short breaks, are part of a broader range of multidisciplinary support services aimed at supporting families. Residential short breaks are not there to address or respond to the many and varied challenges associated with the spectrum of neuro developmental delay, but do afford families a very valuable period of respite, away from the day to day challenges of their caring role.  The programme identified the reduction or absence of residential short breaks as being a key pressure point for families, and may have left viewers with the impression that there are no additional supports or services being offered.  It is not the South Eastern HSC Trusts intention to articulate each of the young persons assessed need but we have highlighted the core elements of a typical support package that is delivered to families who are caring for young people with severe learning disabilities to assist with the broad range of needs typically faced many families such as;social work servicepsychology led therapeutic service to assist with positive behaviour support plans. community short breaks,community psychiatrycommunity learning disability nursingdirect payments each week to enable parent to directly employ people to take young person on short break outingsfortnightly short breaks with trained nursing staffcogitative behavioural therapytrust funded moderations to the family homes and propertiesprovision of a limited number of residential short breaksprovision of parent support groupscontracted services including after schools, summer schemes, nursery provision, social club services, outreach and in-reach servicesincrease in family network provision supported through CWD SWThe above example of a support package is not untypical of the support packages offered to families with children with very complex presentations.  Additional priorities have been established in relation to the development of short break fostering projects as well as expanding early help, residential short breaks, residential placements and improvements in transition services to adult services.The South Eastern HSC Trust has confirmed that the families featured in the Spotlight programme are in receip

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) when funding for the Duke of Edinburgh scheme through the Education Authority for voluntary grammar and independent schools was withdrawn; (ii) the process by which funding was withdrawn; (iii) when the decision was reached; (iv) when schools were informed of the decision; and (v) whether he will reconsider this decision.
answer_text: when funding for the Duke of Edinburgh scheme through the Education Authority for voluntary grammar and independent schools was withdrawn;the process by which funding was withdrawn;when the decision was reached.There has been no decision taken by my Department or the Education Authority to withdraw funding for the Duke of Edinburgh (DofE) scheme. The DofE Northern Ireland office has notified the Education Authority (EA), that Voluntary Grammar Schools, Grant Maintained Integrated Schools and Independent Schools are not permitted to operate under the current EA Operating Centre Licence as insurance responsibilities cannot be devolved to these individual centres outside of EA’s public liability remit. As a result DofE Northern Ireland advised that a number of schools can no longer remain under the EA DofE Operating Authority Licence and need to become a Directly Licenced Centre. In agreement with DofE Northern Ireland, the EA have written to affected schools. I understand DofE Northern Ireland have at this early stage assured affected schools that they have secured funding for the cost of the licence fee and any other associated costs (staff training, or additional insurance premiums) to ensure that a transition to a Directly Licensed Centre is initially cost neutral for schools and that there is a longer-term plan for ongoing licence fee support.(iv) when schools were informed of the decision: In agreement with DofE Northern Ireland the Education Authority wrote to schools on 22 October 2024 to advise of a change of licensing arrangements for certain schools.(v) whether he will reconsider this decision.	The current position is related to the DofE terms of their Licencing Agreement which is outside my remit. However, discussions are still on-going between the Education Authority and DofE Northern Ireland to clarify remaining concerns in an attempt to resolve this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of his Written Ministerial Statement on Children with Disabilities – Short Breaks, (i) whether this money is going to result in more residential and respite places; and (ii) where these places will be.
answer_text: (i) On 23rd October 2024, I was pleased to be able to approve additional regional funding for the Children with Disability service across all HSC Trusts of up to £13m a year in multi-year funding, including up to £2m for the rest of the current financial year.It is intended that this additional funding will assist with both increasing short breaks capacity and expanding family, therapeutic and behavioural supports in the community across all HSC Trusts.(ii) With regard to the allocation of the funding and where any additional respite places may be – each HSC Trust is currently working intensively with my Department to prioritise their short-term action proposals. This is to confirm financial allocations and delivery plans, which will assist with solutions to improve family support, short breaks, residential care and behavioural support across all HSC Trusts for those families who have children with disability.My Department Officials are currently working with each of the HSC Trusts in terms of the prioritisation of short-term actions, and will continue to work very closely with each of the five HSC Trusts, in terms of the ongoing regional monitoring of Short Break provision for Children with Disability.Department Officials do have a system established to measure the performance of HSC Trusts throughout the year in terms of meeting Directed Statutory Functions (DSF). This is based on the identification of challenges and also identification of non-compliance. The DSF process includes both regular monitoring and aiming to improve the Short Break / Respite availability within HSC Trusts.My Department will be regularly reviewing with each HSC Trust the implementation plan of any proposed actions to assist with addressing the current very concerning issues that exist with access to Short Breaks. There is a Children’s With Disability Workstream, which is seeking to deliver reform within Children’s Social Care services to address a range of known service challenges, particularly in relation to an ever-increasing volume and complexity of case work. This work will also continue to consider the regional short break provision, and the outcomes will inform any future commissioning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department will hold Health and Social Care Trusts to account to deliver respite services for children with a learning disability.
answer_text: (i) On 23rd October 2024, I was pleased to be able to approve additional regional funding for the Children with Disability service across all HSC Trusts of up to £13m a year in multi-year funding, including up to £2m for the rest of the current financial year.It is intended that this additional funding will assist with both increasing short breaks capacity and expanding family, therapeutic and behavioural supports in the community across all HSC Trusts.(ii) With regard to the allocation of the funding and where any additional respite places may be – each HSC Trust is currently working intensively with my Department to prioritise their short-term action proposals. This is to confirm financial allocations and delivery plans, which will assist with solutions to improve family support, short breaks, residential care and behavioural support across all HSC Trusts for those families who have children with disability.My Department Officials are currently working with each of the HSC Trusts in terms of the prioritisation of short-term actions, and will continue to work very closely with each of the five HSC Trusts, in terms of the ongoing regional monitoring of Short Break provision for Children with Disability.Department Officials do have a system established to measure the performance of HSC Trusts throughout the year in terms of meeting Directed Statutory Functions (DSF). This is based on the identification of challenges and also identification of non-compliance. The DSF process includes both regular monitoring and aiming to improve the Short Break / Respite availability within HSC Trusts.My Department will be regularly reviewing with each HSC Trust the implementation plan of any proposed actions to assist with addressing the current very concerning issues that exist with access to Short Breaks. There is a Children’s With Disability Workstream, which is seeking to deliver reform within Children’s Social Care services to address a range of known service challenges, particularly in relation to an ever-increasing volume and complexity of case work. This work will also continue to consider the regional short break provision, and the outcomes will inform any future commissioning.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any in-year monitoring bids their Department made for the Central Good Relations Fund, following a budget reduction last year.

To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the Invest Northern Ireland Annual Report and Accounts 2021-22, in light of the whistleblowing issues previously raised in the report of the Independent Review of Invest Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am assured that Invest NI’s Annual Report and Accounts 2021/22 accurately reflect the position in respect of Whistleblowing/Raising Concerns cases recorded by Invest NI during the accounting period.		Two Whistleblowing/Raising Concerns cases were referred to in the Independent Review of Invest NI report which was published in January 2023. These cases were not recorded in Invest NI’s Annual Report and Accounts 2021/22 for the following reasons:The first case was raised with the then-Chair and then-CEO in November 2021 but there was a delay in identifying it as a Whistleblowing/Raising Concerns case. The Independent Review report noted the delay in Invest NI’s investigating of a matter that was initially raised in November 2021 but not identified as a Whistleblowing/Raising Concerns case until August 2022, after the Annual Report and Accounts for 2021/22 had been finalised.The case was fully investigated and subsequently closed. Invest NI’s Annual Report and Accounts for 2022/23 note that the case was notified to DfE during the course of the financial year and comments on both the delay and the corrective action taken to address this. The second case was raised directly with the Independent Review Panel. This case was not raised with Invest NI and it would not have been appropriate to record it in the Annual Report and Accounts. The case was investigated by DfE’s Fraud and Raising Concerns Branch which did not identify any serious breaches by Invest NI. The case was closed in August 2023.The Independent Review report contained a recommendation for Invest NI to establish a trained, dedicated and appropriately resourced Fraud and Raising Concerns function, and to promote the role of a ‘Raising Concerns Champion’ to act as a confidential contact point and promote a ‘speak up culture’ within the organisation. This recommendation has now been fully implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether those with lived experience and the organisations who support them will be involved in the co-design of respite service, in order to ensure that the services meet the needs of those families.

To ask the Minister of Health, in light of his Written Ministerial Statement on Children with Disabilities – Short Breaks, to detail (i) where the £13 million funding will come from; (ii) whether this money is reallocated from his Department; and (iii) whether there will be any service reduction as a result of this.
answer_text: The funding for Children with Disabilities – Short Breaks was funded for 2024-25 from several easements from across the HSC system that recently have been reported and therefore there is no impact on existing services.It is not possible to provide a definitive response to the future funding position until a Budget settlement for future years has been reached. Whilst my expectation is that the future funding position will not significantly improve for Health this challenging position needs to be offset against the severe service delivery risks in this area, including the potential for increased costs if need is not met in an appropriate manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to his Written Ministerial Statement on Children with Disabilities – Short Breaks, to detail (i) where the funding for this has been taken from; (ii) any impact this will have; and (iii) whether this will result in a loss of service from another section of the health service.
answer_text: The funding for Children with Disabilities – Short Breaks was funded for 2024-25 from several easements from across the HSC system that recently have been reported and therefore there is no impact on existing services.It is not possible to provide a definitive response to the future funding position until a Budget settlement for future years has been reached. Whilst my expectation is that the future funding position will not significantly improve for Health this challenging position needs to be offset against the severe service delivery risks in this area, including the potential for increased costs if need is not met in an appropriate manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment that has been made of (i) the number of people suffering from arthritis; and (ii) the number of working days lost as a result of people being in pain with the condition.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken any assessment of the number of people suffering from arthritis or the number of working days lost as a result of people being in pain with the condition.  Patients with arthritis are managed by primary care, secondary care and tertiary care.  Details are recorded for referrals into rheumatology outpatients, but this would cover a range of conditions and would not be specific to arthritis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any specific support that is routinely offered to patients while they await orthopaedic surgery treatment.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts provide a range of services to support people on waiting lists while they wait for treatment.  This includes information and advice, signposting to additional sources of internal and external support, and access to specific services as appropriate, such as diet and exercise programmes, smoking cessation advice, mental health and wellbeing services and peer support etc.  The updated Elective Care Framework (ECF), published in June 2024, recognises the need to support patients who are on waiting lists to better self-manage the condition they have been referred for, as well as their wider health and well-being needs. It sets out the need for development of a policy in this area, which will aim to ensure patients on waiting lists have the best possible outcomes and, through empowerment and support, ensure that their health is optimised and that they are as prepared and as well as possible for their treatment.  My Department is in the early stages of work in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of fishing licences currently active for Strangford Lough; and (ii) when each of these licences was last used for fishing.
answer_text: For the current calendar year, my Department has issued 11 Strangford Lough Fishing Licenses to individuals, permitting them to fish in Strangford using the vessel nominated on the licence. In 2024, landings of shellfish have been recorded by vessels belonging to 10 of those individuals.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Northern Ireland Inshore Fisheries Management Plan.
answer_text: On 18 December 2024 the UK fisheries policy authorities published the response to a public consultation on proposed amendments to Annex A of the Joint Fisheries Statement (JFS). The consultation response adopted the proposed amendments to the publication dates for the final version of many of the fisheries management plans (FMPs) listed in the JFS. The amendments will enable the fisheries policy authorities to have the time needed to work collaboratively with its key stakeholders to develop and deliver the first versions of these important FMPs. One particular amendment changed the title of the Northern Ireland Inshore FMP to read the Northern Ireland Non-Quota Shellfish FMP to better reflect that its geographical application will apply to all Northern Ireland waters. The species within scope of the FMP remains unchanged and the revised publication date for this FMP is December 2026.My Department is continuing the work in drafting this FMP ahead of a statutory consultation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of people availing of a Home Detention Curfew in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Home Detention Curfew is not applicable in Northern Ireland.  However, since June 2015 the Northern Ireland Prison Service has operated a Conditional Early Release (CER) Scheme, which includes a home curfew condition, under powers contained in Articles 19 and 26 of the Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 2008.  This scheme provides for the release of certain categories of prisoners on a probation officer supervised licence up to 135 days before their custody expiry date.  This CER licence is in place up until the date on which they would have been released ordinarily.  At this point their regular supervised licence comes into effect.CER applicants must meet a range of criteria including a number of statutory and non-statutory exclusions, the latter designed to maximise public confidence in the scheme.  Successful candidates must pass a likelihood of re-offending test; they must have been well behaved during their time in custody; they must have approved, stable and supportive accommodation in the community; and they must have complied fully with all conditions during any prior periods of temporary release. 452 prisoners have been released under the CER Scheme since June 2015.  Of these, three have been recalled to prison during their period of early release, two for alleged further offending and the other because of accommodation issues.  Ten individuals are currently living in the community on a supervised CER licence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 15838/22-27, whether she will, in future, inform constituency MLAs in advance of her Ministerial visits.
answer_text: My diary commitments are not routinely disclosed in advance due to security considerations so, regrettably, I am not in a position to inform constituency MLAs in advance of any Ministerial visits.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 15838/22-27, why there was no correspondence provided to constituency MLAs of the Ministerial visit.
answer_text: My diary commitments are not routinely disclosed in advance of visits due to security considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the current guidelines for the spraying of Hydrocor 204 in areas where it reaches the public; (ii) whether a licence is required to undertake such spraying; and (iii) whether any licences have been issued in the last three years.
answer_text: The spraying of products such as Hydrocor204 relates to the Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003 (COSHH), which falls under the remit of the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland.This is not an activity licensed or regulated by my department.This information is not held by my department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the impact of the Chancellor’s Autumn Budget 2024 on the Executive’s budget.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting times for smear tests (i) currently; and (ii) in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Women aged between 25 and 49 years old are routinely invited by the screening programme for cervical screening every three years, or every five years for women aged between 50 and 64 years. Women are sent letters to inform them that their next screening test is due and they are advised to contact their GP practice to arrange for a test to be taken. Information on waiting times for cervical smear tests is not available as cervical smear tests are taken at GP practices and are arranged at the request of individual women. There is no measurement of appointments, or waiting times to have a test taken, once a request is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 15081/22-27; (ii) AQW 14995/22-27; and (iii) AQW 15613/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Justice to develop joined-up protocols when PSNI intervention is sought to respond to children with autism who have extreme behavioural outbursts.
answer_text: The member should be aware that the PSNI intends to introduce the Right Care Right Person model currently being rolled out by police services in Great Britain. I have met the ACC responsible for the initiative and expressed concerns regarding the pace of rollout and its broad  implications regarding how we jointly respond to people with learning difficulties and mental health issues. Those discussions are ongoing and I encourage you to discuss the matter with your party representative on the Policing Board for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why residential and respite service provision at Willow Lodge Care Home, Belfast, was closed; and (ii) why it has not reopened.
answer_text: (i) Willow Lodge is a two-bedded service that provided short breaks to children between 11 and 18 years old. Since April 2021 due to operational pressures (staffing) and the need to provide full-time care to children with very complex needs, Willow Lodge has been unable to provide formal overnight short breaks. The home subsequently lost its registration via RQIA in June 2022 and required extensive repairs. (ii) Belfast HSC Trust has experienced many significant issues trying to reinstate the short breaks service in Willow Lodge - including unsuccessful recruitment of skilled and experienced staff to ensure safe staffing levels. As a result of these staffing issues, Willow Lodge is not registered with RQIA The Belfast HSC Trust has advised that they understand how extremely difficult this is for some families.  The Belfast HSC Trust is committed to continue to explore how they can reintroduce formal overnight short breaks for those families who require the service. The challenge for the Trust is successfully recruiting appropriately qualified and experienced staff to comply with the registration requirement. Whilst the Belfast HSC Trust’s focus is to re-establish Willow Lodge, in order to increase the availability for short breaks for families who require support, the Trust is also currently exploring all options to repurpose a suitable facility which would provide appropriate accommodation for children with complex disabilities. On 23rd October 2024, I was pleased to be able to approve additional regional funding for the Children with Disability service across all HSC Trusts of up to £13m a year in multi-year funding, including up to £2m for the rest of the current financial year.It is intended that this additional funding will assist with both increasing short breaks capacity and expanding family, therapeutic and behavioural supports in the community across all HSC Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to develop a 24-hour call-out service for families with a child with autism who has extreme behavioural outbursts.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to develop a 24-hour call-out service for families with a child with autism who has extreme behavioural outbursts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his (i) immediate; and (ii) long-term plans to extend residential and respite services for (a) children; and (b) adults with learning difficulties, complex needs and challenging behaviour.
answer_text: My Department held a Children with Disability Urgent Summit which took place on 26 September 2024 to consider potential options in respect of improving the regional access to short breaks and residential provision. This was attended by senior representatives from each of the five geographic Health and Social Care Trusts and senior representatives from RQIA.At the summit the Health and Social Care Trusts where asked to submit short term plans that will support families with behaviour that challenges and increase supports into family homes. These plans are being considered and costed. Long Term Plans for childrenAlongside this following the Review of Children Social Care by Professor Ray Jones in 2023, a number of workstreams were developed as part of the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme to progress the recommendations made within the review. In terms of implementation, the recommendations are being progressed through several workstreams specifically:residential placement capacity;fostering;children with disability; andfamily support.In addition, this work is aligned with the recommendations set out by the Children with Disability Framework, relating to short breaks, residential provision, early help and transitions. 	Immediate Plans for AdultsMy Department is currently finalising the Learning Disability Service Model which sets out Learning Disability Services through the lens of regional consistency and supporting needs at an early stage in the community. The service model sets out the Department’s vision for transitions, health & wellbeing, meaningful lives & citizenship, families & carers, home and mental health & behavioural support services. Long Term Plan for AdultsIt is envisaged that families and carers will be offered flexible, practical and emotional support that they need to support individuals to live in the community and have a meaningful life. This will involve increasing the level and diversity of respite/short breaks services, including consideration to modernise existing services and environments to better support those with more complex needs. Public consultation on the model is anticipated to take place late 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) residential; and (ii) respite service provision available for children and adults with learning difficulties, complex needs and challenging behaviour (a) before March 2020; and (b) post-March 2022.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have been in contact with Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs). A total of 5 children in residential care as at March 2022. There were also short breaks provided to medically complex children via the Belfast Trust facility called Forest Lodge. Forest Lodge also provided short breaks for the South Eastern Trust. It is important to note that short break provision closed for a time on the 18th March 2020 at the outset of the COVID pandemic. In March 2020, Willow Lodge was commissioned to provide short breaks for Belfast Trust children for a maximum of 2 beds x 7 nights per week. Lindsay House was commissioned to provide short breaks for Belfast Trust children for a maximum of 4 beds x 7 nights per week. Forest Lodge was commissioned to provide short breaks for Belfast Trust children for a maximum 2 beds x 7 nights per week for medically complex children.   (ii) Children’s Services Respite/Short Breaks at (b) March 2022, Willow Lodge was repurposed to provide full-time care to a child with very complex needs and ceased operating as a short breaks facility. Lindsay House was providing short breaks to Belfast Trust children at a reduced capacity of, on average, 1 bed x 5 nights per week.Forest Lodge was providing short breaks to Belfast Trust children at a reduced capacity of, on average, 2 bed x 4 nights per week for medically complex children. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the potential for midwifery apprenticeships.
answer_text: The provision of apprenticeship training for the public sector is currently not centrally funded by the Department for the Economy which has limited the development of apprenticeship programmes in the HSC.  The Department for the Economy plans to work with public sector employers to design apprenticeship frameworks that meet their current and future skills and employment gaps, including in healthcare.  This programme will not only help in addressing skills shortages but also widen the pool of talent by creating inclusive, accessible pathways to professional development.My Department is currently liaising with colleagues in the Department for the Economy to explore opportunities for health and social care roles to be included within this programme.  Due to continued challenges with funding the training costs for public sector apprenticeships, a limited number of healthcare professions will be initially considered for inclusion in this programme, with a midwifery apprenticeship not being considered at this stage.  It is however hoped that should this programme demonstrate the viability of public sector apprenticeships within HSC, there will be opportunities to develop apprenticeship programmes for further healthcare roles at which time the potential for a midwifery apprenticeship programme may be considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 16142/22-27, to detail the (i) nature; and (ii) timescales of the litigation proceedings to which he is referring to.
answer_text: A Judicial Review (JR) by RiverRidge Holdings Ltd (the applicant) was lodged against my department relating to a variation granted to the waste management licence (LN/13/35) held by ReGen Waste Limited. This JR Leave Hearing was scheduled to take place on Monday 16 September 2024. The case was adjourned until 14 October 2024 as the judge sought clarity from the applicant. On 14 October 2024, the applicant formally withdrew their application and the case was dismissed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 16142/22-27, whether he has received legal advice instructing that answering this question would directly impact the litigation proceedings.
answer_text: No.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether the Northern Ireland Environment Agency is fit for purpose.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) is an Executive Agency within my Department. As an Agency, NIEA is required to operate within a framework document that complies with the requirements of Managing Public Money NI. The Framework Document  (https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/niea-framework-document) sets out in detail the governance arrangements in place that help to ensure appropriate levels of accountability for performance, the delivery of services, the management and stewardship of resources and assets. This is underpinned by robust governance arrangements for business planning, stewardship reporting, financial reporting, budget procedures, raising concerns and counter fraud processes. The Framework document has been approved by the NIEA Chief Executive, Permanent Secretary, the Department of Finance and me as Minister.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will undertake to work with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency to reduce the level of waste that can be stored at any given time for waste licence LN/13/35.
answer_text: The NIEA must act within the parameters of the waste legislation against which this licence has been authorised.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether it is appropriate for the Northern Ireland Environment Agency to grant a licence for the storage of thousands of tonnes of household waste close to a town centre.
answer_text: In accordance with the Waste and Contaminated Land Order 1997, a waste management licence application cannot be considered by my department unless planning permission is in force in relation to the use to which the land will be put.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider amending waste licence at LN/13/35 to only a transit site.
answer_text: The ReGen Waste Limited (ReGen) waste management licence LN/13/35 at Warrenpoint Harbour is currently classed as a non-hazardous waste transfer facility for the storage and transfer, prior to shipment, of waste Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF). This therefore constitutes an authorised waste transit site.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Northern Ireland Environment Agency will conduct investigations at night.
answer_text: I can confirm the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) does conduct investigations at night.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) what constitutes a full inspection from the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA); (ii) why all site areas are not inspected during NIEA inspections; and (iii) whether the NIEA will change its practice so that when it undertakes a full inspection that all areas are inspected.
answer_text: NIEA carries out several different types of inspection across a number of different regulatory regimes. In most regimes, the inspector will aim to cover all aspects of the permit/licence within a single compliance cycle (usually a year). However, areas with the potential to cause significant pollution would usually be looked at during each routine inspection at a site, particularly where there had been prior non-compliance. This may not involve visiting all areas of a site where some of those areas are considered low risk. There are no plans at present for NIEA to change how it carries out inspections.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Northern Ireland Environment Agency will change its practice of not notifying a landowner who is not the licence-holder of breaches of compliance.
answer_text: I refer to Assembly Question reference AQW 9243/22-27, where a full answer to this question has previously been provided.My department is responsible for issuing waste management licences (WML).My department’s statutory responsibility is with a WML holder, not the landowner, and compliance with the conditions of a WML is solely the responsibility for the licence holder. There are no plans to change this.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of a number of breaches, why Re-Gen Waste was permitted an export of waste increase from 116,000 tonnes to 500,000 tonnes a year at Warrenpoint Port.
answer_text: The matters related to your question are currently subject to ongoing litigation proceedings and I am unable to comment further at this stage.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) why the level of refuse derived fuel waste able to be stored at any one time at Warrenpoint Harbour for waste licence LN/13/35 increased to 20,000 tonnes; and (ii) to detail any consultation that was undertaken by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency in relation to this.
answer_text: The waste management licence (WML) LN/13/35 was granted to ReGen Waste Limited (ReGen) on 28 October 2013. The maximum storage capacity has decreased and increased since this date following requests from ReGen, through WML modification applications or site working plan updates. In considering the application for the new WML LN/13/35, consultation was undertaken in line with the Waste and Contaminated Land Order 1997, with no objections received.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what measures he can take to improve the management of school transport provision at the start of the school year.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail each malfunction in the water treatment plant at Market Street, Downpatrick, since 1 September 2024.
answer_text: The management of public water and wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:There is no water treatment plant at Market Street, Downpatrick. However, there is a wastewater pumping station and there have been no reported issues with this asset since 1 September 2024.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

answer_text: Breakdown of actual expenditure each year of activity for the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services.
