question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether the money to fund the implementation of the outcomes in the Review of Flooding in the South East, Autumn 2023 is available; and (ii) whether he has submitted a bid for funding for this purpose.
answer_text: The recently published review of the South East Flooding in Autumn 2023 detailed a number of recommendations which covers the work of many multi-agency partners as well as that of my own Department. Whilst my Department is content to take the lead in the coordination and oversight of the implementation of all the recommendations, the funding of other relevant government departments is outside my control.This year’s funding allocation includes for the progression of a number of the recommendations from the review, including a feasibility study for Downpatrick, progression of the Portadown Flood Alleviation Scheme to the procurement phase, site investigation works and development of an outline Business Case for Newry Flood Alleviation Scheme and the development of a Business Case for the provision of a Flood Forecasting Centre.  Budgets have not yet been set for future years however my Department will continue to bid for the funding to progress these and other critical flood risk management projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider commissioning the use of the QbTest for the diagnosis and treatment of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder.
answer_text: The use of QbTesting is a component feature of diagnostic assessment process within children's services and already in use across all five Health and Social Care Trusts.QbTesting is not used for Adult ADHD within any of the five Health and Social Care Trusts. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals, there are currently no specifically commissioned services and it is recognised that this has led to variations in service provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways. My officials are now considering how best to progress the issue, in discussion with relevant stakeholders. Preparatory work is at an advanced stage to commission research/analysis of ADHD demand to inform options for future commissioning strategies, subject to budget availability.  It is anticipated that this research will include a consideration of how to ensure regional consistency across Trusts in any future ADHD commissioned service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether an action plan for the implementation of the 22 outcomes in the Review of Flooding in the South East, Autumn 2023, has been published.
answer_text: The recently published review of the South East Flooding in Autumn 2023 detailed a number of recommendations for which my Department, and many other multi-agency partners, will be responsible for delivering. Although my Department is not the lead agency for the delivery of all the recommendations from the review, I am content for my Department to take the lead in the coordination and oversight of the implementation of all recommendations through the establishment of a Flooding Review Recommendations Group. This will ensure a centralised approach with effective communication and the continuation of the good working relationships already developed during the flooding review itself. This group is currently developing terms of reference to provide the necessary framework for delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will introduce legislation for the compulsory microchipping of cats, as introduced in England in June 2024.
answer_text: My Department’s Code of Practice for the Welfare of Cats already recommends that owners ensure their cats are identified, using either a collar or microchip. However, I am aware that from 10 June 2024, the microchipping of pet cats in England became mandatory.    I am considering the compulsory microchipping of pet cats in the context of all pressing needs for animal welfare reform in Northern Ireland.  It is my intention to meet with key stakeholders in the time ahead to discuss the need for animal welfare reform. This will assist me in setting my department’s priorities in this area until the end of the mandate.However, ultimately, my capacity to enact legislation will be constrained by the resources available to me and the time left in this shortened mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will bring forward legislation to (i) give safeguards to protect the welfare of cats and kittens; and (ii) regulate cat breeding.
answer_text: The welfare of cats and kittens is protected under The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, which places a duty of care upon the owner and the person who has responsibility for the animal.  My Department’s Code of Practice for the Welfare of Cats provides guidance for owners to support them in meeting their cat’s needs.  It recommends that owners who choose not to breed their cat should have it neutered and provides guidance for those who do decide to breed their cat.In terms of further legislation, while I have no immediate plans to legislate to bring forth regulations for the cat breeding sector, I do intend to deliver Lucy’s Law to regulate the third-party sale of both kittens and dogs. It is anticipated that a consultation will be published on this matter early in the new year.Further, it is my intention to meet with key stakeholders in the time ahead to discuss the need for animal welfare reform. This will assist me in setting my department’s priorities in this area until the end of the mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her recent visit to Newry Street Unite, Kilkeel, was an official Ministerial visit.
answer_text: I visited Newry Street Unite’s Community Centre as Minister of Justice to see the good work being done through a community safety project funded under the Asset Recovery Community Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether there has been any removal of silt from the plank drain in Downpatrick following the publication of the Review of Flooding in the South East, Autumn 2023.
answer_text: To alleviate the impacts of flooding from rivers, my Department will continue its routine inspection and maintenance regime for designated watercourses and associated infrastructure within the Downpatrick area. Maintenance works which had been identified from a recent inspection of the Plank Drain are currently ongoing and are due to be completed mid- October 2024. These works have involved the necessary removal of aquatic weed growth and obstructions from the channel which can restrict the free flow of water.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions his Department has taken since the publication of the Review of Flooding in the South East, Autumn 2023, to protect Downpatrick from future flooding incidents.
answer_text: Following the significant flood event in November 2023, my Department has already re-prioritised its capital works programme ahead of the review publication and commissioned a Civil Engineering Consultant to undertake a feasibility study for the Downpatrick area. The study, which will involve complex analysis given the nature of the flooding at this location, will identify the flooding mechanisms in Downpatrick and assess if there are any viable solutions to alleviate future flooding. The report is due to be completed in Spring 2025.Rivers Directorate will also continue its inspection and maintenance regime in relation to designated watercourses and their associated infrastructure within the Quoile River catchment. If an inspection determines that there is a blockage significantly impeding the free flow of water within the watercourse or that drainage infrastructure is in an unsatisfactory condition, then works will be programmed.In addition, my officials have also reached out to Newry Mourne and Down Council to establish links with the local community with a view to establishing a community flooding resilience group. If there is local interest in such a group being established practical support my Department can provide includes the provision of local sandbags stores, water level text alerts and support to develop community resilience plans were appropriate. This level of support is not my Department stepping away from its responsibilities but rather an extra layer of support, to the already effective emergency response arrangements which are in place. This approach to managing flood risk has already proved to be useful in other areas with over 40 other resilience groups already established at various locations across the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people that use sign language as their primary language, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The 2021 Census asked the following question with two options: What is your main language?EnglishOther, write in (including British/Irish Sign Languages)Based on responses to this question, the number of individuals aged 3 and over in Northern Ireland who stated that sign language was their main language was as follows:Main languageNorthern IrelandBritish Sign Language539Irish Sign Language90Sign Language (Not otherwise specified)20All usual residents aged 3 and over1,836,615These figures are not available by constituency.Based on the Continuous Household Survey (CHS) 2021/22 one out of every ten adults (10%) in Northern Ireland were able to communicate using Sign language. For the CHS, adults are defined as those aged 16 and over.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people registered as deaf, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Census 2021 identified that 5.8% of the Northern Ireland population or 109,457 people reported that they were deaf or had partial hearing loss.  These figures are not available by constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the statutory provision her Department provides for trauma and counselling intervention for parents of children who have been the victim of sexual abuse.
answer_text: As Minister for Justice my remit does not include a statutory responsibility for the provision of healthcare services including counselling. Statutory responsibility for mental health services and wellbeing sits with the Department for Health. While my Department has no statutory remit for such services, I acknowledge that the criminal justice system must have a trauma informed approach, and this view is shared by leaders across all of the component parts of the system. Much has been done to support children and their families as they engage with the process. My Department provides funding to Victim Support NI (VSNI) and to NSPCC to ensure support services are available to all victims and witnesses of crime, including sexual abuse. VSNI currently delivers a range of services including the Child Independent Sexual Violence Advocate (ChISVA) service, which provides advocacy, emotional and practical support to children and young people who have been victims of sexual violence and to their families. Children may be referred to VSNI by police or by another organisation supporting the child but families can also self-refer to access support. In addition, where there are interdependencies across areas of responsibility, Ministers and Departments work co-operatively to ensure services and supports are joined-up, complementary, and that resources are used effectively. For example, joint funding for the 24-hour Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline is provided by my Department, together with the Department of Health and the Department for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the current waiting lists for gynaecological care.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work that has been carried out to implement the recommendations of the Getting It Right First Time Gynaecology Report.
answer_text: Following the recent publication of the Getting It Right First Time Report, which provided a review of gynaecology services in Northern Ireland, and the 20 recommendations to improve gynaecology services, the Department of Health’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group have established a forum with Trust, GP and PHA representation to oversee ongoing service improvement in this speciality and ensure that this improvement is implemented at a local and regional level. Some of the recommendations will require investment, whilst others will be improved through modernisation and reform.Trusts have been asked to establish their own action plans to take forward recommendations and improvements which are relevant to their Trust. My officials from the Strategic Planning and Performance Group are currently in the process of writing to Trust’s to ask for an update to their action plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the statutory provision his Department provides for trauma and counselling intervention for parents of children who have been the victim of sexual abuse.
answer_text: The following provisions are provided by Trusts.BHSCTAdult Mental Health Services are commissioned to deliver interventions to adults who present with significant mental ill health.  During an individual assessment, as part of the clinical presentation, there may be disclosure of this nature.  Trauma can be part of the background experience of many attending our services and, based on the clinical assessment outcome, the Trusts have a range of psychologically informed services that may be appropriate to offer input.Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS) - Clinicians can refer a young person and their family for family therapy, either within generic CAMHS or through the Family Trauma Centre if it is deemed that the family system has been impacted by the child’s experience of sexual trauma.Within Children’s Community Services, Social Workers will provide support to those parents who have children that have been the victims of sexual abuse.  The caseworker may be the supportive resource or they may make a referral to other specialists for support, for example, the National Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children (NSPCC) will work with parents of children that have been sexually abused.NHSCTNHSCT advised there is no statutory provision for trauma and counselling intervention for parents of children who have been the victim of sexual abuse. Parents may be referred to psychological therapy services in the Trust wherein such issues could be addressed but it is not a specific service.SEHSCTParents may be referred to psychological therapy services in the Trust wherein such issues could be addressed but it is not a specific service.SHSCTSouthern Trust has a newly established service which engage children in recovery work who have been subject to sexual abuse.  This involves direct work with the victim and sessions for parents and siblings in regards to the impact on them, how to deal with emotions and how to support each other.This is not a direct counselling session for parents and is offered where appropriate, during therapeutic intervention with the victim. Training has been completed and the service will go live in the coming months.  Adult Mental Health Services ResponseWhile there is no specific service for parents whose children have experienced sexual abuse, the SHSCT Adult Mental Health Service provide a range of evidence-based trauma informed psychological therapy interventions for individuals responsive to the client’s needs. The limits of confidentiality and duty to report concerns is explained to each person engaging in psychological therapy. If a parent makes a disclosure in regard to alleged abuse, all appropriate safeguarding actions are taken as soon as possible by the responsible clinician. WHSCTWithin the WHSCT Family & Childcare have established a Recovery Team which offers support to young people who have been the victims of sexual abuse and while the Trust do not exclusively provide intervention/ support to p

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work being undertaken to provide the waiting time statistics for Health and Social Care Trusts that are unable to provide them as a result of the roll-out of encompass.
answer_text: Trust information teams and the encompass programme have been working carefully, with support from my Department, to finalise and validate these reports. The schedule for publishing regulatory statistical products is a key focus of this work, with resource being reprioritized from other tasks and teams working hard to meet deadlines and maintain continuity of regulatory reporting.My clear expectation is that inclusion of diagnostic waiting times for the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust and the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is reestablished as soon as possible. I recognise that inclusion of data from both trusts in the November diagnostic waiting time statistical release is contingent on the department receiving quality assured timely information.All Trusts across the UK, that have implemented similar electronic patient record systems, have required a period of stabilisation before resuming the full range of regulatory information returns. Although similar validation will be necessary as each Trust adopts encompass, ongoing efforts in the South Eastern and Belfast Trusts will pave the way for future deployments, ensuring that remaining Trusts are well placed to meet their quarterly reporting deadlines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why the roll-out of encompass is preventing Health and Social Care Trusts from detailing their waiting times.
answer_text: The adoption of the encompass system has introduced new ways of working for clinicians, leading to changes in how some activities are recorded and measured. These changes also require updates to reporting practices, including those for diagnostic procedures. Encompass has been capturing diagnostic waiting time data for both the South Eastern and Belfast Trusts since their respective “Go Lives” and work has been undertaken to ensure the information is accurate, fully validated and meets required standards. My clear expectation is that inclusion of diagnostic waiting times for the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust and the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is reestablished as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of NHS dental practices there were in each year from 2019 to 2024, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust area.
answer_text: The information on the gross amount paid to NHS dentists in each financial year is routinely published on an annual basis in the Accredited Official Statistics FPS General Dental Services Statistics for Northern Ireland publication from the Business Services Organisation. The required information can be found in Table 1.30 at the following link: Annual Dental Statistics Tables 2023-24.The information on the number of Health Service dental practices in each financial year broken down by and Health and Social Care Trust area (Local Commissioning Group) is also routinely published in this same release in Table 1.02 In addition, quarterly updates within a year on the number of dental practices are published at General Dental Services Statistics - Business Services Organisation (BSO).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the gross amount paid to NHS dentists in each year from 2019 to 2024.
answer_text: The information on the gross amount paid to NHS dentists in each financial year is routinely published on an annual basis in the Accredited Official Statistics FPS General Dental Services Statistics for Northern Ireland publication from the Business Services Organisation. The required information can be found in Table 1.30 at the following link: Annual Dental Statistics Tables 2023-24.The information on the number of Health Service dental practices in each financial year broken down by and Health and Social Care Trust area (Local Commissioning Group) is also routinely published in this same release in Table 1.02 In addition, quarterly updates within a year on the number of dental practices are published at General Dental Services Statistics - Business Services Organisation (BSO).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children on a waiting list for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery; (ii) the lower percentile of time on the list; and (iii) the upper percentile of time on the list.
answer_text: The issue of access to percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) insertion for children and young people is an important issue as is the range of all surgical procedures that face children and young people in Northern Ireland.  This procedure is a complex procedure that requires specialised clinical and nursing input prior to, during and post procedure.  There are 93 children currently on the waiting list with the longest waiting patient placed on the waiting list in June 2021.  Each case is constantly assessed by the specialist team at the RBHSC and dealt with as quickly as possible based on clinical priority and chronological order. Due to the specialist nature of this procedure it is only carried out at the RBHSC.  Unfortunately, due to the introduction of the new ICT system, Encompass at BHSCT it is not possible to examine the data further at this stage.  It is hoped that when the Encompass system is fully operational across all Trusts in NI that the extraction and publication of data will be more streamlined.A Paediatric Surgeon in BHSCT is currently going through an urgent validation of Peg Tube waiting lists.  This process will identity which children are suitable for potential transfer to Alder Hey Hospital in Liverpool for the procedure.  BHSCT are carrying out discussions with Alder Hey to ascertain the feasibility of this arrangement.  If agreed clinical criteria, agreed by the specialist team, will be put in place to determine what children will be transferred.Belfast Trust is planning to provide a waiting list initiative in October on a Friday, with patients being nursed in RBHSC over the weekend. This initiative will be dependent on bed and theatre resources and unscheduled bed pressures.  Belfast Trust is also in discussion with South Eastern Trust to determine if Gastrostomy surgery could be provided in the Ulster Hospital by Belfast consultants.  Belfast Gastrostomy Nurses will provide training to staff in SET to help facilitate this. This initiative will be dependent on bed and theatre resources and unscheduled bed pressures.  There is also currently a review of surgical work performed in RBHSC and any work that can be redirected to District General Hospitals will be transferred.  This may free up capacity in RBHSC for Paediatric Surgeons to perform more gastrostomy surgery.The Trust are also examining the potential of partnership working with Private Sector.  This would mean using RBHSC capacity over the weekend to deliver Waiting List Initiatives in the Children’s Hospital. This initiative will be dependent on bed and theatre resources and unscheduled bed pressures.  In the meantime, paediatricians and paediatric surgeons along with other clinical teams at the RBHSC will continue to support these children and their families.My Officials will continue to meet with BHSCT to discuss these potential solutions to examine their progress and success.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on measures taken to manage school traffic along Edward Street, Downpatrick.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work undertaken to reduce the waiting time for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) why the draft 3rd Cycle River Basin Management Plan 2021 to 2027 has not been published in a final form; (ii) when the final plan will be published; and (iii) for his assessment of the impact that the failure to publish the plan has had on water quality.
answer_text: Northern Ireland’s third cycle River Basin Management Plan has been prepared and consulted on, however it is still in draft form. I will need time to review it in the context of emerging information on water quality, including reports from: the Northern Ireland Audit Office; the Office for Environmental Protection; and the Lough Neagh Report, before bringing it to the Executive for approval.While I am not yet in a position to provide a date for the publication of the final River Basin Management Plan, I am aware of the urgency of this work and aim to publish the final Plan as soon as possible.  I am aware that water quality in waterbodies across Northern Ireland is not what it should be. I have made it a priority to drive forward implementation of the Lough Neagh Action Plan which incorporates a range of actions to improve water quality across all our water bodies.     In addition, although the River Basin Management Plan remains in draft, the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) has held a number of working group meetings for key stakeholders to consider sector specific measures. Work has also continued on the delivery of a number of initiatives to improve water quality, including DAERA’s Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS).  This scheme has been recognised globally as a way to provide farmers with data that will help them improve their nutrient management, farm performance and environmental sustainability. The data and evidence provided by the SNHS is a key enabler to improving water quality across all of Northern Ireland’s water courses through better management of slurry and other fertilisers. My Department also provides financial support for water quality improvements, with part funding provided to the SEUPB’s (Special EU Programmes Body) PEACEPLUS programme. The ‘water quality & catchment management’ strand of that programme is currently ongoing. My Department’s Environment Fund also covers the ‘Water Quality Improvement Strand’ grant funding which is administered by NIEA and focuses on community and voluntary group delivery of water quality improvements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the percentage of (i) river water bodies; (ii) lakes; (iii) transitional and coastal water bodies; and (iv) groundwater bodies that currently have a good or high status.
answer_text: The current percentage of water bodies at high or good status is published as official statistics on the DAERA webpage, as part of the Northern Ireland Water Framework Directive Statistics Report December 2021. https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-water-framework-directive-statistics-report-2021
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the draft 3rd Cycle River Basin Management Plan 2021 to 2027, (i) for his assessment on whether Northern Ireland can meet the working target for 70 per cent of all water bodies to be at good status by 2027; and (ii) how this target will be achieved.
answer_text: It is highly unlikely that Northern Ireland will meet the working target for 70 % of all water bodies to be at good status by 2027. Achievement of the River Basin Management Plan target will require significant investment.  DAERA, like all the other Departments, is working within a very tight budget underpinned by significant resource pressures and, while my ambition and determination remains high, the pace of progress will depend to some extent on the funding available.Successful delivery of the target to improve water quality across Northern Ireland will depend on engagement, consensus-building and the creation of strong partnerships across government, the public and private sectors, including local government, and the community.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the investment that will be provided for the final River Basin Management Plan.
answer_text: Northern Ireland’s third cycle River Basin Management Plan has been prepared and consulted on, however it is still in draft form.  I will need time to review it in the context of emerging information on water quality, including reports from the Northern Ireland Audit Office and the Office for Environmental Protection and the Lough Neagh Report, before bringing it to the Executive for approval.  Once this work has been completed, I will be in a position to provide information on potential costs and funding.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why the draft 3rd Cycle River Basin Management Plan 2021 to 2027 does not include environmental objectives for individual water bodies.
answer_text: This matter is referred to in a recent Office of Environmental Protection’s report ‘A review of implementation of the Water Framework Directive Regulations and River Basin Management Planning in Northern Ireland’. This includes the recommendation to have objectives at water body level in future draft and final river basin management plans. My officials are now considering these recommendations and will provide a response in due course.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of funding that has been allocated to the Public Health Agency for public health awareness campaigns in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any review that has been carried out into the efficacy of Public Health Agency public health awareness campaigns, including the findings of any such review.
answer_text: Following the decision to implement an advertising freeze for the 2023/24 year, it was agreed that the Public Health Agency (PHA) would submit a briefing paper to the Department.  The briefing paper consisted of desktop research and published evidence on the role social marketing campaigns can directly and indirectly play on health-related behaviours across large populations.  The paper also included evaluation summary reports for a number of PHA campaigns. The PHA briefing pointed to the existence of a substantial body of evidence that social marketing campaigns can impact health related behaviours. The briefing highlighted findings from three published papers, namely; Wakefield, Loken and Hornik’s review ‘Use of mass media campaigns to change health behaviour’ (2010); Social Market Foundation’s Carrot and sticks: can governments do without public health regulation? (2023) and Public Health England commissioned research (2013).  These included evidence that social marketing interventions can be effective in changing smoking behaviour; increasing the early detection of cancers; improving physical activity; increasing healthy eating and reducing risky sexual behaviour. The paper also outlined that there appears to be some limited evidence in relation to the impact marketing can have on reducing illicit drug use and that evidence of the effectiveness of social marketing interventions in alcohol is inconclusive. The individual papers also highlighted the effectiveness of other non-marketing factors which influence behaviour. For instance, the Social Market Foundation concluded that regulations and taxes appear to have the biggest impact on public health.The Public Health Agency also provided evaluation material on four of its 2023 campaigns. The results showed that the campaigns achieved significant reach, high recognition levels and high levels of positive action.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposal for a School Street Scheme at Edward Street, Downpatrick.
answer_text: My Department commissioned a study to establish if any viable road safety and traffic progression improvements could be made along Edward Street in Downpatrick. Part of this study involves the development of a range of options to help alleviate the current traffic situation on the local road network, including consideration of a School Street scheme. The study is scheduled for completion in the coming months, after which officials in my department will then arrange to meet with representatives from Downpatrick District Electoral Area to discuss the findings.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of budget allocated to address waiting lists in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Funding is allocated non-recurrently each year for Waiting List Initiatives (WLI).  This funding is used to secure additional capacity, both in-house and in the independent sector, across a range of elective specialties.  Funding in the last 5 years is as follows- 2019/20 - £3m2020/21 - £12.1m2021/22 - £91.5m2022/23 - £96m2023/24 - £70m To stand still in terms of cancer and time critical patients, £75-80m per year is required.  Following the approval of the Budget, the Department of Health has committed to investing £76m this year for waiting lists and the focus will be on supporting those red flag (cancer) and time critical (life, limb, sight) waiting lists.  The funding is not sufficient to tackle our lengthy waits.  In addition, up to £135m per year is required for waiting list reduction work (above the £75-80m required for cancer/red flag) and £80m recurrent funding is required for sustainable elective care delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the progress that has been made on the Warrenpoint Front Shore Public Realm Works Scheme; (ii) the timescales for completion of the scheme; and (iii) the consultation that has been undertaken with the public on this scheme.
answer_text: Construction work on the £2.6 million public realm scheme along the Front Shore in Warrenpoint is currently ongoing, and the works are due to complete in March 2025 as scheduled. A public consultation exercise was undertaken at the design and planning stages for the scheme. The construction contractor regularly liaises with local residents and traders to keep them informed about progress on the scheme. The scheme is also overseen by a council led Steering Group comprising elected members and representatives from the Warrenpoint Burren and Rostrevor Chamber of Commerce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 14478/22-27, to detail (i) how the 20,000 tonne limit was determined by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA); (ii) the consultation that was undertaken; and (iii) whether the NIEA will review this limit.
answer_text: The waste management licence LN/13/35 was granted to ReGen Waste Limited (ReGen) on 28 October 2013. ReGen had requested a maximum storage capacity of 70,000 tonnes. On considering all relevant matters relating to the application however, including the information supplied by Regen in support of its application and consultation responses received, NIEA determined, in exercising its judgment, that the maximum storage capacity should be no more than 20,000 tonnes.In considering the application for a new waste management licence by ReGen (LN/13/35), on 23 May 2013 the NIEA consulted the Department of the Environment Planning Service, the Loughs Agency, Northern Ireland Water, the local council Environmental Health Team, NIEA Water Management Unit, NIEA Environmental Crime Unit and the landowner (Warrenpoint Harbour Authority), with no objections received. The NIEA have no plans to review this limit.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount his Department has spent on the development of strategies in the last ten years, broken down by (i) year; and (ii) strategy.
answer_text: The Department does not hold the information requested. Expenditure data stored in the central financial reporting system does not provide for analysis of expenditure by the category ‘development of strategies’.  Staff costs can generally be reported by Directorate and Team and sometimes additional subcategories are used, but generally not by the type of work or units of time.  That is, ‘development of strategies’ is not a subcategory of staff costs analysis in the Department. Furthermore, the Department has considered whether the information could be gathered through a wider data gathering exercise of records held outside the finance system.  However, this could not be done without incurring significant disproportionate costs and the resulting information would be incomplete and inaccurate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the process by which Translink decide what facilities to provide at bus stops; and (ii) whether an asset inventory is kept and regularly maintained of which bus stops have (a) signs; (b) timetables; and (c) shelters.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:“A detailed bus stop database has been developed and maintained by Translink, in partnership with its bus shelter contractor, in relation to inventory at each stop. This database contains asset information such as bus flags, presence of timetables and shelters; this is updated when information changes. Facility provision at existing and new bus stops are subject to available funding.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the cost of an individual (a) bus stop; and (b) shelter; and (ii) whether the Department will consider a scheme allowing community stakeholders to sponsor a bus stop.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:“1) A designated pole and flag approximately £500.2) A standard bus shelter installation is approximately £15,000 - £20,000. This figure will be dependent upon exact requirements, site surveys, specification, power requirements and reinstatement. 3) Translink would be keen to work in partnership with community stakeholders to provide funding for specific installations. All new installations will be subject to statutory consents including planning approval, land ownership confirmation and funding.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:“Translink provide a number of seasonal services to enable connectivity by public transport to tourist sites. During summer 2024 we operated the following services across our bus network;Bangor to Newcastle Causeway Rambler Antrim Coaster  Mourne Rambler Belfast to Giants CausewayOmagh to Portrush Marble Arch CavesWe have ongoing engagement with Tourism NI to discuss strategic priorities and identify mutual opportunities to add value. An example of this is the current partnership on outdoor tourism which will meet again on 13th September in Enniskillen to take a closer look at the economic, social and environmental benefits provided by Greenways. Fermanagh and Omagh District Council will also be present to discuss their outdoor tourism plans and ambition.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the public consultation process that is undertaken by Translink when determining the opening and closing hours for bus stations.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:“Opening and closing hours are reviewed in accordance with passenger usage, operational resource, and associated costs, along with changing technology that can reduce the need for ticket offices to remain open for instance all Translink bus services have contactless ticketing on board.  Translink also engage with local stakeholders to communicate significant changes to hours of operation.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people with a learning disability accessing five days per week day care, up to 1 September 2024, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts have provided the following data regarding the number of adults with a Learning Disability accessing five days per week, Day Care, up to 1 September 2024, in each of the last five years. Please see the table below. It should be noted between 2020-2022 the COVID Pandemic was ongoing and this had a significant impact upon attendance numbers. Day Care Centres were stood down in line with Public Health Guidance at that time. In 2022, the process of remobilisation of Day Care Services and increasing attendance at Day Centres back to pre-pandemic levels commenced.Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT)YearNumber of people with a Learning Disability Accessing Five Days per week Day Care up to 1 September 2024Northern HSCT202017Northern HSCT202187Northern HSCT2022419Northern Trust2023424Northern Trust 2024404South Eastern HSCT2020348South Eastern HSCT2021124South Eastern Trust2022164South Eastern Trust2023213South Eastern HSCT2024251Western HSCT2020356Western HSCT2021291Western HSCT2022315Western HSCT2023315Western HSCT2024311Belfast HSCT2020325Belfast HSCT202182Belfast HSCT2022265Belfast HSCT2023308Belfast HSCT2024327Southern HSCT20202Southern HSCT20218Southern HSCT2022180Southern HSCT2023191Southern Trust 2024183In some cases, individuals did not return to their traditional Day Care Service either because their needs had changed or they wanted to avail of more flexible Day Opportunity support in the community. DoH are currently finalising the Learning Disability Service Model which sets out Learning Disability Services through the lens of regional consistency and supporting needs at an early stage in the community. The service model sets out the Department’s vision for transitions, health & wellbeing, meaningful lives & citizenship, families & carers, home and mental health & behavioural support services. The DOH through the service model plans to carry out a review of Day Care and the Regional Day Opportunities Model on a cross-departmental basis, to define the roles across government to enable people with learning disabilities to have a meaningful life. This will include putting in place arrangements for joint resourcing and management of community services.  The Department will also set and monitor key indicators (KPIs) for the HSC Trusts to expand Day Opportunities and transition away from traditional buildings-based support services. It is however also recognised that some Service Users may have their needs best supported in more traditional Day Care setting and as a result this service will continue to be provided in accordance with their assessed need.Health and Social Care Trusts are responsible for planning and delivering Day Care and Day Opportunities for adults with Learning Disabilities, aligned to their needs. My Department, working with Trusts, is committed to ensuring service users and carers receive appropriate service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the plans of (i) his Department; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trusts to resume full capacity to deliver five days per week day care for those with a learning disability.
answer_text: DoH are currently finalising the Learning Disability Service Model which sets out Learning Disability Services through the lens of regional consistency and supporting needs at an early stage in the community. The service model sets out the Department’s vision for transitions, health & wellbeing, meaningful lives & citizenship, families & carers, home and mental health & behavioural support services. The DOH through the service model plans to carry out a review of Day Care and the Regional Day Opportunities Model on a cross-departmental basis, to define the roles across government to enable people with learning disabilities to have a meaningful life. This will include putting in place arrangements for joint resourcing and management of community services.  The Department will also set and monitor key indicators (KPIs) for the HSC Trusts to expand Day Opportunities and transition away from traditional buildings-based support services. During the COVID pandemic some individuals chose to avail of Day Opportunities rather than traditional Day Care Services, in line with their assessed need. Whilst there is a focus on expanding Day Opportunities, it is also recognised that some Service Users may have their needs best supported in more traditional Day Care settings and as a result such Day Care Services will continue to be provided.Health and Social Care Trusts are responsible for planning and delivering Day Care and Day Opportunities for adults with Learning Disabilities, aligned to their needs. My Department, working with Trusts, is committed to ensuring Service Users and Carers receive appropriate service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the infrastructure and capacity in place across Northern Ireland for patients to avail of Produodopa medication.
answer_text: The Department continues to have a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability in Northern Ireland. Where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In November 2023, NICE published TA934 which recommends the use of Foslevodopa–foscarbidopa for treating advanced Parkinson’s with motor symptoms and was endorsed by my Department as being applicable in Northern Ireland in December 2023. The SPPG issued a service notification in February 2024 to the HSC service advising that the treatment is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland in line with the current NICE guidelines (TA934) on a cost-per-case basis and that any additional infrastructure requirements is subject to agreement with the relevant SPPG Service Team.The SPPG are aware that a number of Trusts have highlighted challenges in regard to infrastructure and staffing and the SPPG is working with Trusts to quantify the requirements and provide additional support where appropriate. Any additional resource will be considered against other competing priorities across the HSC system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) when the new Waste Management Strategy for Northern Ireland will be published; (ii) whether it has an accompanying financial plan and action sheet; and (ii) why the publication has been delayed.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will publish their plans to implement the recommendations of the Commission on Flags, Identity, Culture and Tradition Final Report.
answer_text: The FICT Report tackles some of the most complex issues we face here and we recognise the ongoing need to address these.We have therefore agreed that the recommendations of the FICT Report will be taken forward as part of the current review of the Together: Building a United Community Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of non-compliance reports specific to waste management licence holders in Northern Ireland made to the NIEA by someone external to the licence holder, in each of the last five years; and (ii) any subsequent action taken by the NIEA, broken down by (a) year; and (b) constituency.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) is the only competent authority for monitoring compliance with waste licences issued by NIEA. NIEA conducts regular inspections to ensure compliance with the conditions of those licences. Non-compliances are substantiated and then recorded by the NIEA, not by someone external to the licence holder.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: Northern Ireland exports of waste outside of the UK fall into two main categories. Notifiable waste and green list waste. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of non-compliance reports specific to waste management licence holders in Northern Ireland made to the NIEA by the licence holder, in each of the last five years; and (ii) any subsequent action taken by the NIEA, broken down by (a) year; and (b) constituency.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) is the competent authority for monitoring compliance with waste licences issued by NIEA. The NIEA conduct regular inspections to ensure compliance with the conditions of those licences and non-compliances are substantiated and then recorded by the NIEA, not by the licence holder.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will produce an updated cross-departmental strategy and action plan for reducing offending and reoffending, as recommended by the Northern Ireland Audit Office in its June 2023 report Reducing Adult Reoffending in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 7 - AQO 743/22-27			ANSWERI intend to engage with Executive colleagues with a view to securing agreement to the development of a cross-departmental strategy to reduce offending and reoffending.As Justice Minister, one of my key priorities is to take a more strategic focus on reducing offending, rehabilitation and supporting desistance.  The criminal justice system cannot do this on its own.  This was a fact recognised by the Northern Ireland Audit Office in its report ‘Reducing Adult Reoffending in Northern Ireland’, published in June 2023.  There are many factors, and aspects that require action by other Departments, to bring about material change and impact. Aligned to the commitment made within the report I have also commenced initial engagement with Executive colleagues through input to the new draft Programme for Government.My department’s input to the Programme for Government highlights the collective approach required to tackle societal issues that contribute to offending behaviour and prevent people from entering the justice system. I must stress that buy in, and commitment to change, from other Departments will be critical to success.  Therefore, I would welcome the support of my Executive colleagues to progress this important strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Community Resuscitation Strategy 2014, including whether there have been any reviews and evaluations of the strategy’s outcomes.
answer_text: The Community Resuscitation Strategy was launched in July 2014 by the then Minister for Health, Edwin Poots, with the aim to increase the survival rate for those who suffer an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, to the highest level that can be achieved across Northern Ireland.  The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) is leading on the successful implementation of the Community Resuscitation Strategy and is funded to provide a supporting role in advising organisations, businesses, and individuals regarding siting of defibrillators and best practice in the use of defibrillators. Public engagement and awareness sessions on cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) and use of Automated External Defibrillators (AEDs) in communities, continue to increase in number year on year. Community resuscitation strategy groups within individual council areas, develop specific objectives and action plans to improve survivability from out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, with the aid of local resources.  NIAS monitor and review progress to ensure the anticipated benefits of the Strategy continue to be delivered. NIAS has worked with the British Heart Foundation to develop ‘The Circuit’ (https://www.thecircuit.uk/) which is a national online defibrillator network linking to every ambulance service in the UK.  NIAS can readily provide information on AEDs registered on ‘The Circuit’ to 999 callers, should they require access.There are currently 3,888 AEDs available to the public and registered through NIAS on ‘The Circuit’.  NIAS Community Resuscitation Team continue to be involved in activities within communities which will strengthen the chain of survival, and NIAS have installed 35 publicly accessible defibrillators outside NIAS premises across the province.There are currently 426 Community First Responder (CFR) volunteers and 24 schemes in NI.  NI also has approximately 2,500 members of the public who have a minimum of Basic Life Support training and have registered to be a GoodSAM responder. Since members of the public went live on GoodSAM in February 2023 over 297 people have accepted a GoodSAM alert to a cardiac arrest.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the new mother and baby hospital, including the timeframe for the delivery of this project.
answer_text: A business case for the proposed Mother and Baby Unit at the Belfast City Hospital site is currently being prepared. The timescale for approval of this business case remains subject to the identification of capital and revenue funding to deliver the unit in the context of an overall challenging financial position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide an update on whether an independent inquiry is required into allegations of the surveillance of journalists by the PSNI.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how much funding his Department will allocate to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council for the Irish Open 2024 in Newcastle.
answer_text: While primary financial support for events is provided by Tourism NI, my Department through Sport NI has been able to support legacy activities for a small number of sporting events in previous years, with the main aim of opening up participation opportunities for under-represented groups in those sports.  However, my Department is facing significant financial pressures and is dealing with an extremely challenging budget for the year ahead and neither my Department or Sport NI currently has any funding opportunities available which Newry, Mourne and Down District Council could apply to for legacy activities around the Irish Open 2024 in Newcastle.My officials and Sport NI are available to meet with the Council, if it wishes, to explore what other ways that the local community can benefit from the sporting legacy of the event.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on when the resurfacing of the road at Racecourse View, Downpatrick, will be completed.
answer_text: I can confirm that the road in the development at Racecourse View remains a private unadopted road. Developments cannot be adopted into the public network until the Developer has completed the roads and footways to an adoptable standard.Whilst officials continue to engage with the developer on this issue, all responsibilities for the works required to bring this site to future adoption rest with the developer. As such, the timescale for completion of the surfacing works required to bring this development road to adoption standards is dependent on the developer and remains unknown.

answer_text: As of 3rd June, Newry, Mourne and Down District Council have issued Letters of Offer (LOO) to 27 businesses to the value of £1.6 million.  To date the Department has released funding of £1.7m and the council have made payments of £310k to 22 businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the Birth Trauma Inquiry should be extended to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In light of several reports concerning the safety of maternity services, both locally and nationally, the Department established a Maternity and Neonatal Services Safety Oversight Group to receive assurance on the safety of maternity and neonatal services for the population of Northern Ireland.A broad programme of work is ongoing with an immediate focus on governance and safety issues highlighted across maternity and neonatal services. This includes the commissioning of an independent report into the provision of Midwifery Services and Care in Northern Ireland.The anticipated independent report by Professor Mary Renfrew titled ‘Enabling Safe, Quality Midwifery Services and Care in Northern Ireland’ has as part of the process widely engaged with women and their families around their experience of maternity care in NI. The report has an overall aim to ensure the best possible outcomes in midwifery care for mothers and babies are achieved, this includes ensuring women are at the centre of designing & improving maternity services into the future. Collectively, this work will be fundamental to improving maternity services and improving outcomes for mothers, babies and their families in Northern Ireland.With regards to the Birth Trauma report from the All-Party Parliamentary Group on Birth Trauma, the report will be considered, and learning used to help inform the Maternity and Neonatal Services Safety Oversight Group who will oversee the safety of maternity services within Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he intends to publish a follow-up strategy to the Skin Cancer Prevention Strategy and Action Plan (2011-21).
answer_text: Due to the pressures of COVID-19 and other Departmental pressures, the current Skin Cancer Prevention Strategy and Action Plan 2011-2021 was extended to September 2024 with a view to taking forward a review of the strategy. Unfortunately, it has not been possible to complete that work due to other pressing demands. Appropriate next steps in relation to skin cancer prevention work are currently being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a review of the efficacy of the Skin Cancer Prevention Strategy and Action Plan (2011-21) was completed following its conclusion.
answer_text: Due to the pressures of COVID-19 and other Departmental pressures, the current Skin Cancer Prevention Strategy and Action Plan 2011-2021 was extended to September 2024 with a view to taking forward a review of the strategy. Unfortunately, it has not been possible to complete that work due to other pressing demands. Appropriate next steps in relation to skin cancer prevention work are currently being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the learning disability framework and associated action plan.
answer_text: My Department established a Task & Finish Group to finalise a Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM) as part of a wider exercise to improve outcomes for children and adults. Throughout 2023/24, there has been a significant review of evidence, data, and stakeholder feedback to develop a revised model of service.An updated draft of the Learning Disability Service Model was presented to Trust Directors of Mental Health and Learning Disability in March 2024, representative of the feedback received from groups engaged since December 2023.  The current draft model has the support of all HSC Trusts and has received positive feedback from providers and the families engaged. Work is now underway to produce a costed implementation plan.  A draft was shared with Trusts and providers in May 2024.  In parallel, a review of LD finances is underway to better understand how the service is funded and identify areas of spend that can be reprioritised to deliver better outcomes. This review is due to conclude in June 2024.In terms of next steps, the Department is undertaking pre-engagement ahead of a wider consultation on the service model and implementation plan.  While engagement to date has been significant, it is critical to involve those delivering general healthcare and mental health services.  The target date for consultation is summer 2024, subject to my decision.To improve HSC transitions, the Department established a regional sub-group in November 2023 to convene key stakeholders from Trusts, policy and SPPG involved in the delivery of services for children and adults.  The sub-group has held three workshops since November, which has included Education colleagues.  A finalised draft protocol has been developed to produce a regionally standardised approach to transitions for children with learning disability services. The draft protocol is aligned to the Learning Disability Service Model. The draft approach to Transitions was shared with providers and Trusts for feedback and is expected to be finalised in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on engagement between his Department and the Department for Work and Pensions in relation to the findings of the Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman report on changes to Women’s State Pension age.
answer_text: The Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman report into how changes to women’s State Pension age were communicated by the Department for Work and Pensions was published on 21 March 2024. At present the Westminster Government is considering the report.  Although pensions are a devolved matter, in general Northern Ireland’s pensions policy and legislation operate in line with corresponding pension provision in Great Britain in accordance with section 87 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998. In effect there is a single pension system and regulatory regime across the United Kingdom.State pension expenditure in Northern Ireland is funded as Annually Managed Expenditure directly from His Majesty’s Treasury. Therefore given section 87 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, and that the issues raised and potential expenditure implications are UK-wide, I consider it prudent to await the Westminster Government’s response to the PHSO report. My officials continue to engage with DWP on this matter on the basis of a single pensions system across the UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what engagement she has had with the UK Government regarding the routine surveillance of journalists by police and intelligence services.
answer_text: I have spoken with the Chief Constable on this matter.While this is an operational policing matter, and not all within the devolved sphere, I share the concerns raised around the routine surveillance of journalists and solicitors.  However, the PSNI is accountable to the Policing Board and it is important to allow the Policing Board the opportunity to consider this issue fully in the first instance.

In their response, SHSCT have noted that in 2020/21 and 2021/22 expenditure levels may have been impacted by Covid-19 in the under-delivery against some contracts.WHSCT have advised that expenditure with C&V organisations is not separately identifiable within their Finance systems and therefore an estimate of expenditure with C&V organisations has been provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider introducing redress mechanisms in his Department which will provide resolution and compensation to patients without the need for legal action.
answer_text: The resolution of a Medical Negligence Claim is a complex process requiring expert evidence and, in many cases, quantifying the harm and loss caused by negligent care and treatment can prove problematic and time consuming due to the nature and length of the treatment, often complex patient histories and availability of expert witnesses.The breadth and scale of an overarching redress scheme which would appropriately address issues of liability, causation and quantum in respect of the wide ranging and complex medical negligence claims is difficult to envisage.  Redress schemes are most appropriate for consideration when there are key common areas between claims.The current protocols afford opportunity for parties to engage in early collation of expert evidence, a cards on the table approach and where properly followed, early decisions on liability in the hope of avoiding unnecessary litigation.  Medical Negligence practitioners are strongly encouraged to appropriately engage with the Pre-action Protocol on Clinical Negligence, consider appropriate use of Standstill agreements and obtain expert evidence in a timely fashion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to reduce legal costs in his Department.
answer_text: Similar to any other costs, branches in the Department who commission any legal work are responsible for reviewing and scrutinising the costs to ensure they are reasonable, and that the Management of Public Money Northern Ireland guidance is adhered to.In the current financial environment, everyone is being encouraged to bear down on all costs in an effort to find savings that can be channelled to mitigate potential cuts to services to our citizens. However, it is necessary for the Department to receive appropriate advice in relation to legal issues, as not to do so could have wider cost and other consequences.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department is taking to clear the backlog of outstanding medical negligence cases.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the processes he will put in place to ensure medical negligence cases are settled or closed in the shortest possible timeframe.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10664/22-27, and given his Department has oversight of the five Health and Social Care Trusts, for his assessment of the questions contained in AQW 9135/22-27.
answer_text: As referenced previously, I am not aware of any impending withdrawal of Red Cross services and possible impact on waiting times.  As you have rightly stated the DOH has oversight of the five HSC Trusts and therefore I am providing you with an update on each of their positions in relation to this matter:Belfast Trust recognise the vital service provided by the Red Cross and continue to be involved in discussions and negotiations with them regarding this year’s contract and proposed changes.  The proposed increase in costs of the contract will mean that access criteria will have to be reviewed so that the trust can stay within budget.  At this stage the Belfast Trust are not exploring alternative providers.  Belfast Trust does not monitor the waiting time for wheelchairs, as service users’ family or carers organise the pick-up of the wheelchair directly with the Red Cross office. Northern Trust have a two-year contract in place with the Red Cross which runs from April 2023 until March 2025. The British Red Cross contacted the Trust in February 2024 to request a review of the current contract values due to concerns about the sustainability of the service. The Trust are currently in communication with the British Red Cross in relation to the contract and the future provision of this service.South Eastern Trust contractual arrangements with the Red Cross are currently under review and negotiation is ongoing.  The Trust recognises the vital service provided by the Red Cross and is not exploring alternative providers.Southern Trust- The British Red Cross have written to the Southern Trust seeking additional funding to enable them to continue providing this service. The Red Cross have asked for an increase of £20,818 per annum or alternatively accommodation to operate the service from and an additional £8,000 - £9000 funding. The Southern Trust has met with the British Red Cross regarding this request and is currently considering this. A follow up meeting is to be arranged in the coming weeks.   Western Trust are not withdrawing from their contract with the Red Cross.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the development of the Area Integrated Partnership Boards; and (ii) what boards have been established.
answer_text: To support learning during development of the Integrated Care System for Northern Ireland model, a Test Area Integrated Partnership Board (AIPB) was established in May 2023 in the Southern Trust area. This exercise presented an opportunity to test this key element of the model and assess the balance of the membership mix, understand the needs of the organisations and sectors involved, and in essence highlight any challenges, and where we need to focus our attention moving forward.The Test AIPB completed eleven meetings by December 2023, at which point an evaluation of the Test was undertaken, the findings and recommendations of which have been used to support the future implementation. Whilst the Test has provided invaluable learning to the model, it has been recognised that this is just one aspect, and the roll-out of the entire model in shadow form will provide an additional period of learning over and above that derived from the Test to further inform the model.A roll-out schedule for standing up the ICS NI model has been developed, with a view to having the model operational over the coming months. This will include the five shadow AIPBs being established by the autumn.To support this, the selection and nomination processes for members of all five shadow AIPBs has commenced in January and this process is nearing completion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Integrated Care System NI framework.
answer_text: The Integrated Care System for Northern Ireland (ICS NI) Framework has recently been updated to reflect the development of the model.The updated version of the Framework is available online.Work has commenced on the roll-out of the ICS NI model in shadow form and the Framework will be kept under review in light of any emerging learning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people awaiting a domiciliary care package to enable hospital discharge, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: Information on the number of patients in hospital unable to be discharged as they were awaiting a domiciliary care package to be put in place is not held centrally. This has been provided by each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust for the position on 1st May 2024 and is detailed in table 1 overleaf.Table 1: Number of in-patients awaiting a domiciliary care package to enable hospital discharge, broken down by hospital, for the position on 1st May 2024 HospitalNumber of InpatientsAltnagelvin Area Hospital4South West Acute Hospital13Waterside Hospital4Omagh Hospital & Primary Care Centre2Ulster Hospital4Royal Victoria Hospital11Mater Hospital2Musgrave Park Hospital9Belfast City Hospital1Antrim Area Hospital3Causeway Hospital1Dalriada Community Hospital2Inver Community Hospital1Mid Ulster Hospital2Robinson Hospital3Craigavon Area Hospital9Daisy Hill Hospital7South Tyrone Hospital5Lurgan Hospital6Total89Source: Health and Social Care TrustsDomiciliary care services continue to experience significant pressures across the region, and there are a range of factors currently impacting upon delivery of this service; this includes significant retention issues in regard to domiciliary care staff.Where delivery of care packages has been negatively impacted due to challenges regarding capacity, Trusts have in place mitigation measures to try to ensure services continue to be provided. Since September 2022, measures have been in place for hospital discharge where a domiciliary care package cannot be provided on a timely basis. In such circumstances, individuals should be offered a place (without charge) in appropriate residential or nursing accommodation on a temporary basis if their needs cannot be met safely at home without some form of support.	In recognition of the challenges facing the independent social care sector, I recently announced a £70m funding package across a range of service provision, including domiciliary care. The purpose of this funding is to ensure independent sector providers have the necessary funding to meet the challenges of increases to national living wage from the beginning of April.  I have also established structures to consider how we move the sector to a Real Living Wage employer base and significant work is ongoing to build the case for the additional funding that will be required to deliver this.	There is clearly a longer term fundamental need to deliver reform and transformation in Health and Social Care. In light of this my Department has established the Adult Social Care Collaborative Forum. There are a number of workstreams in progress, including one specifically considering maximising capacity within domiciliary care services. Work is ongoing to develop a plan to grow capacity in domiciliary care across the region; a new model for domiciliary care, and to take forward key aspects of social care workforce development.My Department remains committed to the provision of a high quality domiciliary care service, and will con

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 9135/22-27, who holds a regional contract with the British Red Cross for provision of mobility aid services.
answer_text: The Five Health and Social Care Trusts hold direct contacts with the British Red Cross.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether GPs attending the Respiratory Forum meetings or working groups will be provided with locum cover.
answer_text: I can confirm that GPs attending the Respiratory Forum will be provided with locum fees from an existing funding source in place for 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to set a smoke-free target date.
answer_text: The Ten-Year Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland has an overall aim to create a tobacco-free society. A final review of the current strategy was published in September 2023. The final review of the Strategy acknowledged the stakeholder support for a smoke-free target date, and this will be further considered during the development of the successor strategy. It is important that the setting of such ambitious targets includes considered, resourced and realistic plans as to how to achieve the target. I have recently announced my intention to seek NI Assembly support for inclusion of NI in the UK Government Tobacco and Vapes Bill.  I believe that the measures set out in that Bill have the potential to make a huge impact on smoking prevalence rates here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on a lung health strategy.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how many people availed of the cross-border healthcare directive; (ii) the amount of money spent; and (iii) the procedures which were carried out under the directive, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Cross-Border Healthcare Directive (CBHD) ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU at the end of December 2020. Therefore, figures are only available for 3 of the last 5 financial years. Table 1 (EU Cross-Border Healthcare Directive) Financial Year2019/202020 (April – Directive closure in Dec)2021/22 *No. of reimbursement claims received11681163847Overall cost of reimbursement claims£5.5m£5.8m£4.0mAs you will be aware, I introduced the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce NI waiting lists. The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the CBHD. In June 2022, I announced that the RoIRS would be extended beyond the planned 30 June 2022 deadline, until an additional £5m of new funding had been committed. The RoIRS closed to new applications on Wednesday 21 September 2022 when the additional £5m funding had been exhausted. You may be also interested in the figures for RoIRS.Table 2 (Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme)Financial Year2021/222022/232023/24*No. of reimbursement claims received4722183907Overall cost of reimbursement claims£3.92m£13.5m£3.98mOn average the vast majority of claims under the CBHD and the subsequent RoIRS above related to Orthopaedic treatments (approx. 86% and 88% respectively). With Ophthalmology treatments (approx. 5% and 3%) being the second most common speciality.*Please note: Both the CBHD and RoIRS were pre-approval schemes. As such, patients who had been approved for treatment may not have submitted their receipts in the same year as approved. Therefore, committed costs in each financial year may not be paid within the same year the application is approved. As a result, there were also claims processed during the period that the schemes were being wound down after they were closed to new applicants.
