question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the next Respiratory Forum meeting will take place.
answer_text: The three-year Service Framework for respiratory services came to an end in 2019 and as the Regional Respiratory Forum was planning to review the work programme, the pandemic took hold.  The Forum has not met since, but proposals are currently being developed by the Department of Health and the Public Health Agency for agreement for a reformed Forum which will have oversight of all respiratory improvement plans. It is expected that the Forum will be re-launched by Autumn 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether mesothelioma is prioritised in the ongoing work of the Respiratory Forum as a priority theme.
answer_text: Mesothelioma as a disease is managed alongside lung cancer care. This means that it is out of the scope of the NI Respiratory Improvement Plan but within the scope of the NI Cancer Programme. Plans are in place to recruit the first regional Mesothelioma Clinical Nurse Specialist within Belfast Health & Social Care Trust this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the budget allocation for Talking Therapy Hubs for 2023-24.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the geographic areas currently not serviced by the Primary Care Talking Therapy Hubs, in each Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) whether there are plans to address these gaps.
answer_text: Talking Therapy Hubs provision across all localities is currently available in both the Belfast and South Eastern Trust areas. The Western Health and Social care Trust currently have one Primary Care Talking Therapy Hub which is situated in Oldbridge House, Londonderry, within the Foyle constituency. Within Southern Trust the Talking Therapy Hubs are encompassed within the Steps to Wellness Service. Steps to Wellness is based on the NHS England ‘Talking Therapies, for anxiety and depression programme (formerly known as Improving Access to Psychological Therapies, IAPT’) The IAPT model offers evidence based cognitive behavioural therapy approaches for individuals with mild-moderate mental health difficulties.  Steps to Wellness is provided digitally in group work format and is available across all of the Southern Trust, there are no physical hubs.  The Northern Trust do not currently provide a Talking Therapy Hub. However, Northern Emotional Well-being Service (NEWS) is a Hub service which is provided by a Community &Voluntary sector partner, LINKS.  NEWS operates Trust Wide via a locality-based model across the four Trust localities Antrim/Ballymena, East Antrim, Mid Ulster and Causeway and The Glens.Action 5 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-31 relates to expanding therapy hubs, that should be managed with primary care and link with the wider work on establishing mental health as an integral part of the primary care multi-disciplinary team.  The future roll out of Talking Therapy Hubs is dependant on securing funding in 2024 and beyond, to support a regional approach to implementation. This is in line with the published Mental Health Strategy Funding plan which accompanies the Mental Health Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of people who accessed talking therapy; (ii) the average length of waiting time, from referral received from the GP to first therapy appointment; (iii) the total spend; and (iv) the underspend recorded, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The department does not hold this information centrally and as such each Trust was required to provide their respective data.  However, Trusts advised that they are unable to provide the breakdown as requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) plans to move Talking Therapy Hubs to primary care; and (ii) the development of an agreed regional model for expansion, as outlined in the Mental Health Plan 2021-2031.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether the immediate uplift of £504,000 per annum in the Health and Social Care Trusts, as recommended by the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, has been allocated; and (ii) for an update on additional funding provided for Talking Therapies since 2021, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment on workforce capacity in Community Dental Services, including what steps are being taken by his Department to achieve workforce growth as detailed in the 2018 report Skills for Health Workforce Review for Dental Services in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has recently consulted on Oral Health Improvement Plans for Children and Older People in Northern Ireland.  These include recommendations on new models of care delivery supporting General Dental Services to provide preventive and operative care for patients with complex needs, including the growing ageing population and those with multiple co-morbidities.  There are also recommendations to increase capacity within Community Dental Service to support greater utilisation of skills of all members of the dental team. Feedback received through this consultation, which ended on 4 March 2024, will inform further review and finalisation of oral health priorities in respect of children and older people and the future requirements for the Community Dental Service workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress made on the range of dental workforce issues highlighted at the Dental Workforce Review stakeholder engagement session in September 23 and the associated Engage Report.
answer_text: The Chief Dental Officer is currently developing an Implementation Plan addressing relevant recommendations from the Workforce Review for Dental Services in Northern Ireland, which will be informed by feedback obtained through the stakeholder engagement event in September 2023.Developing the roles and contributions of all members of the dental team to support patient care and treatment most effectively is likely to require changes to regulation and contractual arrangements, and will have implications for the numbers of dental professionals required in the system.  It will be important to secure support from the dental profession and the public for these changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail whole-time-equivalent figures for the number of dentists employed in Community Dental Services, in each of the last five years; and (ii) what plans will be put in place to increase this, in light of increased pressures including from a growing ageing population and addressing the patient backlog post-COVID.
answer_text: (i) Information on the number of whole time equivalent dentists employed in HSC Community Dental Services in the last five years is in the following table.At dateNumber of whole-time equivalentHSC Community Dentists31 December 201968.8631 December 202069.2031 December 202165.1531 December 202264.9531 December 202366.15Source: HRPTS(ii) The Community Dental Service (CDS) is a small, specialised service that provides dental care to patients who, because of their particular needs, cannot be treated within a general dental practice setting.  This often includes older people residing in care homes, many of whom are keeping their natural teeth into later life.While the COVID-19 pandemic presented unprecedented challenges to the planning and delivery of health services, including dental services, Community Dental teams have been rebuilding their services in line with other HSC Trust delivered care through HSC Trust Delivery Plans.  This is monitored through the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group.My Department has recently consulted on Oral Health Improvement Plans for Children and Older People in Northern Ireland.  These include recommendations on new models of care delivery supporting General Dental Services to provide preventive and operative care for older adults.  There are also recommendations to increase capacity within the CDS and support greater utilisation of skills of all members of the dental team. Feedback received through this consultation, which ended on 4 March 2024,  will inform further review and finalisation of oral health priorities in respect of children and older people and the future requirements for the Community Dental Service workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on steps his Department is taking to address the lack of regional access to special care consultant referrals for Community Dental Services dentists across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: People with disabilities, and complex needs receive dental care across a range of settings in Northern Ireland – General Dental Services, Community Dental Services, and the Hospital Dental Services.Special Care Dentistry cares for and treats patients over the age of 13 years who may display behaviours that challenge, have significant co-morbidity and have complex care needs. Those patients with the most complex needs may require treatment under a general anaesthetic due to their inability to cooperate or cope with invasive dental proceduresDental care and treatment for this group are provided across the services by the dental workforce. These include dental care professionals (DCPs); General Dental Practitioners, Community Dental Officers, Specialists and Hospital Dental Consultants.Health and Social Care Trusts across Northern Ireland have different staff configurations. There is currently no regional referral access to a Special Care Consultant for Community Dental Service dentists.The Belfast Trust provides care to this group of patients within the Community Dental Service and the Consultant led Special Care Dentistry Service, based in the Royal Victoria Hospital. The remaining four Trusts provide care to this group within the Community Dental Service. Development and commissioning of new services for local populations are dependent on Trusts submitting their cases to the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG)  and DoH. This will require HSC Trusts to provide sufficient evidence and current data to support the consideration of a Business Case for Special Care Dentistry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the progress of the Developing a More Strategic Approach to 14 to 19 Education and Training - a Framework to Transform 14 to 19 Education and Training Provision.
answer_text: The 14 to 19 Framework is a joint programme between my Department and the Department of Education. There are 34 Actions within the Framework of which my Department is directly responsible for eight, the Department of Education is directly responsible for 18 with the rest taken forward as a joint responsibility. Progress has been made in a number of key areas:In relation to careers, work has commenced with both DfE and DE to introduce new measures for careers guidance outcomes, seeking to develop clear, common, transparent and accountable quality standards. Work has also commenced into the investment in the development of a consolidated Careers Portal.In DfE a review of Vocational Qualifications is looking at the current qualification landscape and considering future delivery models. Consideration is being given to several alternative delivery models to ensure we have a stable supply of vocational qualifications that meet the needs of learners and employers. In DE the roll out of the NI Curriculum Refresh has commenced through a series of awareness sessions for primary school principals. The next stage is planned to commence in Autumn 2024 with a roll out of further sessions to curriculum co-ordinators. In DE, officials are currently drafting a document for engagement with stakeholders, to propose a draft vision statement and guiding principles for Sixth Form provision. Work is ongoing to identify post 16 & 19 opportunities for Special Educational Needs learners, including gaps to inform alignment of 14-19 education delivery to support these pathways/opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any current discussions or plans to amalgamate any of the South Eastern Regional College campuses.
answer_text: Following engagement by my officials with the College, I can confirm that there are no current discussions or plans to amalgamate any of the South Eastern Regional College campuses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the funding secured for the resurfacing of the Hilltown and Rathfriland Road, Newry; (ii) when this resurfacing will be completed; and (iii) when he became aware that the resurfacing work was confirmed.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the condition of the Rathfriland to Hilltown Road and I can advise that it will be considered for inclusion in a future programme of work when it will be prioritised against the many other roads in need of investment.The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of students enrolled in the South Eastern Regional College, for each campus, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Following engagement by my officials with the College, I can confirm that South Eastern Regional College does not hold the number of students enrolled in each of its campuses. A student may have more than one enrolment at different campuses depending on the programme being studied. However, I can confirm that for the academic year 2022/23 there were 11,285 students enrolled at South Eastern Regional College.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the active engagement undertaken to develop new courses, broken down by each South Eastern Regional College campus.
answer_text: Following engagement by my officials, I can confirm that South Eastern Regional College has developed numerous new courses across all of its campuses in recent years covering both Further Education and Higher Education provision. South Eastern Regional College engages extensively with a wide range of stakeholders from the community, voluntary and statutory sector as well as employers to ensure that the curriculum provides students with relevant skills, meets the demand of local employers, and is aligned to government priorities. Stakeholder engagement takes place using both Sectoral Partnerships and the Curriculum Hubs where collaborative provision is being developed, and through various departmental programmes including Skills and InnovateUs programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what budget has been allocated to the Healthy Child Healthy Future universal programme in each of the last five years; (ii) how many nurses service schools in Northern Ireland under the School Nursing Service; and (iii) to detail the schools that have a dedicated school nurse.
answer_text: The Healthy Child Healthy Future (HCHF) Universal Programme is delivered across the Trusts by Midwives, Health Visitors and School Nurses through core budget funding from DoH’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group which is recurrently allocated to Trusts and is not ringfenced for the Programme. It is therefore not possible to provide a disaggregated figure. In addition to the Universal Programme, the enhanced school nursing budget is funded by the Department of Education through the Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework.Regionally, there is a Specialist Community Public Health (School Nursing) workforce of 61.1 whole-time equivalent.  Additional support staff are also involved in the delivery of the programme. The schools which have a dedicated school nurse are as follows:  Mercy College, Belfast (BHSC Trust area); Abbey Community College, Newtownabbey (NHSC Trust area); St Malachy’s High School, Castlewellan (SEHSC Trust area); St Patrick’s College, Dungannon (SHSC Trust area); and Lisneal College, Derry/Londonderry (WHSC Trust area).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many staff in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust will be redeployed from administration duties to other roles as a result of the Encompass programme; and (ii) for an update on the timescales for this.
answer_text: It was agreed by all Trusts within the HSC that there would be no job losses due to the implementation of encompass. The South Eastern Trust is currently working through the management of change process with any affected staff following encompass implementation. The encompass programme will potentially create new roles and staff will have the opportunity to apply for those.Timescales for such changes are not currently known.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the targets that have been set for ASSIST NI; and (ii) whether these targets are published on the Department’s website.
answer_text: (i) The current key performance indicators are listed in Annex A.(ii) These are not published on the Department’s website.Annex AKPI CategoryASSIST NI - Key Performance Indictors (KPIs)MinimumTargetKPI 1 – ACCESS TO SERVICE. To provide victims of domestic and sexual violence and abuse, who have engaged with the criminal justice system, the MARAC or the Rowan, with access to a co- ordinated and consistent advocacy service capable of supporting them across Northern Ireland, and to report on service provision to the client.The service provider must have attempted direct contact with triaged cases (where there are correct contact details) within 48 hours of the referral being made to the service. 90%At least 2000 victims to be accepted into service each year (on average 166 per month), adjusting for proportionate staffing levels where necessary. 90%Where appropriate, an attempt will be made to signpost those accepted into service whose needs cannot be met by the advocacy service, to an appropriate agency for alternative intervention with records and reasons being maintained.80%	1.4  Onward referrals (transfer out) to other agencies will be attempted within one working day of a decision to refer, or within one hour if immediate assistance is required.  Information should be captured to demonstrate to which organisation the referral was made.90%1.5   The Contractor will contact service users and request Section 75 information, including age and gender specific information.  This must be shared with the Clients on request and as part of the annual report 90%1.6   The Contractor will share feedback, research, case studies and other learning on victims to contribute to the development of strategy and policy development and implementation of strategic plans.  This must be shared as part of the annual report.N/A KPI 2 - QUALITY OF SERVICE.To ensure all victims referred to the advocacy service are provided with high level support staff will be appropriately skilled, trained and resourced. Victims will demonstrate whether they feel supported and are satisfied with the quality of the support provided. 2.1   The Contractor will gather feedback from service users upon entry to the service, periodically thereafter, and on exit from the service, where practicable, in order to measure the impact of service delivery.  80%2.2     Service users report feeling safer, as a consequence of using the service.80%2.3     Service users report an improvement in their coping abilities and well-being as a consequence    of using the service.80%2.4     Service users are satisfied with the quality of engagement and how well they are kept informed. 80%    Service users feel supported and helped by the service. 80%2.6   PSNI officers and SARC staff are satisfied with the quality of advocacy service, from the      perspective of their respective organisations. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of (a) the Women Against State Pension Inequality campaign; and (b) the proposed £3,950 compensation cap; and (ii) what plans he has to engage with the Department for Work and Pensions in relation to the compensation scheme.
answer_text: The Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman report into how changes to women’s State Pension age was published by the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) on 21 March 2024. I await the Westminster Government’s response to that report. In the meantime, my officials will continue to engage with DWP on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the process by which ASSIST NI was appointed; (ii) whether it was appointed by an open procurement process; and (iii) how many groups or consortia were considered.
answer_text: (i) ASSIST NI was appointed following a procurement process. The competition was advertised on the eTendersNI portal on 16 December 2020. The closing date for the competition was 3pm on 1 February 2021. The contract was awarded on 7 April 2021.(ii) This was an open process.(iii) Two bids were received.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what options are being considered to address congestion in Edward Street, Downpatrick.
answer_text: The Department is currently carrying out a study to establish if any viable road safety and traffic progression improvements could be made along Edward Street in Downpatrick. Part of this project involves the development of a range of options to help alleviate the current traffic situation on the local road network. This work is ongoing. It is the Department’s intention to consult with Downpatrick DEA forum (District Electoral Area) consisting of elected representatives, community representatives and other government departments once this draft is complete.  It is my intention that constituency MLAs will also be made aware of the outcome of the study through officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in order to aid accountability and transparency, whether his Department will consult with constituency MLAs, as well as those listed in his letter COR-1107-2024, in relation to the pilot School Street scheme in Downpatrick.
answer_text: I am advised that the Department’s consultants are currently drafting a feasibility study which is not yet ready to be released.  Once this draft, it is the Department’s intention to consult with Downpatrick DEA (District Electoral Area) forum consisting of elected representatives, community representatives and other government departments.  It is also my intention for the feasibility study to be provided to the constituency MLA`s.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether there is an annual report or evaluation for each year that ASSIST NI delivers its service; and (ii) whether the document is published on the Department’s website.
answer_text: (i) ASSIST NI produces annual reports to assist with the management of the contract. An evaluation of the service is currently being undertaken by the Department’s Analytical and Statistical Group. and Research Agency.(ii) The annual reports are not published on the Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) whether the funding for ASSIST NI is allocated annually; and (ii) the process by which funding is determined.
answer_text: (i) Under the current contract a maximum budget of £800k is allocated annually for each financial year, pro rata. This maximum amount was set out in the Instructions to Tenderers documentation.  (ii) Funding is provided on receipt of invoices from ASSIST NI, based costs incurred.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how much funding ASSIST NI has received in each year since October 2021.
answer_text: The contract was awarded in April 2021. An implementation phase followed, leading to the commencement of service delivery in September 2021. For the financial year of 2021/22, the service received £403,993.72. For 2022/23, the service received £690,538.58. The total amount for this financial year of 2023/24 will be around £513,178, with the figure being confirmed once final invoices are received.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) why landowners do not receive copies of non-compliance reports from NIEA in respect of licence holders leasing their land; and (ii) whether he plans to change this.
answer_text: My department is responsible for issuing waste management licences (WML) and a WML holder may be a landowner or a tenant.In response to your questions I can confirm.NIEA’s statutory responsibility is with the WML holder, not the landowner, and compliance with the conditions of a WML is solely the responsibility for the WML holder.There are no plans to change this; however, local written agreements can be arranged between the landowner, tenant and NIEA.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when his Department will publish a workforce racial equality report.
answer_text: Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act requires public authorities to promote equality of opportunity and good relations amongst specified groups, including racial groups.  As a public authority, the Department of Health is required to submit an Equality Scheme to the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland (ECNI) which sets out how it fulfils these duties.  It is also required to submit an Annual Progress Report to the ECNI on the implementation of its Equality Scheme, including progress on delivery of actions they have identified to promote equality of opportunity and good relations.As public authorities, these requirements also apply to the Non-Departmental Public Bodies sponsored by the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 7931/22-27, how much his Department has spent maintaining car parks in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Safety inspections of car parks are carried out as part of the normal road inspection regime and any necessary repairs are included as part of the overall carriageway maintenance works. Therefore, a full breakdown of the costs associated with maintaining car parks is not available.There is a £45k annual budget for externally charged operational costs of providing Park & Ride/Share sites, and in addition my Department’s internal contractor undertakes environmental maintenance activities such as sweeping and cleaning in car parks.The figures below set out the costs we have been able to identify on car park expenditure in the last five years, including my Department’s internal workforce contractor. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many people have received the meningitis vaccination (i) under the age of eighteen; and (ii) over the age of eighteen, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Meningitis is a clinical presentation that can be caused by a number of different infections with the most common being viral or bacterial pathogens. Not all pathogens that cause meningitis are vaccine preventable. The are therefore several vaccinations offered as protection against meningitis. Vaccine preventable bacterial pathogens that have the potential to cause serious meningitis include meningococcus, pneumococcus, haemophilus and influenzae B (HiB).The vaccines used within the Northern Ireland regional vaccination programmes that protect against the clinical presentation of meningitis are: The Men B vaccine, given at 8 weeks, 16 weeks and a booster at one year of age. The Men B vaccine offers protection against meningococcal group B bacteria, which are a common cause of meningitis in young children. The pneumococcal vaccine, given at 12 weeks and one year of age. The pneumococcal vaccine offers protection against serious infections caused by pneumococcal bacteria, including meningitis. A single dose is also offered to everyone at age 65 years of age.The Hib/Men C vaccine, given at one year of age. The meningitis C component of this vaccine offers protection against a type of bacteria called meningococcal group C bacteria, which can cause meningitis. The HiB component of this vaccine provide booster protection against invasive HiB disease, including meningitis (the primary course of HiB infection is 3 doses given at 8, 12 and 16 weeks). The Men ACWY vaccine, given at 14-15 years of age as part of the school-based vaccination programme. The Men ACWY vaccine offers protection against 4 types of bacteria that can cause meningitis: meningococcal groups A, C, W and Y. Data is collected based on the % of all eligible people who receive the vaccine. Vaccination coverage for the completed primary vaccination programme is assessed when a child is aged 1 and 2 years of age. Children are scheduled to receive additional vaccinations at 3-5 years of age, therefore vaccine coverage is also measured at 5 years of age. These data are available on the Public Health Agency (PHA) website for each quarter, broken down by HSC Trust, from 2011 as follows:Completed primary immunisations at 12 months: 12 months of age Sept 2023.pdf (hscni.net) Completed primary immunisations at 24 months: 24 months of age Sept 2023.pdf (hscni.net) Vaccination coverage at 5 years: 5 years of age Sept 2023.pdf (hscni.net)  The Men ACWY vaccine was introduced in 2015. Data is available from academic year 2015/16, broken down by Trust, on the PHA website school leaver booster website tables (inc num).pdf (hscni.net) (page 2).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he is aware of the impending withdrawal of the British Red Cross’ mobility aid service which provides mobile aids free of charge to those exiting hospital; (ii) , in the absence of this service, what systems he is putting in place to replace it; and (iii) whether he expects waiting times for mobility aids to exceed the two days patients currently wait.
answer_text: The Department of Health do not hold a regional contract with the Red Cross and I am therefore not aware of any impending withdrawal of Red Cross services and possible impact on waiting times.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the legislation he will introduce during this Assembly mandate.
answer_text: As I have previously outlined to the Committee for Infrastructure, there are several key areas for which I am considering introducing primary legislation. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will review (i) his Department’s position on using Enhertu for breast cancer patients; and (ii) its availability in the health service.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. Where NICE has recommended trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) for use, it is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland, where infrastructure is in place and the service has capacity.In the absence of, a published NICE determination, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) which is responsible for commissioning, may also seek and potentially apply decisions made by other well established UK bodies as part of its Managed Entry process, pending any recommendation from NICE to inform longer-term commissioning decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the closing date for applications to the further support package of the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme was (i) set by his Department; and (ii) signed off by the Minister.
answer_text: The councils are responsible for administrating the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme and for setting the closing date for applications.  There was no requirement for the closing date to be signed off by me or my officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will (i) review the efficacy of the Rare Diseases Action Plan; and (ii) consider publishing an updated plan.
answer_text: I am pleased with the progress that has been made by the Northern Ireland Rare Diseases Implementation Group (NIRDIG) on the Northern Ireland Rare Diseases Action Plan, outlined in the Year 1 Progress Report: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/progress-report-northern-ireland-rare-diseases-action-plan-2022-23. Therefore, I have no plans to review the efficacy of the Action Plan at this time.The Action Plan was developed by NIRDIG in consultation with the rare disease community in Northern Ireland, to identify actions to be taken forward over a 5-year period. I have no plans to publish an updated Action Plan within the timeframe of the current Action Plan, however a Year 2 Progress Report is currently in development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what representations he has made; and (ii) what conversations he has had with NI Water regarding the moratorium on development in Dundrum, Co. Down.
answer_text: I have not made any representation to NI Water in respect of any development issues in Dundrum. NI Water has advised that there is no moratorium on development in Dundrum since the NIEA revised the Water Order Consent (WOC) for Dundrum WwTW in 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when the moratorium on development for Dundrum, Co. Down, will be lifted.
answer_text: My Department has not placed a moratorium on development for Dundrum.I can advise that my officials from 2013 issued an ‘objection’ to development which would rely on the NI Water Ltd wastewater infrastructure serving Dundrum Village. This position reflected concerns regarding the deteriorating water quality within Dundrum Bay. Whilst the Dundrum Wastewater Treatment Plant has since seen significant improvements, the Dundrum Village collection system fails to meet the environmental requirements. However, recognising the improvement and supported by investigations carried out by NIW Ltd, the NIEA has now moved to a position of ‘highlighting concern’ due to the constraint in the wastewater collection system serving Dundrum Village. This position is consistent with all other towns and villages suffering from constraint.If consulted on development in the Dundrum Village, Planning Officers and developers will be advised to consult with NI Water to develop a solution for wastewater.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how the criteria for the further support package of the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme was set.
answer_text: When setting the criteria for the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme my officials drew on research of similar flood support schemes in Britain and south of Ireland, and evidence presented by local government and business representatives in the impacted areas. As the strand of government best placed to advise on local issues, Council feedback was considered before finalising the criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail which of the 14 actions in the Rare Diseases Action Plan have been implemented.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Rare Diseases Action Plan includes actions to be taken forward over a 5-year period. The Action Plan is being progressed by the Northern Ireland Rare Diseases Implementation Group (NIRDIG). All the actions in the Plan are at various stages of implementation, however full implementation within the 5-year timeframe is subject to available resource. The Action Plan’s Year 1 Progress Report is provided at the following link:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/progress-report-northern-ireland-rare-diseases-action-plan-2022-23

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of the Rare Diseases Action Plan 2022/23.
answer_text: A Progress Report on the Northern Ireland Rare Diseases Action Plan 2022-23 was published on 7 September 2023. The report details progress during the first year of the Action Plan from March 2022 to March 2023. The Report is available at the following link:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/progress-report-northern-ireland-rare-diseases-action-plan-2022-23A Year 2 Progress Report update is currently being prepared and will be published in due course.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide a list of the Executive Ministers travelling to Washington DC for St Patrick’s Day celebrations.
answer_text: The First Minister and the deputy First Minister travelled to Washington DC to attend events and meetings in the period around St Patrick’s Day. The Minister for Education and the Minister for the Economy also travelled.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when the Rare Disease Forum last met; and (ii) when they will meet again.
answer_text: The UK Rare Diseases Forum last met on 15 January 2024 and is due to meet again on 24 April 2024. The Forum meets four times per year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what funding allocation has been made to the implementation of the Rare Diseases Action Plan.
answer_text: In 2023/24, my Department has allocated £50k of non-recurrent funding to support implementation of the NI Rare Diseases Action Plan.  This is in addition to existing funding in the wider Health and Social Care system (HSC) which supports the diagnosis and treatment of rare diseases, including: recurrent funding of £186k for adult and paediatric rare disease clinical leadership posts; £70k non-recurrent funding for additional genome sequencing to improve rare disease diagnosis; and approximately £35m of expenditure on specialist medicines which is directly related to rare diseases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the report into the cause of flooding in Downpatrick in November 2023; and (ii) when it will be published.
answer_text: Following the significant flooding that occurred in Autumn 2023 across the South and East, including Downpatrick, a joint organisational learning review has been instigated to capture any learning identified in terms of preparedness, response and recovery and to inform any future collective civil contingencies preparations. Whilst this review is being co-ordinated by the TEO, my Department and others will lead on those aspects for which we are responsible. I can confirm that an independent consultant has been appointed by my Department to provide a technical insight into the causes of the flooding and to present considerations to inform any viable solutions. This review is ongoing, and it is anticipated that a final report will be published in June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will review the test requirement for towing a trailer or caravan, in relation to the 3,500 kg maximum authorised mass you are allowed to carry.
answer_text: Legislation introduced in Britain on 16 December 2021 removed testing requirements for towing a trailer (BE entitlement), meaning that all drivers who passed a category B (car) driving test from 1 January 1997 are now entitled to tow trailers up to 3,500 kg Maximum Authorized Mass (MAM).  This change was made in an effort to address the HGV driver shortage situation in England, Scotland and Wales.At that time testing was not seen as a significant reason for HGV driver shortages here. This position has not changed.In EU Member States BE testing is still a requirement, as it is here. Road safety continues to be of paramount importance and therefore I have no plans to introduce similar changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update and timescales for the (i) publication; (ii) implementation; and (iii) commissioning plan of the new learning disability framework.
answer_text: The Department established a Task & Finish Group to finalise a Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM) as part of a wider exercise to improve outcomes for children and adults. Throughout 2023, there has been a significant review of evidence, data, and stakeholder feedback to develop a revised model of service, which was shared with key stakeholders for comment in December 2023.The next phase of this work will involve wider engagement and the development of a costed implementation plan throughout Spring 2024. It is critical that the LDSM is supported by those that use, and those that deliver, LD services and I will consider how we seek wider views on the content and implementation in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) how many car parks DfI Roads are responsible for maintaining; and (ii) to detail the annual budget for the maintenance of these car parks.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for maintaining 85 car parks. The safety inspections of the car parks are carried out as part of the normal road inspection regime and any necessary repairs are included as part of the overall carriageway maintenance works. Therefore, a breakdown of the costs associated with car parks is not available.In addition, there is a £45k annual budget for environmental maintenance and costs associated with buildings in my Department’s park and ride sites.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people in Northern Ireland currently pay the Immigration Health Surcharge.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the information requested.For those not covered by an exemption, an Immigration Health Surcharge (IHS) is collected by the Home Office as part of the UK visa and immigration application process. As such, the IHS is operated and monitored by the Home Office. All UK administrations then receive a pro-rata share of the income generated, in line with the Barnett formula.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy why (i) AQW 5583/22-27; and (ii) AQW 5722/22-27 remains unanswered.
answer_text: I provided responses to AQWs 5583/22-27 and 5722/22-27 on 28 March and 15 April respectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for his assessment of the outcomes of the innovation lab, developed by the Department of Finance, following the fires in the Mourne mountains in April 2021; (ii) to detail the priorities pursuant to these outcomes; and (iii) what financial commitment his Department will make to achieve these outcomes in the year ahead.
answer_text: The former Finance Minister, Conor Murphy MLA, commissioned the Innovation Lab to take forward a project in relation to the long-term sustainable future of the Mourne mountains. Work undertaken during 2022 highlighted the positive contribution that the Mournes make to health, wellbeing, culture and tourism. This also highlighted a number of issues that need to be addressed including a disconnected environmental approach, negative impacts of tourism, overburdened car parks, lack of alternative transport and poor roads, paths and facilities.Subsequently in March 2023, the Innovation Lab convened representatives from various organisations to review the research findings and co-design key actions that could be considered and adopted by an Executive upon its return. One of the suggested actions was for an individual Executive Minister to take a leadership role in relation to the Mournes, and who would publish and progress a Mournes Management Plan.  With the Executive now restored, my officials are engaging with colleagues in the main policy responsible departments to explore how this important work can be taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether his Department will continue to fund the Engagement Ranger service provided by Mourne Heritage Trust; and (ii) how he will ensure that the work undertaken will continue if no funding is available.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency recognises the positive contribution that the Mourne Heritage Trust ‘Engagement Rangers’ delivered during the Covid pandemic due to exceptionally high recreational use during that period.  However, the additional funding for Covid 19 pressures which helped fund these posts is no longer available. It is considered that continued financial support for dedicated ‘Engagement Rangers’ is not sustainable long term through my Department’s Environment Fund. My Department continues to provide significant grant aid to the Mourne Heritage Trust for Mourne AONB Management and Enhancement and the National Trust for Mourne Renewal, both of which include elements of community engagement.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what stage the deceased patient review is at following the Independent Neurology Inquiry; (ii) what the next stage will be; (iii) when the next stage will take place; and (iv) how his Department intends to engage with families.
answer_text: The Expert Review of Records of Deceased Patients of Dr Watt (2008-2018) (i.e. the Deceased Patients Review, or “DPR”) is one of a number of actions taken by the Department in relation to concerns regarding the clinical practice of Dr Michael Watt. The DPR is separate to the Independent Neurology Inquiry (INI), which was established by the Department of Health in 2018 and concluded in 2022.Phase 1 of the DPR has been completed with the formal adoption of operational protocols and a legal framework, agreed in November 2020, to ensure access to the relevant deceased records. Phase 2 of the DPR commenced in April 2021. This phase involved the RQIA commissioning the Royal College of Physicians (RCP) to establish an Expert Panel to review of clinical records of 44 patient records and to report on the findings.  Phase Two comprised two groups: Group One - 29 deceased patients whose family members have approached the RQIA with concerns; andGroup Two - 15 patients who were included in the Belfast Trust’s Cohort One neurology recall but unfortunately died before either attending or completing their re-assessment.The RQIA published the Phase Two RCP overarching reports in November 2022 which are available on the RQIA website - https://www.rqia.org.uk/RQIA/files/2f/2f9d52d7-9ea5-43c4-9b25-9b1998f8ec09.pdf The phased approach to the DPR aims to ensure that the appropriate learning is captured, allowing families to come forward if they wish, and ensuring that any difficulties and challenges arising are given due regard. The RQIA has completed a family engagement process for the families who have subsequently come forward to express an interest in having their relative's clinical records reviewed. This has provided information on what a review of the clinical record of a deceased patient can potentially provide, about its limitations and to share feedback received from several families involved in Phase Two of the Review about their experience of the Review process and its outcomes. My Department and the RQIA are continuing to work together to consider all matters to determine the next steps for the DPR. The pace and extent of the DPR next steps will take all material factors into consideration including: the learnings from the work completed to date (including family expectations and experiences, ethical considerations and systems learning); the time requirements for future work; and the resource requirements (including staff and budget requirements).My Department remains mindful of the delicate nature of this work and intends to provide confirmation regarding the DPR next steps as soon as practicable.The engagement with the families of deceased patients will continue to be approached in a sensitive and ethical manner.   The RQIA is committed to engaging with all interested families in order to enable fully informed decision making and provide support, tailored to individual needs. The RQIA Family Liaison Team continue to keep in regular contact with families

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the pelvic mesh issues; and (ii) to detail what the next stage will be for those impacted.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the report from the Patient Safety Commissioner for England and will take time to consider its findings and recommendations. Officials are also linked to discussions with counterparts in England to understand emerging considerations there.  Anyone who has concerns relating to their pelvic mesh implants should contact either their GP or the consultant who performed their surgery to ensure they receive all appropriate care.The Northern Ireland Mesh Centre at the Belfast City Hospital was approved by Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists in December 2017 as having the specialist expertise to manage women with mesh complications in a multi-professional team. Surgeons at the Mesh Centre, who provide the specialist clinic for mesh complications, continue to collaborate with other surgeons in the UK, developing specialist skills and expertise in the treatment of mesh complications and are now fully trained in this specialist surgery. In addition, the Belfast Trust also delivers pain and psychology clinics as part of the mesh clinic service, which are the first of their kind in the UK, and have received positive feedback from services users and patient representatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the provision of services for those suffering from scoliosis; and (ii) whether he is exploring opportunities for cross-border cooperation to address this issue.
answer_text: (i) Treatment for scoliosis involves the attachment of metal rods to the vertebrae which are subsequently adjusted to straighten the spine over a period of months.  Belfast Trust provides scoliosis surgery for adults and children in Northern Ireland with the procedure undertaken by specialist spinal surgeons in the Royal Victoria Hospital and the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children. Belfast Trust also has a contract with the Royal National Orthopaedic Hospital, and previously non-recurrent funding has been used to support the transfer and treatment of patients at this provider. Whilst current activity delivery continues to face challenges given the intense pressure on the health service, a third of all patients waiting for scoliosis treatment have been waiting for less than 21 weeks.  No patient is waiting longer than 16 weeks for a routine outpatient appointment and there are no urgent patients waiting for assessment.  This is a much-improved position compared to January 2022 when the longest wait for a routine assessment was 132 weeks and the longest urgent waiting time was 17 weeks.My Department remains committed to improving waiting times for scoliosis and continues to engage with the service to this end.(ii) My Department is not currently exploring opportunities for cross-border cooperation in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 5885/22-27, to detail the content of his response to AQW 5744/22-27 which was provided to another Member.
answer_text: I regret that I am unable to reverse the decision to reduce core grant for 2023/24 due to the Department’s continuing financial challenges. Once the budget position for 2024/25 is known, I will be in a position to consider the availability of funding for the Core Grant Scheme for next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the successful school enhancements works application will be funded at St. Mary’s Primary School, Lurganare, Newry.
answer_text: The School Enhancement Programme project at St Mary’s Primary School is currently paused at pre-construction tender stage.At present, budget constraints and pressures in regard to Special Educational Needs placements and urgent health and safety works mean capital funding is not available for the project to proceed to construction. I will continue to make the case for increased investment in the education estate, to allow much-needed projects, including the project at St. Mary’s Primary School to be delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on ongoing pay negotiations.
To ask the Minister of Health what work his Department is undertaking to raise awareness of, and improve care for, rarer forms of cancer, including bile duct cancer.
answer_text: Public Health Agency (PHA) have regular campaigns to raise awareness of cancer.  This includes raising awareness on the signs and symptoms of cancer through awareness campaign activities and on specific dates such as World Cancer Day, Cervical Cancer Prevention Week. All PHA communications on cancer early diagnosis signposts to the 'Be Cancer Aware' website. The website includes information on liver cancer which includes bile duct cancer: https://www.becancerawareni.info/liver-cancerAll cancer patients, including those with rare forms of cancers, are referred to tumour site multidisciplinary team (MDT) meetings, where their cancer diagnosis and treatment are discussed and agreed.The majority of patients also have access to a clinical nurse specialist (CNS) or key worker who will explain the diagnosis and the Plan of Care and support them throughout their journey from the time of diagnosis. CNSs are available under tumour site pathways no matter what the specific type of cancer e.g. a bile duct cancer patient may see a Hepatobiliary Cancer CNS, a rare neuroendocrine tumour patient may see an endocrine CNS, a rare haematological cancer patient may see a relevant haematology CNS, and a rare sarcoma patient may see a Sarcoma CNS.All newly diagnosed patients are provided with an information pack which highlights sources of support and patients are also supported with tumour specific written information regarding their diagnosis and treatment. A Holistic Needs Assessment (HNA) is also offered to patients. Holistic Needs Assessment allows the patient and their CNS to talk through a patient’s needs and concerns, wider than just the concerns for the cancer. This allows the patient and CNS to agree on a plan for the patient’s care and support needs. All cancer patients and their families should be signposted or referred to the Trusts’ Macmillan Health and Wellbeing service, which can refer the patients and carers to available relevant support services and groups including counselling services offered through Cancer Focus. Patients also have access to Macmillan Welfare Rights review for financial advice and support. These Trust support services also provide a listening ear, and further signposting to Trust services and community/voluntary sector services, both for the person with cancer and those who care for and support them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on implementation of the new ten year cancer strategy.
answer_text: The Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022-2032 was launched in March 2022, setting the direction of travel for cancer services for the next decade. Despite the many challenges presented over the proceeding years, considerable progress has been made by my officials within the constrained budgetary resources available on the implementation of a wide range of strategy actions and associated project activities which have provided a foundation for the transformation of cancer services. Two Rapid Diagnosis Centres (RDC) have been established in Whiteabbey, and South Tyrone Hospitals. These are operating a vague symptoms pathway for people with vague but worrying symptoms which may be cancer. The service is available to patients in four out of five Trust areas, with plans to roll out to the South Eastern Trust by Summer 2024.A report on Systemic Anti-Cancer Therapy (primarily chemotherapy) and radiotherapy provision within oncology and haematology was commissioned from York Health Economics Consortium. This has provided a capacity and demand model which will be used to support the planning of Systemic Anti-Cancer Services.  A review of cancer support services for Adolescents and Young Adults (AYA) has also been completed. This review made a number of recommendations which we are currently seeking to implement. Most importantly, work is currently underway to develop regional minimum standards for this service.  This will allow us to ensure that all cancer patients between the age of 13 and 25 can expect to receive the same high quality of service, wherever they live in Northern Ireland.  This also enables us to benchmark AYA services against those in the rest of the UK and Ireland. Northern Ireland is the first part of the UK to commit to implementing an optimal care pathway for pancreatic cancer. This was developed by Pancreatic Cancer UK and the Department is working with the Upper GI Clinical Reference Group, service users and charities to implement this pathway.  A formal implementation update is expected to be published before the end of April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on his Department's approach to the pelvic mesh issue; and (ii) how his Department's approach meets the same timescales as the English model.
answer_text: My Department is aware of report from the Patient Safety Commissioner for England and will take time to consider its findings and recommendations. Officials are also linked in with counterparts in England to understand emerging considerations there.  The Northern Ireland Mesh Centre at the Belfast City Hospital was approved by Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists in December 2017 as having the specialist expertise to manage women with mesh complications in a multi-professional team. Surgeons at the Mesh Centre, who provide the specialist clinic for mesh complications, continue to collaborate with other surgeons in the UK, developing specialist skills and expertise in the treatment of mesh complications and are now fully trained in this specialist surgery. In addition, the Belfast Trust also delivers pain and psychology clinics as part of the mesh clinic service, which are the first of their kind in the UK, and have received positive feedback from services users and patient representatives.Anyone who has concerns relating to their pelvic mesh implants should contact either their GP or the consultant who performed their surgery to ensure they receive all appropriate care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many women will be impacted as a result of recent changes to NHS availability of free fertility treatment for frozen embryo transfers.
answer_text: On 15 February, I announced that publicly funded IVF treatment will increase to one full cycle for eligible women. Until that point, those women eligible for publicly funded IVF treatment were entitled to receive one fresh and one frozen embryo transfer, even if they achieve a live birth with the first transfer. Under the recent change, any eligible woman who does not achieve a live birth with either her fresh or first frozen embryo transfers, and still has frozen embryos, will be entitled to further treatment until either she has a live birth or she has transferred all her frozen embryos. It is expected that this will benefit approximately 150 women each year.A woman will be eligible if she meets the published access criteria for IVF and has been placed on the waiting list for publicly funded IVF/ICSI treatment on or after 1 October 2022. Whilst I would support further expansion of the service, it will require recurrent funding assurances, which have not been provided at this time. In addition, the Regional Fertility Centre will need to increase its physical footprint and ensure the appropriate number of specialist staff are available to deliver any further increase in provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many people work in Health and Social Care services for In Vitro Fertilisation (IVF), in each of the last five years.
answer_text: As figures relating to the number of people working in Health and Social Care Services for In Vitro Fertilisation (IVF) prior to 2021 are not maintained, Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) figures from 2021 to 2024 are detailed below. 2021-2022 – 37.79 WTE2022-2023 – 37.79 WTE2023-2024 – 39.35 WTE

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many people access services for In Vitro Fertilisation (IVF) annually, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The information requested is not held centrally within the Department, so Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) were asked to provide figures. Please find overleaf table with number of IVF treatments which have taken place in each of the last five years.Number of patients availing of IVF1/ICSI2 and Frozen Embyro Transfer (FET) treatments in each of the last five yearsYearIVF1/ICSI2 patientsFET patients2018-196052172019-206711782020-21100862021-223521742022-23614185Source: Belfast HSCT1In Vitro Fertilisation (IVF) can be defined as a series of procedures utilized to assist women with fertility problems, genetic problems or to assist in the conception of a child. During these treatments, mature eggs are retrieved from ovaries to be fertilized by sperm. The fertilized egg is then transferred to a uterus. These treatments can involve eggs, sperm or embryos from an anonymous donor. This treatment can also involve a gestational carrier (a woman who has the fertilised egg implanted into her uterus).2Intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) can be defined as a specialised form of IVF treatment that is used primarily in severe cases of male-factor infertility. This treatment involves the injection of a single sperm cell into an egg.3All infertility treatments in Northern Ireland are carried out exclusively in the Regional Fertility Centre in Belfast HSCT.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will have discussions with the UK Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology, to outline how the £95 million funding package announced as part of the Dame Barbara Windsor Dementia Mission will benefit Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Dementia remains one of the greatest health care, social and economic challenges facing society and demographics indicate that this is likely to continue for the foreseeable future or until a cure for Dementia is found. However, there have been significant advances in our understanding of factors which contribute to prevention, together with improved diagnosis and treatment of Dementia. The Department accepts that more can and should be done and collectively we must continue to pursue improvements in the care and support provided for those with Dementia, and importantly, for those who care for them, including exploring funding avenues such as the Dame Barbara Windsor Dementia Mission (DBWDM).The DBWDM was launched by the UK Government in August 2022. It ringfenced an additional £95 million in funding to support a national dementia mission with the aim of boosting the number of clinical trials and innovative research projects. Going forward, my officials will seek further information from the DBWDM regarding this funding and its potential use in Northern Ireland to help with the treatment and research for dementia.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to lift the moratorium on funding public health awareness-related campaigns, including media advertisements.
answer_text: My Department took the decision to pause most health-related advertising spend in the 2023/24 financial year as a direct result of the significant pressures on the budget. No decision has been taken for next year but all the indications are that the budget will remain under severe pressure in 2024/5.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment he has made of the use of (i) lecanemab; and (ii) donanemab in the treatment of dementia for patients in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: At present, there are no UK marketing authorisations (licenses) in place for either lecanemab or donanemab. Granting a marketing authorisation is only the first stage in the medicines access pathway; following licensing, these treatments will be assessed for clinical and cost effectiveness by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). NICE is currently preparing to assess lecanemab and donanemab when marketing authorisations are granted, and once the NICE assessment has been made to recommend or not recommend the treatment my Department will carry out the endorsement process as set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/1113 available at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has to increase the participation of people living with dementia in clinical trials.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, clinical research studies are coordinated through the Northern Ireland Clinical Research Network (NICRN), which is funded by HSC Research and Development (HSC R&D) Division of the Public Health Agency, which provides trial delivery support for clinical research studies across a wide range of specialties, creating opportunities for people in NI to participate in research studies. Currently, there are a number of initiatives nationally to increase participation in dementia  research. The Clinical Leads of the Neurodegenerative Diseases Specialty Group of NICRN are members of:A UK-wide Brain Health Clinic Coalition funded by Alzheimer’s Research UK, a primary remit of which is to increase participation in research;  andThe National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR) Dementia-Translational Research Collaboration, which was recently awarded £50M to increase productivity in clinical trials.     In addition, Be Part of Research (Be Part Of Research (nihr.ac.uk) allows members of the public to register their interest to participate in research and is open to NI population. 725 NI citizens have registered to date.         Join Dementia Research is a collaboration between the NIHR, Alzheimer’s Research UK, Alzheimer’s Society and Alzheimer Scotland.  Anyone aged 18 years or over can sign up themselves on-line or on behalf of someone else at www.joindementiaresearch.nihr.ac.uk. To date, 69,012 people have registered their interest in research. There are currently 598 registered volunteers from Northern Ireland (79 with a specific diagnosis of dementia and 42 with a memory concern).          The NIHR Dementia Translational Research Collaboration (Dementia TRC) comprises of a group of research active dementia themes, within the national network of NIHR Biomedical Research Centres, along with Clinical Research Facilities and members of the devolved nations. https://www.nihr.ac.uk/explore-nihr/support/dementia.htmFurthermore, there are number of other workstreams being undertaken regionally, including:NI Dementia/Neurodegenerative clinics are working towards a  system whereby patients can be enrolled as ‘research ready’ and willing to participate.Bloods from all patients at memory clinics are biobanked, so that the utility of blood-based biomarkers can be assessed in future research studies.A lumbar puncture service has been established to facilitate increased patient enrolment in clinical trials in the absence of amyloid or treatment, as Positron Emission Tomography (PET) scans are not currently available in Northern Ireland.A Central Governance Facility, which will provide a streamlined Governance Approvals Service, has recently been established in Northern Ireland.  This significant change is expected to improve the timelines for research governance approvals, by providing a Study-wide Governance Review, on behalf of all NI Health and Social Care Trusts. In August 2022, the UK Government ringfenced an additional £95 millio

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many general practice nurses (GPNs) are employed in each of the Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) how many of these GPNs are not employed directly by Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: General Practice Nurses (GPNs) are normally employed directly by General Practitioners, who are independent contractors.Of the five regional Health & Social Care (HSC) Trusts, the Southern and Western HSC Trusts are currently managing 5 General Practices on a temporary basis. The number of GPNs employed by the Trusts for those practices is shown in the following table. General Practice Nurses employed by HSC Trusts at 31 December 2023HSC TrustGP PracticeHeadcountWhole-time EquivalentSouthern Kilkeel Medical Practice and Mullaghbawn Surgery41.57Western Dromore Surgery, Racecourse Medical Group and Brookeborough & Tempo Primary Care Services84.75Source: HSC TrustsInformation on the number of GPNs employed directly by General Practitioners is not held by the Department or HSC Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will bring forward a new maternity strategy.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the reply I gave to AQW 6063/22-27 on 22 February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when she will introduce legislation to address (i) cyberbullying; (ii) online harassment; and (iii) online intimidation.
answer_text: Telecommunications, including internet services, are reserved rather than devolved matters and therefore the introduction of legislation falls predominantly to the UK Government.  The Online Safety Act 2023 establishes a new regulatory regime to address illegal and harmful content online. It imposes legal requirements on providers of internet services.  Services within the scope of the Act will have to follow certain rules, including protecting users from illegal content and activity online, as well as protecting children from harmful content.In Northern Ireland there are a range of offences that can be used to tackle and prosecute offensive online behaviour including the Protection from Harassment (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, the Protection from Stalking Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, and the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act.  In light of the recent additions to the statute book, I have no plans to introduce any further legislation to address these harms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of what constitutes (i) cyberbullying; (ii) online harassment; and (iii) online intimidation.
answer_text: Online harms are widespread and can take many different forms and their description and definition often depends on the particular event or scenario.  The words to describe these harms are frequently used interchangeably.  It is also important to distinguish between what may constitute these behaviours and what would be deemed to involve a criminal offence; the two are frequently not the same.  Cyberbullying is generally understood to be bullying involving the use of digital technologies. It can take place on social media, messaging platforms, gaming platforms and mobile phones. It may include a range of behaviours aimed at scaring, humiliating or shaming those who are targeted.Online harassment and online intimidation, are similarly behaviours involving the use of digital technologies. With respect to the law, they are subject to the same legal tests and definitions as harassment and intimidation without the use of digital technologies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will undertake to quantify the totality of the Health and Social Care workforce, including those not directly employed by the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My Department publishes official statistics on the workforce directly employed  by Health & Social Care (HSC) organisations on a quarterly basis in the Northern Ireland Health and Social Care Workforce Statistics bulletin which can be found at the following link:  Northern Ireland health and social care (HSC) workforce statistics December 2023 | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk)In addition, official statistics on General Practitioners, General Dental Practitioners and General Ophthalmic Practitioners working within Family Practitioner Services are published annually by the Business Services Organisation at the following link:  FPS Information Unit - Business Services Organisation (BSO) Website (hscni.net)  The Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for our People, recognises the need to further develop the scope of workforce data available from across the entire health and social care system to support effective monitoring of workforce trends and issues.Against this objective, the Strategy’s second action plan contains an action to undertake an exercise to examine where current gaps in health and social care workforce data exist and seek ways in which these can be addressed.Work against this action to date has focussed on the areas of social work and social care where the need for more comprehensive workforce data for these professions has been identified.   My Department has commissioned the Northern Ireland Social Care Council who are currently progressing work to scope the feasibility of developing workforce datasets for these professions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how patients with (i) Spinal Muscular Atrophy; and (ii) Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy can access (a) Spinraza; and (b) Translarna.
answer_text: In August 2019, the Department endorsed National Institute for health and Care Excellence (NICE) Technical Appraisal (TA) 588 guidance which recommends nusinersen (Spinraza) as an option for treating 5q spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) only if people have pre-symptomatic SMA, or SMA types 1, 2 or 3 and the conditions in the managed access agreement are followed. This has been issued in line with guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 2/13, and can be accessed where infrastructure is in place and Health and Social Care (HSC) Service has capacity, interim commissioning of this drug is accepted on a cost-per-case basis.Under Highly Specialised Technologies guidance (HST) 22, NICE recommended the use of ataluren (Translara) as an option for treating Duchenne muscular dystrophy resulting from a nonsense mutation in the dystrophin gene in people 2 years and over who can walk, in accordance with a commercial arrangement with the marketing authorisation holder. This was accepted for use in HSC where infrastructure is in place and the Service has capacity, interim commissioning of this drug is accepted on a cost-per-case basis.I can advise that patients have been able to access both of these treatments in line with the NICE recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how patients with Spinal Muscular Atrophy will avail of Risdiplam after 31 March 2024; and (ii) how they will be informed of the arrangements for service provison.
answer_text: NICE TA755, when originally issued in December 2021, initially recommended the use of Risdiplam as an option for treating 5q spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) in people 2 months and older with a clinical diagnosis of SMA types 1, 2 or 3 or with pre-symptomatic SMA and 1 to 4 SMN2 copies. In accordance with DoH Circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 the Department of Health made provision for patients on this basis.In December 2023, NICE updated its recommendation to include people of all ages with a clinical diagnosis of SMA types 1, 2 or 3 or with pre-symptomatic SMA and 1 to 4 SMN2 copies to take account for the license extension to the marketing authorisation.In accordance with DoH Circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which sets out the process for endorsement, implementation, monitoring and assurance in Northern Ireland, the Department endorsed the updated NICE TA as applicable in Northern Ireland on 26 January 2023.In accordance with the requirements of the Circular, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the Department are now preparing a Service Notification for issue to the HSC Trusts and other relevant providers and stakeholders setting out the expectations for implementation within 15 weeks of DoH endorsement i.e. no later than 9 May 2024.In the interim period, the SPPG has arrangements in place to maximise access to patients through the Managed Entry of New Medicines Process (Managed Entry) which normally ensures that NICE approved treatments are made available on a cost per case basis once NICE has published a Final Draft Guidance (FDG).For NICE TA755, hospital clinicians, on behalf of their patients are currently able to make application to the SPPG for the use of Risdiplam for all ages who meet the necessary criteria via the Managed Entry process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy will a pay award to Further Education lecturers restore equity and fairness, by matching the salary of school teachers.
answer_text: While Further Education lecturers’ pay is a matter for the Further Education colleges as employers, I am committed to improving pay for Further Education lecturers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there will be a formal link for Further Education lecturers to receive the same pay award as school teachers in future pay awards.
answer_text: While Further Education lecturers’ pay is a matter for the Further Education colleges as employers in their own right, I do wish to improve the pay of Further Education lecturers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider extending the availability of Risdiplam to Health and Social Care Trusts outside Belfast.
answer_text: Risdiplam, for the treatment of spinal muscular atrophy (SMA), is available to all eligible patients in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what work his Department is doing to address the impact of badgers on the bee population.
answer_text: My officials are unaware of any evidence indicating that badgers are having an impact on native bee populations in Northern Ireland, consequently I can confirm that my Department is not undertaking any work in relation to this issue.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what work his Department is doing to combat Bovine Tuberculosis.
answer_text: Bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) is one of the most challenging issues faced by the cattle farmers in Northern Ireland.  Herd incidence currently stands at 10.05% and last year total spend on the control of bTB in cattle was £53million, with a similar amount expected this year.  The high cost of controlling this disease is not sustainable for either the public purse or for hard working farm businesses. 	To combat bTB, my Department delivers a bTB eradication programme in cattle in addition to progressing a long-term Strategy to reduce, and ultimately eradicate the disease in Northern Ireland.  As you may be aware, the wildlife intervention element of the Strategy was overturned by a judicial review decision in October 2023. 				Tackling this disease and all the factors which contribute to its spread and endurance will be a top priority for me in my new role.  I will be working closely with my veterinary and policy officials in the coming weeks on next steps for the bTB Eradication Strategy, in addition to engaging with stakeholders to examine the best ways to help lower the rate of bTB in our herds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
