question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when he became aware of the broken scanner at the Belfast City Hospital; (ii) to outline a timeframe for when the scanner will be repaired; and (iii) how many people will lose a cancer scan appointment as a result of this.
answer_text: (i) 	A fault was detected in the Belfast City Hospital (BCH) CT scanner, located in the Tower Block.  This was reported on Monday 12th February 2024.  It was reported to Engineers at 9.30am, same date.(ii) 	BCH CT Tower Block scanner fault was identified and parts were ordered and fitted by the Engineers on Wednesday 14th February 1pm.   Equipment quality assurance was completed successfully by engineers.  BCH CT staff completed equipment quality assurance and scanner was operational by 4pm Wednesday 14th February 2024.(iii)	The BHSCT confirmed that no patients on active treatment within the Belfast Trust were cancelled as a result of the downtime of the scanner.  A total of 24 routine surveillance patients impacted by downtime on 14th February have been reappointed between 19th February and 6 March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what work his Department is doing to limit the adverse impact of badgers on natural wildlife.
answer_text: My officials are unaware of any evidence that would indicate that badgers are having an adverse impact on natural wildlife in Northern Ireland. Badgers are omnivores, eating a wide range of plant and animal foods, with earthworms making up approx 70-80% of their diet. They have no natural predators in Ireland and are therefore an apex species at the top of the food chain. They play an important role in a complex relationship with many species further down the food chain and have evolved in the Irish ecosystem over thousands of years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how many designated sites are currently in place that require ammonia levels to be kept below a certain level; and (ii) how many designated sites there have been, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, there are 394 Areas of Special Scientific Interest (ASSIs), 58 Special Areas of Conservation (SACs) and 16 Special Protection Areas (SPAs). Of these, 293 ASSIs, 54 SACs and all SPAs are sensitive to atmospheric nitrogen deposition associated with ammonia emissions.		Critical levels are the ammonia concentrations in the atmosphere, above which direct damage to sensitive vegetation may occur, and are set to protect lichens and bryophytes (>1 µg m3) and higher (vascular) plants (>3 µg m3)		Critical loads define the rates of nitrogen deposition below which significant harmful effects are not expected to occur on sensitive habitats.		The proportion of N Ireland sensitive sites exceeding critical levels and loads:Sites exceeding critical levels of ammonia concentrations (Cle)Sites exceeding critical loads for N deposition (Clo)>1 µg m3*>3 µg m3**ASSI100%14.8%100%SAC100%14.8%100%SPA98.6%12.8%99.5%	*Lichens/bryophytes	**Higher, vascular plants	Source: Air Pollution Trends Report 2023: Critical load and critical level exceedances in the UK. Report to Defra		The number of sites declared in each of the last 10 years are listed below:	Year	2014	2015	2016	2017	2018	No. of Sites Declared	15	10	2	5	2	N.B. Between 2019-2023, no sites have been declared
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many Health and Social Care nurses are allocated to special educational needs schools, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: There are a variety of nursing services provided to pupils in special education needs school settings.  It is not possible to quantify the number of nurses allocated for most of these services, due to the nature of how nursing services are provided.Firstly, the HSC School nursing service provide an in-reach service to all primary, post-primary & special education needs schools in the delivery of the Healthy Child Healthy Future Universal programme.  This includes Primary 1 & Year 8 Health Appraisals (assessments). They also provide other targeted support to pupils as required.  HSC School Nurses and School immunisations teams attend all schools including special schools to administer the annual influenza vaccinations (special schools are prioritised in the flu programme), human papillomavirus (HPV) and school leaver vaccinations.  As this is an in-reach service, these HSC staff will be working across school types at different times during the year, therefore staff numbers by school type are not available. Secondly, HSC nurses are not commissioned by the Department for special educational schools. However, as the result of historical issues within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust there are five Band 5 Community Children’s Nurses and three Band 3 Health Care Assistants working across five special needs schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of special educational needs schools; (ii) how many special educational needs schools are on multiple sites; and (iii) the number of sites they are split across.
answer_text: Figures from the 2022/23 census exercise report that there were 39 special schools.  In addition to this, there were 102 primary and 48 post primary schools with pupils enrolled in Specialist Provision in Mainstream (SPiM) on census day 2022.There are nine special schools operating on multiple sites. Eight of these special schools have dual sites; one has three sites.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 5451/22-27, to detail the different contractual arrangements that post graduate doctors are under between Northern Ireland and Great Britain.
answer_text: In 2016 the UK Government was approached by the BMA to enter into negotiations on a new contract for junior doctors. England, Wales and Northern Ireland were negotiating parties, whilst Scotland was an observer.  A Framework Agreement was reached between the parties, however the BMA membership voted against the contract, which was later imposed only in England.The key changes included shortened pay scales, with incremental pay no longer available. The basic salary was enhanced with additional payments available for out of hours work, night work and weekend work, based on the frequency of the work.  Trainees in Northern Ireland remain under the 2002 contract, where they receive basic pay plus an additional percentage of that as a supplementary allowance. The supplement (or banding) relates to how onerous the individual’s rota is and ranges from an additional 20% for the least onerous roles to an additional 100% for the busiest roles.  They are entitled to incremental progression through the pay scale.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what level of funding will be released to Translink for the installation of new bus shelters; and (ii) when this funding will be released.
answer_text: With budgets not currently set for the next financial year, it is not possible to plan for the installation of new bus shelters at this time. However, I do recognise the benefit that they can add to our citizens and, in conjunction with Translink, I will keep the situation under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what funding will be made available to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council in advance of this year’s Irish Open; and (ii) what funding was made available to Causeway Coast & Glens Borough Council in relation to hosting the Open Championship in 2019.
answer_text: I can confirm that officials in my Department’s Regional Development Office recently engaged with Newry, Mourne & Down District Council regarding potential funding for a project to revitalise Castle Park in Newcastle, in order to contribute to the regeneration of the area.  Funding will be subject to a positive economic appraisal and budget being available.While there are no current funding opportunities in my Department or Sport NI to support legacy activities for sporting events, such as the Irish Open and The Open, I am happy for officials to engage with Councils to explore how local communities can benefit from the legacy of such events.  As yet, my Department has not received a request to meet with NMDDC to hear of their plans in advance of the Irish Open.I can advise that officials from my Department and Sport NI have met with Golf Ireland, the governing body of sport for golf - formerly Golf Union of Ireland, to hear of their plans to develop golf as part of the legacy of hosting the Irish Open this year in Royal County Down, Newcastle and also for The Open returning to Royal Portrush in 2025.In respect of funding provided for hosting The 148th Open in 2019, I can confirm that my Department provided funding to Causeway Coast & Glens Borough Council to run legacy programmes to promote participation, inclusion and community engagement:£35,000 to deliver The Open Legacy Primary Schools Programme; and £6,750 for an Inclusive Golf Programme.In addition to the funding provided for golfing activities, the NI Executive, in 2017, approved a programme of regeneration works for Portrush in advance of the tournament.  My Department with approval from the NI Executive invested a total of £14.7M in the programme overall, of which £8.75m funding was made available to Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the development of the revised Whistleblowing HSC Framework and Model Policy; (ii) for an update on the timescales for implementation; and (iii) to outline the associated cost with the implementation of this policy.
answer_text: The refreshed Raising Concerns in the Public Interest Framework and Model Policy is being finalised with the intention to publish by the end of March.Each organisation will be responsible for implementing a policy on the basis of the Framework, and will be making preparations in readiness for the launch. Any costs in relation to the implementation of the policy will be determined by the organisations locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will reinstate the Core Grant Funding Scheme for the Voluntary and Community Sector.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 5744/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the Duty of Candour and Being Open Framework; (ii) when it is expected to be completed; and (iii) how the framework will be implemented.
answer_text: My officials are currently developing proposals for a regional Being Open Framework with plans for further consultation in the months ahead. I anticipate the framework will be in place in late 2024. Part of the work will focus on development of an implementation strategy and on measurement and evaluation of outcomes. This is a key contributing piece of work to help further support an open, just and learning culture in our HSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will amend the Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance to allow businesses and charities to apply for funding in the immediate aftermath of flooding events.
answer_text: The Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance was introduced by the Executive in 2007. Its purpose is to provide immediate financial support to households who have suffered from flooding. The Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance is not a compensation scheme and does not purport to be one; nor is it intended to replace or displace the assistance that households should receive from their insurers. For example, the £1,000 payment is not taken into account by insurance companies when calculating payment of insurance compensation to households who have suffered from flooding.As Minister, I have responsibility for activating the Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance and making emergency funds available to cover council costs incurred when responding to the needs of householders across Northern Ireland in the event of any flooding following rainfall or tidal surge.Any additional support for businesses or charities in the event of flooding would require Executive agreement and the allocation of additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the National Guardian’s Office in England, whether he would consider establishing an arm's-length body to oversee the new Freedom to Speak Up roles.
answer_text: A regional HSC Raising Concerns Framework is currently being readied for issue by my Department. As part of this, HSC Trusts will put in place trained Raising Concerns Advocates who will provide a range of services to support those wishing to raise a concerns in the public interest.  Further, my officials are currently developing proposals for a regional Being Open Framework with plans for further consultation in the months ahead. Implementation and evaluation of these policy frameworks will inform consideration of any further measures that may be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will extend the support for those businesses in Downpatrick and Newry who, whilst not flooded, lost significant earnings as a result of the flooding and will be left with higher insurance costs.
answer_text: On 21st February I announced details of the support package for businesses affected by recent flooding.In addition to the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme, which will provide further support up to £100,000 to businesses that were directly flooded following the severe weather in October and November, I am also introducing two Hardship schemes to provide support for businesses and some other non-domestic premises that were impacted by the flooding but were not eligible for the previous £7,500 payment or the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme.As with the previous Flood Scheme these new schemes will be administered by the local councils. The councils plan to open the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme for applications early next week with the two Hardship schemes in the following couple of weeks. Further details on scheme administration, including the eligibility criteria and how to apply, will be provided in the forthcoming week.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what financial support will be offered to the Downpatrick and County Down Railway in the wake of recent flooding.
answer_text: Under the terms of the existing Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance my Department is not able to offer financial support to the Downpatrick and County Down Railway. The purpose of the existing Scheme is to provide immediate financial support to households who have suffered from flooding – this does not extend to include local businesses or charities.Any future Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance to include businesses or charities would require a paper to be tabled and agreed at the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will ban the practice of private companies clamping private vehicles on private land.
answer_text: As answered in AQW 18696/17-22, my Department previously carried out a public consultation which focused exclusively on identifying the best option for the future regulation of vehicle immobilisation on private land in Northern Ireland.  The results of the consultation did not provide sufficient evidence to suggest a need to ban vehicle immobilisation on private land in Northern Ireland. Given the lack of evidence, the significant pressures facing my Department, and the reduced mandate which we have to progress legislative and policy change, I do not believe there is sufficient justification to reopen this matter and commence a further consultation at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will institute a government-backed scheme of indemnity, with an interim reimbursement scheme, for NHS practising GPs.
answer_text: I am aware that arrangements for clinical negligence indemnity are a matter of concern for GPs locally and I am committed to identifying solutions to address this issue for Northern Ireland GPs.  Recognising this, my Department recently provided funding on a non-recurrent basis to support GPs with indemnity costs as part of a wider package to support General Practice this winter by boosting capacity.  My Department is continuing to take forward work to look at the future model of GP indemnity provision in Northern Ireland. A number of possible options have been identified as part of this work, ranging from the current model to state intervention that includes a number of state backed model options. Departmental officials are continuing to take forward options appraisal through the business case process which will conclude with the identification of a proposed preferred way forward for indemnity arrangements in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when Phase 2 of the support to those businesses impacted by recent flooding in Downpatrick and Newry will commence.
answer_text: On 21 February, I announced details of the support package for businesses affected by recent flooding. I am pleased to confirm that the two hardship schemes have opened for applications on 13 March.  As with the previous Flood Scheme these new schemes will be administered by the local councils.   Applications for a hardship payment can be made via the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will amend the Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance to include businesses.
answer_text: The Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance was introduced by the Executive in 2007. Its purpose is to provide immediate financial support to households who have suffered from flooding. It is not a compensation scheme and does not purport to be one; nor is it intended to replace or displace the assistance that households should receive from their insurers.	On 10 March 2016, the Minister of Agriculture and Rural Development obtained Executive agreement for an exceptional one-off extension to the Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance. This enabled flood assistance to be provided to non-domestic properties and small businesses including farm businesses impacted by flooding incidents following the heavy rainfall between 7 November 2015 and 31 January 2016. It also included recreational and community buildings as well as places of worship. Its remit was never extended and therefore it was not applicable outside of the specific timeframe of 7 November 2015 to 31 January 2016.		Any future Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance for small businesses and non-domestic properties would require a paper to be tabled and agreed at the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will provide funding to the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice to ensure they are able to continue their essential work.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the media statement that I issued on Friday in relation to this issue.  I recognise and applaud the vital work of the NICH and all hospices in Northern Ireland.As requested by me, DoH officials have engaged in detailed discussions last week with NI Hospice.It is clear to me that the financial pressures facing NICH go much deeper than any reduction of Departmental support.  This reduction involved the ending last summer of an additional but temporary annual payment of £170k. This decision was notified to the NICH in June 2023 and took effect from September 2023 which resulted in £85K reduction in this financial year.  I took the decision on Friday 09/02/2022 to reinstate that funding in full for this year.This payment is in addition to the core annual funding of £1.6m which my Department is providing directly to NICH this year. This core funding included a £420,000 component which had not been recurrent, meaning it would have to be subject to confirmation each year. I can confirm that, going forward, this will be made recurrent, giving the Hospice greater certainty.I am keenly aware that the financial challenges facing NICH go much deeper than this or next year. Like all health care providers, it has encountered significantly rising running costs, reflecting inflationary pressures.Regrettably, the NICH has advised my Department that the restoration of the full £170k for this year will not lead to the reversal of the service reduction made public this week.The NICH and NI Hospice parent body will need to develop longer-term solutions, to which I am sure Government will want to be supportive.Given the current absence of a budget for next year, and the well-documented financial uncertainties and challenges for Government, my Department is at the limit of what it can do right now.I am also very conscious that other hospice organisations, and many other health and care providers, are also grappling with growing budget pressures and will be seeking further statutory finance.I am grateful for the support and personal interest of the First Minister and deputy First Minister regarding NICH’s position. I am also aware of the fact that other NI Departments have provided financial support to NICH in the past.I believe the long-term need for financial stability across the hospice sector would benefit from cross-Departmental input, helping to assist organisations in identifying sustainable ways forward.Consequently, I wrote on Friday 09/02/2002 to the Finance Minister to seek further discussions between the charities and our two Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he will take to reduce waiting lists.
answer_text: Tackling our lengthy waiting times is one of my priorities and is indeed an Executive priority.  It is, however, undoubtedly a long-term issue that requires sustainable and recurrent funding, workforce development and system wide transformation.  The Elective Care Framework published in 2021 sets out a detailed plan for addressing hospital waiting lists. Much good work is underway to tackle the backlog of patients waiting, for example through the development of elective care centres, rapid diagnosis centres and mega clinics.  In addition, my Department is working with Trusts to increase productivity and efficiency through the use of a wide range of best practice service improvements.  As a result the in-patient and day case treatment waiting list has been reduced for the five quarters in a row. In total 11,612 less people were waiting for in-patient and day case treatment in September 2023 compared to June 2022. That represents a 9.1% reduction of the treatment waiting list.The Framework is, however, clear that, in addition to the multi-year funding needed to tackle the backlog of patients waiting, sustained recurrent investment is required to address the central issue of demand outstripping the current capacity. I am working with my Executive colleagues to secure the funding needed for time critical and cancer patients, to tackle our lengthy waiting lists and to continue the transformation of the system on a sustainable basis in line with the Elective Care Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Primary Care sector and the pressures it faces; and (ii) what priority and immediate work will he take to address these pressures.
answer_text: I am fully committed to ensuring that patients will be able to access high quality, sustainable primary care services now and in the future.  I also recognise the continued work and commitment of GPs and their practice teams in caring for patients in the face of increasingly challenging circumstances.  Stabilisation of GP practices is a key concern and one which the Department is pro-actively taking steps to address. There has been significant work undertaken in recent months to support continuity of GMS provision in the current challenging climate.  Over the past two years, 20 contracts have been returned – 13 in 2022/23 and 7 in 2023/24. Despite this high level of contract hand-back, through working with key stakeholders including HSC Trusts and GP Federations it has been possible to ensure that no practice has closed and that patients can continue to access GP services in their areas.In addition, my Department has provided significant financial support to help stabilise primary care services. In October 2023, as part of my Department’s Winter Pressures Plan, £3.4million in funding was provided to General Medical Services and Out of Hours Services.  A further £4.3m was made available to assist GP practices to deliver the Northern Ireland Local Enhanced Service - Proactive General Practice Care for Nursing and Residential Homes. In November, the Department agreed to reprioritise £2.7m from the existing GMS budget to fund a package to support General Practice this winter by boosting capacity.  In the longer term, sustainable development of primary care services will require development of the primary care workforce. My Department has been pursuing this agenda through increasing the number of GP training places to an all-time high of 121 places over the past two years. At the same, the Department is expanding the Primary Care workforce through initiatives such as the Practice Based Pharmacy programme and Primary Care MDT, which together represent recurrent investment of £44m in the Primary Care workforce. Further implementation of the Primary Care MDT model across all parts of Northern Ireland is a key priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he expects to update the Assembly on health service transformation and sustainability.
answer_text: Transformation of our Health Service to become more efficient and deliver a better service to all our citizens, young and old, is a key priority for my Department.  The Transformation Agenda cuts across all areas of my Department and officials have continued to progress this important work over the past couple of years despite the lack of an Executive and extreme funding constraints.  It is my intention to update the Assembly at the earliest opportunity on progress within the various strands.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work he will undertake to (i) ensure pay parity with junior doctors in Britain; and (ii) improve the working conditions for junior doctors.
answer_text: Post Graduate Doctors and Dentists in Training [previously referred to as junior doctors] in the HSC are not on the same contractual arrangements as their medical and dental counterparts in Great Britain, they therefore possess different pay arrangements.I have asked officials from my Department’s Workforce Policy Directorate to engage with the British Medical Association to discuss matters relating to the concerns of the medical and dental workforce within the HSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) what work will be undertaken to address the issue of pay parity for nurses; (ii) whether such work will include addressing safer working practices; and (iii) what timescales will be associated with resolving these matters.
answer_text: As I have stated publicly, my immediate priority is to secure our health staff a long overdue pay increase for the current year. The first discussion between regional Agenda for Change Trades Unions negotiating leads, officials and HSC employers was held on 8 February 2024. A follow up meeting will take place on 14 February 2024. A commitment to consider safe staffing legislation for Nursing & Midwifery was made as part of the January 2020 Framework Agreement. The scope was expanded in October 2022 to include all professions within the Health & Social Care Trusts. Please be assured that a pay award for all HSC staff is an urgent priority for my department and work is ongoing in conjunction with trades unions to agree and implement pay awards as soon as possible.I intend to launch a public consultation later this year to seek views on the legislative proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people have presented to Emergency Departments and have departed before being seen by medical staff, in each of the last five years, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: Information on the number of people who present at emergency departments (EDs) and depart before being seen by medical staff is not available. This information is published on an annual basis on page 61 of the ‘Northern Ireland Hospital Statistics: Emergency Care’ report, which can be viewed at the link below:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/hs-emergency-care-21-22.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he will bring forward a new 10-year Tobacco Control strategy; (ii) to detail what work has been completed; and (iii) what work is being taken forward to design such a strategy.
answer_text: It is my intention to bring forward a successor to the current Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland. The current strategy has been extended to February 2024. This extension allows time to complete the end review of the current strategy. The end review process will seek to identify both achievements and remaining challenges, which will be important in informing future strategic direction in relation to tobacco control. The Department will work closely with stakeholders in the design, and in the identification of priorities, of the successor strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to recommendations which relate to his Department, (i) to detail the progress his Department has made on implementing the hyponatraemia inquiry report recommendations; (ii) which recommendations have not been implemented; (iii) why these recommendations have not been implemented; and (iv) to provide associated timescales and costs for the implementation of the recommendations.
answer_text: Progress has been made by my Department to implement the recommendations set out in the report of the Inquiry into Hyponatraemia-related Deaths. However, I wish to offer an update to the families first and thereafter I will issue a public statement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what work (i) has been completed; and (ii) is being taken forward, to review the 10-year Tobacco Control Strategy and its efficacy.
answer_text: Departmental officials are currently working in conjunction with the Public Health Agency (PHA) and the Institute of Public Health in Ireland (IPH) to review the Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland. The IPH facilitated a stakeholder event in late August 2022 which captured views on the current strategy and established initial thoughts on our future strategic approach. Officials are also in the process of finalising additional evidence requirements with the IPH and documenting strategy outcomes in conjunction with the PHA.Given the proximity to the comprehensive mid-term review of the Strategy in 2020, we will also refer to that report in our assessment of the efficacy of the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the reasons why smoking remains among the five largest inequality gaps for the majority of Trusts and Local Government Districts; and (ii) how is his Department addressing this.
answer_text: Whilst the Health Inequalities Annual Report 2022 does not classify smoking as one of the largest health inequality gaps, smoking rates are significantly higher in the most deprived areas compared to the least deprived areas. The impacts are demonstrated by the health inequality gaps relating specifically to smoking related deaths (in the most deprived areas these were 131% higher than in the least deprived areas in 2016-20, and 57% more than the NI average). Across the five Trusts, Belfast showed the largest inequality gap in smoking related deaths where the rate in the most deprived areas was 65% higher than the Trust average, however death rates have decreased over the last five years within the Trust and its most deprived areas. Lisburn & Castlereagh had the largest inequality gap in smoking related deaths among the 11 LGDs where the rate in the most deprived areas was 68% higher than the LGD average, however similar to the Belfast Trust, the smoking related death rate has decreased over the last five years within the LGD and its most deprived areas.’Over the last 10 years, those living in the most deprived areas have consistently been between two and three times as likely to smoke as those living in the least deprived areas. However, it is important to recognise that smoking prevalence has fallen from 40% to 27% in the most deprived quintile over the lifetime of the current Tobacco Control Strategy. Numerous academic studies across the UK have concluded that smoking behaviours are often influenced by societal factors such as occupation, and parental smoking status. There is also a correlation with poor mental health. Tobacco use is generally accepted as a key driver of poor health outcomes and of health inequalities. Disadvantaged people who smoke are identified as a key priority group in the Department’s Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland and actions in the accompanying implementation plan are aimed at reducing smoking prevalence amongst this group. The high smoking prevalence rates in the most deprived areas, (particularly in pregnant women) and amongst those with mental health conditions, has been further highlighted in the recent mid-term review of the strategy and further actions to address these disparities will be required in the successor strategy.Many smoking cessation services are available within or near socially and economically disadvantaged communities. The Public Health Agency (PHA) works with Healthy Living Centres to ensure Stop Smoking services are delivered in a community setting at times and locations which suit local people. In time, such initiatives will hopefully lead to more smokers quitting in deprived areas, and consequently, fewer numbers of children taking up the habit.  The PHA have also engaged with community and voluntary partners specifically regarding tailored promotion of services in these areas. In implementing the Tobacco Control Strategy, the Public Health Agency commission anti-smoking pro

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the availability of NHS dental places given recent research conducted by the BBC highlighting that 90% of practices are not taking new adult patients and 88% are not taking on new child patients.
answer_text: Access to routine dental care continues to be significantly affected by the COVID-19 pandemic, which has caused reduced activity and therefore reduced capacity in dental practices. In order to increase activity in General Dental Services, I introduced the Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS), which rewards dentists based on treatments being provided.  The RSS has seen an increase in activity levels of around 16.5% over the first 5 months of 2022/23.Any General Dental Practitioner signed up to the RSS is required to treat patients based on clinical need, regardless of their registration status. This is to ensure that those patients most in need are able to obtain a dental appointment.In addition, any patient who has an urgent or emergency dental need, including those on pension credit or those entitled to free dental treatment, is able to make use of an Emergency Dental Clinic during weekends or Public Holidays. There the patient will be triaged remotely, and appropriate advice given or treatment arranged.To further help those in most need, on 3 October 2022, my Department commenced a scheme throughout Northern Ireland to provide access to unregistered patients who are experiencing an emergency or urgent dental condition.Support being provided to dental practices will remain under ongoing review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact of the reduction from 25 to 10 per cent in the uplift on dental fees paid for health service treatments to help dentists recover from the impact of Covid.
answer_text: The General Dental Services Rebuilding Support Scheme was introduced in April 2022 to support dental practitioners with the impact of the pandemic. With the impact of the pandemic significantly reducing over the past few months and most restrictions now removed, the justification for ongoing support has also reduced. However, it is acknowledged that the impacts have not entirely receded with inefficiencies created by increased “Did Not Attends” and associated staff absences. The impact of these inefficiencies is assessed to be a 10% reduction in the GDS capacity. It is for this reason that I committed to continuing support to the end of the financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what plans are in place to recruit permanent staff to the Strangford Ferry service; (ii) what action is being taken to avoid future cancellations of ferry crossings due to a lack of staff; and (iii) whether the impact of ferry crossing cancellations, on both people and businesses, has been assessed.
answer_text: The Department is currently progressing Senior Pursers and a Senior Engineer recruitment competitions.The current staffing and contingency arrangements are usually sufficient to keep the Ferry Service running, however if a member of staff is unexpectedly unavailable we try to bring in off-duty staff on voluntary overtime, or they may be asked to change shifts.  In addition, there are other staff who have been trained to provide cover, and agency staff with the relevant qualifications are being utilised. To further increase the resilience of the Ferry Service a number of staff are currently receiving training to enable them to carry out additional roles.My officials are very aware of the impact of ferry cancellations on people and businesses, and they work very hard to keep the service operational. You will be interested to know that the evidence shows that the Ferry Service is very reliable with over 99% of all scheduled sailings completed in the period January to August 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many nursing students applied for a (i) means tested bursary; and (ii) non-means tested bursary, in each of the last ten years, broken down by year and stream.
answer_text: The total number of nursing and midwifery students who applied for a means tested bursary, for each of the last ten years, is set out in the table below: YearNumber of applications for means tested bursaries2012/132472013/142432014/152472015/162442016/172652017/182632018/192752019/202682020/212802021/22291A breakdown by Nursing and Midwifery field of practice is not available. All Nursing and Midwifery students on a pre-registration training place, commissioned by my Department from QUB and UU, automatically receive a non-means tested bursary on commencement of their programme. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many computers have been removed from schools by C2K as part of the completion of their contract, broken down by school; and (ii) what is the expected impact of this reduction in school IT capacity.
answer_text: (i)  I refer the Member to the reply I gave to AQW 3303/22-27, and can advise that C2k has not removed any devices from schools.  C2k is working alongside each individual school to ascertain the impact of dropping devices from the network due to the fact that they are end of life and a security vulnerability in terms of potential data loss.  The decision as to which machines need to be removed will be negotiated with the school at individual school level.(ii) The removal of these additional devices will have no impact on the core number of devices schools have under the managed service. Furthermore, C2k will be supporting the development of an improved device to pupil ratio under the Education Information Solutions (EdIS) Programme, which will replace Education Network NI (EN(ni)) over the coming year.  This will ensure that the impact of the removal of these additional devices is reduced.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to advise (i) on the current position of the bursary for student nurses; (ii) whether this bursary has increased with inflation; and (iii) when was the last time the bursary increased with inflation.
answer_text: (i) My Department currently pays the tuition fees, and provides a non means-tested bursary of £5, 165 per annum, to nursing students on a commissioned pre-registration training place at our local universities. Students may also be eligible for a range of means-tested allowances, depending on their individual circumstances. Rates are set out in the Bursary Booklet provided to all students before commencing pre-registration programmes. Payment of these allowances is made by the BSO’s Bursary Administration Unit.(ii) and (iii) I can confirm that the last increase in the non means-tested bursary rate was in the 2012/13 academic year. However, I announced on 27 September 2022 changes to the rates of specific means-tested allowances available to pre-registration nursing and midwifery students to help to alleviate the impact of the current cost of living pressures. A 15% increase will be applied to the dependants allowance for spouse or first child; the allowance for subsequent children; and parents learning allowance. In addition, the mileage allowance for students on clinical placement will be increased by 5% to bring it into line with the current NICS public transport rate of 25.7p per mile. These increases apply from 1 September 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients from Northern Ireland who have travelled to other countries for gastric band surgery in the last five years have subsequently died as a result of their surgery.
answer_text: The information requested is not held centrally within the Department. As a result, all five Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) were asked for input. Northern, South Eastern and Belfast HSCTs have responded that they do not hold this information. The other two HSCTs were unable to provide a response in the timeframe given.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people, who have went to other countries for gastric band surgery in the last five years, have subsequently presented in primary or secondary care for urgent post-operative care with complications.
answer_text: The information requested is not held centrally within the Department. Consequently, all five of the Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) were asked for input. Belfast HSCT confirmed that the Oesophagogastric Service have treated 9 people for gastric band complications post-surgery but this information has only been collated in the past few years. Unfortunately, Northern and South Eastern HSCTs responded that they do not hold this information. The remaining HSCTs were unable to provide a response in the timeframe given.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people resident in Northern Ireland have travelled to other countries for gastric band surgery in the last five years.
answer_text: The information requested is not held centrally within the Department. Consequently, all five of the Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) were asked for input. Unfortunately, Northern, South Eastern and Belfast HSCTs responded that they also do not have this information, while the remaining HSCTs were unable to provide a response in the timeframe given.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will launch a campaign to make people aware of the dangers and possible complications arising from having gastric band surgery in other countries.
answer_text: My Department does not currently have any plans to launch a campaign on this matter, however, this will be kept under review.  Every individual has the right to choose to travel abroad for private surgery if they wish to do so; this is not something that my Department can or should try to prevent.  Those who choose to travel for surgery or other treatment should ensure they fully understand the risks associated with this, as they would when accessing care privately in Northern Ireland.  If an individual does experience complications on their return from surgery abroad, they may access HSC services for emergency care if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many schools will lose computers due to upgrades that cannot be completed before 30 September 2022; (ii) how many computers will be lost; (iii) whether schools have been advised to dispose of these computers at their own cost; (iv) why less than one months' notice was given to schools; and (v) how many of these computers her Department will replace.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s C2k team has communicated with schools throughout the life of the C2k Project. In 2017, they advised all schools that old devices would continue to be supported on the service at no cost to the school, until the end of March 2019. In 2019, schools were then advised that old devices running on the Windows 10 operating system would no longer be supported, although C2k covered the cost of these audio visual licences until 31 March 2021. At that time, schools were informed that they would be responsible for dealing with any subsequent hardware issues going forward and were provided with relevant guidance.Some schools chose to hold on to unsupported equipment (some 8 or 9 years old), which obviously presents a number of risks, including the potential for increased system vulnerabilities and Cyber Security threats. The number of devices in question varies depending on the individual school, as does the overall impact on the individual school. Some schools have disposed of a significant number of outdated and incompatible devices, while adequate planning for others has largely mitigated against any significant disruption.C2K has offered schools the opportunity to dispose of devices during the last refresh and they continue to work with individual schools to dispose of equipment at no cost to the schools. It should be noted that these devices are in addition to the core allocation of managed devices for these schools, and the Department has committed funding through the EdIS Programme to replace the core devices when the refresh is due.C2K is contacting all schools impacted by this to help them in resolving any outstanding issues. They have committed to meeting with and working with the individual schools concerned to develop plans to support them in successfully transitioning away from the old devices. These plans will be developed on a school by school basis to more effectively address each school's particular contextual needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what the current position of the bursary is for student nurses; (ii) whether this bursary has increased with inflation; and (iii) when was the last time the bursary increased with inflation.
answer_text: (i) My Department currently pays the tuition fees, and provides a non means-tested bursary of £5, 165 per annum, to nursing students on a commissioned pre-registration training place at our local universities. Students may also be eligible for a range of means-tested allowances, depending on their individual circumstances. Rates are set out in the Bursary Booklet provided to all students before commencing pre-registration programmes. Payment of these allowances is made by the BSO’s Bursary Administration Unit.(ii) and (iii) I can confirm that the last increase in the non means-tested bursary rate was in the 2012/13 academic year. However, I announced on 27 September 2022 changes to the rates of specific means-tested allowances available to pre-registration nursing and midwifery students to help to alleviate the impact of the current cost of living pressures. A 15% increase will be applied to the dependants allowance for spouse or first child; the allowance for subsequent children; and parents learning allowance. In addition, the mileage allowance for students on clinical placement will be increased by 5% to bring it into line with the current NICS public transport rate of 25.7p per mile. These increases apply from 1 September 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how much money allocated to Healthy Happy Minds was (a) returned; and (b) unspent; and (ii) to outline the reasons why the money was returned.
answer_text: In relation to funding allocated to the Healthy Happy Minds pilot, I can confirm the following:1 November 2021- 31 March 2022Allocation - £5mExpenditure Reported to DE - £2.894mUnderspend Reported to DE - £2.106m1 April 2022 - 30 June - 2022Allocation - £1.820mExpenditure confirmed to date - £1.072mFinal expenditure detail is not yet available1 September 2022 – 31 December 2022Allocation - £2.250m The pilot was intended to begin in September 2021 however a delay resulted in it launching in November 2021. Schools were initially asked to employ directly, suitably qualified and experienced counsellors and therapists but in order to comply with Public Contract Regulations and NI Public Procurement Policy the Education Authority, within a limited timescale, completed a tendering process for a procurement framework of providers. As part of this process potential service providers and professional bodies were made aware of the tender opportunity when it was advertised. However, only a limited number of counselling/therapy providers were successful which reduced the availability of counsellors and therapists that could be engaged by schools for the pilot. The delay in beginning the pilot and having the procurement framework in place with limited numbers of providers both therefore impacted on schools’ use of the funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how funding was allocated for Healthy Happy Minds; and (ii) whether an assessment has been carried out on the (a) need within local areas; and (b) availability of providers in local areas.
answer_text: Funding for the Healthy Happy Minds Therapeutic and Counselling Service Pilot was allocated based on pupil enrolment figures for each primary setting;(a) and (b) The Healthy Happy Minds Pilot was developed by my Department, in conjunction with a number of professional bodies, to provide an opportunity for a broad range of therapeutic interventions to be tested at primary school level.  The pilot is subject to an evaluation during the autumn term 2022 to provide a clear evidence base to determine the appropriateness of a counselling and therapeutic service for primary school pupils on a permanent basis going forward, subject to securing the necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what is the budget for the Engage programme in 2022-23.
answer_text: I issued a Direction to officials to fund the Engage III programme from September 2022 until the end of December 2022 with a budget of £10m in the first instance.  This will enable the programme to be delivered at the same level for settings as the Engage II programme.I intend that the programme will continue during the remainder of the 2022/23 academic year subject to further funding being available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of the long-term sustainable benefits to teaching mindfulness and breathwork, in reducing mental health issues later in life.
answer_text: Unfortunately my Department cannot comment on the request for an assessment of the long-term sustainable benefits to teaching mindfulness and breathwork in reducing mental health issues in later life. This requires research and an evidence base which is not part of my Department’s policy role or the Education Authority’s operational role.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she intends to review the Healthy Happy Minds programme to include provision for mindfulness.
answer_text: The Healthy Happy Minds Therapeutic and Counselling Pilot is subject to an evaluation during autumn 2022 which has been commissioned by my Department and will help to inform future provision subject to availability of the necessary budget. This pilot is for counselling and therapeutic interventions for primary aged pupils who require targeted therapeutic support. Mindfulness rather than a counselling or therapeutic intervention would be considered a useful coping strategy for any pupil and therefore would be a universal approach rather than a targeted therapeutic intervention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what recommendations the Department has implemented from the Independent Neurology Inquiry report since its publication; (ii) what recommendations it will implement in the next three months; (iii) what recommendations it will implement within the next year; and (iv) what recommendations the Minister is prioritising.
answer_text: I refer to my response to AQW 2693/22-27. The Department continues to work to ensure the Report of the Independent Neurology Inquiry (INI) receives the careful consideration it deserves, alongside putting in place the appropriate governance arrangements and workstreams to deliver implementation of the INI recommendations.  I intend to provide more detail on these arrangements in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many people have died as a result of oral health issues in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Oral health issues may encompass a range of conditions represented by a range of International Statistical Classification of Diseases codes (ICD-10), including cancers, infections, oral cavity conditions and other complications.NISRA provides a detailed breakdown of ICD-10 causes of death in Table 6.4 of the Registrar General Annual Report on the NISRA website.https://www.nisra.gov.uk/system/files/statistics/Section%206%20-%20Cause_Death_Tables_2020_Revised%20June%202022.xlsxIn absence of a definition for 'oral health issues', the ICD-10 Codes which are likely to be of primary interest are 'diseases of oral cavity, salivary glands and jaws' (ICD-10 Codes C00-C14), malignant neoplasms of lip, oral cavity and pharynx (ICD-10 Codes K00-K14) and benign neoplasms of the mouth, pharynx and major salivary glands (ICD-10 Codes D10-D11).The table below gives the number of deaths from these conditions over the last 5 years.ICD-10 CodesCause of Death Description20162017201820192020TotalC00-C14Malignant neoplasm of lip, oral cavity and pharynx9094104103108499D10-D11Benign neoplasm of mouth, pharynx and major salivary glands--11-2K00-K14Diseases of oral cavity, salivary glands and jaws11--24Total9195105104110505Table 1: Number of Deaths Registered in Northern Ireland from Oral-Related Causes of Death, 2016 - 2020

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many people have died as a result of road accidents in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office, which includes cause of death. The information requested is provided in the table below.  Currently, only up to 2020 is available via the Annual Report of the Registrar General. However, the report for 2021 will be published on Wednesday 21 September 2022 and the equivalent data will be available from the cause of death tables (see table 6.10 for a transport accidents summary) at the following link:https://www.nisra.gov.uk/statistics/births-deaths-and-marriages/registrar-general-annual-report Table 1:  Deaths from transport accidents, by registration1 year, 2016-2020Name20162017201820192020All transport accidents (inc non land transport) (ICD 10: V01-V99)6480705235Land transport accidents involving injuries to a pedestrian596114Land transport accidents involving injuries to a pedal cyclist00110Land transport accidents involving injuries to a motor cyclist or (pillion) passenger84742Land transport accidents involving injuries to occupants to a motor vehicle1617212414Land transport accidents involving injuries to other persons20110Non land transport accidents3350341115Mortality statistics are published as the number of deaths registered within a calendar year, as opposed to the number of deaths which that actually occurred within that period.  There can sometimes be a lag between the date of death and the date the death is registered e.g. death is referred to The Coroner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how many victims of the Infected Blood scandal are to receive interim compensation payments; (ii) when the Infected Blood interim compensation payments will be made to victims; (iii) how much money has been made available for this from his Department; and (iv) what lessons the Department has taken from this scandal.
answer_text: There are 103 beneficiaries (infected and bereaved spouses/partners) registered with the Infected Blood Payment Scheme (NI) who will receive the interim compensation payment. The payments are being funded by HM Treasury and the initial allocation required for Northern Ireland is £10.3million. My Departmental officials are working closely with colleagues in Westminster to ensure the payments can be made as soon as possible, aiming for completion by the end of October 2022. The use of contaminated blood and blood products in the 1970s and 1980s was a tragic episode in the history of the NHS. Many people have suffered terribly as a result and I welcomed the UK public inquiry to examine the circumstances of this tragedy. Addressing the financial hardship and suffering of those who have been infected and/or affected by contaminated blood in Northern Ireland was an immediate priority for me and shortly after taking up post as Health Minister I met with people affected to hear about their experiences, which has helped to shape my decisions.  Significant progress has been made, including increased payment rates for those who became infected with Hepatitis C and HIV and the introduction of annual financial support for bereaved spouses / partners. We now have greater parity of financial support across the UK, with the recent introduction of enhanced support for HCV stage 1 beneficiaries in NI. The NI Scheme will be kept under review and I have agreed with my UK counterparts that any future changes made to any Scheme will require consultation with the other UK administrations.Our people are at the centre of the health service and I believe there's a responsibility not just on the Department of Health NI but Government as a whole to do what we can to help those who have been infected and / or affected, in recognition of the devastating impact contaminated blood has had on their lives. Whilst in post I have remained committed to doing everything that I can to ensure they get the support they need and deserve. The Inquiry is expected to report within the coming year and I hope that my successor will honour the commitments that I have made in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) what work he has undertaken to progress the A24 Ballynahinch Bypass; (ii) the current status of the scheme; (iii) whether he has approved the business case; (iv) whether the statutory procedures have been cleared; and (v) whether funding has been secured.
answer_text: Development work on this scheme has substantially concluded including the preparation of a draft business case, which will be submitted for formal approval once there is certainty in relation to delivery timescales.The timescales for progression to procurement, construction and completion will depend upon the level of funding made available to my Department in future budget settlements.  Subject to funding being made available, it is estimated that it would take around 18 months to commence construction from any decision to move to procurement, and a further 18 months to complete construction of the scheme.A study is ongoing to consider how best to utilise the road and footway infrastructure and to explore the potential for further active travel opportunities in the town when the bypass is complete. The initial phase of this work including the identification of constraints and opportunities is due to complete in the autumn.The Direction Order for Ballynahinch Bypass was made on 16 July 2021.  The statutory processes for the scheme are now largely complete, however the Vesting Order for the scheme cannot be made until the necessary funding has been secured to deliver the project.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work his Department has done to implement the recommendations of the Independent Neurology Inquiry Report, including the timescales for such implementation; and whether finance has been made available for implementation.
answer_text: The Department is currently ensuring the Report of the Independent Neurology Inquiry receives the careful consideration it deserves, and appropriate governance arrangements will be put in place to ensure the recommendations are appropriately implemented. I intend to provide more detail on these arrangements in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for her assessment of the right of education staff to strike; and (ii) why staff may face pay deductions for doing so.
answer_text: I recognise that teachers have a right to vote for, and participate in, industrial action.  Teachers’ terms and conditions, including pay, are negotiated by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC). The management of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) is not within the remit of the Department. Management Side of the TNC includes representatives from the employers, sectoral interests and the Department of Education.  Teachers’ Side of the TNC includes representatives from the five main teaching unions (INTO, UTU, NAHT, NASUWT and NEU).I understand that TNC Management Side has undertaken an assessment of the industrial action and determined that the actions of those participating in the current action short of strike amounts to a breach of teachers’ contractual duties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the 2022/23 departmental budget; and whether this budget has been confirmed.
answer_text: In the absence of an Executive it has not been possible to agree a final 2022-25 Budget.  With no budget confirmed for 2022-23 the Finance Minister wrote to departments in March setting out a way forward which involved DoF issuing departments with contingency planning envelopes for the 2022-23 financial year.  These envelopes provided departments with an assessment of the minimum funding they could reasonably expect for 2022-23 and allowed departments to plan for expenditure until such times as a Budget could be agreed. I have agreed an approach for my Department to proceed with funding allocations which will continue to fund activity at current levels in 2022/23 while controlling spending in line with the advice from the Finance Minister. However there remains a great deal of uncertainty on our future financial position. Health and Social Care’s reliance in recent years on significant levels of non-recurrent funding means that we are expecting to face an extremely challenging financial outlook. While we are anticipating significant allocations for Health once a Budget is agreed the 2022/23 budget will continue to require careful managing in order to develop a break even position. As we move further through the year without certainty on our allocation, it is becoming increasingly difficult to balance the need to provide services with the need to ensure that the budget can be balanced.

answer_text: The Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) collates and publishes data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office. The information requested is provided in the table below. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work has been undertaken by her Department since 8 March 2021 to ban conversion therapy.
answer_text: Working closely with representatives of those most impacted is ongoing.The issue was discussed with the LGBTQI+ Co-design Group as part of the work on development of the Strategy. 	Policy work is continuing to inform the drafting of legislation. It is essential that legislation is robust and fit for purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when she will publish legislation to end conversion therapy.
answer_text: One of the serious consequences to the continued absence of a functioning Executive is that no new primary legislation can be progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when his Department will publish guidance for Health and Social Care Trusts on continuing healthcare.
answer_text: Guidance on Continuing Healthcare was circulated to Health and Social Care Trusts and published on the Department’s website on 18 May 2022. It is available at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-guide-healthcare-ni-18-may-2022.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the work his Department has done to recognise the Mournes as a regional asset and put in place an overarching management approach and resources.
answer_text: I have commissioned my Department’s Innovation Lab to lead extensive engagement in relation to the Mournes area.  This is to understand the needs and aspirations of all stakeholders and to build a collective view of the measures needed to secure its long-term future.I anticipate this will be completed in Spring 2023, and it will inform how the Executive might best support that long term view.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what work has been done by his Department's Innovation Lab to deliver a visitor management strategy in the Mournes.
answer_text: I have commissioned my Department’s Innovation Lab to lead extensive engagement in relation to the Mournes area to help build a collective view of the measures needed to secure its long-term future.  This will inform how the Executive might support the area into the future.  A visitor management strategy may be raised during this process of engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the reasons for the failure in the E-Referrals operating system; (ii) when she became aware of this failure; (iii) what action her Department took to address this failure; (iv) how many people were affected by this failure, broken down by benefit strand; and (v) how much money was overpaid to claimants in the period this system failed.
answer_text: The E-referrals system is owned and operated by Department for Work and Pensions (DWP).  My Department was made aware of three technical issues impacting the e-referrals system in July 2020 and officials began working with DWP at that time to progress a resolution.  Some of the issues impacted both DWP and DfC while others related specifically to DfC.  My officials fully engaged with DWP from July 2020 until July 2021 to progress and resolve these issues, including undertaking a full programme of testing as fixes were identified incrementally.  Fixes were applied from April 2021 with the e-referrals system fully operational from 21 July 2021 for all benefits

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has sought funding to commission the prevention strategy for wildfires in (i) the Mournes; and (ii) Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department together with our lead partner the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) are developing the Strategic Way Forward for addressing Wildfires in Northern Ireland using existing resources, no additional financial resources have been required or sought at this time. My Department is currently developing proposals to commission a range of wildfire management work and research projects across the Mournes and additional funding support will be sought for implementation of this work if required.  In the meantime my Department continues to support, through the Environment Fund, the delivery and implementation of the Eastern Mournes Wildfire Project.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce a cap on the amount that a family has to pay for Continuing Healthcare.
answer_text: As you are aware, the policy on continuing healthcare was updated as of February 2021, with the aim of creating a fairer system where all individuals needing to avail of residential or nursing home care services are subject to the same charging policy. This followed a full public consultation which was undertaken in 2017.The new policy is applicable as of the 11 February 2021 and involves a single eligibility question which is ‘can your care needs be properly met in any other setting other than a hospital?’, If the answer to this question is ‘yes’, then the individual will be discharged to the appropriate care setting and depending on the type of care package, be subject to the relevant charging policy. Information on the current charging policies can be found at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/guidance-charging-residential-accommodationAs you may be aware, a public consultation recently closed on 1 July, which sought views relating to proposals on the Reform of Adult Social Care. As outlined in the proposals, in relation to care home charging, the Department will be undertaking a detailed review to fully consider the advantages, disadvantages and impact of a variety of charging approaches. The review will come forward with recommendations for future charging approaches, including any proposed changes to cap and floor thresholds. In the interim, the Department will continue to focus on improving the amount and quality of service provision. Any future revision to the issue of charging will be dependent on the outcome of the consultation.  However it should be noted that, this reform package will require sustained additional investment and budgetary certainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how much each Health and Social Care Trust has spent on Continuing Healthcare in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The current Continuing Healthcare Policy for Northern Ireland was amended in February 2021 following a period of public consultation. The intention was to support regional consistency in decision making in regard to continuing healthcare across all Health and Social Care Trusts. The current guidance introduced a single eligibility criteria question for continuing healthcare: can your care needs be properly met in any other setting other than a hospital?The Guidance also provides a framework for decisions to be based around some core principles: a)  As far as possible, hospitals should not be places where people live, unless their needs cannot be met outside of a hospital setting and the key aim for everyone that does not need to be in hospital for a longer period of time is to get them well enough to return to whatever setting is most suitable for them in the community while ensuring that their health and social care needs are supported.b) Assessment of individuals, and decision making, in relation to continuing healthcare should be transparent. c) Communication with individuals, families and carers should be appropriate and clear. d) Assessment and decision making for individuals should be timely.In answer to your question on how much each Health and Social Care Trust has spent on Continuing Healthcare in each of the last five years,my officials contacted the five Health and Social Care Trusts. I wish to advise that there has been no Continuing Health Care expenditure   over the past five years. I am confident that the current Continuing Healthcare Policy for Northern Ireland still provides a clear and easily understood test that supports a consistent and standardised approach to continuing healthcare outcomes across Northern Ireland. The key aim for everyone that does not need to be in hospital for a longer period of time is to get them well enough to return to whatever setting is most suitable for them in the community while ensuring that their health and social care needs are supported.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people in each Health and Social Care Trust were in receipt of funding for continuing healthcare provision in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This information is not held centrally so the information was requested from each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT). There was no one in receipt of continuing healthcare in each of the HSC Trusts in the past 5 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for a breakdown of the work completed to date to deliver a prevention strategy for wildfires in (i) the Mournes; and (ii) Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department together with our lead partners the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) are progressing towards a Strategic Way Forward for Wildfires and to date have established a cross departmental NI Strategic Wildfire Group and Wildfire Stakeholder Forum. This work has included a range of consultations and workshops with key Government Departments and Agencies and non-government land owner/manager organisations. Through these consultations proposed goals, themes and recommendations for actions for a strategic way forward in Northern Ireland have been drafted. Regarding the Mournes, an Eastern Mournes Wildfire Plan has been developed implemented and reviewed. Other work to develop further wildfire plans for the Mournes is in progress. My department has provided substantial funding to a number of eNGOs, over the last year, to facilitate wildfire recovery and mitigation in the Mournes which includes managed grazing on Slieve Donard and management interventions at key locations within the Mournes to reduce the risk and impacts of wildfires.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will instigate a review into the removal of asbestos from schools in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department manages asbestos in schools in accordance with the Control of Asbestos Regulations (CAR) (NI) 2012. The Health and Safety Executive advises that if asbestos containing materials are in good condition, then they should be left undisturbed, and a system of management introduced.  A system of management is in place in all school sectors to manage the risk to ensure that as far as reasonably practicable no one can come to harm from asbestos on the premises. As a result, there are currently no plans to instigate a review into the removal of Asbestos from schools in Northern Ireland.The system of management includes monitoring the condition of and keeping up to date records of the condition and location of any known asbestos containing materials in the school, assessing risks from the materials and making plans to manage those risks. These plans must be put into action and should any deterioration of the materials occur and based on the assessed risk, removal, repair or encapsulation must be carried out by a licensed asbestos company. Every school in the schools’ estate, where asbestos is present, has an asbestos register that gives detail of any asbestos containing materials known to be present in the school. The information to populate these registers was obtained from asbestos surveys of the schools’ estate. Following the surveys any asbestos that was deemed to pose a ‘high risk’ was removed by a licensed asbestos company. It must also be ensured that anyone who is likely to work on, or disturb, the asbestos is provided with information about its location and condition. The school sectors follow these recommendations and manage the asbestos in the estate rather than remove it unless this is deemed necessary.
