question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Chief Nursing Officer will lead work to co-design a new framework in partnership with the care home sector for the provision of clinical care.
answer_text: On 17th June 2020 I asked the then Chief Nursing Officer to work in partnership with the independent care home sector to coproduce a framework to further enhance the high standard of clinical care provided in care homes. Work on the Enhancing Clinical Care for Care Homes Project, including the production of a new co-designed framework, continues under the remit of the current Chief Nursing Officer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what cross-departmental actions can be taken, in the absence of an Executive, to address health inequality.
answer_text: Making Life Better (MLB) is the Executive’s the overarching strategic framework to improve health and address health inequalities. It was developed based on the evidence that health and wellbeing, and health inequalities, are shaped by many factors including age, family, community, workplace, beliefs and traditions, economics, and physical and social environments. Many of these are outside the control of the Department of Health.Reducing inequalities in health remains a huge challenge. Through MLB, and across a range of key issues, my Department continues to collaborate across departments and agencies, local government, the community and voluntary sector, and with communities themselves to address the factors which impact on health and wellbeing locally and regionally.We are also working with colleagues from the Public Health Agency to further build community development capacity and approaches within Northern Ireland. Community development is a long-term, value-based process which aims to redress imbalance and bring about change founded on social justice, equality, and inclusion.I also recently participated in engagement with my Ministerial colleagues on a Four Nations basis on this topic, as health inequalities are a challenge faced right across the UK. There is a shared commitment to tackling this issue as we emerge from the COVID pandemic and respond to the rising cost of living. There is already significant work underway, and we agreed to share learnings and consider new opportunities to work together.Through continued strengthened co-ordination and partnership working in a whole system approach, we will seek to create the conditions for individuals and communities to take control of their own lives and move towards a vision for Northern Ireland where all people are enabled and supported in achieving their full health and wellbeing potential.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will undertake a review of health inequality indicators.
answer_text: Over the past 20 years, the Northern Ireland Health and Social Care Inequalities Monitoring System (HSCIMS) has been constantly reviewed and expanded to include additional indicators to meet user demand and make best use of new data sources. In the last two years, new indicators including babies born small for gestational age, COVID-19 mortality and a range of dental health indicators have been developed and published within the Health Inequalities Annual Reports.Additional health inequality analyses are available from the Health Survey Northern Ireland (HSNI) (https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/hsni-trend-tables-20-21.xlsx).The Department works closely with partners within the wider HSC, including the Public Health Agency, and with local councils to identify information gaps and opportunities for data and indicator development.A number of health inequality indicators are included as key indicators within the Making Life Better (MLB) public health strategic framework. As part of the forthcoming review of MLB, an assessment of the suitability and appropriateness of the indicators is being examined with a view to utilise new information sources.The HSCIMS only includes robust indicators produced from reliable, high quality data sources in line with the UK Statistics Authority Code of Practice standards for production of official statistics.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions have been taken to implement the No More Silos plan since its publication in 2020.
answer_text: The No More Silos Action Plan published in October 2020 allowed some of the emerging conclusions from the Review of the Urgent and Emergency to be implemented in light of the COVID-19 pandemic.  Overseen by the clinically-led No More Silos Network, this work has included:Urgent Care Centres being operationalised in a number of Trusts, providing alternatives to Emergency Departments for patients who require urgent treatment but are not facing an immediately life threatening illness;  The introduction of localised Phone First services in the Northern, Southern and Western Trust areas, with interim services also available in Lagan Valley and Downe hospitals;  New rapid access and treatment pathways being introduced across all five Trusts; and  The allocation of funding to support and develop a number of programmes, including: scheduling unscheduled care; Hospital at Home; ambulance arrival and handover zones; an enhanced framework for care homes; and timely discharge. Once the consultation on the urgent and emergency care review has concluded, it is expected that No More Silos will be included within a wider overarching governance structure for the implementation of the recommendations arising from the Review.It is important to recognise that there is no quick fix to the issues that we are currently seeing in our Emergency Departments – what is needed is recurrent investment over a number of years to improve capacity and bring forward meaningful change.  My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.  Funding elements of the health service on a month to month or quarter to quarter basis is not an efficient or effective way to build for the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why Health and Social Care staff are limited in the mileage expenses that they can claim.
answer_text: Mileage rates are designed to reimburse the individual for use of their vehicle for business use. The 56p per mile rate is payable for the first 3,500 miles each year of business use under AfC Terms and Conditions. This rate was designed to compensate for normal wear and tear on a vehicle, depreciation, and to contribute to insurance costs and the road fund licence, in addition to an amount for fuel. After 3,500 miles have been exceeded, the rate of reimbursement of 20p per mile is towards fuel costs only. While reviewed in June and November each year, despite fluctuating fuel costs the rates have remained unchanged since July 2014, when petrol prices were 131.3 in Northern Ireland and 129.9 on average throughout the UK.Staff are limited only to claiming actual miles incurred in business use, and by HMRC regulations which permit up to 10,000 miles at a rate of 45p per mile or less, and 25p per mile thereafter, to be paid free of tax. Where any employee receives less than these approved rates, they are eligible to apply to HMRC for tax relief for the difference.In light of the recent significant increases in fuel costs, my Department, Employers and Trade Unions have been involved in national and local discussions regarding a potential review of the current Agenda for Change (AfC) Mileage Rates. The national discussions have been led through the NHS Staff Council and have been exploring the options in relation to mileage reimbursement rates. Unfortunately it has not been possible to conclude a national agreement on an increase in fuel reimbursement rates.In light of no nationally agreed position, temporary increases have been agreed in Scotland and Wales until the end of July and June respectively. At the time of those agreements, no decision could be made locally due to election period restrictions. My Department is, however, urgently considering options for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 368/17-22, to detail the costs his Department has incurred since the publication of the Final report from the Fishing and Seafood Development Programme on 07 May 2021.
answer_text: Since, 07 May 2021, my Department has incurred costs totalling £17,027.02 in preparing the Strategic Outline Case necessary to allow the Department secure approval to procure the Integrated Consultant Teams (ICTs) for the proposed Ardglass and Kilkeel investments.  		As a result, my Department has since secured approval to spend between £1.065m and £1.470m in respect of the procurement of ICTs for the Ardglass and Kilkeel works and at least £213,000 of this funding is profiled to be incurred within the current financial year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many staff from his Department are currently in place to work alongside staff from the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, the Central Procurement Directorate and the Northern Ireland Fishery Harbour Authority to develop the tendering specification process for delivery of the Fisheries and Seafood Development Programme.
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs has requested support from Construction & Procurement Delivery (CPD) to take forward the Fisheries and Seafood Development Programme. Currently four CPD staff are working with the Programme team to prepare the tender documentation for the procurement of consultants. The number of CPD staff working on the Programme will vary to meet the demands of the Programme as the individual projects progress through the design, business case, planning, contractor procurement and construction phases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 368/22-27, to detail (i) when the tendering specification process will be complete; and (ii) whether the procurement of the Integrated Consultant Teams remains on course to be completed by the end of 2022.
answer_text: My Department expects to sign off the tendering specification documentation and launch the Pre-Qualification Questionnaire by the end of July. The process from launch to the evaluation of invitation to tender responses and appointment of Integrated Consultant Teams will take approximately six months, meaning appointment end December 2022/early January 2023.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the costs his Department has incurred resulting from actions relating to the Fishing and Seafood Development Programme report of 7 May 2021.
answer_text: My Department has not incurred any costs associated with the actions relating to the Fishing and Seafood Development Programme report of 7 May 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 368/22-27, to detail how many staff his Department has currently in place to develop the tendering specification process.
answer_text: One member of DAERA’s staff is responsible for progressing the tendering process in partnership with the NI Fisheries Harbour Authority (NIFHA) and DoF’s Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD). As you will be aware from previous responses on this subject, this element of work has now been largely completed. 	DAERA’s role over the next few months will be to ensure the tenders launch in a timely fashion; facilitate technical discussions between the NIFHA and CPD as required; and, that the work is consistent with both the Strategic Outline Case and the approved budget. During the tendering phase, this work will be delivered within Marine and Fisheries Division’s existing staffing complement with one official leading the process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what funding has been made available for the Glens of Antrim Medical Practice in Carnlough.
answer_text: This information would be classed as commercial in confidence and could not be released unless the Practice specifically gave the SPPG permission to release it.I can confirm that the Glens of Antrim Medical Centre continues to receive funding on the same basis as every other GP practice across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what is the status of the Glens of Antrim Medical Practice in Carnlough.
answer_text: The Glens of Antrim Medical Practice has a full time surgery operating from the village of Cushendall and a part time branch surgery currently operating 2 sessions per week in the village of Carnlough.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what discussions have been held between his Department and the Glens of Antrim Medical Practice regarding services in Carnlough.
answer_text: The Practice have been offered additional recurrent funding for a nurse to provide treatment room services at the Carnlough premises. This would be of particular benefit to older patients - for instance for blood pressure checks. The available funding would enable up to five half day treatment room sessions every week at either Carnlough or the main surgery premises at Cushendall.For those patients who cannot make it into the main or the branch surgery, house calls will still be offered by the Practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there is scope to extend the opening hours of the Glens of Antrim Medical Practice in Carnlough.
answer_text: The decision to reduce the hours of operation in Carnlough Branch Surgery was a Practice decision. The Practice could decide to extend its operating hours in the Branch at any time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what steps are being taken to correct the Business Services Organisation's underpayment of staff mileage allowance in May 2022; (ii) how many staff were affected; (iii) how much money is owed to staff; and (iv) how quickly this will be rectified.
answer_text: The Business Services Organisation notified the employer (South Eastern HSC Trust) and initiated an off-cycle payment for the impacted staff for the variance.  This will be received into the individual bank accounts on Tuesday 7th June.This issue affected 173 staff members.c.£28k in total across the 173 staff members, averaging c.£160 per staff member.The May salaries were paid on Friday 27th May.  Affected staff will receive an off cycle for the underpayment.  The  first available off cycle payment was initiated straight away and the payment will be received into individual bank accounts on 7th June (delay due to two bank holidays this week).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether daycare staff, who were redeployed to a care setting, are eligible for the £500 Special Recognition Payment.
answer_text: Day care staff, who were redeployed to a role in a health and social care setting during the qualifying period and who meet the criteria, are eligible for the Special Recognition Payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether daycare staff were eligible to apply for the £500 Special Recognition Payment.
answer_text: Day care employees, who worked for a registered provider who hold a contract with a statutory Health and Social Care organisation, were eligible for the £500 Special Recognition Payment. On 11 May 2022, my officials also wrote to the Trusts to invite claims from unregulated providers of day opportunities for people with learning disabilities.As at 29 June 2022, a total of over £16.67m has been paid out under Phase 1 to independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers covering 28,470 individuals. Over £4.49m has been paid out to 10,399 individuals under Phase 2; Payments to eligible day opportunity and buildings-based day service providers so far, include – 38 claims, with 897 claimants totalling £504,486.24.  It should also be noted that where a provider offers services that cross between sectors they may have included eligible staff under an umbrella claim rather than claiming individually for specific service types.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many ad-hoc members of staff have received the Special Recognition Payment.
answer_text: Agency staff employed for shift work on an ad-hoc basis were not eligible for the scheme.When the Special Recognition Payment was proposed, I specifically requested this scheme be extended to include agency workers on longer term placements.The eligibility criteria for this were that individual workers must have covered one post for a period of 12 weeks or more, and on a salary commensurate with the role covered. The 12 week qualifying period adopted the same principle established under employment law for equal treatment for agency workers.While it is appreciated that a number of individual employees may not have met the qualifying criteria, the design of the scheme reflected my intention to recognise the contribution of agency workers on longer term placements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when payments under Phase 2 of the Special Recognition Payment scheme will resume.
answer_text: Phase 2 had been briefly paused to allow time to seek clarity on a number of issues raised relating to the impact of the increase in national insurance contributions which are included as part of the payment provided to the former employer.  A further letter was issued to employers on 14 April 2022 providing clarity on the issues raised and confirming recommencement of the payment process. Claims are being processed by BSO in date order when all relevant information is provided by the claimants. No date has been set for the closure of the scheme and claims will continue to be processed by the Business Services Organisation during 2022/23. I can confirm that as at 26 May 2022, a total of over £16.6m has been paid out under Phase 1 to independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers covering 28,457 individuals. As at the same date over £3.3m has been paid out to 7,999 individuals under Phase 2; these payments include staff previously employed in independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers who have since moved employer or left the sector, and staff employed with eligible day opportunity providers and buildings-based day services.In addition we have also opened the Scheme to eligible applicants in the following groups, Personal Assistants employed by Independent Living Fund recipients, Thalidomide Health Grant recipients, and Personal Assistants employed by those in receipt of Direct Payments from HSC Trusts. This element of the scheme was launched on 23 March and is being delivered by ILF Scotland for the payments, Scotland Excel who set up and run the online portal, and SDS Scotland who manage the dedicated helpline. These delivery partners were heavily involved in delivering the equivalent scheme for the Scottish Government.  To date £300k has been paid to 863 individuals. To encourage uptake on this element Trusts have now written to all individuals in receipt of direct payments. My officials are also finalising arrangements to include eligible staff whose employers are no longer trading and I hope to be in a position to include this cohort of individuals in the coming weeks.A FAQ on the Special Recognition Payment has been published on the Department’s website as below:-https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/hsc-staff-recognition-payment-faqsThis continues to be updated with relevant information as each phase of the claims process progresses. I remain personally committed to ensuring the recognition payment continues to be paid to independent sector staff as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people living with dementia, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Dementia is one of the disease registers in the Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF); QOF disease prevalence data is published annually and the dementia register contains the number of people diagnosed with dementia.  As at 31st March 2022, the most recent data available, there were 13,283 people on the dementia register.  QOF data is not broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, but can be broken down by Local Commissioning Group (LCG); this data is shown in Table 1 overleaf. Further information is available at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/prevalence-statistics.Table 1 Number of people on the dementia register, by LCG, at 31st March 2022.LCGNumber of people on the QOF Dementia Register, 31st March 2022 Belfast2,895 South Eastern2,527 Northern2,850 Southern2,363 Western2,648Northern Ireland13,283Source: Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will publish the results of the consultation on Reshaping Stroke Care, which closed in August 2019.
answer_text: My Department received in excess of 19,000 responses to the ‘Reshaping Stroke Care Consultation’. In the time since the consultation closed, I have been giving careful consideration to the consultation analysis, the evidence base for reform and emerging evidence on stroke treatments. Having done so, I can advise that it is my intention to set out the way forward for the reform of stroke care in the coming weeks and that the Reshaping Stroke Care consultation analysis will be published alongside this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will outline the revised targets and timescales for progressing the Reshaping Stroke Care commitments that his Department made in the consultation document in March 2019.
answer_text: My Department received in excess of 19,000 responses to the ‘Reshaping Stroke Care Consultation’. In the time since the consultation closed, I have been giving careful consideration to the consultation analysis, the evidence base for reform and emerging evidence on stroke treatments. Having done so, I can advise that it is my intention to set out the way forward for the reform of stroke care in the coming weeks and that the Reshaping Stroke Care consultation analysis will be published alongside this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what was the payment threshold that ad-hoc staff needed to earn to qualify for the £500 Special Recognition Payment.
answer_text: To be eligible to receive the payment, staff must have worked normal contracted hours as part of a contract of employment for a minimum of one continuous period of 4 weeks between 17/3/2020 and 31/1/2021.  Staff on a zero hours contract in the relevant time period will also be considered eligible.Where part time staff have at least one month’s continuous service during the period 17 March 2020 and 31 January 2021, a pro rata calculation is applicable and the payment will be worked out according to their average hours during the qualifying period. To get the maximum Special Recognition Payment an individual will need to have worked full time hours for the whole qualifying period.Anyone who was off work shielding or on any form of maternity/shared parental/adoption leave is treated for the purposes of the payment as having been in work, and will have their contracted hours reflected in their paymentA FAQ on the Special Recognition Payment has been published on the Department’s website as below:-https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/hsc-staff-recognition-payment-faqsThis continues to be updated with relevant information as each phase of the claims process progresses. I remain personally committed to ensuring the recognition payment will be paid to independent sector staff as soon as it is practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the funding for the Fishing and Seafood Development Programme has been secured.
answer_text: Securing sufficient capital funding for the Fishery Harbour Development Programme is vital.  It will have to be in place by the time that final approvals and decisions to proceed with construction are taken.  This will be after the Outline Business Case stage which is likely to be 2024 at the earliest.  At present sources for this capital funding have not yet been identified or secured but my Department continues to actively explore potential sources of funding.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on phase 2 of the Health and Social Care Special Recognition Payment.
answer_text: Phase 2 had been temporarily paused to allow officials to seek clarity on a number of issues raised relating to the impact of the increase in national insurance contributions which are included as part of the payment provided to the former employer.  A further letter was issued to employers on 14 April 2022 providing clarity on the issues raised and confirming recommencement of the payment process. Claims are being processed by BSO in date order when all relevant information is provided by the claimants. No date has been set for the closure of the scheme and claims will continue to be processed by the Business Services Organisation during 2022/23. I am not in a position to provide a date as to when all payments will be made.I can confirm that as at 18 May 2022, a total of over £16.6m has been paid out under Phase 1 to independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers covering 28,457 individuals. As at the same date over £2.8m has been paid out to 6,847 individuals under Phase 2; these payments include staff previously employed in independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers who have since moved employer or left the sector, and staff employed with eligible day opportunity providers and buildings-based day services.In addition we have also opened the Scheme to eligible applicants in the following groups, Personal Assistants employed by Independent Living Fund recipients, Thalidomide Health Grant recipients, and Personal Assistants employed by those in receipt of Direct Payments from HSC Trusts. This element of the scheme was launched on 23 March and is being delivered by ILF Scotland for the payments, Scotland Excel who set up and run the online portal, and SDS Scotland who manage the dedicated helpline. These delivery partners were heavily involved in delivering the equivalent scheme for the Scottish Government.  To date £300k has been paid to 863 individuals. To encourage uptake on this element Trusts have now written to all individuals in receipt of direct payments. My officials are also finalising arrangements to include eligible staff whose employers are no longer trading and I hope to be in a position to include this cohort of individuals in the coming weeks.A FAQ on the Special Recognition Payment has been published on the Department’s website as below:-https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/hsc-staff-recognition-payment-faqsThis continues to be updated with relevant information as each phase of the claims process progresses. I remain personally committed to ensuring the recognition payment will be paid to independent sector staff as soon as it is practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the guidance issued for independent agency staff on the £500 Special Recognition Payment, why ad-hoc members of staff were not eligible for the scheme.
answer_text: When the Special Recognition Payment was proposed, I specifically requested this scheme be extended to include agency workers on longer term placements.The eligibility criteria for this were that individual workers must have covered one post for a period of 12 weeks or more, and on a salary commensurate with the role covered. The 12 week qualifying period adopted the same principle established under employment law for equal treatment for agency workers.While it is appreciated that a number of individual employees may not have met the qualifying criteria, the design of the scheme reflected my intention to recognise the contribution of agency workers on longer term placements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the Fishing and Seafood Development Programme; and (ii) the funding allocations for the Programme.
answer_text: The Department of Finance approved the Strategic Outline Case in October 2021, but it only allows my Department to incur resource expenditure on work required to inform the later Outline Business Cases (OBC) upon which any later decisions to proceed with infrastructure development will be based.  We are currently working with Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD) of the Department of Finance and the NI Fishery Harbour Authority (NIFHA) to develop the tender specifications to procure Integrated Consultant Teams (ICTs) in relation to Ardglass and Kilkeel.   Due to the scale of the costs for the works proposed for each harbour a two stage procurement will have to be undertaken and we expect to complete procurement by the end of 2022. Proposals for Portavogie are anticipated to be of a more modest scale that could be funded through conventional fisheries support funding. One of the main pieces of work for the ICTs will be to carry out detailed environmental and technical studies, including preparation of Habitats Regulations Assessments and Environmental Statements, and work required to gain planning and marine licensing approvals and consents.In relation to funding allocations, the current estimated capital costs for the proposed works at Ardglass and Kilkeel are £31-£35m and £73m-£91m, and these will have to be updated again after completion of the technical and environmental studies and OBC stage (likely to be 2024 at the earliest).   It is only after the OBC stage, once decisions are taken to construct, and final approvals and consents are obtained, that budget allocations can be made.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: Approximately £92.2m of the £118m allocated by the Department for the Special Recognition Payment has been spent at June 1st 2022, across the following categories of staff CATEGORYExpenditure to Dateat 1st June 2022Payroll58.9mAgency2.2mFHS10.5mHospice / Palliative382kIndependent Sector 20.2mTOTAL SPEND TO DATE92.18mIn addition, we have also opened the Scheme to eligible applicants in the following groups, Personal Assistants employed by Independent Living Fund recipients, Thalidomide Health Grant recipients, and Personal Assistants employed by those in receipt of Direct Payments from HSC Trusts. This element of the scheme was launched on 23 March and is being delivered by ILF Scotland for the payments, Scotland Excel who set up and run the online portal, and SDS Scotland who manage the dedicated helpline. These delivery partners were heavily involved in delivering the equivalent scheme for the Scottish Government.  To date £419k has been paid.  My officials are also finalising arrangements to include eligible staff whose employers are no longer trading and I hope to be in a position to include this cohort of individuals in the coming weeks.A FAQ on the Special Recognition Payment has been published on the Department’s website as below:-https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/hsc-staff-recognition-payment-faqsThis continues to be updated with relevant information as each phase of the claims process progresses. I remain personally committed to ensuring the recognition payment continues to be paid to independent sector staff as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the areas currently under consideration by the Medicines Management Team; and (ii) plans for its future work programme.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) Pharmacy and Medicines Management Team (PMMT) leads on improving and standardising the safety, quality and cost effectiveness of medicines’ use in primary care. In relation to Northern Ireland’s 528 Community Pharmacies the PMMT oversees all aspects of clinical governance, contract management, and service development,The operational objectives for the PMMT in 2022/23 are outlined below. This includes (i) areas currently under consideration by the PMMT and (ii) plans for its future work programmeTo ensure financial management of the pharmaceutical budget. To develop and implement a prescribing efficiency plan which will deliver targeted efficiencies on the Northern Ireland Primary Care prescribing budget by end March 2023 and beyond.To commission Community Pharmacy Services in line with the Contract FrameworkTo manage and develop the Community Pharmacy contract which will lead to quality improvement in service provision.To support the Department of Health (DoH) Controlled Drugs Accountable Officer (CDAO) to fulfil the requirements of the legislation with regard to governance for Controlled Drugs.To provide on-going input into commissioning processes as relevant.To maintain and develop the Northern Ireland Medicines Formulary and manage a standardised mechanism for managed entry of medicines to Health and Social Care (HSC) in Northern Ireland To support the delivery of regional vaccination programmes which  include the Childhood Vaccination Programme, Annual Flu Vaccination Programme and the COVID-19 Vaccination Programmes, through maintaining and developing Patient Group Directions (PGDs) and associated guidanceTo support clinical governance with relation to medicines management in primary careTo implement the DoH Five Year ‘Transforming medication safety in Northern Ireland’ planTo ensure best practice in the safe, effective and efficient use of medicines by resource development, monitoring and education provision as well as engagement at an individual practice level with General Practitioners and Community PharmacyTo support the development and implementation of New Models of Prescribing (NMOP) which will enable patients to receive medicines prescribed by the most appropriate healthcare professional through independent prescribing.Further detail on any specific area can be provided as requested.
